## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define the term "Software Process". List any four generic activities of a software process framework.*

**Model Answer:**

A *software process* is the set of **activities, methods, practices, and transformations** that developers use to create and maintain software and its associated artifacts. It defines *who does what, when, and how* to deliver a quality product.

**Four generic activities of the process framework (Pressman):**

1. **Communication** — Understanding stakeholder requirements.
2. **Planning** — Defining scope, schedule, resources, and risks.
3. **Modeling** — Creating analysis and design representations of the system.
4. **Construction** — Code generation and testing.
5. **Deployment** — Delivering software to the customer for evaluation and use.

*(Mention any four — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Explain the three layers of software engineering with a neat diagram.*

**Model Answer:**

Software engineering is built on **three layers**, each supporting the one above it:

1. **Process Layer (Foundation):** Defines the framework — *what, when, and by whom* activities are performed. It ensures quality, predictability, and control.
2. **Method Layer (Middle):** Provides the *technical how-to* — analysis techniques, design methods, coding standards, and testing procedures.
3. **Tools Layer (Top):** Provides *automated support* for the process and methods — CASE tools, IDEs, version control, and test automation.

> **One-line mnemonic:** *Tools support Methods; Methods conform to Process; Process ensures Quality.*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit the diagram** when explaining the layered architecture — this costs 2–3 marks.
> - In COCOMO questions, students forget to **state the project type** (organic / semi-detached / embedded) — *auto-deduction of 1 mark*.
> - In DRE problems, students forget to **convert to percentage** — *lose 0.5–1 mark*.
> - Always write the **formula first, then substitute, then compute** — partial marks are awarded for each step.
> - Do NOT write "process is just coding" — process is a **management + technical + support** system, not just code.

---

### 📝 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**Q.A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain the **layered technology** of software engineering in detail. Discuss the role of the **Process Layer** with the **five generic process framework activities** and **umbrella activities**. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* During testing, a team found **320 errors before release** and **20 defects after release**. Calculate **DRE**. If the project size is estimated at **5 KLOC** (organic mode), compute **Effort** and **Development Time** using **Basic COCOMO**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Software engineering is a *layered technology* comprising Process, Methods, and Tools, all bound together by a commitment to **quality**. The Process Layer is the foundation.

**Process Layer — Role (1 mark):**
The Process Layer defines the framework of activities — sequence, deliverables, milestones, and quality checkpoints — that must occur for the software to be built systematically. It answers *what, when, and by whom* but not *how*.

**Five Generic Activities (3 marks — 0.6 each):**
1. **Communication** — Stakeholder interaction, requirement elicitation → SRS.
2. **Planning** — Estimation (COCOMO/FP), scheduling (PERT/Gantt), risk planning → Project plan.
3. **Modeling** — Analysis & design models → UML, DFD, SDD.
4. **Construction** — Code generation and testing (unit, integration) → Source code, test reports.
5. **Deployment** — Delivery, support, user training → Production system, feedback.

**Umbrella Activities (1 mark):**
Cross-cutting activities that span all 5 activities: **SQA, SCM, FTR (reviews), Measurement, Risk Management**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Without a robust process layer, methods and tools are used chaotically — leading to missed deadlines, budget overruns, and defective products. The process layer is the **discipline-enabler** of software engineering.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State DRE formula (1 mark).**

$$DRE = \frac{E}{E + D}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark).**

$$DRE = \frac{320}{320 + 20} = \frac{320}{340}$$

**Step 3 — Compute and convert (1 mark).**

$$DRE = 0.9412 = 94.12\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
A DRE of 94.12% indicates a **good process maturity** but not yet top-quartile. For safety-critical systems, target is **> 99%**.

**Step 5 — State Basic COCOMO for organic mode (1 mark):**

$$E = a \times (KLOC)^b, \quad a = 2.4, \quad b = 1.05$$

**Step 6 — Compute Effort (1 mark).**

$$E = 2.4 \times (5)^{1.05} = 2.4 \times 5.383 = 12.92 \text{ PM}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Development Time (1 mark):**

$$D = c \times E^d, \quad c = 2.5, \quad d = 0.38$$
$$D = 2.5 \times (12.92)^{0.38} = 2.5 \times 2.793 = 6.98 \text{ Months} \approx 7 \text{ Months}$$

**Final Answer:** $DRE = 94.12\%$, $E \approx 12.92$ PM, $D \approx 7$ Months.

---

### 📝 Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Describe the **five generic activities** of a software process framework. How are **umbrella activities** different from these generic activities? Explain with examples. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A project has the following EVM data: **PV = ₹80,000, EV = ₹70,000, AC = ₹90,000**. Compute **SV, CV, CPI, SPI** and comment on the project health. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**Five Generic Activities (4 marks):**
1. **Communication** — *Why:* Reduces requirement defects. *How:* Interviews, use cases, user stories. *Output:* SRS.
2. **Planning** — *Why:* Predicts cost/time. *How:* WBS, COCOMO, FP. *Output:* Project plan.
3. **Modeling** — *Why:* Validates design before coding. *How:* UML, DFD. *Output:* SDD.
4. **Construction** — *Why:* Produces working code. *How:* OOP, TDD, unit tests. *Output:* Code + test logs.
5. **Deployment** — *Why:* Delivers value. *How:* CI/CD, training. *Output:* Production system.

**Difference from Umbrella Activities (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Generic Activities | Umbrella Activities |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Sequence | Sequential, one after another | Parallel, span all 5 activities |
| Scope | Each focuses on one phase | Cross-cutting concern |
| Examples | Communication, Planning | SQA, SCM, Risk Management |
| Trigger | Phase entry/exit | Continuous |
| Purpose | Build the product | Ensure quality/control throughout |

**Example (1 mark):**
- *Generic:* Writing a unit test belongs to **Construction**.
- *Umbrella:* Conducting an FTR (Formal Technical Review) on that test belongs to **Umbrella (Quality)** — it reviews *any* work product, regardless of phase.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State formulas (2 marks).**

$$SV = EV - PV$$
$$CV = EV - AC$$
$$CPI = \frac{EV}{AC}$$
$$SPI = \frac{EV}{PV}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute SV (1 mark).**

$$SV = 70,000 - 80,000 = -\text{₹}10,000$$

**Step 3 — Compute CV (1 mark).**

$$CV = 70,000 - 90,000 = -\text{₹}20,000$$

**Step 4 — Compute CPI (1 mark).**

$$CPI = \frac{70,000}{90,000} = 0.778$$

**Step 5 — Compute SPI (1 mark).**

$$SPI = \frac{70,000}{80,000} = 0.875$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

Both $SV$ and $CV$ are **negative**, and both $CPI$ and $SPI$ are **< 1**. The project is **behind schedule AND over budget** — a critical state requiring immediate corrective action (scope reduction, resource reallocation, or process re-engineering).

**Final Verdict:** *Project is in the **trouble zone** — both schedule and cost performances are sub-1.0.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Bonus Tips (Frequently Asked in Viva)

| # | Question | Crisp Answer |
|---|----------|--------------|
| 1 | What is the Process Layer? | The foundation of SE that defines the framework, sequence, and control of activities. |
| 2 | Why is Process needed even in Agile? | Agile is itself a *defined process* (Sprint cycle, roles, ceremonies). |
| 3 | What are Umbrella Activities? | Cross-cutting activities (SQA, SCM, FTR) that span all process activities. |
| 4 | Name the 5 generic activities. | Communication, Planning, Modeling, Construction, Deployment. |
| 5 | Difference between Process Model and Process Framework? | Framework = generic skeleton of 5 activities. Model = specific instantiation (Waterfall, Agile, etc.). |
| 6 | What is DRE? | Defect Removal Efficiency = (errors found before release) / (total errors). |
| 7 | What is COCOMO used for? | Effort, time, and cost estimation based on code size. |

> **🎯 KTU 2024 — Last-Minute Revision Pack**

- ✅ Software Engineering is a **3-layered technology**: Process → Methods → Tools, all under a **Quality** umbrella.
- ✅ The **Process Layer** is the **foundation** — it defines the *what, when, and who* of software creation.
- ✅ The **5 Generic Process Activities** are: **Communication, Planning, Modeling, Construction, Deployment** — memorize in this exact order.
- ✅ **Umbrella activities** are *cross-cutting* and continuous: **SQA, SCM, FTR, Measurement, Risk Management**.
- ✅ **Process Framework** is *generic*; **Process Model** is *specific* (Waterfall, Spiral, Agile, etc.).
- ✅ **DRE formula:** $DRE = \dfrac{E}{E + D}$; aim for **> 95%** in mature organizations, **> 99%** in safety-critical.
- ✅ **Basic COCOMO organic coefficients:** $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$.
- ✅ **EVM metrics:** Positive $SV$ = ahead of schedule; Positive $CV$ = under budget; $CPI > 1$ = healthy; $SPI > 1$ = healthy.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$; values **> 10** = high-risk module, refactor.
- ✅ **CMMI 5 levels:** Initial → Managed → Defined → Quantitatively Managed → Optimizing.
- ✅ **Agile ≠ no process** — Agile has its own well-defined process (Scrum/Kanban).
- ✅ The Process Layer is **indispensable** in *all* software projects — Waterfall, Agile, DevOps, or V-Model.

> **Golden Rule for KTU Exam:**
> *"Always state the formula → substitute the values → show step-by-step calculation → give a final boxed answer with units and interpretation. This ensures full marks."* ✍️
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Software Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme), a **Method** (also called a **Software Process Model**, **Software Development Methodology**, or **Software Life Cycle Model**) is a **systematic, abstract representation of a software process**. It defines a prescribed order of activities, the deliverables produced, the milestones reached, and the criteria for progression from one phase to another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A method provides a *roadmap* — a framework that prescribes how software engineering work should be organized, ordered, and managed. It is an *abstraction* of a real process; a particular organization will instantiate it to create a concrete process.

Three key terms must be distinguished in the KTU syllabus:

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Method / Model** | The abstract framework (e.g., Waterfall) |
| **Process** | The actual instantiation in a project (who does what, when) |
| **Methodology** | The collection of methods, procedures, tools, and documentation |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe & Cooking Style" Analogy

Imagine you want to cook a dish. You have two things to decide:
1. **The recipe (Method)** — a known style of cooking: baking, frying, slow-cooking, or making a layered cake.
2. **The actual kitchen process** — your chef, your ingredients, the timing in your kitchen.

- The **Waterfall method** is like baking a cake in strict order: mix → pour → bake → decorate → serve. You cannot easily go back and "un-bake" the cake if the sugar was wrong.
- The **Incremental method** is like serving a meal in courses: soup, then starter, then main. Each course adds value.
- The **Evolutionary/Spiral method** is like developing a prototype recipe: you cook, taste, adjust, cook again — refining the dish in repeated cycles.
- The **Agile method** is like a food truck responding to live customer feedback — you keep tweaking the menu throughout the day based on what people actually want.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why methods matter in KTU exams:** Almost every Module 1 question either asks you to *compare* methods or to *draw the lifecycle diagram* of a specific method. Mastery of these diagrams is worth 7–14 marks in ESE.

### 1.3 Classification of Methods (Big Picture)

Software engineering methods fall into **three broad families** that the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes:

- **Plan-driven (Predictive) methods** — activities are pre-planned in detail (e.g., Waterfall).
- **Incremental methods** — the system is built and delivered in slices (e.g., Incremental model).
- **Evolutionary/Iterative methods** — the system is refined across versions (e.g., Spiral, Prototyping).
- **Agile methods** — embrace change, customer collaboration, short iterations (e.g., Scrum, XP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Syllabus Highlight:** Generic process models covered in Module 1 of PBCGT504 include the **Waterfall model**, **Incremental model**, **Evolutionary models (Prototyping & Spiral)**, and a brief introduction to **Agile methods**. Each must be studied with its phases, advantages, disadvantages, and applicability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Waterfall Method (Linear Sequential Model)

The **Waterfall** model, proposed by **Winston W. Royce in 1970** (though he actually argued against its naive use), prescribes a top-down, phase-by-phase progression. Each phase must be completed and *formally signed off* before the next begins. **Feedback (iteration) back to a previous phase is allowed only with significant cost.**

**Phases in order:**
1. **Requirement Analysis & Definition** — gather and document all system requirements.
2. **System & Software Design** — derive system architecture, data structures, interfaces.
3. **Implementation & Unit Testing** — code the modules and test them in isolation.
4. **Integration & System Testing** — integrate modules and test the whole system.
5. **Operation & Maintenance** — deploy, fix defects, add minor enhancements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is called "Waterfall":** Like water flowing down a staircase, you can only go downward — not backward. Any "return flow" is considered costly rework.

**Strengths:**
- Simple, easy to understand and manage.
- Strict documentation enforces discipline.
- Suitable for projects with **stable, well-understood requirements**.

**Weaknesses:**
- **Working software is delivered very late** (typically near the end).
- Late discovery of defects → very expensive fixes.
- **Poor fit for complex, object-oriented projects** and for systems with evolving requirements.

### 2.2 The Incremental Method

The system is **designed, implemented, and tested incrementally** — a little more is added each time. The **first increment** delivers a *partial* but *usable* subset of the total functionality. The **core product** is delivered first; subsequent increments add features.

**Why it is used:**
- Customers get value earlier.
- Each increment delivers a working slice, so business value is realised progressively.
- Risk is reduced because early increments expose problems.

**Phases (per increment):**
1. Define the *increment scope* (which requirements this slice fulfils).
2. Design the increment's modules.
3. Implement and unit-test.
4. Integrate and validate the increment.
5. Deliver to the customer; obtain feedback → plan the next increment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Incremental model is *not* the same as the Iterative/Evolutionary model. **Incremental = add new functionality in slices**, but each slice is *fully completed*. **Iterative = refine the same functionality across cycles** without necessarily adding new features. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.3 The Evolutionary Method — Prototyping

A **prototype** is an *initial version of a system* used to demonstrate concepts, try out design options, and uncover requirements. It is *quickly built* (often using high-level tools, throwaway code) and is then **refined through user feedback** into the final system.

**Two sub-variants:**
- **Throwaway (Rapid) prototyping** — the prototype is *discarded* once requirements are understood; the actual system is built from scratch.
- **Evolutionary prototyping** — the prototype is *progressively refined* into the final product.

**Strengths:**
- Excellent when **requirements are unclear** at the start.
- Reduces risk of building the wrong system.
- Encourages strong user-developer interaction.

**Weaknesses:**
- Risk of becoming an *unsupported prototype* (becomes the product by accident).
- May lock in poor design decisions made for the sake of speed.
- User may mistake the prototype for the *finished* system.

### 2.4 The Evolutionary Method — Spiral Model

Proposed by **Barry Boehm in 1988**, the Spiral model explicitly **combines iterative development with risk management**. Each cycle (a "spiral loop") has four quadrants:

1. **Planning** — define objectives, alternatives, and constraints.
2. **Risk Analysis** — identify and resolve risks; build prototypes to mitigate.
3. **Engineering** — develop and verify the next version of the product.
4. **Customer Evaluation** — obtain customer feedback; plan the next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The number of spirals is **not fixed**; it depends on project complexity. The Spiral model is the *most* preferred model for **large, complex, high-risk projects** according to KTU syllabus notes.

### 2.5 Agile Methods

Agile is a family of **iterative, incremental, and adaptive methods** unified by the **Agile Manifesto (2001)**, which values:
- **Individuals & interactions** over processes & tools
- **Working software** over comprehensive documentation
- **Customer collaboration** over contract negotiation
- **Responding to change** over following a plan

**Popular Agile methods:**
- **Scrum** — fixed-length sprints (typically 2–4 weeks), roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team), ceremonies (Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective).
- **Extreme Programming (XP)** — emphasises engineering practices: pair programming, test-driven development, continuous integration, refactoring.
- **Kanban** — visual workflow management with WIP (work-in-progress) limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU highlight:** Agile is best suited for **small, co-located teams**, when **requirements are volatile**, and when **rapid delivery** is critical. It is *not* ideal for very large distributed teams or safety-critical systems with heavy documentation needs.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Criterion | Waterfall | Incremental | Prototyping | Spiral | Agile |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Linear, sequential | Slice-based | Build → refine | Cyclical + risk | Iterative + adaptive |
| **Customer involvement** | At start & end | After each increment | Continuous | Per spiral | Continuous |
| **Requirements stability** | Must be stable | Partially stable | Unclear | Evolving | Highly volatile |
| **Risk handling** | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** | Good |
| **Documentation** | Heavy | Moderate | Light | Moderate | **Light** |
| **Delivery** | Single, late delivery | Multiple, working | Working proto | Multiple versions | Frequent, working |
| **Best for** | Simple, well-defined projects | Mid-size, phased delivery | Unclear requirements | Large, high-risk | Small teams, dynamic needs |
| **Cost of change** | Very high | Moderate | Moderate | Lowered by risk mgmt | Low (by design) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defence** (e.g., avionics) → **Spiral model** because safety-critical risk analysis is mandatory.
- **Banking & ERP** → **Incremental or Waterfall** because regulatory compliance requires heavy documentation and stable specs.
- **Startups & mobile apps** → **Agile / Scrum** because requirements change weekly with market feedback.
- **R&D and exploratory AI/ML systems** → **Prototyping** because the algorithm's viability is unknown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a KTU exam, when asked "*Which method is most suitable for project X?*", always reason along three axes: (1) **Requirement stability**, (2) **Project size & complexity / risk**, (3) **Customer involvement needed**. This is the examiner's expected mental model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Method Selection Logic

Because the topic "Methods" in PBCGT504 is **qualitative and model-based** (not algebraic), the analytical content here is the *step-by-step reasoning process* you must write in the exam to **justify** selection and application of a method. KTU examiners award marks for the *logic chain*, not just the final answer.

### 3.1 Analytical Framework: How to Choose a Method

**Step 1 — Identify requirement stability.**
- If requirements are *fully known, unchanging, and unambiguous* → Waterfall is feasible.
- If requirements are *partially known* and *some features are clearer than others* → Incremental.
- If requirements are *uncertain, evolving, or unknown* → Prototyping, Spiral, or Agile.

**Step 2 — Identify project size and risk.**
- Small, low-risk → almost any model works.
- Large, high-risk, mission-critical → Spiral (because it embeds risk analysis in every cycle).
- Safety-critical (medical, aerospace) → **V-Model** (a strict variant of Waterfall) — though not always in KTU 2024 syllabus, it is worth mentioning.

**Step 3 — Identify customer & team dynamics.**
- Customer available *continuously* → Agile, Prototyping.
- Customer available *at milestones* → Waterfall, Incremental.
- Distributed or very large team → formal plan-driven (Waterfall/Incremental) is safer.

**Step 4 — Identify the deliverable cadence.**
- Need a working slice *every few weeks* → Incremental / Agile.
- Acceptable to deliver a full system *only at the end* → Waterfall.

**Step 5 — Match the answers of Steps 1–4 to the comparison table in Section 2.6 and select the method.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In KTU Part B questions worth 14 marks, the *explanation of "why this method"* is what carries the majority of marks — not just the diagram. Always produce a 5–7 line justification after drawing the lifecycle.

### 3.2 Worked Reasoning: Selecting a Method for Three Scenarios

#### Scenario A — Hospital Management System
Given: large organization, clear & stable requirements, regulatory compliance, distributed team.

**Reasoning chain (what to write in the exam):**

$$
\text{Project Nature} = \{\text{Large}, \text{Stable Requirements}, \text{High Compliance}\}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Customer Involvement} = \text{At Milestones}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Risk Level} = \text{Moderate}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \textbf{Selected Method: Incremental (preferred) or Waterfall}
$$

Justification (write this verbatim in the exam):

> *The Hospital Management System has well-defined and stable requirements from day one (driven by regulatory standards). The project is large in size, the team is distributed, and compliance requires heavy documentation. Under these conditions, the **Incremental model** is most suitable because it allows parallel development of independent functional slices (e.g., patient registration, billing, pharmacy) while still preserving formal documentation. The Waterfall model would be a close alternative if the customer prefers a single delivery, but the Incremental model gives earlier business value and easier risk containment.*

#### Scenario B — AI-based Medical Diagnosis Tool
Given: unclear requirements, research-oriented, performance data unknown, small expert team.

**Reasoning chain:**

$$
\text{Project Nature} = \{\text{Small}, \text{Unclear Requirements}, \text{Exploratory}\}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Risk Level} = \text{High (technically unknown)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Customer Involvement} = \text{Continuous}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \textbf{Selected Method: Prototyping (Evolutionary) or Spiral}
$$

Justification:

> *The AI diagnosis tool's effectiveness depends on data and algorithm choices that are not known upfront. A **Prototyping approach** allows the team to build a quick model, evaluate accuracy with clinicians, and iteratively refine. If budget and risk management are critical, the **Spiral model** is preferred because it adds a formal risk-analysis quadrant to every cycle.*

#### Scenario C — Startup Mobile App
Given: volatile market, small team, need to release fast, frequent user feedback.

**Reasoning chain:**

$$
\text{Project Nature} = \{\text{Small}, \text{Volatile Requirements}, \text{Fast Delivery}\}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \textbf{Selected Method: Agile (Scrum or Kanban)}
$$

Justification:

> *The mobile app's success depends on rapid release, learning from real users, and pivoting features based on feedback. **Agile**'s short sprints, daily standups, and continuous delivery are ideal.*

### 3.3 KTU-Mandated Lifecycle Sequence (Must be Memorized)

For the **Waterfall model**, the canonical phase order is:

$$
\text{Req. Analysis} \to \text{Design} \to \text{Implementation} \to \text{Testing} \to \text{Deployment} \to \text{Maintenance}
$$

For the **Incremental model**, the canonical order per increment is:

$$
\text{Req. (subset)} \to \text{Design (subset)} \to \text{Impl. (subset)} \to \text{Test} \to \text{Deliver Increment}
$$

For the **Spiral model**, each loop has:

$$
\text{Planning} \to \text{Risk Analysis} \to \text{Engineering} \to \text{Customer Evaluation}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error:** In the Spiral diagram, students often forget the *radial dimension* (cumulative cost). The radius of the spiral grows with each cycle, representing the increasing cost committed. KTU examiners specifically look for this radial axis in the diagram.

### 3.4 Decision Logic: Incremental vs Iterative (High-Yield Distinction)

KTU frequently tests this. Use the following precise definitions:

| Dimension | Incremental | Iterative |
|---|---|---|
| **What's added each cycle?** | **New** features / modules | **Refinement** of existing features |
| **Goal per cycle** | Deliver more functionality | Improve the same functionality's quality |
| **Example** | Banking: 1st = accounts, 2nd = loans, 3rd = insurance | Banking: 1st cycle = basic account mgmt (crude), 2nd cycle = add validation & UI polish, 3rd = add audit logs |
| **Combined?** | Most modern methods are **both** incremental *and* iterative (e.g., Scrum) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU one-liner to memorize:** *Incremental adds breadth, Iterative adds depth.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid syntax** with safe alphanumeric node identifiers. No reserved keywords are used as node names.

### 4.1 Waterfall Model Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Requirement Analysis"] --> A2["System and Software Design"]
    A2 --> A3["Implementation and Unit Testing"]
    A3 --> A4["Integration and System Testing"]
    A4 --> A5["Operation and Maintenance"]
    A5 --> A6["Project End"]
    A1 -.->|"feedback (costly)"| A3
```

### 4.2 Incremental Model Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["Define Increment 1 Scope"] --> B2["Design Increment 1"]
    B2 --> B3["Build and Test Inc 1"]
    B3 --> B4["Deliver Increment 1"]
    B4 --> B5["Customer Feedback"]
    B5 --> B6["Plan Increment 2"]
    B6 --> B1
```

### 4.3 Prototyping Model Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1["Identify Basic Requirements"] --> C2["Build Initial Prototype"]
    C2 --> C3["User Tests Prototype"]
    C3 -->|"Refine"| C2
    C3 -->|"Acceptable"| C4["Develop Final System"]
    C4 --> C5["Validate Final System"]
```

### 4.4 Spiral Model Lifecycle (Four Quadrants per Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["Loop Start: Planning"] --> D2["Risk Analysis"]
    D2 --> D3["Engineering Development"]
    D3 --> D4["Customer Evaluation"]
    D4 -->|"Next Cycle"| D1
    D1 -->|"Radial axis = Cumulative Cost"| D5["Outer Loop means Higher Cost"]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram of All Methods (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlanDriven["PLAN DRIVEN METHODS"]
        E1["Waterfall Model"]
        E2["V Model Variant"]
    end
    subgraph Incremental["INCREMENTAL METHODS"]
        F1["Incremental Model"]
    end
    subgraph Evolutionary["EVOLUTIONARY METHODS"]
        G1["Prototyping Model"]
        G2["Spiral Model"]
    end
    subgraph AgileFamily["AGILE METHODS"]
        H1["Scrum"]
        H2["Extreme Programming"]
        H3["Kanban"]
    end
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the block diagram:** The arrows show *evolutionary sophistication* — earlier models were extended and combined to produce the more adaptive methods. This is a common 7-mark explanation KTU examiners award full marks for.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a software process model. List any two advantages of the Waterfall model.**

**Model Answer:**
A software process model (or method) is an abstract representation of a software process that defines the phases, order of activities, deliverables, and control flow for software development.

Two advantages of the Waterfall model:
1. **Simple and easy to manage** — strict phase ordering makes project tracking and milestone verification straightforward.
2. **Strong documentation discipline** — every phase has formal outputs, making it suitable for regulated industries and maintenance handovers.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Incremental development and Iterative development.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Incremental | Iterative |
|---|---|---|
| Functionality | New features added per cycle | Same feature refined per cycle |
| Focus | Breadth | Depth |
| Customer value | Earlier partial delivery | Continuous quality improvement |

Incremental adds *new* modules in each cycle, whereas Iterative *refines the existing* module in each cycle. Modern methods (e.g., Scrum) are usually both.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has a choice of two questions (OR). Each sub-part is for 7 marks. Total = 14 marks. Provide both alternatives.

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(a) Explain the Waterfall model in detail. Draw the lifecycle diagram and list its phases. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Waterfall model is a **linear, sequential software development method** in which each phase is completed before the next begins, and there is minimal iteration back to earlier phases.

**Phases (must be drawn in order):**

$$
\text{Req. Analysis} \to \text{System Design} \to \text{Implementation} \to \text{Testing} \to \text{Deployment} \to \text{Maintenance}
$$

(Insert the mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 in the answer sheet.)

**Advantages (write 4):**
- Simplicity, ease of project management and tracking.
- Strong, formal documentation discipline at each phase.
- Suitable for projects with **clearly understood, stable requirements**.
- Easier to assign accountability per phase.

**Limitations (write 4):**
- Working software is delivered very late in the schedule.
- Late discovery of defects → very high cost of change.
- Poor fit when requirements evolve or when the project is large and complex.
- Limited user involvement after the requirement phase.

---

**(b) Explain the Incremental model with a neat diagram. How is it different from the Waterfall model? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In the Incremental model, the system is **designed, implemented, and tested in increments**, where each increment delivers a *working subset* of total functionality. The first increment is a *core* product; subsequent increments add features on top.

**Phases per increment (must be drawn):**
Define subset → Design → Implement → Test → Deliver → Feedback → Plan next increment.

(Insert the mermaid diagram from Section 4.2 in the answer sheet.)

**Difference from Waterfall (write 4 lines):**

| Aspect | Waterfall | Incremental |
|---|---|---|
| Delivery | One, at the end | Multiple, working slices |
| Customer feedback | Only at start and end | After each increment |
| Risk exposure | Late | Spread across increments |
| Change cost | Very high | Lower, as feedback is incorporated early |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(a) Describe the Spiral model in detail. Explain the four quadrants of each spiral loop and discuss why the Spiral model is preferred for large, high-risk projects. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Spiral model** (Barry Boehm, 1988) combines **iterative development with explicit risk management**. Each loop of the spiral represents a development phase, and the *number of loops is determined by project complexity*.

**Four quadrants per loop (must be drawn):**

1. **Planning** — define objectives, alternatives, and constraints for this cycle.
2. **Risk Analysis** — identify, evaluate, and mitigate risks (often via prototypes, simulations).
3. **Engineering** — develop and verify the next version of the product.
4. **Customer Evaluation** — customer reviews the product; feedback drives the next cycle.

(Insert the mermaid diagram from Section 4.4 in the answer sheet. Also indicate the radial axis showing cumulative cost.)

**Why preferred for large, high-risk projects (write 4 lines):**
- **Risk analysis is a first-class activity** in every cycle, not an afterthought.
- The model *naturally* handles changing requirements because each cycle ends with customer evaluation.
- Prototypes can be built cheaply within the risk-analysis quadrant.
- The cumulative cost grows visibly (radial dimension), giving management a clear view of budget progress.

---

**(b) What are Agile methods? List the four values of the Agile Manifesto. Briefly explain Scrum. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Agile methods** are a family of *iterative, incremental, and adaptive* software development methods that emphasize **customer collaboration, working software, and responsiveness to change** over rigid plans and heavy documentation.

**Four values of the Agile Manifesto (2001):**
1. **Individuals and interactions** over processes and tools.
2. **Working software** over comprehensive documentation.
3. **Customer collaboration** over contract negotiation.
4. **Responding to change** over following a plan.

**Scrum (key points to mention):**
- Time-boxed iterations called **Sprints** (typically 2–4 weeks).
- Three core roles: **Product Owner** (prioritizes backlog), **Scrum Master** (facilitates process), **Development Team** (builds the increment).
- Ceremonies: **Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective**.
- Artefacts: **Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment (potentially shippable product)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When explaining the Spiral model, students often draw it as a *simple circular loop* and forget the **radial axis (cost progression)** and the **quadrant labels**. This alone costs **2–3 marks** out of 7. Also, when listing Agile values, students paraphrase incorrectly — write the *exact* four value statements from the Manifesto, in the prescribed "X over Y" format.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **method/model** is an *abstract framework*; a **process** is its *instantiation* in a real project.
- [ ] The **Waterfall model** is linear, with strict phase ordering; best for *stable requirements*.
- [ ] The **Incremental model** delivers the system in *slices of new functionality*; suitable for *partial early delivery*.
- [ ] **Incremental ≠ Iterative.** Incremental adds *breadth*; Iterative adds *depth*. Modern methods (Scrum) are both.
- [ ] The **Prototyping model** is best when *requirements are unclear*; the prototype can be throwaway or evolutionary.
- [ ] The **Spiral model** (Boehm, 1988) has **four quadrants per loop** — Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Customer Evaluation — and the *radial axis denotes cumulative cost*.
- [ ] The **Spiral model** is preferred for **large, high-risk, safety-critical** projects.
- [ ] **Agile** values *individuals, working software, customer collaboration, and response to change* — in that priority order.
- [ ] **Scrum** uses *Sprints, Product/Sprint Backlogs, and three roles* (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team).
- [ ] Always end a "select-a-method" answer with a **5–7 line justification** linked to the project's *requirement stability, size, risk, and customer involvement*.
- [ ] **High-yield exam drawings**: Waterfall (linear arrow chain), Incremental (loop with "feedback → next increment"), Prototyping (cycle with refine arrow), Spiral (4-quadrant loop with radial axis).
- [ ] **Viva/short-note favorites**: Difference between Incremental and Iterative; When to use Spiral vs Waterfall; Four values of Agile Manifesto.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Quality Focus; Software Process Models - Waterfall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tools and Quality Focus; Software Process Models - Waterfall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Software Engineering Tools – The "Toolbox" of a Developer

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> *Software Engineering Tools* are computer-based aids (hardware/software utilities) that support activities of the software process, ranging from requirement specification and design to coding, testing, and maintenance. When these tools are organized into an integrated framework, they are collectively termed **CASE (Computer-Aided Software Engineering)** tools.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Carpenter's Toolbox 🪚**
Think of a software engineer as a carpenter building a house. A carpenter never uses a single tool for every job; they carry a *toolbox* containing hammers, saws, levels, and measuring tapes. Each tool speeds up a specific job (cutting wood, measuring length, joining pieces). Similarly, in software engineering, no single tool solves every problem — we use **requirements tools** (to capture what the customer wants), **design tools** (to plan the architecture), **coding tools** (IDEs, compilers), **testing tools** (JUnit, Selenium), and **maintenance tools** (debuggers, profilers).

### Classification of CASE Tools

| Category | Full Form | Phase Supported | Examples (Conceptual) |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Upper CASE** | Front-end CASE | Planning, Analysis, Design | Requirements traceability matrices, UML modeling tools (e.g., Rational Rose, StarUML), project planners (MS Project) |
| **Lower CASE** | Back-end CASE | Implementation, Testing, Maintenance | Compilers, debuggers, test runners (JUnit, NUnit), version control (Git) |
| **Integrated CASE (I-CASE)** | Full Lifecycle CASE | All phases seamlessly | Enterprise-grade platforms (e.g., Eclipse IDE, Visual Studio) |
| **Toolkit** | Single-task tools | One specific activity | A standalone compiler, a static analyzer, a documentation generator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The examiner frequently asks for the *difference between Upper CASE and Lower CASE tools*. Memorize the phase boundary: **Design phase ends Upper CASE, Implementation phase begins Lower CASE.**

---

## 1.2 Software Quality Focus — "Doing it Right the First Time"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> *Software Quality* is the degree to which a software product satisfies stated and implied needs (functionality, reliability, usability, efficiency, maintainability, portability) when used under specified conditions. The *Quality Focus* in software engineering is a process-driven mindset to build correctness into the product, rather than inspecting defects out at the end.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳**
Imagine two restaurants:
- *Restaurant A* cooks quickly, but customers frequently complain about stale food, wrong orders, and slow service.
- *Restaurant B* follows a strict recipe book, trains chefs, checks ingredients before cooking, and tastes dishes before serving. Their food is consistently good.

Restaurant B has **Quality Focus**. In software terms:
- The *recipe book* = defined process (waterfall, agile).
- The *trained chefs* = skilled engineers using the right **tools**.
- The *ingredient check* = **reviews and inspections**.
- The *tasting before serving* = **testing and QA**.

### The Three Pillars of Quality Focus

1. **Software Quality Assurance (SQA)** — *Prevention-oriented*. The planned, systematic activities (standards, training, process audits) that ensure the process is capable of producing quality software.
2. **Software Quality Control (QC)** — *Detection-oriented*. The operational techniques (testing, code reviews) that verify whether the product conforms to requirements.
3. **Software Quality Metrics** — *Measurement-oriented*. Quantitative measures (e.g., defect density, MTBF, cyclomatic complexity) that make quality *visible* and *manageable*.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "APC"**: **A**ssurance = **P**revent, **C**ontrol = Detect. SQA prevents defects; SQC detects them.

### Generic Software Quality Attributes (McCall's Model — KTU Frequent)

McCall's quality model groups 11 quality factors into three perspectives:

- **Product Operation (5):** Correctness, Reliability, Efficiency, Integrity, Usability.
- **Product Revision (3):** Maintainability, Flexibility, Testability.
- **Product Transition (3):** Portability, Reusability, Interoperability.

---

## 1.3 Software Process Models — The "Recipe" for Building Software

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> A *Software Process Model* (also called Software Development Life Cycle — SDLC model) is an abstract representation of a software process. It defines the **phases**, the **order** in which they are executed, the **deliverables**, and the **coordination** (feedback loops) among activities.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Cooking Recipe 📖**
A recipe tells you: *(1) gather ingredients, (2) chop vegetables, (3) heat pan, (4) cook, (5) garnish, (6) serve*. You do not "garnish before cooking." A **process model** is the *recipe* for software — it dictates the order of phases (requirements → design → code → test → maintain).

---

## 1.4 The Waterfall Model — The "Classical Linear Cascade"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> The *Waterfall Model*, first formally described by **Winston W. Royce in his 1970 paper** (and often mis-attributed as his recommendation — he actually described it to point out its limitations), is a **sequential, linear, plan-driven** software process model in which each phase must be completed before the next begins, with minimal or no overlap between phases. The output of one phase acts as the input to the next.

**Intuitive Analogy — Building a Multi-Story House 🏗️**
You cannot pour the 5th-floor slab before laying the 4th-floor columns. In the Waterfall model, you cannot start *Design* before *Requirements* is signed off, and you cannot start *Coding* before *Design* is frozen. Just as construction engineers sign off on the foundation before the walls go up, each phase in Waterfall ends with a **formal review/verification**, producing a **documented deliverable** that becomes the contract for the next phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** *"The Waterfall model is also called the Linear Sequential Model."* Always mention both names for full marks.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Software Engineering Tool — The 4-Tool Stack

A mature software engineering environment layers tools as follows:

1. **Planning & Management Tools** — schedule estimation, work-breakdown structures, Gantt charts.
2. **Analysis & Design Tools** — UML diagrams, ER diagrams, data flow editors, requirement repositories.
3. **Implementation Tools** — IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ, VS Code), compilers, linkers, formatters, linters.
4. **Testing & Maintenance Tools** — unit-test frameworks, bug trackers (Jira, Bugzilla), static analyzers (SonarQube), profilers, CI/CD pipelines.

> [!TIP]
> A tool becomes a *CASE* tool only when it **automates** a phase of the SDLC. A simple text editor is *not* a CASE tool; a UML modeler *is*.

### Why Tools Matter — The "Tool-Capability Hypothesis"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Studies (and the KTU syllabus) emphasize that the **right tool reduces accidental complexity** (the difficulty caused by the medium rather than the problem), letting the engineer focus on **essential complexity** (the inherent difficulty of the problem).

## 2.2 Quality Focus — The Mathematical & Conceptual Core

### 2.2.1 Software Quality Dimensions (ISO/IEC 25010:2011 — supersedes ISO 9126)

ISO/IEC 25010 organizes quality into **8 characteristics**:

$$
Q_{\text{product}} = f(\text{FunctionalSuitability}, \text{PerformanceEfficiency}, \text{Compatibility}, \text{Usability}, \text{Reliability}, \text{Security}, \text{Maintainability}, \text{Portability})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> While KTU older papers referenced **McCall's 11 factors**, the **2024 Scheme syllabus aligns with ISO 25010's 8 characteristics**. Be prepared to write *either* in the exam depending on the question wording.

### 2.2.2 Defect Density — The Most Tested Metric

**Defect Density (DD)** measures the number of confirmed defects per unit size of software:

$$
DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Confirmed}}{\text{Size of Software (KLOC \text{ or } FP)}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $DD$ | Defect Density | defects per KLOC, or defects per FP |
| $D$ | Total confirmed defects | count (integer) |
| $S$ | Software size | KLOC (thousand lines of code) **or** Function Points |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write "defects per LOC" using raw lines. KTU follows the standard convention: **per KLOC (thousand)** or **per Function Point (FP)**. Using raw LOC loses marks.

### 2.2.3 Other Important Metrics

| Metric | Formula (Conceptual) | Interpretation |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | Total operating time $\div$ Number of failures | Higher = more reliable |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | Total time $\div$ Number of failures (including repair) | Higher = more reliable |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of time system is up |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Independent paths in code |
| **Code Churn** | Lines added + modified + deleted | Stability indicator |

For McCabe's cyclomatic complexity:
- $E$ = number of edges in the control flow graph
- $N$ = number of nodes
- $P$ = number of connected components (typically 1 for a single program)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question:** "What is the cyclomatic complexity of a single sequential function with no branches?" Answer: **V(G) = 1** (because $E = N - 1$ for a linear graph, so $V(G) = (N-1) - N + 2 = 1$).

## 2.3 Waterfall Model — Phase-by-Phase Deep Dive

The Waterfall model has **5 (or 6) core phases**, executed in strict sequence:

| Phase # | Phase Name | Key Activity | Deliverable |
|---------|------------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Requirement Analysis & Definition** | Gather, analyze, document user needs | Software Requirement Specification (SRS) document |
| 2 | **System & Software Design** | Translate requirements into architecture (HLD), database schema, module interfaces | Software Design Description (SDD), DFDs, ER diagrams |
| 3 | **Implementation & Unit Testing** | Code the modules; developer-level tests | Source code, unit test reports |
| 4 | **Integration & System Testing** | Integrate modules; verify functional & non-functional requirements | Test reports, integrated software |
| 5 | **Deployment & Maintenance** | Install at user site; fix post-delivery defects | Working production system, patches |

> [!NOTE]
> The **6th phase** (sometimes separated): **Feasibility Study** precedes Phase 1. Some textbooks place it as a separate preliminary phase; KTU often folds it into the "Planning" or "System Engineering" prelude.

### 2.3.1 Internal Mechanics of Each Phase

- **Phase 1 (Requirements):** Uses techniques like interviews, questionnaires, observation, and use-cases. Output is the **SRS** following IEEE 830 standard.
- **Phase 2 (Design):** Produces two levels of design:
  - *High-Level Design (HLD)* — architecture, module breakdown, data flow.
  - *Low-Level Design (LLD)* — detailed logic, algorithms, data structures.
- **Phase 3 (Coding):** Translates design into a programming language. *Coding standards* (naming conventions, indentation) are enforced.
- **Phase 4 (Testing):** Hierarchy: **Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance**. Bug reports flow back as defects (in Waterfall, the iteration is expensive).
- **Phase 5 (Maintenance):** Four types — *Corrective* (fix bugs), *Adaptive* (port to new platform), *Perfective* (new features), *Preventive* (anticipate future issues).

### 2.3.2 Why "Waterfall"?

The flow is **strictly top-down** (like a waterfall). Each phase "drops" its output (a document or code) to the next phase. **No phase is revisited in a clean Waterfall**, though Royce's original paper did show a small iterative feedback arrow from testing back to design.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | When to Use | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Defect Density** | $DD = D \div S$ | Estimating quality of code module | Defects per KLOC or per FP |
| 2 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Reliability assessment of deployed system | Percentage (0–100%) |
| 3 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Estimating number of test cases needed | Integer (≥ 1) |
| 4 | **Effort Estimation (Basic COCOMO)** | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b$ | Rough effort in person-months | a, b depend on project mode |
| 5 | **Function Point Count** | $FP = UFC \times VAF$ | Size estimation independent of language | UFC = Unadjusted Function Count, VAF = Value Adjustment Factor (0.65–1.35) |
| 6 | **Reliability (Simple)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of no failure up to time $t$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown LaTeX rule:** Inside the table above, I used `\vert` placeholders implicitly and avoided raw `|`. Do not write $\vert x \vert$ with literal pipes inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tools** enable *automation, repeatability, and traceability* in safety-critical domains (avionics DO-178C, medical devices IEC 62304).
- **Quality Focus** underpins *process maturity models* such as **CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration)** — organizations rated at Level 5 (Optimizing) are statistically less likely to deliver defective software.
- **Waterfall** remains the mandated model in **defense, aerospace, and government contracts** (e.g., U.S. Department of Defense standards) because the *V-Model* (a Waterfall variant) demands rigorous verification at every phase.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Defect Density

> A team delivers a module of size **4.2 KLOC**. During acceptance testing, the customer reports **18 confirmed defects**. The team also internally finds **7 defects** during system testing. The Quality Manager wants to compute the **Defect Density** reported by the customer.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$
D_{\text{customer}} = 18 \text{ defects}, \qquad S = 4.2 \text{ KLOC}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Defect Density formula.**

$$
DD_{\text{customer}} = \frac{D_{\text{customer}}}{S} = \frac{18}{4.2}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division explicitly.**

$$
18 \div 4.2 = \frac{180}{42} = \frac{30}{7} \approx 4.2857
$$

**Step 4 — State the result with proper units.**

$$
DD_{\text{customer}} \approx 4.29 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

**Step 5 (Optional) — Total Defect Density (if asked).**

$$
DD_{\text{total}} = \frac{18 + 7}{4.2} = \frac{25}{4.2} \approx 5.95 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark (Industry Data, Indicative):** A defect density below **1 defect/KLOC** is considered excellent (mature, well-tested code). Between **1–5 defects/KLOC** is typical for medium-complexity systems. Above **10 defects/KLOC** signals serious quality problems.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

> A programmer writes a function that contains: 1 `if-else` (2 branches), 1 `for` loop, and 1 `while` loop. The control flow graph has **N = 8 nodes** and **E = 10 edges**, and it is a single connected component (P = 1).

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
V(G) = 10 - 8 + 2(1)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
V(G) = 10 - 8 + 2 = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The function has **4 independent paths**. Therefore, the **minimum number of test cases** required for *branch coverage* is **4** (one for each path).

$$
\boxed{V(G) = 4 \text{ independent paths} \implies \text{minimum 4 test cases}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — System Availability

> A server has MTBF = **900 hours** and MTTR = **100 hours**.

**Step 1 — Apply the availability formula.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
A = \frac{900}{900 + 100} \times 100\% = \frac{900}{1000} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
A = 0.9 \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

The server is operationally available **90% of the time**, or equivalently down for about **876 hours/year** ($24 \times 365 \times 0.10 = 876$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Waterfall Phases vs Tools vs Quality Activities

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision block**. Examiners love questions like *"Which tool supports which phase?"* or *"What quality activity is done in design phase?"*

| Waterfall Phase | Primary Tools | Quality Activity Performed | Deliverable |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------------------|-------------|
| **1. Requirement Analysis** | Requirements repositories (e.g., DOORS), UML use-case tools, survey tools | **SQA**: Standardize SRS template (IEEE 830); **Reviews**: Requirement review with stakeholders | SRS Document (signed off) |
| **2. System & Software Design** | UML modeling (StarUML, Rational Rose), ER tools, architecture tools | **SQA**: Design standards check; **Reviews**: Design walkthrough; **Metrics**: Coupling, cohesion | SDD (HLD + LLD) |
| **3. Implementation** | IDE (Eclipse, VS Code), compilers, static analyzers (SonarQube), formatters | **SQA**: Coding standards enforcement; **QC**: Unit testing, code reviews | Source code, unit test logs |
| **4. Integration & System Testing** | Test management (TestRail, JIRA), automation (Selenium, JUnit), bug trackers | **QC**: Integration testing, system testing; **Metrics**: Defect density, test coverage | Test reports, bug list |
| **5. Maintenance** | Profiling tools, debuggers, configuration management (Git) | **QC**: Regression testing; **SQA**: Change management process | Patches, new releases |

## 3.5 Advantages, Disadvantages, and Applicability of Waterfall — Complete Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 3-column table is a **14-mark goldmine** in KTU exams. Memorize it.

| **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** | **When to Use** |
|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Simple and easy to understand & manage | Real projects rarely follow a strict linear flow | Requirements are well-understood, clear, fixed |
| Disciplined approach with clear milestones | Working software is delivered only at the end of the cycle | Projects where the technology is well-known |
| Documentation-driven → easier maintenance later | Late discovery of defects → expensive to fix | Safety-critical systems (avionics, medical, defense) |
| Suitable for projects where requirements are stable | No working model visible to customer until late | Small to medium projects |
| Facilitates resource planning (time, cost) | High risk and uncertainty for new, complex systems | When the customer demands a *rigid contract* with fixed scope |
| Phases are processed and completed one at a time | Poor model for long, ongoing projects | Government / regulatory projects with strict documentation norms |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT list "disadvantages" without contrasting them. KTU's marking scheme awards marks for *linking disadvantage → impact on project*. E.g., "Late discovery of defects is *expensive* because changing requirements 80% into the project costs up to 200× more than fixing them at the requirements stage (Boehm's cost-of-change curve)."

## 3.6 The V-Model — Waterfall's "Verification & Validation" Extension

A frequent KTU sub-question: *"Explain the V-Model. How is it related to Waterfall?"*

The **V-Model** is a variant of Waterfall where each development phase is **explicitly paired** with a corresponding testing phase, forming a "V" shape:

- Requirements (left top) ↔ Acceptance Testing (right top)
- System Design ↔ System Testing
- Architecture Design ↔ Integration Testing
- Module Design ↔ Unit Testing
- Coding (bottom)

$$
\text{V-Model emphasizes: Verification (Are we building the product right?)} \longleftrightarrow \text{Validation (Are we building the right product?)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut:** The V-Model is **NOT a separate model** in some texts — it is a **reformulation of Waterfall** that makes testing the central concern. Both are plan-driven, sequential, and documentation-heavy.

```mermaid
    MID2 --> LOW1["STANDARDS, TRAINING, PROCESS AUDITS, REVIEWS"]
    MID3 --> LOW2["TESTING, INSPECTIONS, CODE REVIEWS"]
    MID1 --> LOW3["DEFECT DENSITY, AVAILABILITY, MTBF, CYCLOMATIC COMPLEXITY"]

    style TOP fill:#4A148C,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    style MID1 fill:#7B1FA2,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style MID2 fill:#7B1FA2,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style MID3 fill:#7B1FA2,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style LOW1 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:1px,color:#4A148C

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Read Before Answering):**
> 1. **Never use the word "process" alone** without specifying *which* process (e.g., software process, QA process, build process). Vague vocabulary costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always pair Waterfall advantages with their corresponding disadvantages** in compare-type questions. A standalone list loses half the marks.
> 3. **In numerical questions on Defect Density / MTBF / Cyclomatic Complexity, ALWAYS show the substitution step** before computing. Skipping directly to the answer is penalized.
> 4. **Distinguish SQA from SQC** explicitly. SQA = prevention (process); SQC = detection (product). Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024]**
**Define CASE tools. Differentiate between Upper CASE and Lower CASE tools with examples.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
CASE (Computer-Aided Software Engineering) tools are software applications that automate and support one or more phases of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). They enhance developer productivity by reducing manual effort in analysis, design, coding, testing, and maintenance.

- **Upper CASE tools** support the *front-end* activities — planning, requirements analysis, and design. *Example:* UML modeling tools, requirements management tools (e.g., DOORS).
- **Lower CASE tools** support the *back-end* activities — implementation, testing, debugging, and maintenance. *Example:* Compilers, debuggers, test automation tools (e.g., JUnit).

The key boundary is the **design phase**: phases *before* design = Upper CASE; phases *after* design = Lower CASE. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**
**What is Software Quality Assurance (SQA)? How is it different from Software Quality Control (SQC)?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
**SQA (Software Quality Assurance)** is a *process-oriented*, *prevention-focused* set of planned activities (standards definition, training, process audits, reviews) that ensure the software development process is capable of producing high-quality software. SQA is *proactive* — it aims to *prevent* defects.

**SQC (Software Quality Control)** is a *product-oriented*, *detection-focused* set of operational techniques (testing, inspections, code reviews) that verify whether the *delivered product* conforms to requirements. SQC is *reactive* — it aims to *find and fix* defects.

**Key distinction:** SQA governs *how* the software is built; SQC verifies *what* was built. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> *Per KTU 2024 Scheme, each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is given between two full questions.*

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Waterfall Model** in detail, listing its phases, deliverables, and the activities performed in each phase. Why is it also called the *Linear Sequential Model*? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of the Waterfall Model. Under what conditions would you recommend its use? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Definition (1 mark):** The Waterfall Model is a *plan-driven*, *sequential* software process model in which each phase is completed before the next phase begins, with formal verification at the end of each phase. It is called the *Linear Sequential Model* because the phases flow linearly (one after another) like a waterfall.

**Phases and Deliverables (5 marks):**

| Phase | Key Activity | Deliverable |
|-------|--------------|-------------|
| 1. Requirement Analysis | Gather, analyze, document user needs using interviews, observation, use-cases | Software Requirement Specification (SRS) document |
| 2. System & Software Design | Create architecture, HLD, LLD, data flow diagrams, ER diagrams | Software Design Description (SDD) |
| 3. Implementation & Unit Testing | Code the modules in chosen language; perform unit testing | Source code, unit test logs |
| 4. Integration & System Testing | Integrate modules; test for functional and non-functional requirements | Integrated software, test reports |
| 5. Deployment & Maintenance | Install at customer site; correct, adaptive, perfective, preventive maintenance | Production system, patches |

**Why Linear (1 mark):** There is *no overlap* between phases; each must be signed off (verification review) before the next begins. This makes planning, tracking, and resource allocation straightforward.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Simplicity and clarity** — easy for managers and developers to understand and follow.
2. **Discipline and documentation** — every phase produces a documented deliverable, aiding future maintenance.
3. **Easy progress tracking** — milestones are clear; progress can be measured phase by phase.
4. **Resource planning** — since each phase is well-defined, the time and cost for each can be estimated accurately.
5. **Suitable for stable-requirement projects** — when the customer knows exactly what they want.

**Disadvantages (3 marks):**
1. **Late discovery of defects** — testing happens late; bugs found late are *expensive* to fix (Boehm's cost-of-change curve).
2. **No working software until the end** — customer sees a working product only after months of development.
3. **Poor for ambiguous or changing requirements** — real projects rarely have frozen requirements; Waterfall does not adapt well.
4. **High risk for new technology** — if the chosen technology turns out unsuitable, huge rework is needed.
5. **Limited customer involvement** — after SRS sign-off, customer waits until the final delivery.

**When to Recommend (1 mark):** Waterfall is best suited for *small-to-medium projects* with *well-understood, stable requirements*, where the *technology is mature*, the *customer demands strict documentation* (e.g., government/defense projects), and *safety/compliance* mandates step-by-step verification (e.g., medical devices, avionics).

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024, May 2024] (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**(a)** What is a **Software Process Model**? Explain the **Waterfall Model** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between the Waterfall Model and the V-Model. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **Defect Density** and **Cyclomatic Complexity**. A module of size **3.5 KLOC** is delivered. During testing, **14 defects** are identified. The control flow graph of a function in this module has **N = 12 nodes, E = 16 edges, P = 1**. Compute the Defect Density and the Cyclomatic Complexity, and interpret the results. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Software Process Model (1 mark):** A *Software Process Model* (or SDLC model) is an abstract, simplified description of the software development process, specifying the **phases, their order, deliverables, and the relationships (feedback, iteration)** among them.

**Waterfall Model (3 marks):** The Waterfall model is a *linear sequential* model with 5 phases — Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Maintenance. Each phase ends with a *verification review* and a *documented deliverable* (SRS, SDD, code, test reports, deployed system). The diagram is shown below (or refer to Section 4.1):

```
Requirement Analysis (SRS) → System Design (SDD) → Implementation (Code) → System Testing (Test Reports) → Maintenance (Production System)
```

**Waterfall vs V-Model (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Waterfall | V-Model |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Shape | Linear (straight line) | V-shaped |
| Testing focus | Testing is a separate phase at the end | Each development phase is *paired* with a testing phase |
| Verification & Validation | Implicit | Explicit and central |
| Defect detection | Late (during system testing) | Early (unit, integration, system, acceptance all defined upfront) |
| Use case | General projects | Safety-critical, regulated industries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The V-Model is an *extension* of Waterfall — it *does not* contradict the linear flow; it makes the testing counterpart of each phase explicit. Both are *plan-driven* and *documentation-heavy*.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Defect Density (DD):** Number of confirmed defects per unit size of software.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G)):** A measure (by McCabe) of the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. $V(G) = E - N + 2P$.

**Defect Density Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
DD = \frac{\text{Number of defects}}{\text{Size in KLOC}} = \frac{14}{3.5} = 4.0 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

**Cyclomatic Complexity Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 16 - 12 + 2(1) = 4 + 2 = 6
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- A defect density of **4.0 defects/KLOC** is *typical* for medium-complexity software (industry benchmark: < 1 is excellent, 1–5 is acceptable, > 10 is poor).
- A cyclomatic complexity of **6** means the function has **6 independent paths**; therefore, the **minimum number of test cases** needed for *branch/path coverage* is **6**. (If V(G) were > 10, the function would be considered *high-risk* and should be refactored.)

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **CASE** = Computer-Aided Software Engineering tools — automate SDLC phases.
- 🔹 **Upper CASE** = front-end (requirements, design). **Lower CASE** = back-end (code, test, maintenance). **I-CASE** = both.
- 🔹 **Software Quality Focus** has three pillars: **SQA (prevention), SQC (detection), Metrics (measurement)**.
- 🔹 **SQA vs SQC** mnemonic: **"SQA = Standards & Audits (prevention); SQC = Checks & Tests (detection)."**
- 🔹 **Quality Models:** McCall (11 factors) and ISO 25010 (8 characteristics).
- 🔹 **Defect Density Formula:** $DD = D \div S$ — express size in KLOC or FP, **never** raw LOC.
- 🔹 **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — counts independent paths; informs test case count.
- 🔹 **Availability:** $A = MTBF \div (MTBF + MTTR)$ — expressed as a percentage.
- 🔹 **Waterfall = Linear Sequential Model** — 5 phases, no overlap, no early working software.
- 🔹 **Waterfall Origin:** Winston W. Royce, 1970 (often mis-attributed as his recommendation; he highlighted its limitations).
- 🔹 **Waterfall Deliverables:** SRS → SDD → Source Code → Test Reports → Production System.
- 🔹 **Waterfall best for:** stable requirements, mature technology, safety-critical, government/defense projects.
- 🔹 **V-Model** = Waterfall with explicit test pairing; *not* a separate fundamental model.
- 🔹 **Maintenance types:** Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive — remember the acronym **"CAPP."**
- 🔹 **Quality in Waterfall** is enforced via *formal reviews/inspections* between phases (verification gates).
- 🔹 **Tool-Capability Hypothesis:** Right tools reduce *accidental complexity* and let engineers focus on *essential complexity*.
- 🔹 **Industry benchmark defect density:** < 1/KLOC = excellent; 1–5 = typical; > 10 = poor.
- 🔹 **McCabe threshold:** Cyclomatic complexity > 10 implies high risk → refactor the function.
- 🔹 **For KTU numericals:** Always write the formula, substitute values, simplify, then state the unit (e.g., defects/KLOC, %, paths).
- 🔹 **Avoid these examiner pet hates:** "Process" without context; "Testing ensures quality" (too vague — say SQA prevents *and* SQC detects); "Waterfall is bad" (it's context-dependent).

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second revision mantra:** *"CASE tools, SQA/SQC, McCall/ISO 25010, Defect Density, McCabe's V(G), Waterfall phases, V-Model, Royce 1970."* If you can recite these fluently, you are exam-ready for Module 1's Tools/Quality/Waterfall portion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prototype
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Prototype

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prototype (KTU Definition):** A *prototype* is an early, executable working model of a software system that represents a partial or complete functional manifestation of the proposed system, used to expose and resolve user requirements ambiguities *before* full-scale development is committed.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education framework), the term maps to the following Course Outcome (CO) domains:

- **CO1 / K3 (Apply):** The ability to *apply* prototyping as a requirements-elicitation and validation technique within the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).
- **CO2 / K4 (Analyze):** *Analyzing* the suitability of prototype forms (Throwaway vs. Evolutionary) for a given project scenario.

A prototype is **NOT** the final product. It is a *learning vehicle* — a disposable or evolutionary artifact whose primary engineering value is **information discovery**, not delivery.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Architectural Mock-Up

Imagine an architect designing a skyscraper. Before pouring a single ton of concrete, the architect builds a small, scaled-down cardboard mock-up of the building. The client walks through the mock-up and says:

- *"This lobby is too narrow for the foot-traffic we expect."*
- *"The staircase should be relocated to the east side."*
- *"I never realized the conference room would feel this claustrophobic."*

These discoveries are **invaluable**, yet they cost almost nothing compared to discovering the same flaws in the finished 50-story tower.

A **software prototype** is the digital equivalent of that cardboard mock-up. It is a *quick-and-dirty* version of the system that lets the end-user and developer discover and correct design flaws when they are still **cheap to fix**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Examiners frequently expect the student to distinguish *prototype* from *pilot system* and from *proof-of-concept (POC)*. Keep these definitions crisp:
> - **Prototype:** Working model for *requirements clarification*.
> - **POC:** Demonstrates *technical feasibility* (e.g., can we use a quantum-resistant algorithm?).
> - **Pilot System:** A *full system* released to a *limited user base* in production.

### 1.3 Why is Prototyping Used? (The "Why")

The motivation is rooted in a fundamental engineering reality:

> **Stakeholders (users, managers, customers) cannot reliably articulate complete, consistent, and correct requirements before seeing *something tangible*.**

Karl Wiegers famously summarized this: *"A picture is worth a thousand words; a working prototype is worth a thousand pictures."* This is the philosophical heart of the prototyping paradigm in software engineering.

### 1.4 When is Prototyping *Especially* Useful?

- **High uncertainty** in user requirements.
- **Novel / unfamiliar** problem domains where the user has no prior system to mirror.
- **Unclear or evolving algorithms** that need to be validated with the end-user.
- **High-cost-of-failure** downstream (e.g., medical, aviation, banking systems).
- **Short feedback cycles** with non-technical stakeholders.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Prototyping Process — A Step-by-Step Engineering Walkthrough

The prototyping methodology is not ad-hoc. It follows a structured sequence (commonly attributed to the Gomaa & Scott model used in KTU reference materials):

1. **Identify the Basic Requirements** — Determine the *known* core requirements (e.g., login, data input, report generation). Output: an initial requirement list.
2. **Develop an Initial Prototype** — Construct a partial system demonstrating those core features. Tools: rapid-application development (RAD) tools, high-level languages, UI builders, fourth-generation languages (4GLs).
3. **User Interaction / Review** — The user interacts with the prototype, exercises its features, and provides concrete feedback. This is the **information-gathering stage**.
4. **Revise and Refine the Prototype** — Feedback is incorporated; new requirements surface; ambiguities are resolved. The prototype is iteratively refined.
5. **Repeat Steps 3 and 4** — Until the user is satisfied and requirements are fully understood.
6. **Develop the Final / Production-Quality System** — The insights from the prototype are codified into a *specification*, and engineering-grade software is built with quality standards (modularity, documentation, testing, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The prototype itself is **disposable** in the classical throwaway model. The *specification* produced by the prototype is the actual deliverable. Do not conflate "prototype" with "system" in exam answers.

### 2.2 Classification of Prototypes (Taxonomy)

Prototypes are categorized along three orthogonal axes:

#### A. By Purpose
| Category | Definition | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Prototype** | Simulates the *full UI / user interaction layer* with little or no underlying business logic. | Usability testing, screen-flow validation. |
| **Vertical Prototype** | Implements a *complete slice* of one feature end-to-end (UI + logic + data) but for limited functionality. | Performance-critical algorithm validation. |

#### B. By Lifecycle Role
| Category | Definition | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Throwaway (Rapid) Prototype** | Built quickly, used for learning, then discarded. | Specification document. |
| **Evolutionary Prototype** | Continuously refined and grown into the *final* system. | The final product itself. |
| **Incremental Prototype** | Several small prototypes built and merged to form the whole. | Modular final product. |

#### C. By Engineering Form
| Form | Description |
|---|---|
| **Paper Prototype** | Sketches on paper / whiteboard — no execution. |
| **Storyboard / Wizard-of-Oz** | Simulated interactions by a human behind the screen. |
| **Functional / Working Prototype** | Real (often RAD-built) executable software. |
| **Mock-up / Hi-Fi Prototype** | Pixel-perfect UI without backend logic. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The Prototype domain does not involve heavy numerical formulas, but it has **qualitative engineering metrics** that KTU examiners love to test. Memorize the following table verbatim.

| Metric / Concept | Definition / Value | KTU Mark-Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Prototyping Paradigm** | A software engineering paradigm focused on iterative development of working models. | 2 marks (definition) |
| **Horizontal Prototype** | Surface-layer UI prototype, no deep logic. | 2 marks |
| **Vertical Prototype** | End-to-end thin slice of one feature. | 2 marks |
| **Throwaway Prototype** | Built to learn, then discarded. | 3 marks |
| **Evolutionary Prototype** | Refined iteratively into final system. | 3 marks |
| **RAD** | Rapid Application Development — 4GLs, visual tools, component reuse. | 2 marks |
| **Wizard-of-Oz** | Human simulates system responses to test user expectations. | 2 marks |
| **Time-Boxing** | Prototyping is strictly *time-boxed* to prevent scope creep. | 2 marks |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Prototypes are Used Professionally

- **Embedded systems:** Prototype user interface flows on a touch-screen before manufacturing hardware.
- **Banking software:** Validate the *customer journey* of a new loan-application portal.
- **Healthcare informatics:** Test clinical workflows with nurses/doctors before EMR deployment.
- **Defense / Aerospace:** Validate human-machine interaction (HMI) in cockpit simulators.
- **E-commerce:** A/B test checkout flows as a prototype to maximize conversion rates.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Time-Box Rule

A prototype without a time limit degenerates into the *final system*. This violates the engineering principle of **cost-of-change escalation** — every change in a finished system is exponentially more expensive than during prototyping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
