Sprint Retrospective] RF1 --> P1 R1 -.Customer Feedback.-> P1 RF1 -.Process Improvement.-> P1 style P1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style B1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style T1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style R1 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style RF1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Value-to-Principle Mapping Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD V1[VAL1 Individuals and Interactions] --> P5[PR5 Motivated Individuals] V1 --> P6[PR6 Face to Face] V1 --> P11[PR11 Self Organizing Teams] V1 --> P12[PR12 Reflect and Adjust] V2[VAL2 Working Software] --> P1[PR1 Early Delivery] V2 --> P3[PR3 Frequent Delivery] V2 --> P7[PR7 Working Software Progress] V2 --> P9[PR9 Technical Excellence] V2 --> P10[PR10 Simplicity] V3[VAL3 Customer Collaboration] --> P1 V3 --> P4[PR4 Daily Business Collaboration] V4[VAL4 Responding to Change] --> P2[PR2 Welcome Change] V4 --> P8[PR8 Sustainable Pace] V4 --> P12 style V1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style V2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style V3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style V4 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`** **Q1. List and explain the four values of the Agile Manifesto.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The four values of the Agile Manifesto (2001) are: 1. **Individuals and interactions** over processes and tools — emphasizes that people and their communication are the primary drivers of project success. 2. **Working software** over comprehensive documentation — prioritizes delivering functional, tested software over voluminous design documents. 3. **Customer collaboration** over contract negotiation — advocates continuous engagement with the customer instead of relying on rigid signed contracts. 4. **Responding to change** over following a plan — treats changing requirements as a competitive advantage, not a threat. The phrase *"over"* indicates a *priority order*, not a complete rejection of the right-hand items. *(Word limit as per KTU guidelines: ~100–120 words)* --- **Q2. What is the significance of Principle 12 (Regular Reflection) in Agile projects?** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** Principle 12 states: *"At regular intervals, the team reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts its behavior accordingly."* Its significance lies in making the process *self-correcting and continuously improving*. In Scrum, this is operationalized through the **Sprint Retrospective**, held at the end of every sprint, where the team identifies what went well, what went poorly, and commits to specific process improvements for the next sprint. This institutionalized reflection prevents recurring mistakes and promotes long-term velocity growth. --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`** --- #### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **Q(a)** Explain the four core values of the Agile Manifesto in detail. How do these values differ from the principles of traditional plan-driven development? `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The **Agile Manifesto (2001)** defines four core values that reframe how software teams should think about their work. Each value contrasts a *human/adaptive* item with a *process/rigid* item. **Value 1: Individuals and Interactions over Processes and Tools** This value asserts that motivated, communicating humans outperform even the most sophisticated process frameworks. In traditional plan-driven models, the process is treated as the source of productivity — extensive role definitions, gating procedures, and tool mandates. Agile rejects this and instead invests in *people*: cross-functional teams, pair programming, and direct communication channels. The process exists to serve the team, not vice versa. **Value 2: Working Software over Comprehensive Documentation** Traditional models (e.g., older Waterfall variants) demanded exhaustive Software Requirement Specifications (SRS), Design Documents (SDD), and Test Plans *before* coding began. Agile reduces documentation to *just enough* — the primary, tangible measure of progress is *running, tested, deployable software*. Documentation is still produced, but it is not the goal. **Value 3: Customer Collaboration over Contract Negotiation** In traditional models, the contract is the source of truth, and the customer's involvement ends at sign-off. Agile treats the customer as a *co-creator* — Product Owners, demos every sprint, and continuous feedback loops replace the adversarial contract-negotiation posture. **Value 4: Responding to Change over Following a Plan** Traditional plan-driven models lock requirements via *change-control boards* and treat scope changes as defects. Agile welcomes late-stage changes, treating them as opportunities to deliver higher customer value. Plans are short-horizon (sprint-level) and continuously revised. **Contrast with Plan-Driven Development:** | Aspect | Plan-Driven | Agile | |---|---|---| | Primary Success Measure | Plan adherence | Working software | | Change Response | Discouraged, expensive | Welcomed, cheap | | Customer Role | Sign-off authority | Active collaborator | | Documentation | Comprehensive | Just-in-time | | Team Structure | Specialized silos | Cross-functional | *Conclusion:* The four values collectively reorient the software team from *predict-and-control* thinking to *adapt-and-deliver* thinking. --- **Q(b)** Describe any six of the twelve Agile principles, grouping them under their parent value. `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** Below are six principles grouped under their parent Agile value: **Grouped under Value 1 (Individuals and Interactions):** - **Principle 5 — Motivated Individuals:** Projects are built around motivated individuals. Agile trusts teams to self-direct and provides them with the environment, support, and trust they need. - **Principle 6 — Face-to-Face Conversation:** Within a development team, face-to-face dialogue is the most effective communication method, reducing miscommunication and rework. - **Principle 11 — Self-Organizing Teams:** The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from teams that are not micro-managed but are empowered to make technical decisions collectively. **Grouped under Value 2 (Working Software):** - **Principle 1 — Early & Continuous Delivery:** Customer satisfaction is highest when working software is delivered early and then continuously, providing rapid business value. - **Principle 7 — Working Software as Progress Measure:** Progress is measured by *working features delivered*, not by lines of code written or documents produced. - **Principle 10 — Simplicity:** The art of maximizing the amount of work *not done* is essential — build only what is needed, avoid over-engineering (YAGNI). These six principles operationalize the abstract values into daily, observable team behaviors, forming the foundation of frameworks such as **Scrum and Extreme Programming (XP)**. --- #### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks** **Q(a)** What is the Agile Manifesto? Discuss the background and historical context that led to its creation. `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The **Agile Manifesto** is a foundational document in software engineering, authored in **February 2001** at **Snowbird, Utah (USA)** by 17 software practitioners. The signatories included industry luminaries such as **Kent Beck, Martin Fowler, Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob), Jeff Sutherland, Ken Schwaber, Mike Beedle, and Andrew Hunt**, among others. **Historical Context:** By the late 1990s, the software industry was largely dominated by *plan-driven methodologies* — primarily variants of the **Waterfall model** and the **Rational Unified Process (RUP)**. These methodologies were characterized by: - Heavy upfront requirements gathering - Exhaustive documentation - Rigid, sequential phase-gate progression - Resistance to late changes - Multi-year delivery cycles Practitioners observed that such methods frequently led to: 1. **Late discovery of defects**, since testing was relegated to the end. 2. **Customer dissatisfaction**, as the delivered product often no longer matched evolving market needs. 3. **High cost of change**, since modifying a completed phase was prohibitively expensive. 4. **Demoralized teams**, stuck in specialized silos with no end-to-end ownership. In response, several *lightweight* methodologies had independently emerged in the 1990s — **Scrum (Schwaber & Sutherland), Extreme Programming (Beck), DSDM, Crystal (Cockburn), Feature-Driven Development, and Adaptive Software Development**. The 17 authors recognized the *philosophical common ground* across these methodologies and convened at Snowbird to formalize their shared beliefs. The result was the **Agile Manifesto** — a four-value, twelve-principle declaration that unified these methods under one mindset. The manifesto was revolutionary because it was a *mindset shift*, not a process prescription. It deliberately avoided mandating any specific framework, leaving implementation to the practitioner community — which led to the explosion of Agile frameworks that followed. --- **Q(b)** Explain the role of customer collaboration, working software, and sustainable pace in delivering successful Agile projects. `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** **1. Customer Collaboration (Value 3 & Principle 4):** In Agile, the customer is not a distant stakeholder but an *embedded team member*, typically represented by the **Product Owner**. Their continuous involvement ensures: - The product backlog accurately reflects current business priorities. - The team receives immediate feedback after every sprint demo. - Misunderstandings are caught early, reducing costly rework. **2. Working Software (Value 2 & Principles 1, 3, 7):** Working software is the *primary, tangible measure of progress*. Each sprint (typically 2–4 weeks) must produce a *potentially shippable* increment. This ensures: - Business value is realized continuously, not in one big-bang release. - Integration and deployment risks are surfaced early. - Stakeholders can see real progress, not just status reports. **3. Sustainable Pace (Principle 8):** Agile explicitly rejects the *crunch culture* common in late-stage Waterfall projects. Sponsors, developers, and users should maintain a *constant, predictable pace indefinitely*. This means: - No "death marches" or excessive overtime. - Velocity is measured and respected when planning future sprints. - Teams remain healthy, motivated, and productive over the long term — directly supporting **Principle 5 (Motivated Individuals)**. *Synergy:* These three concepts work together — sustainable pace keeps the team healthy enough to collaborate; customer collaboration refines what working software should be; and the focus on working software ensures that effort is always directed at delivering tangible value. This synergy is what makes Agile projects statistically more successful than traditional plan-driven ones. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Treating the "over" as rejection** — Many students wrongly state Agile *rejects* documentation. The manifesto explicitly says "while there is value in the items on the right." Use the word *prioritize*, not *reject*. > 2. **Memorizing but not mapping** — Examiners reward students who *map each principle to its parent value*. A flat list of 12 principles without grouping will lose at least 2 marks. > 3. **Confusing Agile with Scrum** — Agile is the *philosophy*; Scrum, XP, Kanban are *frameworks*. Examiners deduct marks when students treat them as synonyms. > 4. **Skipping Principle 12 (Retrospective)** — Many answers forget the reflection/adaptation loop, which is a hallmark of Agile's *closed feedback* nature. > 5. **No real-world example** — A single concrete example (e.g., "Spotify's squad model" or "Amazon's two-pizza teams") elevates a 7-mark answer to a 9-mark answer in valuation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** > - The Agile Manifesto was authored in **2001 at Snowbird, Utah** by **17 practitioners**. > - It contains exactly **4 Values** and **12 Principles** (this is a *favourite* fill-in-the-blank question). > - The keyword in every value is **"over"** — meaning *priority*, not *rejection*. > - Memorize the value acronym: **I W C R** — *Individuals, Working software, Customer collaboration, Responding to change*. > - **Principle 12** (Retrospective) is the *self-correction loop* — do not skip it in long answers. > - **Principle 10** emphasizes *simplicity* — the *art of NOT doing work* (YAGNI). > - Agile is a **mindset**; Scrum/XP/Kanban are **frameworks** that implement the mindset. > - Sustainable pace (Principle 8) explicitly rejects **death-march crunch culture**. > - Working software (Principle 7) is the *primary measure of progress* — not documents. > - Customer is a **co-creator**, represented by the **Product Owner** in Scrum. > - Be ready to draw a simple Agile **iterative cycle** (Plan → Build → Test → Review → Reflect). > - Always provide **at least one industry example** (Spotify, Amazon, Google) in long answers to earn full valuation marks. ---

topics.title: Requirement Engineering: Functional and Non-functional Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Requirement Engineering: Functional and Non-functional Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Requirement Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** **Requirement Engineering (RE)** is the systematic and disciplined approach to the elicitation, analysis, specification, validation, and management of software requirements. It establishes the *what* of the system before the *how* (design) is decided.

In plain engineering terms, Requirement Engineering is the **bridge between human needs and machine implementation**. Before any line of code is written, software engineers must understand, capture, and document *exactly* what the customer wants the system to do, and *how well* it should do it.

### Conceptual Analogy: Building a House

Imagine you want to build a house. Before construction starts, you sit with an architect and discuss:
- **How many rooms?** How many floors? A kitchen? A garden? *(These are Functional Requirements — the features the house must have)*
- **How strong should the walls be?** What is the wind resistance rating? How energy-efficient are the windows? *(These are Non-Functional Requirements — the quality attributes of the house)*

If the architect just starts building without asking these questions, you will end up with a house that is either useless (no kitchen) or unsafe (walls collapse in monsoon). **Requirement Engineering does the same for software** — it prevents "building the wrong software perfectly."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per the revised NEP 2020 OBE framework, Requirements Engineering is mapped to **CO1 (Understand)** and **CO2 (Apply)**. Students must be able to *classify* requirements and *construct* requirement documents for a given problem scenario.

## 1.2 Classification of Requirements

Software requirements are broadly divided into two major categories as prescribed by the **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148** standards referenced in the KTU syllabus:

| # | Requirement Type | Core Question it Answers | Focus |
|---|------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Functional Requirements (FR)** | *"What should the system DO?"* | Features, functions, behavior |
| 2 | **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** | *"How WELL should the system do it?"* | Quality, constraints, performance |

### 1.2.1 Functional Requirements (FR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A **Functional Requirement** specifies a particular function, behavior, or feature that the system must support. It describes the *inputs, behavior, and outputs* of the system under specific conditions.

**Key Properties of FRs:**
- Always **testable** through functional/black-box test cases
- Describe *what* the system does, not *how* it does it
- Are **declarative** of system behavior
- Are **traceable** to specific use cases

**Illustrative Examples:**
- *"The ATM shall dispense cash when a valid PIN and sufficient balance are entered."*
- *"The system shall generate a monthly salary slip for every employee."*
- *"The e-commerce portal shall allow a user to add items to a shopping cart."*

### 1.2.2 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A **Non-Functional Requirement (NFR)** specifies a **quality attribute, constraint, or performance characteristic** that the system must satisfy. NFRs are also known as **Quality Attributes** or **Quality of Service (QoS) requirements** in industry literature.

**Key Properties of NFRs:**
- Often **un-testable directly** — they require *measurement* (e.g., response time in milliseconds, availability as a percentage)
- Define **system-wide constraints**
- Apply to the system *as a whole*, not just one feature
- Are **critical for user satisfaction**, even if users never explicitly state them
- If violated, the system is *technically* functional but *practically* unusable

**Illustrative Examples:**
- *"The web page shall load within **2 seconds** under normal network conditions."*
- *"The system shall be available **99.9%** of the time (the 'three nines' rule)."*
- *"User passwords shall be encrypted using **SHA-256**."*
- *"The application shall support at least **10,000 concurrent users**."*

## 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Board Examiner Insight):** Many students write "the system should work fast" as a *Functional Requirement*. This is incorrect. "Work fast" is a quality — it belongs under Non-Functional Requirements, and it must be **measurable** (e.g., response time ≤ 2 seconds).

A software system can be:
- ✅ **Functionally correct but non-functionally broken** → e.g., a login feature works correctly, but takes 5 minutes to respond (useless).
- ✅ **Non-functionally excellent but functionally incomplete** → e.g., lightning-fast response, but the "Forgot Password" feature is missing (incomplete).

Both are failures. Hence, **both FRs and NFRs are equally important** in Requirement Engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Requirement Engineering (RE) Process — 5 Core Activities

The IEEE / KTU syllabus prescribes **5 sequential but iterative activities** within Requirement Engineering. These are not a strict waterfall — they often loop back when new information emerges.

### Activity 1: Requirements Elicitation
- **Goal:** Discover, gather, and uncover requirements from stakeholders.
- **Techniques:** Interviews, Questionnaires, Observation, Workshops (JAD — Joint Application Development), Brainstorming, Use Case Analysis, Domain Analysis.
- **Key Challenge:** *Stakeholders often don't know what they really want*, and they may have *conflicting* requirements.

### Activity 2: Requirements Analysis
- **Goal:** Refine, classify, prioritize, and resolve conflicts among elicited requirements.
- **Techniques:** Prioritization (MoSCoW: Must, Should, Could, Won't), Negotiation, Conflict Resolution.
- **Output:** A classified, prioritized list of FRs and NFRs.

### Activity 3: Requirements Specification (Documentation)
- **Goal:** Produce a formal, structured **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** document.
- **Standard:** **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148-2018** format.
- **Output:** SRS document that serves as the *contract* between customer and developer.

### Activity 4: Requirements Validation
- **Goal:** Ensure the documented requirements are *correct, complete, consistent, unambiguous, testable, and traceable*.
- **Techniques:** Reviews, Walkthroughs, Inspections, Prototyping, Acceptance Test-Case Generation.
- **Key Question:** *"Did we document the RIGHT requirements?"* (versus *"Did we document the requirements right?"* — which is verification).

### Activity 5: Requirements Management
- **Goal:** Handle changes, versioning, and traceability of requirements throughout the project lifecycle.
- **Activities:** Change Control, Version Control, Traceability Matrix maintenance, Baselining.

## 2.2 Deeper Classification of Non-Functional Requirements

NFRs are not a monolith. The KTU syllabus (and ISO/IEC 25010 standard) categorizes them into the following sub-types:

### 2.2.1 Performance Requirements
The most critical NFR category. Defines speed, throughput, and efficiency.

$$\text{Response Time} = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$$

Where $T_{\text{start}}$ is the moment a user initiates an action, and $T_{\text{end}}$ is the moment the system fully responds.

**Example:** *"Search results must appear within 1.5 seconds for 95% of queries."*

### 2.2.2 Availability & Reliability Requirements

$$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $\text{MTBF}$ = **Mean Time Between Failures** (system runs successfully)
- $\text{MTTR}$ = **Mean Time To Repair** (system is down and being fixed)

**Industry Standards for Availability (the "Nines"):**

| Availability % | Downtime per Year | Use Case |
|----------------|-------------------|----------|
| **99%** (two nines) | ~3.65 days | Internal tools |
| **99.9%** (three nines) | ~8.77 hours | E-commerce sites |
| **99.99%** (four nines) | ~52.6 minutes | Banking, telecom |
| **99.999%** (five nines) | ~5.26 minutes | Military, emergency services |

### 2.2.3 Security Requirements
Define authentication, authorization, encryption, audit logging.

**Example:** *"All passwords must be hashed using bcrypt with salt; session tokens must expire after 30 minutes of inactivity."*

### 2.2.4 Usability Requirements
Define ease-of-use, learnability, and user satisfaction.

**Example:** *"A new user shall be able to complete checkout within 3 clicks and 60 seconds without prior training."*

### 2.2.5 Maintainability & Portability
- **Maintainability:** Effort required to modify software (patches, enhancements).
- **Portability:** Effort required to transfer software to a different environment (OS, hardware).

### 2.2.6 Scalability Requirements
Define how the system handles growth in users, data, or transactions.

**Example:** *"The system must support horizontal scaling up to 50 application server nodes."*

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision summary** of all key concepts, formulas, and classifications. Memorize this for the 3-mark short-answer section.

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Key Formula or Rule | Unit / Example |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Functional Requirement | FR | Describes **what** the system does | "Shall generate report" |
| Non-Functional Requirement | NFR | Describes **how well** the system does it | "Within 2 seconds" |
| Response Time | $T_{resp}$ | $T_{resp} = T_{end} - T_{start}$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Throughput | $\lambda$ | Requests handled per unit time | **requests/second** |
| Availability | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | **percentage (%)** |
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | Probability of failure-free operation until time $t$ | dimensionless [0, 1] |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | Average successful run time | **hours** |
| Mean Time To Repair | MTTR | Average downtime per failure | **hours** |
| IEEE SRS Standard | — | **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148-2018** | Document structure |
| Prioritization Method | MoSCoW | Must, Should, Could, Won't | Categorization |
| Traceability Matrix | RTM | Links FR $\rightarrow$ Design $\rightarrow$ Code $\rightarrow$ Test | Forward & backward |
| NFR Categories (ISO 25010) | — | Performance, Security, Usability, Reliability, Maintainability, Portability, Compatibility | 8 quality model |
| Validation vs Verification | V\&V | Validation = *right product?*; Verification = *product right?* | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In industry, the distinction between FR and NFR is the **first cut** made during any System Requirements Review (SRR). Misclassification is one of the top 3 causes of project cost overruns, as NFR violations (e.g., slow performance discovered late) are **10x to 100x more expensive to fix post-deployment** than during the requirements phase (Brooks' Law of Requirement Errors).

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: The Availability Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation shows how the standard availability formula emerges from first principles. This is frequently asked as a 3-mark derivation question in KTU University Exams.

### Step 1: Define the Operational States
A system alternates between two states over its operational lifetime:
- **UP state:** The system is operating correctly and serving users.
- **DOWN state:** The system has failed and is being repaired.

Let:
- $T_{up}$ = total time the system was UP
- $T_{down}$ = total time the system was DOWN
- $T_{total} = T_{up} + T_{down}$ = total observation time

### Step 2: Define MTBF and MTTR
Over many failure cycles (say, $N$ cycles):

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{up}}{N}$$

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{T_{down}}{N}$$

### Step 3: Express Availability as a Ratio
By definition, availability is the *fraction of time* the system is operational:

$$A = \frac{T_{up}}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute MTBF and MTTR
Substitute $T_{up} = N \cdot \text{MTBF}$ and $T_{down} = N \cdot \text{MTTR}$ into the numerator and denominator:

$$A = \frac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot \text{MTBF} + N \cdot \text{MTTR}}$$

### Step 5: Cancel the Common Factor
Since $N$ appears in both numerator and denominator, it cancels out:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### Step 6: Express as a Percentage
For industrial reporting, multiply by 100:

$$A_{\%} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$

This is the **final, industry-standard availability equation**, as cited in the KTU syllabus and ISO/IEC 25010.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Availability Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical numerical problem** that appears almost every year in KTU University Exams. Practice it thoroughly.

### Problem Statement
A cloud-based banking server has an **MTBF of 800 hours** and an **MTTR of 2 hours**. Calculate:
1. The availability of the system.
2. The total downtime per year (assume 365 days).
3. Identify which "nines" level of availability this represents.

### Solution

**Part 1: Availability Calculation**

Substituting into the derived formula:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{800}{800 + 2} = \frac{800}{802}$$

$$A \approx 0.997506...$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$A_{\%} \approx 99.75\%$$

This falls within the **"three nines"** availability bracket (99% – 99.9%).

**Part 2: Annual Downtime**

Total hours in a year:

$$T_{year} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

Annual downtime:

$$T_{down, year} = T_{year} \times (1 - A)$$

$$T_{down, year} = 8760 \times (1 - 0.997506)$$

$$T_{down, year} = 8760 \times 0.002494$$

$$T_{down, year} \approx 21.85 \text{ hours per year}$$

**Part 3: Interpretation**
A downtime of ~21.85 hours/year is acceptable for many online services, but for a **real-time banking system**, this is borderline. To achieve the "four nines" (99.99%), the MTTR would need to drop to:

$$0.9999 = \frac{800}{800 + \text{MTTR}} \implies \text{MTTR} = \frac{800}{0.9999} - 800 \approx 0.08 \text{ hours} \approx 4.8 \text{ minutes}$$

This shows how **stringent NFRs drive architectural decisions** like redundant servers and hot-failover mechanisms.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: FR vs NFR Decision Flow

The following decision matrix helps a requirements engineer classify a given statement:

| Diagnostic Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---------------------|----------|---------|
| Does it describe a **specific behavior or feature**? | Likely **FR** | Likely **NFR** |
| Can it be tested with a **pass/fail test case**? | Likely **FR** | Likely **NFR** (requires measurement) |
| Does it contain a **measurable metric** (time, %, count)? | Likely **NFR** | Likely **FR** |
| Does it use words like "fast," "secure," "easy," "reliable"? | Likely **NFR** | Likely **FR** |
| Does it describe an **action** the system performs? | Likely **FR** | Likely **NFR** |
| Does it apply to the **system as a whole**? | Likely **NFR** | Likely **FR** (feature-specific) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When in doubt, ask: *"Can I write a single, deterministic, pass/fail test for this?"* If yes → FR. If you need *measurement tools, statistical sampling, or a stopwatch* → NFR.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Classifying a Real Problem Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Given the following requirements for an Online Examination System, classify each as Functional or Non-Functional with justification."* This is a **7-mark application question** in Part B.

**Problem Statement (Online Examination System):**

> *"The system shall allow registered students to log in, view available exams, attempt multiple-choice questions, and submit answers. The system shall display the result immediately. The login page must load within 1.5 seconds. The system must support 5,000 concurrent students during peak hours. All communications between the browser and server must be encrypted using TLS 1.3. The system shall provide a 'Forgot Password' feature."*

### Classification with Justification

| # | Requirement Statement | Classification | Justification |
|---|----------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Allow students to **log in, view exams, attempt, and submit** | **Functional** | Describes specific system behaviors/actions |
| 2 | **Display the result immediately** | **Functional** | Describes a specific output behavior (despite "immediately," it's a *what*, not a *how well*) |
| 3 | **Login page must load within 1.5 seconds** | **Non-Functional (Performance)** | Contains a measurable time metric (1.5s) |
| 4 | **Support 5,000 concurrent students** | **Non-Functional (Scalability/Capacity)** | Contains a measurable count metric |
| 5 | **Encrypt using TLS 1.3** | **Non-Functional (Security)** | Specifies a security constraint/standard |
| 6 | **Provide 'Forgot Password' feature** | **Functional** | Describes a specific feature |

> [!WARNING]
> **Trap Question (Frequently Tested):** Requirement #2 — *"display the result immediately"* — is **Functional**, not Non-Functional! The word "immediately" is misleading. To make it NFR, it must specify *how fast*: e.g., *"display the result within 3 seconds"*. This subtle distinction is a **favorite of KTU examiners** to test conceptual clarity.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Requirement Engineering Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **5 sequential activities** of Requirement Engineering, along with their key techniques and outputs. This is the standard process flow referenced in the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Requirement Engineering] --> B[Elicitation]
    B --> C[Analysis and Negotiation]
    C --> D[Specification and Documentation]
    D --> E[Validation]
    E --> F[Management]
    F --> G[Approved SRS Document]
    G --> H[END: Handoff to Design Phase]
    B -. Conflicts Found .-> C
    C -. New Requirements .-> B
    E -. Defects Found .-> D
    F -. Change Requests .-> C
```

**Node Definitions:**
- `Elicitation`: Discover requirements via interviews, workshops, observation
- `Analysis and Negotiation`: Classify, prioritize, resolve conflicts
- `Specification and Documentation`: Produce the SRS document per IEEE 830
- `Validation`: Reviews, walkthroughs, acceptance test design
- `Management`: Change control, version control, traceability

## 4.2 Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Software Requirements] --> R1[Functional Requirements]
    R --> R2[Non-Functional Requirements]
    R1 --> R1A[Business Rules]
    R1 --> R1B[Use Case Behaviors]
    R1 --> R1C[Data Manipulations]
    R1 --> R1D[User Interactions]
    R1 --> R1E[System Outputs]
    R2 --> R2A[Performance]
    R2 --> R2B[Security]
    R2 --> R2C[Usability]
    R2 --> R2D[Reliability and Availability]
    R2 --> R2E[Maintainability]
    R2 --> R2F[Portability]
    R2 --> R2G[Scalability]
    R2A --> R2A1[Response Time]
    R2A --> R2A2[Throughput]
    R2A --> R2A3[Resource Utilization]
    R2B --> R2B1[Authentication]
    R2B --> R2B2[Authorization]
    R2B --> R2B3[Encryption]
    R2B --> R2B4[Audit Logging]
    R2D --> R2D1[MTBF and MTTR Metrics]
    R2D --> R2D2[Recovery Time Objective]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: FR and NFR Interaction

The following block diagram illustrates how a single use case is composed of both FR (functional steps) and NFR (quality constraints applied at each step). This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**, useful for explaining the *interplay* between FR and NFR in real systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UI[User Interface Layer] --> A1[Step 1: User Login - FR]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Authentication Check - FR]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Session Creation - FR]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Dashboard Render - FR]
    A1 -. applies .-> N1[NFR: Response Time under 2 sec]
    A2 -. applies .-> N2[NFR: Encrypted Password Hash]
    A3 -. applies .-> N3[NFR: Session Timeout 30 min]
    A4 -. applies .-> N4[NFR: 99.9 percent Availability]
    UI -. applies .-> N5[NFR: Usability WCAG 2.1 Compliant]
    A1 -. applies .-> N6[NFR: Scalability 10000 concurrent]
    A2 -. applies .-> N7[NFR: Audit Log of All Attempts]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid arrows (→) represent the **functional flow** — what the system does step-by-step.
- Dotted arrows (-. applies .->) represent the **non-functional constraints** that wrap around each functional step — how well it must do it.
- Together, they show that **NFRs are orthogonal to FRs** — they cut across multiple functional steps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This orthogonality is why NFRs are sometimes called **"cross-cutting concerns"** in industry. They cannot be implemented in one place; they must be *enforced* across all functional components. This is a **frequently tested short-answer question** (3 marks).

## 4.4 Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Conceptual Block Diagram

A **Requirements Traceability Matrix** links each requirement to its design, code, and test cases. The KTU syllabus explicitly mentions traceability as a quality criterion for a good SRS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FR1[FR 1: Login Feature] --> D1[Design: LoginModule]
    FR1 --> T1[Test Case TC01: Valid Login]
    FR1 --> T2[Test Case TC02: Invalid Login]
    FR2[FR 2: Generate Report] --> D2[Design: ReportGenerator]
    FR2 --> T3[Test Case TC03: Empty Report]
    FR2 --> T4[Test Case TC04: Populated Report]
    NFR1[NFR 1: Response Time 2s] --> T5[Performance Test PT01]
    NFR2[NFR 2: TLS 1.3 Encryption] --> T6[Security Test ST01]
    D1 --> C1[Code: AuthService.java]
    D2 --> C2[Code: ReportService.java]
```

**Key Concept — Traceability Directions:**
- **Forward Traceability:** Requirement → Design → Code → Test *(ensures every requirement is implemented and tested)*
- **Backward Traceability:** Test → Code → Design → Requirement *(ensures no extra/undocumented features exist)*

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements. Give two examples of each for an Online Banking System.**

**Model Answer:**

**Functional Requirements (FR):** These describe the specific functions, features, and behaviors that the system must support. They answer *"what the system should do."*

Examples for Online Banking:
1. *"The system shall allow registered users to transfer funds between their own accounts."*
2. *"The system shall generate a monthly account statement in PDF format."*

**Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):** These describe the quality attributes, performance constraints, and operational characteristics of the system. They answer *"how well the system should perform its functions."*

Examples for Online Banking:
1. *"The fund transfer transaction shall complete within 3 seconds under normal load."*
2. *"The system shall be available 99.99% of the time."*

**Key Distinction:** FRs are testable with pass/fail test cases; NFRs require measurement against quantitative metrics.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Non-Functional Requirement? List any four categories of NFRs with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Non-Functional Requirement (NFR)** specifies a quality attribute, constraint, or performance characteristic that the system must satisfy, defining *how well* the system operates rather than *what* it does.

**Four Categories with Examples:**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Performance** | "Page load time shall be ≤ 2 seconds for 95% of requests." |
| 2 | **Security** | "All sensitive data must be encrypted using AES-256 at rest." |
| 3 | **Usability** | "A new user shall complete onboarding within 5 minutes without help." |
| 4 | **Reliability** | "MTBF shall be ≥ 5,000 hours with MTTR ≤ 30 minutes." |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions of 14 marks offer an **internal choice**. Students must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full. Each choice has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**A. (a)** Explain the **Requirement Engineering process** in detail. List and describe any **five activities** involved in it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Requirement Engineering (RE) is a systematic process of defining, documenting, and maintaining software requirements. It is performed during the early phases of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) and ensures that the final software product meets stakeholder needs. RE consists of the following five core activities:

**1. Requirements Elicitation:**
This is the first and most critical activity, involving the discovery and collection of requirements from stakeholders, users, customers, and domain experts. Common techniques include:
- **Interviews** (one-on-one structured/unstructured sessions)
- **Questionnaires** (for large user groups)
- **Observation** (watching users perform current tasks)
- **Workshops / JAD sessions** (Joint Application Development)
- **Brainstorming and use case analysis**

**2. Requirements Analysis:**
Elicited requirements are often ambiguous, conflicting, or incomplete. The analysis activity involves:
- **Classification** into Functional vs Non-Functional
- **Prioritization** using the MoSCoW technique (Must, Should, Could, Won't)
- **Conflict resolution** via negotiation between stakeholders
- **Feasibility analysis** (technical, economic, operational)

**3. Requirements Specification:**
The analyzed requirements are documented in a formal **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** document following the **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148-2018** standard. The SRS includes:
- Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements, Appendices
- Both functional and non-functional requirements
- Use case diagrams, data flow diagrams, and data dictionaries

**4. Requirements Validation:**
This activity verifies that the documented requirements are *correct, complete, consistent, unambiguous, testable, verifiable,* and *traceable*. Techniques include:
- **Reviews and Inspections** by peers and stakeholders
- **Walkthroughs** led by the author
- **Prototyping** to validate user expectations
- **Acceptance Test-Case generation** to ensure testability

**5. Requirements Management:**
Since requirements evolve throughout the project, this activity handles:
- **Change Control** — formal process for accepting/rejecting change requests
- **Version Control** — tracking revisions of the SRS
- **Traceability** — maintaining a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)
- **Baselining** — freezing a stable version of the SRS for a given iteration

These five activities are **iterative**, not strictly sequential — feedback loops exist between them.

---

**A. (b)** With a suitable example, explain the **MoSCoW prioritization technique** used in requirement analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **MoSCoW technique** is a widely-used time-management and prioritization method for requirements analysis, particularly popular in **Agile** methodologies. It helps stakeholders and development teams decide which requirements are most critical for delivery within a given release cycle.

**The Name "MoSCoW" is an acronym for:**

| Priority | Full Form | Meaning | Example (E-commerce site) |
|----------|-----------|---------|---------------------------|
| **M** | **Must Have** | Critical; system *cannot* function without this. Release is impossible if missing. | User login, product search, cart checkout |
| **S** | **Should Have** | Important but not vital. The system can function (painfully) without it. | Wishlist, order history |
| **C** | **Could Have** | Desirable; included only if time and resources permit. | Product reviews, recommendations |
| **W** | **Won't Have (this time)** | Agreed to be deferred to a future release. Explicitly recorded to manage expectations. | Voice search, AR product preview |

**Working Example — Food Delivery App (First Release):**

- **M — Must Have:** User registration, restaurant listing, food ordering, payment integration, order tracking.
- **S — Should Have:** Delivery rating, customer support chat, in-app coupons.
- **C — Could Have:** Loyalty points, social media sharing, dietary filters.
- **W — Won't Have:** Drone delivery, AI-based recommendation engine.

**Advantages of MoSCoW:**
1. Forces explicit discussion and consensus among stakeholders
2. Prevents *scope creep* by clearly identifying "Won't Haves"
3. Aligns delivery with business value
4. Simple to understand and apply
5. Facilitates release planning and sprint scoping in Agile

**Limitations:**
1. Subjective — different stakeholders may disagree on "Must" vs "Should"
2. Does not address *effort estimation* directly
3. Works best for short to medium-term releases

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**B. (a)** Define **Functional Requirements** and **Non-Functional Requirements**. Compare them across any **five dimensions** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Functional Requirements (FR):** Specifications of the functions, features, and behaviors the system must perform. They describe *what* the system does.
*Example:* "The system shall allow users to reset their password via email."

**Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):** Specifications of the quality attributes, constraints, and performance characteristics of the system. They describe *how well* the system performs.
*Example:* "Password reset email must be delivered within 60 seconds."

**Comparison Across Five Dimensions:**

| # | Dimension | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements |
|---|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Definition** | What the system does | How well the system does it |
| 2 | **Testability** | Tested with pass/fail test cases | Tested via measurement (timers, load tools) |
| 3 | **Scope** | Specific to a feature/module | Often system-wide, cross-cutting |
| 4 | **Stakeholder Focus** | End users, business analysts | Architects, operators, security teams |
| 5 | **Measurement** | Boolean (works / doesn't work) | Numerical (response time = 1.8s, uptime = 99.95%) |
| 6 | **Examples** | Login, search, report generation | Response time, security, scalability, usability |
| 7 | **Documentation** | Listed as use cases, user stories | Listed under "Quality Attributes" section in SRS |

**Example (Hospital Management System):**
- FR: *"The system shall allow doctors to view a patient's medical history."*
- NFR: *"The medical history page must load within 2 seconds even with 10 years of records."*

---

**B. (b)** Explain the **Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM)**. Describe its **types** and **significance** in software engineering. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is a document (usually a table) that maps each requirement to its corresponding design elements, source code modules, and test cases. It establishes a **traceable link** from requirement to implementation and verification.

**Structure of an RTM (Conceptual Table):**

| Requirement ID | Requirement Description | Design Document | Code Module | Test Case ID | Test Status |
|----------------|--------------------------|------------------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| FR-001 | User login | LoginDesign.doc | AuthService.java | TC-001, TC-002 | Pass |
| FR-002 | Generate report | ReportDesign.doc | ReportService.java | TC-003, TC-004 | Pass |
| NFR-001 | Response time ≤ 2s | Architecture.doc | — | PT-001 | Pass |

**Types of Traceability:**

1. **Forward Traceability:**
   - Maps each requirement → design → code → test cases
   - Ensures every requirement is *implemented* and *tested*
   - Used to track project completion (coverage analysis)

2. **Backward Traceability (Reverse Traceability):**
   - Maps each test case → code → design → requirement
   - Ensures no *undocumented* features exist (no gold-plating)
   - Used to verify scope discipline

3. **Bi-Directional Traceability:**
   - Combination of forward and backward
   - Most thorough; required in safety-critical systems (aerospace, medical)

**Significance of RTM:**

1. **Completeness Verification:** Ensures 100% of requirements are implemented and tested — no missing feature.
2. **Change Impact Analysis:** If a requirement changes, RTM quickly reveals *which design, code, and test cases* are affected.
3. **Defect Tracking:** When a test fails, RTM identifies which requirement is impacted.
4. **Audit Compliance:** Required by regulatory standards (FDA for medical devices, DO-178C for avionics).
5. **Project Progress Monitoring:** Percentage of requirements with passing tests = project completion metric.
6. **Prevents Scope Creep:** Backward traceability ensures developers don't add features not in the SRS.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Verified by KTU Board Examiner Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "what" with "how well":** Writing "the system should be fast" as a Functional Requirement costs 2–3 marks. Always include a measurable metric.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to write a formula derivation:** In availability questions, students often state $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ without showing the derivation. The KTU 2024 Scheme awards **2 marks** for the derivation steps — losing them is a costly mistake.
>
> 3. **Not using IEEE 830 terminology:** When discussing the SRS, students use vague terms like "documentation." Examiners expect explicit mention of **"IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148-2018 standard"** for full credit.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Validation and Verification:** A classic KTU trap. **Validation = "Are we building the RIGHT product?"** and **Verification = "Are we building the product RIGHT?"** Students often swap these and lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping examples in FR vs NFR comparison questions:** The examiner's model answer *always* includes an example for each category. Omitting examples typically costs 1 mark per missing example.
>
> 6. **In MoSCoW answers, not explaining "Won't Have":** Many students explain M, S, C but skip W. The "Won't Have" category is what makes MoSCoW *unique* — omitting it is a 1-mark deduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Requirement Engineering** = Elicitation + Analysis + Specification + Validation + Management (5 activities, iterative).
- ✅ **FR (Functional Requirement)** = *What* the system does. Always testable with pass/fail.
- ✅ **NFR (Non-Functional Requirement)** = *How well* the system does it. Requires measurement; quality attributes.
- ✅ **NFR Categories:** Performance, Security, Usability, Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, Portability, Scalability (per ISO/IEC 25010).
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ — know both the formula AND its derivation.
- ✅ **Three Nines (99.9%)** = ~8.77 hours downtime/year — memorize for fast comparison questions.
- ✅ **IEEE Standard for SRS:** **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148-2018** — mention explicitly in any documentation question.
- ✅ **MoSCoW Prioritization:** Must, Should, Could, Won't — *all four* are required for full marks.
- ✅ **Validation vs Verification:** Validation = *right product?*; Verification = *product right?* — never swap.
- ✅ **Traceability Matrix (RTM):** Forward (req → test) and Backward (test → req) — both directions.
- ✅ **Cross-cutting nature of NFRs:** NFRs apply to *multiple* features — they are *orthogonal* to FRs.
- ✅ **The "immediately" trap:** *"Display result immediately"* = FR; *"Display result within 3 seconds"* = NFR.
- ✅ **Requirement qualities (a good requirement is):** Correct, Complete, Consistent, Unambiguous, Testable, Traceable, Modifiable.
- ✅ **Why RE matters:** Late-discovered requirement errors are **10x–100x more expensive** to fix than those caught in the requirements phase.
- ✅ **Common elicitation techniques:** Interviews, Questionnaires, Observation, JAD Workshops, Brainstorming, Use Case Analysis, Domain Analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System and User Requirements; Requirement Elicitation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System and User Requirements; Requirement Elicitation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What are System Requirements?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (System Requirements):**
> *System requirements* are **structured, detailed descriptions of the system's functions, services, and operational constraints**. They precisely define *what the system shall do* and are typically expressed as *individual, testable statements* documented in a Software Requirements Specification (SRS). System requirements are written for the **development team** and are usually derived from user requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (User Requirements):**
> *User requirements* are **high-level, abstract statements of what the user (or customer) expects the system to accomplish**, expressed in natural language, diagrams, or simple tables. They focus on the *user's goals and tasks* and are intended for non-technical stakeholders. User requirements are typically translated into detailed system requirements during analysis.

### 1.2 The House-Building Analogy 🏠

Imagine you are commissioning a builder to construct your dream home.

- **User Requirements** are like your *informal wish list*: *"I want a cozy 3-bedroom house with a big kitchen, natural light, and a garden for my kids."* These statements are easy to understand, focus on the **outcome**, and are written by the *client (user)*.
- **System Requirements** are like the *architect's engineering blueprint*: *"Living room dimensions: 20'×24', load-bearing wall thickness: 9 inches, electrical points: 14, plumbing outlets: 5."* These are **precise, measurable, technical** statements written for the *construction crew (developers)*.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** A single user requirement typically expands into *multiple* system requirements. For example, *"cozy house"* could translate into ≥6 m² floor area, soundproof walls, and proper ventilation specs.

### 1.3 Classification of Requirements

Requirements are broadly classified as:

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Requirements (FR)** | Describe specific behaviors or functions of the system | "The system shall allow users to reset their password via email." |
| **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** | Define quality attributes, constraints, and performance | "The system shall respond within 2 seconds under 10,000 concurrent users." |
| **Domain Requirements** | Reflect characteristics of the application domain | "All financial transactions must comply with RBI regulations." |
| **User Requirements** | High-level goals stated by users/customers | "The ATM should be easy to use for senior citizens." |
| **System Requirements** | Detailed technical specifications for developers | "Authentication tokens expire after 15 minutes of inactivity." |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus explicitly distinguishes between **system requirements** (engineered) and **user requirements** (natural-language goals). Students must be able to **transform a user requirement into multiple measurable system requirements** — this is a high-yield exam topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Requirements Engineering (RE) Process

Requirements Engineering is the **systematic, disciplined approach** to describing system behavior, capturing stakeholder needs, and managing evolving requirements. It comprises **four core activities** that operate iteratively throughout the development lifecycle.

The **RE Process** can be broken down into:

1. **Elicitation / Discovery** — *Finding out* what stakeholders need through interviews, observation, and workshops.
2. **Analysis & Negotiation** — *Resolving conflicts* between competing requirements (e.g., security vs. usability).
3. **Specification / Documentation** — *Recording* requirements formally in an SRS document.
4. **Validation & Verification** — *Checking* that requirements are correct, complete, and represent the user's actual needs.

### 2.2 The Why & How Behind Each Activity

- **Why Elicitation?** Stakeholders often do not know what they want, or they express requirements in conflicting ways. Studies show that **misunderstood or incomplete requirements are the #1 cause of software project failure**, accounting for an estimated **56% of all defects** in delivered systems.
- **How Analysis?** Through trade-off analysis, feasibility studies, and formal modeling (UML, data flow diagrams).
- **Why Documentation?** Without written, ratified requirements, scope creep becomes inevitable. The SRS acts as a **legal contract** between customer and developer.
- **Why Validation?** Because errors in requirements are **the most expensive to fix post-deployment** — cost can be 50–200× higher than fixing them during elicitation.

### 2.3 Requirement Elicitation Techniques — Detailed Breakdown

Elicitation techniques are the **practical methods** used to discover, extract, and understand stakeholder needs. Each technique has strengths, weaknesses, and ideal use-cases.

#### 2.3.1 Interviews 🗣️
- **What:** Direct, structured or unstructured conversations with stakeholders.
- **How:** Open-ended questions (e.g., *"Tell me about your daily workflow"*) allow rich discovery. Closed questions confirm specifics.
- **Why:** Best for understanding *deep context* and uncovering *tacit knowledge* that stakeholders take for granted.
- **Weakness:** Time-consuming; depends heavily on interviewer skill; can be biased by dominant personalities.

#### 2.3.2 Questionnaires & Surveys 📋
- **What:** Written lists of questions distributed to a large audience.
- **How:** Scales (Likert 1–5), multiple choice, and free-text fields capture quantitative trends.
- **Why:** Cost-effective for **large user populations**; produces statistically analyzable data.
- **Weakness:** Low response rates; no opportunity to ask follow-up questions; may miss the *"why"*.

#### 2.3.3 Observation (Ethnography) 👀
- **What:** Analysts watch users perform their real-world tasks in their natural environment.
- **How:** Note-taking, video recording, task analysis without intervening.
- **Why:** Reveals **actual work practices** that differ from *reported* practices (the famous *"work-as-done vs. work-as-imagined"* gap).
- **Weakness:** Hawthorne effect (people change behavior when watched); time-intensive.

#### 2.3.4 Workshops & Focus Groups 👥
- **What:** Group sessions with multiple stakeholders collaborating to identify requirements.
- **How:** Facilitated discussions, joint application development (JAD) sessions, brainstorming.
- **Why:** Promotes **shared understanding**, surfaces conflicts early, and builds consensus.
- **Weakness:** Dominant voices can suppress minority views; groupthink risk.

#### 2.3.5 Brainstorming 💡
- **What:** Free-flowing idea generation session with no criticism.
- **How:** No idea is "stupid"; quantity over quality; deferred evaluation.
- **Why:** Encourages **creative, out-of-the-box** requirements, especially for innovative systems.
- **Weakness:** Requires skilled facilitator; can drift off-topic.

#### 2.3.6 Prototyping 🎨
- **What:** Building a quick, throwaway mock-up (paper, wireframe, or executable) of the system.
- **How:** Show prototype to users, gather feedback, iterate rapidly.
- **Why:** Users react better to **concrete artifacts** than abstract documents — *"I'll know it when I see it."*
- **Weakness:** Users may mistake the prototype for the final product; scope creep.

#### 2.3.7 Document Analysis 📄
- **What:** Studying existing artifacts — legacy system manuals, regulations, business forms, competitor systems.
- **How:** Extract implicit requirements from existing workflows and standards.
- **Why:** Provides **domain context** and ensures regulatory compliance.
- **Weakness:** Existing documents may be outdated or incomplete.

#### 2.3.8 Use Case / Scenario Analysis 🎬
- **What:** Narrating step-by-step stories of how users will interact with the system to achieve a goal.
- **How:** "Actor → Action → Outcome" structure; often rendered as UML use case diagrams.
- **Why:** Bridges the gap between *user requirements* and *system requirements* elegantly.
- **Weakness:** Can be over-elaborated; requires skilled analysts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Elicitation Technique Selection

There is no mathematical formula, but a **decision matrix** is essential for KTU problems:

$$
\text{Best Technique} = f(\text{Stakeholder Count}, \text{Domain Complexity}, \text{Time Budget}, \text{Knowledge Type})
$$

| Technique | Best When | Stakeholder Count | Knowledge Type | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Interviews | Deep insight needed | Small (1–10) | Tacit, contextual | High |
| Surveys/Questionnaires | Large population | Large (100+) | Quantitative, opinion | Low |
| Observation | Real workflow needed | Small | Tacit, procedural | High |
| Workshops / JAD | Consensus building | Medium (5–20) | Conflicting, multi-view | Medium |
| Brainstorming | Innovative ideas | Small–Medium | Creative, undefined | Low |
| Prototyping | Unclear UI/UX needs | Small–Medium | Visual, interactive | Medium |
| Document Analysis | Regulated domain | N/A | Explicit, documented | Low |
| Use Cases | Functional modeling | Any | Sequential, behavioral | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world KTU-relevant projects (e.g., a hospital management system), requirements engineers combine **interviews with doctors + observation in wards + document analysis of medical regulations + use case modeling** — this is called *triangulation*, and it dramatically increases requirement accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 The Requirements Transformation Pipeline — Step-by-Step

One of the most examinable KTU concepts is the **derivation of system requirements from user requirements**. Here is the exhaustive step-by-step process:

**Step 1 — Identify the Stakeholder**
First, determine *who* has a stake in the system. Stakeholders include end users, managers, regulators, and external systems.

**Step 2 — Capture the User Requirement**
Record the user's high-level need in natural language, using the *user's own vocabulary*.

**Step 3 — Decompose the User Requirement**
Break down the single user requirement into multiple smaller, testable behaviors. This is the **functional decomposition** step.

**Step 4 — Add Measurable Constraints (NFRs)**
For each decomposed behavior, add quantitative constraints: response time, accuracy, security level, etc.

**Step 5 — Assign a Unique Identifier**
Tag each system requirement with a unique ID (e.g., `SR-001`, `SR-002`) for traceability.

**Step 6 — Validate Testability**
Ensure every system requirement can be verified by a specific test case. If it cannot be tested, it is not yet a *proper* system requirement.

### 3.2 Worked Example: ATM System

Let us derive system requirements from a single user requirement, step by step.

**User Requirement (UR-1):** *"Customers should be able to withdraw cash quickly and securely from any ATM."*

**Step 1 — Stakeholder Identification:**
- Customer (primary)
- Bank operations team
- Network security team
- Hardware maintenance team

**Step 2 — Capture the User Need:**
The user wants: (a) cash withdrawal, (b) speed, (c) security, (d) location-independence.

**Step 3 — Functional Decomposition:**

$$
\text{UR-1} \rightarrow \{ \text{SR-1}, \text{SR-2}, \text{SR-3}, \text{SR-4}, \text{SR-5} \}
$$

**Step 4 — Add Measurable Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SR-1 (Authentication)} &: \text{System shall validate 4-digit PIN within } \leq 1.5 \text{ seconds.} \\
\text{SR-2 (Cash Dispensing)} &: \text{System shall dispense cash in denominations of } \unicode{8377}100, \unicode{8377}500. \\
\text{SR-3 (Transaction Limit)} &: \text{System shall enforce a daily withdrawal limit of } \unicode{8377}40{,}000. \\
\text{SR-4 (Encryption)} &: \text{All PIN data shall be encrypted using AES-256 during transmission.} \\
\text{SR-5 (Availability)} &: \text{System shall have } \geq 99.9\% \text{ uptime measured monthly.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — ID Assignment:** Each SR now has a unique tag for traceability back to UR-1.

**Step 6 — Testability Check:** Each SR is verifiable — SR-1 via load-time test, SR-3 via boundary test, SR-4 via security audit, etc.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — User vs. System Requirements

| Dimension | User Requirements | System Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Audience** | Customers, managers, end users | Developers, testers, architects |
| **Language** | Natural, informal, possibly with diagrams | Structured, formal, technical |
| **Level of Detail** | High-level goals | Detailed, measurable specifications |
| **Testability** | Often untestable as-is | Must be testable |
| **Source** | Stakeholder interviews, observation | Derived from user requirements |
| **Format** | Prose, bullet points, scenarios | Numbered, tagged statements in SRS |
| **Number per Goal** | 1 statement | Multiple (3–10) detailed statements |
| **Change Frequency** | Stable once approved | May evolve during development |

### 3.4 Conflict Resolution Algorithm (For Elicitation Disputes)

When stakeholders disagree, requirements engineers follow a structured negotiation:

$$
\text{Conflict Score} = w_1 \cdot \text{Business Value} + w_2 \cdot \text{Technical Feasibility} + w_3 \cdot \text{Cost Impact}
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ are stakeholder-weighted priorities.

The requirement with the **highest weighted score** is selected, and the rejected one is documented as a *future enhancement* in the SRS appendix.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often test the *process* of deriving system requirements from user requirements. Always show the **decomposition, measurement, and traceability** steps explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 The Requirements Engineering Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stakeholder Identification] --> B[Requirement Elicitation]
    B --> C[Requirement Analysis and Negotiation]
    C --> D[Requirement Specification SRS]
    D --> E[Requirement Validation]
    E --> F{Approved?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Baseline Requirements]
    G --> H[Requirement Management]
    H --> I[Change Request]
    I --> J{Impact Analysis}
    J --> K[Approved Change]
    J --> L[Rejected Change]
    K --> H

    classDef startNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef processNode fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionNode fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef endNode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class A startNode
    class B,C,D,E,H processNode
    class F,J decisionNode
    class G endNode
```

### 4.2 Elicitation Techniques Selection Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Project Initiated]) --> Q1{Stakeholder Count}
    Q1 -- Small 1-10 --> Q2{Goal Type}
    Q1 -- Large 100+ --> Survey[Questionnaire Survey]
    Q1 -- Medium 5-20 --> Workshop[Workshops JAD]

    Q2 -- Deep Insight --> Interview[Structured Interviews]
    Q2 -- Workflow Discovery --> Observation[Ethnographic Observation]
    Q2 -- Unclear UI --> Prototype[Prototyping]
    Q2 -- Regulated Domain --> DocAnalysis[Document Analysis]
    Q2 -- Creative Ideas --> Brainstorm[Brainstorming Sessions]

    Interview --> Output[Validated User Requirements]
    Observation --> Output
    Prototype --> Output
    DocAnalysis --> Output
    Brainstorm --> Output
    Workshop --> Output
    Survey --> Output

    Output --> Spec[SRS Document]
    Spec --> SysReq[System Requirements Derived]

    classDef gateNode fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef techNode fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#22c55e,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class Q1,Q2 gateNode
    class Interview,Observation,Prototype,DocAnalysis,Brainstorm,Workshop,Survey techNode
    class Output,Spec,SysReq outputNode
```

### 4.3 User-to-System Requirement Transformation Pipeline (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UR[User Requirement Statement] --> Decomp[Functional Decomposition]
    Decomp --> SR1[System Requirement 1]
    Decomp --> SR2[System Requirement 2]
    Decomp --> SR3[System Requirement 3]
    Decomp --> SR4[System Requirement 4]

    SR1 --> NFR1[Add NFR Constraints]
    SR2 --> NFR2[Add NFR Constraints]
    SR3 --> NFR3[Add NFR Constraints]
    SR4 --> NFR4[Add NFR Constraints]

    NFR1 --> ID1[Assign Unique ID SR-001]
    NFR2 --> ID2[Assign Unique ID SR-002]
    NFR3 --> ID3[Assign Unique ID SR-003]
    NFR4 --> ID4[Assign Unique ID SR-004]

    ID1 --> Trace[Traceability Matrix]
    ID2 --> Trace
    ID3 --> Trace
    ID4 --> Trace

    Trace --> SRS[Approved SRS Document]

    classDef inputNode fill:#6366f1,stroke:#4338ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef procNode fill:#06b6d4,stroke:#0e7490,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#10b981,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class UR inputNode
    class Decomp,NFR1,NFR2,NFR3,NFR4,ID1,ID2,ID3,ID4,Trace procNode
    class SRS outputNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In KTU exams, when asked to *illustrate* the requirement engineering process, the lifecycle flowchart (Section 4.1) is the most commonly expected answer. Always include the **feedback loop** from validation back to elicitation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between user requirements and system requirements with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | User Requirements | System Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | High-level, abstract statements of what the user expects the system to achieve, expressed in natural language. | Detailed, precise, and testable technical descriptions of the system's functions and constraints. |
| **Audience** | End users, customers, managers | Developers, testers, architects |
| **Example** | *"The system should allow customers to check their account balance."* | *"The system shall display the account balance within 2 seconds of PIN authentication, using the HTTPS protocol on port 443."* |
| **Level** | Goal-oriented (the "what") | Implementation-oriented (the "what" + "how much") |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example of each, 1 mark for difference table or clear distinction — award 3 marks total.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**List any four requirement elicitation techniques. Explain any one in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Four requirement elicitation techniques are:
1. **Interviews** — Direct conversations with stakeholders.
2. **Questionnaires / Surveys** — Written questions for a large audience.
3. **Observation (Ethnography)** — Watching users perform real tasks.
4. **Prototyping** — Building mock-ups for user feedback.

*Explanation of Interviews (1 mark for technique + 1 mark for explanation):*
Interviews are structured or unstructured face-to-face (or virtual) sessions between the requirements analyst and stakeholders. The analyst uses open-ended questions (e.g., *"Describe your daily workflow"*) to uncover tacit knowledge and follow-up questions to clarify ambiguities. Interviews are best suited for small groups and deep contextual understanding but are time-intensive and depend on the interviewer's skill.

*(1 mark for listing four techniques, 1 mark for choosing one, 1 mark for explanation — total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Students must answer either **Question A OR Question B**. Each has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the various requirement elicitation techniques. Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Requirement elicitation is the process of **discovering, gathering, and understanding** the needs and constraints of stakeholders for a software system. The major elicitation techniques are:

**1. Interviews (1 mark)**
- *Strengths:* Deep insight, allows follow-up questions, captures tacit knowledge.
- *Weaknesses:* Time-consuming, requires skilled interviewer, prone to bias.

**2. Questionnaires / Surveys (1 mark)**
- *Strengths:* Cost-effective for large populations, statistically analyzable results.
- *Weaknesses:* No follow-up, low response rates, may miss context.

**3. Observation (1 mark)**
- *Strengths:* Reveals actual work practices, uncovers unspoken requirements.
- *Weaknesses:* Hawthorne effect, time-intensive, observer bias.

**4. Workshops / JAD Sessions (1 mark)**
- *Strengths:* Builds consensus, surfaces conflicts early, promotes shared understanding.
- *Weaknesses:* Groupthink risk, dominant voices may suppress others.

**5. Prototyping (1 mark)**
- *Strengths:* Concrete feedback, reduces ambiguity, engages users.
- *Weaknesses:* Users may confuse prototype with final product, scope creep.

**6. Document Analysis (1 mark)**
- *Strengths:* Provides domain context, ensures compliance with regulations.
- *Weaknesses:* Documents may be outdated, may not reflect actual practice.

**7. Brainstorming (1 mark)**
- *Strengths:* Generates creative ideas, encourages participation.
- *Weaknesses:* Can drift off-topic, requires skilled facilitator.

**Conclusion:** In practice, requirements engineers use a **combination of techniques** (triangulation) to validate findings and ensure comprehensive requirement discovery.

---

**Q. (b)** Consider an online food delivery system (e.g., Zomato). Identify the stakeholders and derive **three user requirements** with corresponding **system requirements** for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Stakeholders Identified (1 mark):**
- Customers (end users ordering food)
- Restaurant owners / managers
- Delivery partners
- System administrators
- Payment gateway providers

**User Requirement 1 (2 marks):**
- **UR-1:** *"Customers should be able to browse restaurants and place food orders easily."*
- **Derived System Requirements:**
  - **SR-1.1:** The system shall display a list of restaurants within 5 km radius, sorted by rating.
  - **SR-1.2:** The system shall allow customers to add items to a cart and modify quantities before checkout.
  - **SR-1.3:** The system shall confirm order placement via SMS and email within 30 seconds.

**User Requirement 2 (2 marks):**
- **UR-2:** *"Delivery partners should be able to accept and fulfill orders efficiently."*
- **Derived System Requirements:**
  - **SR-2.1:** The system shall notify the nearest available delivery partner within 60 seconds of order confirmation.
  - **SR-2.2:** The system shall provide real-time GPS navigation to the delivery address.
  - **SR-2.3:** The system shall update the order status (Accepted → Picked Up → Delivered) in real-time.

**User Requirement 3 (2 marks):**
- **UR-3:** *"The system should handle payments securely."*
- **Derived System Requirements:**
  - **SR-3.1:** The system shall support UPI, credit card, and wallet payments via a PCI-DSS compliant gateway.
  - **SR-3.2:** All payment data shall be encrypted using TLS 1.3 during transmission.
  - **SR-3.3:** The system shall generate and email a digital receipt within 1 minute of successful payment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** What are functional and non-functional requirements? Explain with **at least five examples** of each for a **hospital management system**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Functional Requirements (FRs)** describe the **specific behaviors or functions** the system must perform.

**Examples for a Hospital Management System (3.5 marks):**
1. **FR-1:** The system shall allow patients to book appointments with specific doctors.
2. **FR-2:** The system shall generate and print prescriptions in PDF format.
3. **FR-3:** The system shall maintain electronic health records (EHR) for each patient.
4. **FR-4:** The system shall calculate and display the total bill including consultation, tests, and pharmacy charges.
5. **FR-5:** The system shall send automated SMS reminders to patients 24 hours before their appointment.

**Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs)** describe **quality attributes, performance, and constraints** of the system.

**Examples for a Hospital Management System (3.5 marks):**
1. **NFR-1 (Performance):** The system shall retrieve any patient record within 2 seconds.
2. **NFR-2 (Security):** All patient data shall be encrypted at rest using AES-256 and accessible only to authorized staff via role-based access control (RBAC).
3. **NFR-3 (Availability):** The system shall have ≥ 99.99% uptime, as hospital operations run 24/7.
4. **NFR-4 (Compliance):** The system shall comply with HIPAA (or India's DISHA) regulations for patient data privacy.
5. **NFR-5 (Usability):** The system interface shall be navigable by nurses and doctors with under 30 minutes of training.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Requirements Engineering process** in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss why requirements validation is critical, with at least **three validation techniques**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**The Requirements Engineering Process (3 marks):**

Requirements Engineering (RE) is a systematic process comprising four interrelated activities:

1. **Elicitation / Discovery:** Gathering requirements from stakeholders using techniques like interviews, observation, and prototyping.
2. **Analysis & Negotiation:** Resolving conflicts, prioritizing requirements, and modeling them using UML or DFDs.
3. **Specification:** Documenting requirements in a Software Requirements Specification (SRS) using natural language, structured templates, or formal methods.
4. **Validation & Verification:** Ensuring requirements are correct, complete, consistent, and represent user needs.

*(Include the lifecycle diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)*

**Why Requirements Validation is Critical (2 marks):**
- Errors in requirements are the **most expensive defects** to fix post-deployment.
- Invalid requirements lead to **rework, missed deadlines, and customer dissatisfaction**.
- Validation ensures the system solves the **right problem** (verification confirms it solves it right).

**Three Validation Techniques (2 marks):**
1. **Requirements Reviews / Inspections:** Structured walkthroughs by a team of reviewers to find defects, ambiguities, and omissions.
2. **Prototyping:** Building a working model and validating it with users to ensure requirements are correctly understood.
3. **Test-Case Generation:** Writing test cases against each requirement; if a requirement cannot be tested, it is likely ambiguous or incomplete.
4. *(Bonus: User Demonstrations / Acceptance Testing)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Writing user requirements and system requirements *interchangeably*. KTU strictly distinguishes them — never use technical jargon (e.g., *"AES-256"*) in a user requirement.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting **measurability** in system requirements. *"The system should be fast"* earns 0 marks. *"The system shall respond within 2 seconds"* earns full marks.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Listing elicitation techniques *without* explaining their strengths/weaknesses. Marks are awarded for **critical analysis**, not just enumeration.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Skipping the **traceability** step. Every system requirement must be **traceable back to a user requirement** for full marks.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Not providing a **diagram** in Q.(b) of Part B. KTU examiners specifically ask for *"neat diagrams"* and award 1–2 marks for them.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📌

- ✅ **User Requirements** = *High-level, natural-language goals* of stakeholders (the "what").
- ✅ **System Requirements** = *Detailed, testable, technical specifications* for developers (the "what + how much").
- ✅ **One User Requirement** typically expands into **multiple System Requirements** via *functional decomposition*.
- ✅ **Every system requirement must be:** Unique, Testable, Measurable, Traceable, and Complete.
- ✅ **Elicitation Techniques** = Interviews, Surveys, Observation, Workshops, Brainstorming, Prototyping, Document Analysis, Use Cases.
