- ✅ **Triangulation** (using multiple techniques) is the **industry best practice** for accurate elicitation.
- ✅ **Non-Functional Requirements** cover: Performance, Security, Usability, Reliability, Maintainability, Compliance, Portability.
- ✅ **Requirements Validation** prevents the **most expensive class of defects** in software engineering.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** Distinguish user vs. system requirements; derive system requirements from user requirements; explain elicitation techniques with examples.
- ✅ **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.
- ✅ **Use measurable terms**: "2 seconds", "AES-256", "99.9% uptime" — never vague terms like "fast" or "secure".
- ✅ **Traceability Matrix** is a high-value concept linking UR → SR → Test Cases → Modules.
- ✅ **Stakeholder identification** is the *first step* in any requirements engineering activity.

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — System and User Requirements; Requirement Elicitation Techniques**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirement Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Requirement Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Requirement Validation** is the systematic process of evaluating the discovered, elicited, negotiated, and documented software requirements to ensure that they accurately represent the real needs of the stakeholders, are internally consistent, complete, unambiguous, and form a solid foundation for subsequent design and construction activities.

According to the **IEEE 830-1998 Standard for Software Requirements Specifications (SRS)**, validation is the process of evaluating a system or component during or at the end of the development life cycle to determine whether it satisfies the specified requirements. In the context of requirements engineering, validation specifically confirms that the requirements are the *right* requirements, not merely that they have been *correctly documented*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Verification**: "Are we building the product *right*?" → Confirms the system conforms to its specifications (process-oriented, internal).
> - **Validation**: "Are we building the *right* product?" → Confirms the system meets the user's actual needs (product-oriented, external).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you commissioned an architect to design your dream home. After weeks of discussion, the architect presents a beautiful 3-floor blueprint with a swimming pool, a home theatre, and a massive garden.

- **Verification** would check: "Does the blueprint match the agreed drawing? Are the dimensions, room counts, and door positions exactly as discussed in the meeting minutes?"
- **Validation** would check: "Wait, you live alone with one elderly parent — do you really *need* 3 floors? Is the swimming pool safe for the parent? Did you actually want the garden in the *north* side where there is no sunlight?"

Requirement Validation is the architectural equivalent of *stepping back and asking the client to walk through the blueprint* to ensure the design genuinely fulfils their lifestyle needs — not just the words they spoke during the requirement-gathering meetings.

### 1.3 The Six Pillars of Validated Requirements

Every validated requirement should satisfy the following **six measurable quality attributes**, each of which is a standard KTU 2-mark short-answer topic:

| # | Attribute | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Correctness** | The requirement accurately represents a real stakeholder need |
| 2 | **Completeness** | All possible scenarios, inputs, and constraints are covered |
| 3 | **Consistency** | No requirement conflicts with another requirement |
| 4 | **Feasibility** | The requirement can be implemented within known constraints |
| 5 | **Traceability** | Each requirement can be traced back to its source and forward to design/code |
| 6 | **Testability** | A concrete, observable acceptance test can prove the requirement is met |

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Validation"** in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 is treated as a sibling activity of Elicitation, Analysis, Negotiation, Specification, and Management in the Requirements Engineering process model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Position of Validation in the Requirements Life Cycle

Validation is **not a single step at the end** — it is a *continuous, iterative discipline* applied throughout the Requirements Engineering process. The classical positions where validation is most rigorously applied are:

1. **Post-Elicitation Check** — after every round of stakeholder interviews/workshops.
2. **Post-Specification Check** — after the draft SRS document is produced.
3. **Pre-Design Gate Review** — the formal sign-off before the design phase begins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost-of-Defect Principle:** Fixing a requirement defect caught during validation costs roughly **5–10× less** than fixing the same defect after design, and approximately **50–100× less** than fixing it post-deployment. This is the primary *economic justification* for validation in KTU viva questions.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Requirement Validation Cheat Sheet

| # | Validation Technique | Core Mechanism | Best Used For | Limitation |
|---|----------------------|----------------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | **Requirements Reviews / Walkthroughs** | Author presents SRS to a peer team meeting | Detecting omissions, inconsistencies, ambiguities | Prone to human error; only as good as reviewers |
| 2 | **Formal Inspections (Fagan Inspection)** | Highly structured, role-based (Author, Reader, Scribe, Moderator, Inspector) review with entry/exit criteria | Mission-critical, safety-critical systems | Time-intensive; needs trained moderators |
| 3 | **Prototyping (Throwaway / Evolutionary)** | Build a working mock-up to let users *experience* the system | Unclear or evolving user-interface requirements | Can mislead users into thinking the system is finished |
| 4 | **Model Validation** | Apply formal methods, state machines, Z-notation, or UML behavioural models to mathematically prove correctness | Real-time, embedded, safety-critical systems | Requires mathematical expertise; high cost |
| 5 | **Acceptance Test Derivation** | Translate each requirement directly into one or more test cases | Demonstrating traceable, testable SRS | Cannot detect missing requirements — only verifies present ones |
| 6 | **Requirements Simulation / Animation** | Execute the specification in a tool that simulates the proposed system's behaviour | Complex dynamic, concurrent systems | Tool availability and learning curve |
| 7 | **Checklist-Based Review** | Use a pre-defined taxonomy of common requirement defects | Quick first-pass validation | Misses project-specific or novel defects |

### 2.3 The Six Steps of a Formal Fagan Inspection (Detailed Logical Breakdown)

The Fagan Inspection is the **gold-standard structured validation technique** and is a recurring 7-mark KTU question. The six logical steps are:

1. **Planning** — The Moderator defines the scope, schedules the inspection meeting, distributes the SRS document and checklist to the inspection team.
2. **Overview (Optional)** — The Author briefs the team on the document, especially for large or unfamiliar projects.
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each Inspector (Reader, Scribe, others) studies the SRS independently, marking potential defects using the checklist.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — The Reader walks the team through the document **page-by-page**. The Scribe logs every defect. **No solutions are proposed** — only defects are identified.
5. **Rework** — The Author fixes the identified defects and re-submits the document.
6. **Follow-up** — The Moderator verifies that all logged defects have been addressed, and either closes the inspection or returns to step 5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no solutions in the meeting?** Defect detection is a divergent (creative) cognitive task; defect fixing is a convergent (analytical) task. Mixing them destroys meeting efficiency. This is a high-yield 1-mark KTU point.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In safety-critical domains such as **aerospace (DO-178C)**, **medical devices (IEC 62304)**, and **automotive (ISO 26262)**, requirement validation is a *legally mandated activity* governed by international standards. A failure to validate requirements in the Boeing 737 MAX MCAS system (2018–2019) is widely cited as a multi-billion-dollar real-world consequence of inadequate requirement validation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Process Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Walkthrough: The Requirements Validation Pipeline (Process Derivation)

The complete validation pipeline is derived as a six-stage analytical transformation from a raw, elicited requirement to a validated, signed-off SRS entry. Each stage is presented as a logical transition.

**Step 1: Raw Elicitation Input**
A stakeholder says: *"The system should be fast."*
This is an **un-validated, ambiguous input**. "Fast" is subjective — it could mean 100 ms, 1 s, or 10 s depending on the stakeholder's frame of reference.

**Step 2: Initial Classification**
The analyst classifies the input as a **non-functional requirement (NFR)** of the *Performance* category. Classification triggers selection of appropriate validation techniques — in this case, **prototyping** and **acceptance test derivation**.

**Step 3: Negotiation and Refinement**
Through negotiation (covered in Module 1's negotiation topic), the requirement is refined to:
*"The system shall respond to any user query within **2 seconds** under a peak load of **1000 concurrent users**."*
This is now **measurable, testable, and unambiguous**.

**Step 4: Consistency Check**
The refined requirement is checked against all other requirements for conflicts. If a different requirement states *"the system shall generate a 50-page analytical report on demand"*, a **conflict** is detected: the report-generation requirement violates the 2-second response time. Resolution: split the requirement or use asynchronous processing.

**Step 5: Traceability Tagging**
The validated requirement is assigned a unique identifier (e.g., **NFR-PERF-007**) and linked both:
- **Backward** → to the stakeholder who originated it (User Story 12)
- **Forward** → to the design component (Load Balancer Module) and test case (TC-PERF-007)

**Step 6: Acceptance Test Derivation**
A concrete acceptance test is written:
- Given: 1000 concurrent simulated users
- When: Each user issues a standard query
- Then: 95th percentile response time $\le$ 2 seconds

This is a fully **validated, testable, traceable** requirement.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Validation Technique Selection

Given a requirement type, the appropriate validation technique is derived by mapping the requirement's *risk profile* against the technique's *defect-detection capability*. The KTU-recommended selection matrix is:

| Requirement Type | Risk Level | Recommended Primary Validation | Recommended Secondary Validation |
|------------------|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Functional, well-understood | Low | Checklist Review | Walkthrough |
| Functional, complex business logic | Medium | Formal Inspection (Fagan) | Acceptance Test Derivation |
| Non-Functional (Performance, Security) | High | Prototyping | Acceptance Test Derivation |
| Safety-Critical / Real-Time | Critical | Formal Methods (Model Validation) | Formal Inspection |
| User-Interface / UX | High | Throwaway Prototyping | User Walkthrough |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Logic:** The *higher the consequence of failure* (safety, financial, legal), the *more rigorous and mathematical* the validation technique must become. This is the core principle behind ISO 26262 and DO-178C.

### 3.3 Numerical Self-Check Formula (Cost-of-Defect Amplification)

While KTU typically does not require numerical calculation in this topic, examiners occasionally award bonus marks for applying the cost-amortization principle. The relative cost multiplier is given by:

$$
C(n) = C_0 \cdot R^{(n-1)}
$$

Where:
- $C(n)$ = relative cost to fix a defect discovered at phase $n$
- $C_0$ = baseline cost to fix the defect at the requirements phase
- $R$ = cost-amplification ratio per phase (industry standard is **$R \approx 5$**)
- $n$ = phase index (1 = Requirements, 2 = Design, 3 = Coding, 4 = Unit Test, 5 = Integration, 6 = Acceptance, 7 = Post-Deployment)

**Worked Example (for conceptual clarity):**
A requirement defect costs ₹10,000 to fix if caught at the requirements phase ($C_0 = 10{,}000$, $n=1$, $R=5$).

What is the cost if the same defect is discovered post-deployment ($n=7$)?

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(7) &= 10{,}000 \cdot 5^{(7-1)} \\
C(7) &= 10{,}000 \cdot 5^6 \\
C(7) &= 10{,}000 \cdot 15{,}625 \\
C(7) &= \text{₹}15{,}62{,}50{,}000 \quad (\text{₹ 1.56 Crore})
\end{aligned}
$$

This single calculation explains why **Requirement Validation is non-negotiable** in any mature software engineering process — a single missed defect at the requirements stage can grow in remediation cost by **four orders of magnitude**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Requirements Validation Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Draft SRS Document] --> step1A[Step 1: Planning of Review]
    step1A --> step2A[Step 2: Distribute Document and Checklist]
    step2A --> step3A[Step 3: Individual Inspector Preparation]
    step3A --> step4A[Step 4: Inspection Meeting Page by Page]
    step4A --> decisionA{Defects Found?}
    decisionA -- Yes --> step5A[Step 5: Log Defects via Scribe]
    step5A --> step6A[Step 6: Author Reworks the SRS]
    step6A --> step7A[Step 7: Moderator Follow Up Verification]
    step7A --> checkA{All Defects Resolved?}
    checkA -- No --> step6A
    checkA -- Yes --> endA[End: Validated and Signed Off SRS]
    decisionA -- No --> endA
```

### 4.2 Verification vs Validation — A Block-Level Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph leftBlock[Verification Block]
        v1Input[Input: Software Artifact] --> v2Process[Process: Check Against Specifications]
        v2Process --> v3Output[Output: Conformance Report]
    end
    subgraph rightBlock[Validation Block]
        val1Input[Input: Software Artifact] --> val2Process[Process: Check Against User Needs]
        val2Process --> val3Output[Output: Fitness For Use Report]
    end
    specDoc[Specification Document] -.feeds.-> v2Process
    userNeed[User Real World Need] -.feeds.-> val2Process
    v3Output --> meta1[Answer: Are we building the product RIGHT]
    val3Output --> meta2[Answer: Are we building the RIGHT product]
```

### 4.3 Validation Technique Selection — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Start: Incoming Requirement] --> q1{Safety Critical or Real Time?}
    q1 -- Yes --> nodeA[Use Formal Methods and Model Validation]
    q1 -- No --> q2{User Interface or UX Heaviness?}
    q2 -- Yes --> nodeB[Use Throwaway Prototyping]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Complex Business Logic?}
    q3 -- Yes --> nodeC[Use Formal Fagan Inspection]
    q3 -- No --> q4{High Volume Requirements?}
    q4 -- Yes --> nodeD[Use Checklist Based Review]
    q4 -- No --> nodeE[Use Walkthrough with Acceptance Test Derivation]
    nodeA --> finishNode[End: Validated Requirement]
    nodeB --> finishNode
    nodeC --> finishNode
    nodeD --> finishNode
    nodeE --> finishNode
```

### 4.4 Architecture: Where Validation Fits in the Requirements Engineering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Elicitation] --> phase2[Analysis]
    phase2 --> phase3[Negotiation]
    phase3 --> phase4[Specification Draft SRS]
    phase4 --> valLoop[Validation Loop]
    valLoop --> checkPhase{Quality Attributes Met?}
    checkPhase -- No --> phase2
    checkPhase -- Yes --> phase5[Management and Sign Off]
    phase5 --> phase6[Baseline Frozen SRS]
    valLoop -.feedback.-> phase1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **Verification** and **Validation** in software engineering. Which of the two ensures that the *right* product is being built?

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Verification | Validation |
|-------|--------------|------------|
| Question Answered | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| Focus | Internal — checks against specifications, design, and standards | External — checks against real user needs and expectations |
| Activities | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, unit testing, integration testing | User acceptance testing, prototyping, beta testing, model validation |
| Phase | Mostly during development | Mostly at the end of each phase and at delivery |
| Nature | Static or dynamic; process-oriented | Dynamic, execution-oriented; product-oriented |

**Validation** is the activity that confirms the *right* product is being built, because it checks the system against actual stakeholder needs rather than against the document we ourselves wrote.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for the core question each answers, 1 mark for activity distinction, 1 mark for concluding statement.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List any **six** quality attributes that a validated software requirement specification (SRS) must satisfy.

**Model Answer:**

A validated SRS must satisfy the following six quality attributes:

1. **Correctness** — Every requirement truly represents a stakeholder need.
2. **Completeness** — All possible scenarios, inputs, outputs, and error conditions are described.
3. **Consistency** — No requirement contradicts any other requirement.
4. **Feasibility** — Each requirement can be implemented within the project's known technical, time, and budget constraints.
5. **Traceability** — Every requirement can be traced forward to design and test, and backward to its source stakeholder.
6. **Testability** — A concrete, observable, and measurable test can be written to verify each requirement.

*(3 marks: 0.5 mark per attribute.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Requirement Validation**. Why is it considered the most cost-effective activity in the entire software development life cycle? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **six-step Fagan Inspection Method** for validating a Software Requirements Specification. Highlight the role of the **Moderator** and **Scribe** in this process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Requirement Validation is the process of evaluating the requirements defined for a software system to ensure that they represent the real needs of the stakeholders, are complete, consistent, unambiguous, and form a sound basis for design and construction. It answers the question "Are we building the right product?"

**Why it is the most cost-effective activity (5 marks):**

1. **Cost Amplification Across Phases:** The cost of fixing a defect grows exponentially as the project progresses. A defect caught at the requirements phase costs approximately **5 times less** than one caught in design, and roughly **100 times less** than one caught post-deployment.

2. **Root Cause Elimination:** Most downstream defects (design errors, code bugs, integration failures) trace back to ambiguous, missing, or incorrect requirements. Validation attacks the *root cause* rather than the *symptom*.

3. **Rework Reduction:** Validated requirements reduce the number of design changes, code re-writes, and re-tests downstream.

4. **Stakeholder Alignment:** Validation ensures that every stakeholder has a shared, common understanding, drastically reducing the risk of delivering a system that the customer rejects at acceptance testing.

5. **Risk Mitigation:** For safety-critical and financial systems, validation prevents catastrophic failures and associated legal/financial liabilities.

6. **Documented Evidence:** Validation produces a sign-off record that serves as legal and contractual evidence of agreed scope.

---

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The **Fagan Inspection** is a highly structured, systematic, and well-documented method for validating software artefacts, originally proposed by Michael Fagan of IBM in 1976. The six steps are:

1. **Planning (1 mark):** The **Moderator** (inspection leader) selects the team, distributes the SRS document and a defect-checklist, and schedules the meeting. The team typically consists of an Author, a Reader, a Scribe, and one or more Inspectors.

2. **Overview (Optional, 0.5 mark):** The Author presents the document's structure and key areas to the team, especially useful for large documents or new team members.

3. **Individual Preparation (1 mark):** Each Inspector studies the document independently and lists potential defects using the checklist. This is essential because the meeting itself is for *consolidation*, not first-time reading.

4. **Inspection Meeting (1.5 marks):** The Reader walks the team through the SRS page-by-page. The **Scribe** logs every defect raised. **Crucially, no solutions are proposed** during the meeting — only defects are identified, to keep the meeting focused and time-boxed.

5. **Rework (1 mark):** The Author takes the logged defect list and fixes each defect in a new version of the SRS.

6. **Follow-up (1 mark):** The Moderator verifies that every logged defect has been properly addressed and decides whether to close the inspection or send the document back to the Rework stage.

**Role of Moderator (free, 0.5 mark):** Schedules meetings, leads the team, ensures entry/exit criteria, decides closure, mediates disputes.
**Role of Scribe (free, 0.5 mark):** Records every defect raised, ensuring no idea is lost; produces the formal inspection log.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Requirement Reviews, Prototyping, and Formal Methods** as Requirement Validation techniques. When would you choose each? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Requirement Traceability** and describe the construction and use of a **Traceability Matrix** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Dimension | Requirements Reviews | Prototyping | Formal Methods |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Mechanism** | Manual peer examination of the SRS document | Build a working mock-up to let users experience the system | Mathematical models (Z, VDM, state machines) |
| **Defect Types Detected** | Ambiguity, inconsistency, omissions | Missing or misunderstood user needs, UI defects | Logical contradictions, unreachable states, race conditions |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Skill Required** | General analyst skill | UI/UX and development skill | Mathematical, formal-method expertise |
| **Best Used For** | Most projects, first-line defence | Unclear user requirements, UI-heavy systems | Safety-critical, real-time, embedded systems |
| **Limitation** | Human error, reviewer fatigue | May mislead users about completeness | Steep learning curve, slow |

**When to choose (free-text, embedded in marks):** Choose **Reviews** as the baseline for almost every project. Add **Prototyping** when user requirements are unclear, evolving, or UI-heavy. Reserve **Formal Methods** for systems where failure is catastrophic — avionics, medical devices, nuclear controls.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for table content, 2 marks for comparison statements, 1 mark for selection guidance.)*

---

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Requirement Traceability (2 marks):**
Requirement Traceability is the ability to follow the life of a requirement in both a *backward* direction (from requirement to its originating stakeholder, user need, or business goal) and a *forward* direction (from requirement to its design, code, and test artefacts). Traceability is one of the six pillars of a validated SRS.

**Why Traceability Matters (1.5 marks):**
- Enables impact analysis when a requirement changes
- Provides coverage proof that every requirement is implemented and tested
- Supports audit and regulatory compliance
- Reduces the risk of gold-plating (un-needed features)

**Traceability Matrix — Construction (2 marks):**
A Traceability Matrix is a table whose rows are requirements (each with a unique ID) and whose columns are the downstream and upstream links. A typical forward-traceability matrix contains:

| Req ID | Description | Source Stakeholder | Design Component | Code Module | Test Case ID |
|--------|-------------|--------------------|------------------|-------------|---------------|
| FR-01 | User login with password | Customer A | Login Module | Auth.java | TC-LOGIN-01 |
| FR-02 | Generate monthly report | Manager B | Report Module | ReportGen.py | TC-REPORT-04 |
| NFR-PERF-007 | Response time $\le$ 2 s | Operations Head | Load Balancer | PerfTune.js | TC-PERF-007 |

**Use of the Matrix (1.5 marks):**
- During change management, locate all downstream artefacts affected by a changed requirement.
- During test planning, prove that every requirement has at least one test case.
- During audit, demonstrate end-to-end coverage from stakeholder need to validated test.
- Detect orphan requirements (no design/code/test) or orphan code (no parent requirement).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** — Examiners deduct up to **2 full marks** for using them interchangeably. Always quote the standard "right product vs. right way" phrasing.
> 2. **Listing techniques without explanation** — Naming "Fagan Inspection" or "Prototyping" without describing *how* they validate requirements earns only partial credit. Always pair the name with its mechanism and the type of defect it detects.
> 3. **Skipping the cost-amortization argument** — A "Why is validation important?" question without the cost-multiplier argument is incomplete. Always state the **5× to 100× cost amplification** principle.
> 4. **Forgetting the "no solutions in the meeting" rule** — A direct 1-mark KTU question tests this. The Fagan Inspection meeting identifies defects **only**; solutions are deferred to the Rework stage.
> 5. **Traceability matrix without an example** — A theoretical definition of traceability without a worked-out matrix sample is treated as a half-answer. Always provide a 3-row example matrix.
> 6. **Ignoring feasibility** — Many students list only four or five SRS quality attributes. **Feasibility** is the most-frequently forgotten one and is a direct 0.5-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Requirement Validation** confirms that the documented requirements represent the *real* stakeholder needs.
- Core question answered: **"Are we building the RIGHT product?"**
- Pairs with Verification: **"Are we building the product RIGHT?"**
- Six quality attributes of a validated SRS: **Correctness, Completeness, Consistency, Feasibility, Traceability, Testability**.
- Seven primary validation techniques: **Reviews, Walkthroughs, Fagan Inspections, Prototyping, Model Validation, Acceptance Test Derivation, Checklist Reviews**.
- **Fagan Inspection** has six steps: **Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up**.
- The Fagan Inspection meeting is for **defect detection only**, never for solutions.
- **Cost-of-Defect Principle:** Fixing a defect post-deployment can cost up to **$R^6$ times** the requirements-phase cost (where $R \approx 5$).
- **Prototyping** is the best technique when user requirements are unclear or UI-heavy.
- **Formal Methods** are reserved for safety-critical and real-time systems.
- **Traceability Matrix** links requirement IDs to source stakeholders, design components, code modules, and test cases.
- Validation is **continuous**, not a one-time event at the end of requirements gathering.
- Always answer with: definition + technique + example + 2-line summary to score full marks on a KTU 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feasibility Analysis and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feasibility Analysis and Its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Feasibility Analysis** (also called the **Feasibility Study** or **Preliminary Investigation**) is a systematic, multi-dimensional evaluation conducted during the initial phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to determine whether a proposed software project is **viable, practical, and worth the required investment of time, money, and resources**. It serves as the critical "go / no-go" decision gate that precedes detailed system analysis and design.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, feasibility analysis is positioned within the broader **Software Process Framework** and is considered the first managerial activity after problem identification. It answers the central question:

> *"Should we build this system — and can we build this system — under the current organizational, technical, and economic constraints?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Feasibility Analysis belongs to the *Process Framework & Project Initiation* segment of Module 1. It is a frequently tested 14-mark topic in End Semester Evaluations (ESE) and must be understood both definitionally and analytically (especially Economic Feasibility with numerical problems).

## 1.2 The TELOS Acronym — A KTU Favourite

The five classical dimensions of feasibility are often remembered by the acronym **TELOS**:

| Letter | Dimension | Core Question Answered |
|:------:|-----------|------------------------|
| **T** | **T**echnical | *Can we build it?* |
| **E** | **E**conomic | *Can we afford it, and will it pay off?* |
| **L** | **L**egal | *Are we allowed to build it?* |
| **O** | **O**perational | *Will it be used effectively?* |
| **S** | **S**chedule | *Can we finish it in time?* |

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU textbooks (notably Roger S. Pressman-style references) list only **four** types — merging *Legal* into *Operational*. Always check the lecture notes. For exam purposes, presenting **all five** is the safest, most comprehensive approach and earns full credit.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Road-Trip Planner

Imagine three friends planning a road trip from **Thiruvananthapuram to Delhi** (≈ 3,000 km) in a 15-year-old hatchback:

- **Technical Feasibility** → *"Will the car make it? Do we have a spare tyre? Can the engine handle the Himalayas?"* → Checks if the **tools/technology** are sufficient.
- **Economic Feasibility** → *"Do we have ₹15,000 for fuel, tolls, and food? Is the trip worth the cost?"* → Checks if the **budget** justifies the journey.
- **Operational Feasibility** → *"Will we actually enjoy the trip? Are we physically fit to drive 12 hours a day? Does everyone have a valid license?"* → Checks if the **end-users (us)** can and will use the outcome.
- **Legal Feasibility** → *"Do we have valid vehicle papers, pollution certificates, and state-entry permits?"* → Checks **regulatory compliance**.
- **Schedule Feasibility** → *"We only have a 7-day vacation. Is the trip realistic in that window?"* → Checks **time constraints**.

If **any** check fails badly, the trip (or project) is declared **infeasible**.

## 1.4 Position in the SDLC

Feasibility Analysis is performed **after** the *Problem Statement* is defined but **before** detailed *Requirements Analysis*. It is part of the **Project Initiation** phase.

> [!NOTE]
> The deliverable of a feasibility study is a **Feasibility Report** (sometimes called a *Feasibility Study Document*) submitted to management for approval. It typically contains: project scope, candidate solutions, cost-benefit summary, risk assessment, and a final recommendation (Proceed, Defer, or Reject).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Types of Feasibility — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Technical Feasibility

**Definition:** Technical Feasibility assesses whether the organization possesses the **hardware, software, technical infrastructure, and human expertise** required to design, develop, implement, and maintain the proposed system.

**Key Evaluation Points:**
- Availability of required **technology stack** (programming languages, frameworks, databases).
- Skill set of the **development team** — gap analysis between existing skills and required skills.
- Compatibility with **existing legacy systems**.
- Scalability, performance, and security considerations of the proposed architecture.
- Hardware capacity (servers, network bandwidth, storage).

**Why it matters:** A project is technically infeasible if the required technology does not exist, is too immature, or if the team lacks the competence to deliver. Modern example: attempting a real-time quantum-cryptography project with a team of only web developers.

---

### 2.1.2 Economic (Financial) Feasibility

**Definition:** Economic Feasibility — often the **most decisive** type — evaluates whether the projected **benefits outweigh the costs** and whether the project is financially justifiable over its lifetime.

**Key Tools Used:**
1. **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)**
2. **Return on Investment (ROI)**
3. **Net Present Value (NPV)**
4. **Payback Period (PBP)**
5. **Break-Even Analysis (BEA)**

**Cost Categories:**
- **Development Costs:** Salaries, hardware purchase, software licenses, training.
- **Operational Costs:** Maintenance, support, infrastructure, upgrades.
- **Intangible Costs:** Employee morale, organizational disruption.

**Benefit Categories:**
- **Tangible Benefits:** Increased revenue, reduced labor cost, error reduction.
- **Intangible Benefits:** Improved customer satisfaction, better decision-making, brand image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to explain *Economic Feasibility*, always present it with **at least two numerical formulas** (e.g., ROI and Payback Period). Pure conceptual answers without formulas typically receive only 5–6 out of 7 marks in sub-parts.

---

### 2.1.3 Operational Feasibility

**Definition:** Operational Feasibility evaluates whether the proposed system will be **accepted, adopted, and effectively used** by its intended end-users and whether it integrates smoothly into the existing operational workflow of the organization.

**Key Evaluation Points:**
- **User Acceptance** — Will employees actually use the system, or resist it?
- **Management Support** — Is top management committed to change?
- **Training Requirements** — Can existing staff be retrained?
- **Organizational Culture** — Does the project align with the company's work style?
- **Workflow Disruption** — How badly will current processes be affected?

**Why it matters:** Many technically perfect and financially sound projects **fail** at the operational level because users reject them. Example: a hospital management system that doctors refuse to use because the UI is not clinician-friendly.

---

### 2.1.4 Legal Feasibility

**Definition:** Legal Feasibility determines whether the proposed system **complies with all applicable laws, regulations, contractual obligations, and intellectual property rights**.

**Key Evaluation Points:**
- Data protection and privacy laws (e.g., India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**, EU's GDPR).
- Software licensing compliance (proprietary vs. open-source).
- Labor laws (especially in HR-related systems).
- Contractual obligations with vendors and clients.
- Industry-specific regulations (e.g., HIPAA for healthcare, PCI-DSS for payments).

---

### 2.1.5 Schedule (Time) Feasibility

**Definition:** Schedule Feasibility assesses whether the project can be **completed within the required timeline**, given the available resources and dependencies.

**Key Evaluation Points:**
- Realistic estimation of development duration.
- Critical milestone analysis.
- Resource availability over the project lifespan.
- External dependencies (vendor delivery, regulatory approvals).
- Risk of schedule slippage.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and conditional notation in the formulas below uses `\vert` instead of the markdown pipe `|` to prevent table-rendering breakage.

| # | Metric | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|:-:|--------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100\,\%$ | Net Benefit in ₹, Total Cost in ₹ | Compare profitability of competing projects |
| 2 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $\text{CF}_t$ = cash flow at year $t$, $r$ = discount rate | Decide if project value exceeds opportunity cost |
| 3 | **Payback Period (PBP)** | $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Both in ₹ | Time required to recover initial outlay |
| 4 | **Break-Even Quantity (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$ | All monetary units in ₹ | Units to produce to cover total cost |
| 5 | **Total Cost Function** | $\text{TC}(Q) = \text{FC} + \text{VC} \cdot Q$ | $\text{FC}$ = Fixed Cost, $\text{VC}$ = Variable Cost per unit | Foundation of cost modelling |
| 6 | **Break-Even Revenue** | $\text{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{1 - \dfrac{\text{VC}}{\text{Price}}}$ | All in ₹ | Revenue required to break even |
| 7 | **Discount Factor** | $\text{DF}_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $r$ = discount rate, $t$ = year | Converts future cash flow to present value |
| 8 | **Profit** | $\pi = \text{Revenue} - \text{TC}$ | Monetary units | Absolute gain at a given output |

## 2.3 The Feasibility Study Process — Operational Workflow

A formal feasibility study follows a structured six-step methodology:

1. **Identify the Problem / Opportunity** — Define the business need clearly.
2. **Define the Scope** — Set boundaries; what is in and what is out.
3. **Identify Alternative Solutions** — At least 2–3 candidate approaches.
4. **Analyze Each Alternative** — Apply the TELOS dimensions.
5. **Perform Cost-Benefit Analysis** — Quantify the financial dimension.
6. **Prepare the Feasibility Report** — Recommend **Proceed, Defer, or Reject**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The final **"go / no-go" decision** rests with **management**, not the development team. The engineer's job is to present objective, evidence-based findings.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Software Engineering

Feasibility analysis is not merely academic — it is the **single highest-leverage activity** in the early SDLC. Industry data consistently shows that projects with rigorous upfront feasibility studies have:

- **30–40% lower cost overruns**, and
- **50% fewer post-deployment failures**

compared to projects that skip this step. In KTU project evaluations, the **Phase 0 Report** of any B.Tech main project is, in essence, a feasibility document.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Examples** are essential for the 14-mark Economic Feasibility questions. The derivations below combine algebraic reasoning with concrete numerical evaluation to give a complete KTU-ready treatment.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Break-Even Quantity from First Principles

**Problem Context:** A startup wants to develop a mobile application. The total cost structure of the project is defined by:
- **Fixed Cost (FC):** ₹ 5,00,000 (one-time development, hardware).
- **Variable Cost per unit (VC):** ₹ 200 (per user subscription in cloud hosting, support).
- **Selling Price per unit (P):** ₹ 700 (subscription fee per user).

**Goal:** Derive the break-even quantity and verify with revenue-cost equality.

### Step 1 — Define the Cost Function

The **Total Cost (TC)** is the sum of fixed cost (independent of volume) and variable cost (proportional to volume):

$$\text{TC}(Q) = \text{FC} + \text{VC} \cdot Q$$

This is a **linear function** of $Q$ with y-intercept $\text{FC}$ and slope $\text{VC}$.

### Step 2 — Define the Revenue Function

The **Total Revenue (TR)** is the product of price per unit and the number of units sold:

$$\text{TR}(Q) = P \cdot Q$$

This is a **linear function** of $Q$ passing through the origin with slope $P$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Break-Even Condition

At the **break-even point**, the project makes neither profit nor loss, so Total Revenue equals Total Cost:

$$\text{TR}(Q^{*}) = \text{TC}(Q^{*})$$

$$P \cdot Q^{*} = \text{FC} + \text{VC} \cdot Q^{*}$$

### Step 4 — Algebraic Isolation of $Q^{*}$

Move all terms involving $Q^{*}$ to the left side:

$$P \cdot Q^{*} - \text{VC} \cdot Q^{*} = \text{FC}$$

Factor out $Q^{*}$:

$$Q^{*} \cdot (P - \text{VC}) = \text{FC}$$

Divide both sides by $(P - \text{VC})$:

$$Q^{*} = \frac{\text{FC}}{P - \text{VC}}$$

This is the **Break-Even Quantity formula**.

### Step 5 — Numerical Substitution

Plug in $\text{FC} = 5{,}00{,}000$, $P = 700$, $\text{VC} = 200$:

$$Q^{*} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{700 - 200}$$

$$Q^{*} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{500}$$

$$Q^{*} = 1000 \text{ users}$$

**Interpretation:** The project must acquire **at least 1,000 paying users** to recover all costs. Below this, the project incurs a loss; above this, it generates profit.

### Step 6 — Verification

Compute TR and TC at $Q^{*} = 1000$:

$$\text{TR} = 700 \times 1000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{TC} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 200 \times 1000 = 5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Since $\text{TR} = \text{TC} = ₹\,7{,}00{,}000$, the break-even point is verified. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Present Value (NPV) Step-by-Step

**Problem Context:** A software project requires an initial investment of ₹ 10,00,000 today. It is expected to generate cash inflows of ₹ 3,00,000, ₹ 4,00,000, and ₹ 5,00,000 at the end of years 1, 2, and 3 respectively. The discount rate is 10% per annum.

**Goal:** Compute the NPV and determine if the project is financially viable.

### Step 1 — The General NPV Formula

NPV discounts each future cash flow back to its present value and sums them, then subtracts the initial investment:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^{t}} = \text{CF}_0 + \frac{\text{CF}_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{\text{CF}_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{\text{CF}_n}{(1+r)^n}$$

Here, $\text{CF}_0$ is typically the **negative** of the initial investment (cash outflow at $t=0$).

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

- $\text{CF}_0 = -10{,}00{,}000$ (initial outflow)
- $\text{CF}_1 = 3{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{CF}_2 = 4{,}00{,}000$
- $\text{CF}_3 = 5{,}00{,}000$
- $r = 0.10$

$$\text{NPV} = -10{,}00{,}000 + \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^3}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Each Present Value Term

**Year 1:**

$$\text{PV}_1 = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = 2{,}72{,}727.27 \text{ ₹}$$

**Year 2:**

$$\text{PV}_2 = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 3{,}30{,}578.51 \text{ ₹}$$

**Year 3:**

$$\text{PV}_3 = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = 3{,}75{,}657.40 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Present Values

$$\text{NPV} = -10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}72{,}727.27 + 3{,}30{,}578.51 + 3{,}75{,}657.40$$

$$\text{NPV} = -10{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}78{,}963.18$$

$$\text{NPV} = -21{,}036.82 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 5 — Decision Rule

- If $\text{NPV} > 0$ → **Accept** the project.
- If $\text{NPV} < 0$ → **Reject** the project.
- If $\text{NPV} = 0$ → **Indifferent** (project just recovers cost of capital).

**Conclusion:** Since $\text{NPV} \approx -₹\,21{,}037 < 0$, the project is **not financially viable** at a 10% discount rate. The team could explore lowering the discount rate, reducing initial cost, or increasing projected cash inflows.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Payback Period with Uneven Cash Flows

**Problem Context:** A project requires an initial investment of ₹ 6,00,000. Year-wise cash inflows are: Year 1 → ₹ 1,50,000; Year 2 → ₹ 2,00,000; Year 3 → ₹ 2,50,000; Year 4 → ₹ 3,00,000.

**Goal:** Compute the Payback Period.

### Step 1 — Cumulative Cash Flow Table

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cumulative Cash Flow (₹) |
|:----:|----------------:|-------------------------:|
| 0 | $-6{,}00{,}000$ | $-6{,}00{,}000$ |
| 1 | $1{,}50{,}000$ | $-4{,}50{,}000$ |
| 2 | $2{,}00{,}000$ | $-2{,}50{,}000$ |
| 3 | $2{,}50{,}000$ | $\;\;\,0$ |
| 4 | $3{,}00{,}000$ | $\;\;+3{,}00{,}000$ |

### Step 2 — Identify the Recovery Year

The cumulative cash flow turns **exactly zero at the end of Year 3**, meaning the initial investment is fully recovered in exactly 3 years.

$$\text{Payback Period} = 3.0 \text{ years}$$

### Step 3 — General Formula for Fractional Recovery (if needed)

If the cumulative cash flow becomes positive *during* a year (not at the end), the fractional payback formula is:

$$\text{PBP} = \text{Last Year with Negative CCF} + \frac{\vert \text{CCF in that Year} \vert}{\text{Cash Inflow in Next Year}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Five Feasibility Types

This matrix is a **high-yield revision tool** for KTU 14-mark questions asking for comparison.

| Dimension | Primary Question | Key Inputs | Most Common Method | Failure Mode |
|-----------|------------------|------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Technical** | Can we build it? | Tech stack, team skills, hardware | Gap analysis, prototype | Technology does not exist or team is unskilled |
| **Economic** | Should we build it? | Costs, benefits, discount rate | CBA, NPV, ROI, PBP | Costs exceed benefits over project life |
| **Operational** | Will it be used? | User attitude, training needs | User surveys, workflow analysis | User resistance or poor adoption |
| **Legal** | Are we allowed? | Laws, contracts, licenses | Compliance audit | Violation of data protection or licensing laws |
| **Schedule** | Can we finish in time? | Deadlines, resource calendar | Gantt chart, PERT/CPM | Critical path too long for deadline |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The TELOS Feasibility Wheel (Mermaid Mind Map)

This diagram visualizes how the five feasibility dimensions revolve around the central project concept.

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Feasibility Analysis))
    Technical
      Hardware check
      Software stack
      Team skills
      Scalability
    Economic
      Cost Benefit Analysis
      ROI computation
      NPV calculation
      Payback period
    Operational
      User acceptance
      Training needs
      Workflow fit
      Management support
    Legal
      Data privacy laws
      Licensing compliance
      Contract review
      IP rights
    Schedule
      Timeline analysis
      Milestone check
      Resource calendar
      Critical path
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Sequential Feasibility Study Process Flow

This block diagram shows the procedural flow of a formal feasibility study, from problem identification to the final management decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Project Idea Submitted]) --> stepB[Step 1: Define Problem and Scope]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Identify Alternative Solutions]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Apply TELOS Analysis to Each Alternative]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Perform Cost Benefit and ROI Analysis]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Assess Risks and Constraints]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Draft Feasibility Report]
    stepG --> stepH{Management Review}
    stepH -->|Approve| stepI[Proceed to Requirements Phase]
    stepH -->|Defer| stepJ[Re-evaluate Later]
    stepH -->|Reject| stepK[Project Closed]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Functional Architecture: Inputs, Process, Outputs of Feasibility Analysis

This block-level architecture illustrates the **data flow** in a feasibility study system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        i1[Business Problem Statement]
        i2[Cost Estimates]
        i3[Technical Resources]
        i4[Legal Constraints]
        i5[Time Constraints]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS
        p1[Feasibility Analysis Engine]
        p2[Cost Benefit Calculator]
        p3[Risk Assessor]
        p4[TELOS Evaluator]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        o1[Feasibility Report]
        o2[Recommendation Proceed Defer Reject]
        o3[Risk Register]
        o4[Estimated ROI and NPV]
    end

    i1 --> p1
    i2 --> p2
    i3 --> p1
    i4 --> p1
    i5 --> p1
    p1 --> p4
    p4 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> o1
    o1 --> o2
    o1 --> o3
    p2 --> o4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Feasibility Analysis. List any four types of feasibility. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Feasibility Analysis is a preliminary investigation conducted to determine whether a proposed software project is viable, practical, and worth the investment of organizational resources. It evaluates the project across multiple dimensions before committing to detailed requirements analysis and design.

**Four Types (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Technical Feasibility** — assesses available technology and expertise.
2. **Economic Feasibility** — evaluates cost-benefit and financial returns.
3. **Operational Feasibility** — checks end-user acceptance and workflow fit.
4. **Legal Feasibility** — verifies compliance with laws and regulations.

*(Schedule Feasibility may be added as a 5th type for a more complete answer.)*

---

### Q2. What is Operational Feasibility? Why is it important? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Operational Feasibility determines whether the proposed system will be **accepted, adopted, and effectively used** by the end-users, and whether it integrates smoothly into the existing organizational workflow and culture.

**Importance (1.5 marks — 0.5 each point):**
- It identifies **user resistance** early, allowing the team to plan change management and training.
- It ensures **management commitment** and organizational support, both of which are critical for successful implementation.
- A system that is technically excellent but operationally rejected will **fail post-deployment**, wasting the entire investment. Hence, operational feasibility is the difference between a built system and a *successfully used* system.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

### Question A: Explain the different types of feasibility analysis in detail. Discuss their significance in the software development process. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Part (a) — Explain Technical, Operational, and Legal Feasibility. (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

**1. Technical Feasibility (2.5 marks):**
Technical Feasibility evaluates whether the organization has the necessary **hardware, software, technical infrastructure, and skilled personnel** to develop, deploy, and maintain the proposed system. It involves a **technology stack review**, **skill gap analysis**, and assessment of **system performance, scalability, and security requirements**. A project is technically infeasible if the required technology is unavailable, immature, or if the team lacks the expertise to deliver it. *Example:* Building a real-time fraud-detection system using a team trained only in static web development is technically infeasible without significant upskilling.

**2. Operational Feasibility (2.5 marks):**
Operational Feasibility assesses whether the system will be **accepted and used effectively** by its end-users. It considers **user attitudes, training requirements, management support, and workflow disruption**. Even a technically perfect system fails if users resist it. *Example:* A new hospital management software may be technically superior, but if doctors find the UI confusing and continue to use paper records, the system fails operationally.

**3. Legal Feasibility (2 marks):**
Legal Feasibility verifies compliance with **applicable laws, regulations, contracts, and intellectual property rights**. This includes data protection laws (such as the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** in India, and **GDPR** in the EU), software licensing terms, and industry-specific regulations. A project that violates legal requirements must be redesigned or abandoned regardless of its technical or economic merits.

---

### Part (b) — Explain Economic and Schedule Feasibility with suitable examples. Why is Economic Feasibility often considered the most decisive? (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

**1. Economic Feasibility (3 marks):**
Economic Feasibility evaluates the **financial viability** of the project using tools such as **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA), Return on Investment (ROI), Net Present Value (NPV), Payback Period (PBP), and Break-Even Analysis (BEA)**. It compares the **total development and operational costs** against the **tangible and intangible benefits** expected over the project's lifetime. *Example:* A company considering an ERP system with an upfront cost of ₹50 lakh and projected annual savings of ₹12 lakh would calculate a payback period of approximately 4.17 years. If the company's required payback is 3 years, the project is economically infeasible.

**2. Schedule Feasibility (2 marks):**
Schedule Feasibility checks whether the project can be **completed within the required timeline** given the available resources, dependencies, and constraints. It involves **milestone planning, critical path analysis, and resource calendar review**. *Example:* A government project mandated to launch a citizen-services portal within 6 months is schedule-infeasible if realistic development estimates indicate 12 months of work.

**3. Why Economic Feasibility is Often the Most Decisive (2 marks):**
Economic Feasibility is frequently the most decisive dimension because **organizations operate with finite budgets and must justify every major investment in monetary terms**. While technical, operational, and legal constraints can often be overcome through training, redesign, or compliance changes, an economically unjustifiable project will simply not receive funding. Management approval — and therefore project existence — is overwhelmingly driven by ROI, NPV, and payback metrics. As the saying goes, *"A technically brilliant project that loses money is still a bad project."*

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

### Question B: Discuss Economic Feasibility in detail. With a suitable numerical example, explain Cost-Benefit Analysis, Break-Even Analysis, and Payback Period. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Part (a) — Discuss Economic Feasibility and Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) with a numerical example. (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

**1. Economic Feasibility — Overview (2 marks):**
Economic Feasibility is the most critical dimension of feasibility analysis. It evaluates whether the **benefits derived from a software project will exceed its costs** over the project's useful lifetime. It involves identifying and quantifying all **development and operational costs** (hardware, software, personnel, training, maintenance) and all **tangible and intangible benefits** (revenue increase, cost reduction, improved customer satisfaction, better decision-making). The result is typically expressed through metrics like **ROI, NPV, Payback Period, and Break-Even Point**.

**2. Cost-Benefit Analysis (2.5 marks):**
Cost-Benefit Analysis is a systematic process of **comparing total project costs with total project benefits** over a defined time horizon. Costs include one-time development costs and recurring operational costs. Benefits are categorized as **tangible** (directly measurable in money) and **intangible** (e.g., improved morale, better reputation). A project is considered viable if **Total Benefits > Total Costs**, and the difference is the **Net Benefit**.

**3. Numerical Example for CBA (2.5 marks):**
Consider a customer relationship management (CRM) project with the following 5-year profile:

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------:|
| Development cost | 8,00,000 |
| Annual maintenance | 1,00,000 |
| Annual tangible benefit (revenue + savings) | 4,00,000 |

**Total Cost over 5 years:**

$$\text{TC} = 8{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}00{,}000 \times 5) = 8{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Total Benefit over 5 years:**

$$\text{TB} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Net Benefit:**

$$\text{NB} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 13{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**ROI:**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{13{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 53.85\%$$

Since the ROI is positive and the net benefit is ₹ 7,00,000, the project is **economically feasible**.

---

### Part (b) — Explain Break-Even Analysis and Payback Period with a numerical example. (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

**1. Break-Even Analysis (3 marks):**
Break-Even Analysis determines the **number of units (or revenue) at which total revenue equals total cost**, meaning the project neither makes a profit nor a loss. The break-even quantity $Q^{*}$ is derived from the equation $\text{TR}(Q) = \text{TC}(Q)$, which gives:

$$Q^{*} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$$

**Numerical Example for BEA (included in derivation):**
For a project with **Fixed Cost = ₹ 5,00,000**, **Variable Cost = ₹ 200 per unit**, and **Selling Price = ₹ 700 per unit**:

$$Q^{*} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{700 - 200} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{500} = 1000 \text{ units}$$

**Interpretation:** The project must sell 1,000 units to break even. Units below this generate loss; units above this generate profit.

**2. Payback Period (2.5 marks):**
The Payback Period is the **time required to recover the initial investment** from the project's cash inflows. A shorter payback period is preferred because it indicates faster recovery and lower risk.

**Numerical Example for PBP:**
Initial investment = ₹ 6,00,000. Annual cash inflow = ₹ 1,50,000 (constant).

$$\text{PBP} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = 4 \text{ years}$$

If the inflows are uneven, cumulative cash flow is computed year by year, and the period at which cumulative inflow equals or exceeds the initial investment is identified.

**3. Combined Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
Both BEA and PBP give management **early-stage, quantifiable confidence** in the project. BEA tells *how much must be sold*, while PBP tells *how long until recovery*. Together with ROI and NPV, they form the **quantitative backbone of the feasibility report**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Feasibility Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Naming only 3 types instead of 4 or 5** — Always list the maximum you know (TELOS gives 5). Half-mark deductions apply per missing type.
> 2. **No numerical example in Economic Feasibility answers** — In a 14-mark question, a part (a) or (b) that asks about Economic Feasibility *without* numbers will lose 2–3 marks unless supplemented with even one worked example.
> 3. **Confusing NPV with ROI** — NPV is an **absolute** monetary value; ROI is a **percentage**. Mixing up formulas is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the Feasibility Report** — Most students explain the analysis but forget the *deliverable* (the report and its go/no-go decision). This is a 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Writing operational feasibility as "will it work"** — The technically correct framing is *"will it be **used** effectively"*, which addresses user adoption, not technical functioning.
> 6. **Skipping the legal dimension** — Many students ignore legal feasibility, especially for the Indian **DPDP Act, 2023**, which is a hot topic for KTU's contemporary question papers.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Feasibility Analysis**

- **Feasibility Analysis = "Go / No-Go" decision gate** before detailed SDLC phases. Conducted during **Project Initiation**.
- **TELOS Acronym:** **T**echnical, **E**conomic, **L**egal, **O**perational, **S**chedule. (Some KTU texts merge L into O — but listing all 5 is the safe strategy.)
- **Technical Feasibility** checks: hardware, software, team skills, scalability, integration with legacy systems.
- **Economic Feasibility** checks: cost vs. benefit. Tools = **CBA, ROI, NPV, PBP, BEA**. **This is usually the most decisive dimension.**
- **Operational Feasibility** checks: user acceptance, training, management support, workflow fit, change management.
- **Legal Feasibility** checks: data protection laws (DPDP Act 2023, GDPR), licensing, IP, contracts, industry-specific regulations.
- **Schedule Feasibility** checks: timeline, milestones, critical path, resource calendar.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100\,\%$
  - $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^{t}}$
  - $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$
  - $Q^{*} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$
- **Process Steps:** Define Problem → Define Scope → Identify Alternatives → Apply TELOS → Perform CBA → Draft Feasibility Report → Management Decision (Proceed / Defer / Reject).
- **Deliverable:** The **Feasibility Report** submitted to management.
- **Decision Rule for NPV:** $\text{NPV} > 0$ → Accept; $\text{NPV} < 0$ → Reject; $\text{NPV} = 0$ → Indifferent.
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** Frequently a 14-mark full question in ESE Module 1; also a 3–4 mark short-answer favorite.
- **Real-world mantra:** *"Measure twice, cut once"* — Feasibility analysis is the engineering equivalent of this proverb, saving lakhs of rupees and months of effort in the long run.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SRS Document Characteristics and Structure; Case Study: SRS for College Library Management Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** SRS Document Characteristics and Structure; Case Study: SRS for College Library Management Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of SRS

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (IEEE 29148 / IEEE 830 aligned)**
> A **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal, structured, and comprehensive document that completely describes the **functional**, **non-functional**, and **interface** requirements of a software system, intended to serve as the authoritative reference for developers, testers, project managers, and stakeholders throughout the software development life cycle (SDLC).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Software Engineering syllabus**, the SRS is treated as a **contractual artifact** between the *client* and the *development team*. It captures **what** the system must do (and explicitly **what it must not do**), independent of **how** it will be implemented.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint"

Imagine you are commissioning a house.

| Construction Domain | Software Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Architectural blueprint | **SRS Document** |
| Customer's wishlist (verbal) | Informal user requirements |
| Engineer translating wishes to drawings | Requirements Analyst |
| Builder executing the design | Development Team |
| Municipal inspector verifying the build | QA & Validation Team |
| Renovation plans later | Maintenance & Change Requests |

Just as a blueprint specifies *how many rooms, where the plumbing runs, and what load the foundation must bear* — without telling the mason *which hammer to swing* — an SRS specifies **system behaviour and constraints**, but **not algorithms, code structure, or technology stack**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for KTU Exams**
> An SRS is a **behavioural specification**, NOT a **design specification**. If your answer mentions database schemas, class diagrams, or programming language choices, you are describing a Design Document (SDD), not an SRS — a common error that costs marks.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need an SRS?

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three justifications:

1. **Communication Bridge** — A single source of truth shared by developers, customers, testers, and maintainers.
2. **Validation Baseline** — Allows requirements to be reviewed, tested, and approved *before* coding begins (defect cost magnification: a requirement defect caught at the requirements phase is roughly **10x–100x cheaper** to fix than one caught after deployment).
3. **Change Control Reference** — All future scope changes are measured against the baseline SRS, enabling formal **Change Management** under IEEE 29148.

## 1.4 Need for SRS — The Empirical Motivation

> [!NOTE] **Industry Benchmark (Standish Group CHAOS Report pattern)**
> Projects with clearly written, reviewed, and approved SRS documents show a **failure rate below 15%**, whereas projects starting with vague or undocumented requirements exhibit failure rates exceeding **60%**. The 2024 NEP curriculum uses this statistic to justify the *upfront engineering* emphasis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics of a "Good" SRS (The 8 Pillars)

A high-quality SRS as prescribed by **IEEE Std 830-1998** and adopted in the KTU 2024 scheme must satisfy eight characteristics. These are **high-yield** — almost every 3-mark and 14-mark question tests at least one of them.

### 2.1.1 Correct
- The SRS must correctly represent the **actual needs** of the stakeholders.
- *Test*: Every requirement can be traced back to a documented source (user, regulation, standard).

### 2.1.2 Unambiguous
- Every requirement has **only one possible interpretation**.
- *Test*: Two independent readers (or the KTU examiner) must arrive at the same meaning.
- *Anti-pattern*: "The system shall be user-friendly" — *user-friendly* is subjective.
- *Correct form*: "The system shall respond to a search query within **2 seconds** for a database of up to **50,000 records**."

### 2.1.3 Complete
- All functional, non-functional, and interface requirements are present, including **responses to all input classes**, even invalid/abnormal ones.

### 2.1.4 Consistent
- No requirement contradicts another. Internal logical harmony must exist across all sections.
- *Example of inconsistency*: Section 3.2 says "Maximum 100 concurrent users" while Section 4.5 says "Support 500 concurrent users."

### 2.1.5 Ranked (Prioritized)
- Requirements carry a **priority tag** (e.g., High / Medium / Low or MoSCoW: Must / Should / Could / Won't have).
- *Utility*: Enables incremental delivery and core-version-first release strategy.

### 2.1.6 Verifiable
- Every requirement must be testable. Use measurable terms: **time, count, percentage, accuracy**.
- *Anti-pattern*: "The system shall be fast" (not verifiable).
- *Correct form*: "Search response time ≤ **2.0 s** at **95th percentile** under load of **100 concurrent users**."

### 2.1.7 Modifiable
- Structure must allow changes without losing consistency. Achieved through:
  - Unique requirement IDs (e.g., **REQ-FN-014**)
  - No redundancy
  - Cross-references instead of duplicated text

### 2.1.8 Traceable
- Every requirement has a **forward trace** (to design, code, test cases) and a **backward trace** (to its source — stakeholder, regulation, use case).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — SRS Document Structure (IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mandatory for KTU 14-Mark Questions**
> Memorize the IEEE 830 outline. The 2024 scheme typically asks students to "write the SRS for XYZ system" or "explain the structure of an SRS with a suitable example."

| Section # | Section Title | Purpose | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Introduction** | Scope, definitions, references, overview | Moderate |
| 1.1 | Purpose | Why this document exists | Low |
| 1.2 | Scope | What is in / out of system boundary | High |
| 1.3 | Definitions & Acronyms | Glossary for shared vocabulary | Moderate |
| 1.4 | References | Standards, related documents | Low |
| 1.5 | Overview | How the rest of the document is organized | Low |
| 2 | **Overall Description** | Product perspective, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions | High |
| 2.1 | Product Perspective | Context within larger system (interfaces with hardware, software, users) | High |
| 2.2 | Product Functions | High-level summary of major capabilities | High |
| 2.3 | User Characteristics | Who uses the system and at what skill level | Moderate |
| 2.4 | Constraints | Regulatory, hardware, language, standards | High |
| 2.5 | Assumptions & Dependencies | Assumed conditions for the system to work | Moderate |
| 3 | **Specific Requirements** | The bulk of the document — functional, performance, design constraints | Very High |
| 3.1 | External Interface Requirements | User, hardware, software, communication interfaces | High |
| 3.2 | Functional Requirements | Detailed behaviour for each function (FR-xxx) | Very High |
| 3.3 | Performance Requirements | Speed, capacity, accuracy, throughput | High |
| 3.4 | Design Constraints | Standards, implementation languages, tools | Moderate |
| 3.5 | Software System Attributes | Reliability, security, maintainability, portability | High |
| 3.6 | Other Requirements | Database, legal, reuse, installation | Moderate |
| 4 | **Appendices** | Supporting data, mock-ups, sample I/O formats | Low |
| 5 | **Index** | Alphabetical lookup | Low |

## 2.3 The Quality Equation (For Higher-Order KTU Answers)

Although not a *physical* formula, the relationship among SRS quality dimensions is often framed in requirements-engineering literature as:

$$
Q_{SRS} = f(C, U, V, M, T, R, K, P)
$$

Where:
- $Q_{SRS}$ = Overall SRS Quality Score
- $C$ = Completeness index
- $U$ = Unambiguity index
- $V$ = Verifiability index
- $M$ = Modifiability index
- $T$ = Traceability index
- $R$ = Ranking/Prioritization coverage
- $K$ = Consistency (zero contradictions)
- $P$ = Stakeholder correctness alignment

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility**
> This abstract model is used by organizations to define **SRS Review Checklists** and **automated requirements-quality tools** (e.g., IBM DOORS, Jama Connect). It is **not** for numerical substitution in exams — it is a conceptual expression to demonstrate understanding of the multi-dimensional nature of SRS quality.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Industry Domain | Why SRS Matters |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare (Medical Devices)** | Regulatory compliance with FDA / IEC 62304 demands traceable, verifiable requirements |
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | Every line of code must trace back to a system-level requirement |
| **Banking (PCI-DSS)** | Security and audit requirements must be captured upfront |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Functional safety requirements formalized in SRS |
| **E-Commerce / EdTech** | Faster time-to-market via prioritized incremental delivery |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step SRS Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Characteristics vs. Violation Symptoms

This matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** ("Explain the characteristics of a good SRS with examples of violations").

| # | Characteristic | Definition (Formal) | Violation Symptom | Corrective Technique |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Correct** | Reflects stakeholder's true need | User says "this is not what I asked for" | Source-tracing, stakeholder review |
| 2 | **Unambiguous** | Single interpretation possible | Two testers design two different test cases | Use **shall**, avoid subjective adjectives, glossary |
| 3 | **Complete** | All inputs, outputs, behaviours covered | Edge-case defect in production | Use **decision tables**, state-transition models |
| 4 | **Consistent** | No internal contradictions | Section A says X, Section B says not-X | **Cross-reference check** during formal review |
| 5 | **Ranked** | Each requirement has priority | Release 1 has wrong features | Apply **MoSCoW** or **Kano model** |
| 6 | **Verifiable** | Testable with measurable criteria | "System shall be fast" cannot be tested | Replace with **metrics** (time, count, %) |
| 7 | **Modifiable** | Easy to update without breaking | Changing one line breaks three others | Use **unique IDs**, avoid redundancy |
| 8 | **Traceable** | Bidirectional link exists | Cannot find which test covers requirement | Maintain **RTM** (Requirements Traceability Matrix) |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — From Use Case to SRS Requirement

This derivation walks you through how a textual **Use Case** is **systematically transformed** into a formal **SRS requirement statement**.

### Step 1 — Capture the Use Case Narrative

> *A library student logs into the system using their University ID and password, searches for a book titled "Operating Systems," and reserves it for pickup.*

### Step 2 — Identify Actors, Inputs, Outputs, Pre/Post-Conditions

$$
\text{UseCase} = (\text{Actor},\ \text{Pre},\ \text{Input},\ \text{Output},\ \text{Post})
$$

$$
\text{SearchAndReserve} = (\text{Student},\ \text{LoggedIn},\ \text{ISBN/Title},\ \text{ReservationID},\ \text{BookReserved})
$$

### Step 3 — Decompose into Functional Requirements

Each verb-driven action becomes a discrete requirement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FR}_1 &: \text{The system shall authenticate the student using University ID and password.} \\
\text{FR}_2 &: \text{The system shall accept a book search by Title, Author, or ISBN.} \\
\text{FR}_3 &: \text{The system shall display matching books in a paginated list.} \\
\text{FR}_4 &: \text{The system shall allow the student to reserve a book if available.} \\
\text{FR}_5 &: \text{The system shall generate a unique Reservation ID for each reservation.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Add Non-Functional / Quality Requirements

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NFR}_1 &: \text{The system shall return search results within } 2 \text{ seconds for } 50{,}000 \text{ records.} \\
\text{NFR}_2 &: \text{The system shall support } 200 \text{ concurrent users with } \leq 1\% \text{ error rate.} \\
\text{NFR}_3 &: \text{The system shall encrypt all passwords using SHA-256 or stronger.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Add Constraints, Interfaces, and Assumptions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CON}_1 &: \text{The system shall run on a web browser supporting HTML5 and JavaScript ES6+.} \\
\text{CON}_2 &: \text{The system shall use the central University SSO for authentication.} \\
\text{ASM}_1 &: \text{It is assumed that each student has a valid University-issued ID.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Traceability IDs

$$
\text{REQ-FN-001} \rightarrow \text{UseCase: AuthenticateStudent} \rightarrow \text{Design: LoginModule} \rightarrow \text{Code: Login.java} \rightarrow \text{Test: TC-001}
$$

This chain is the **forward** trace. The **backward** trace runs the same chain in reverse.

## 3.3 Detailed Case Study — SRS for College Library Management Software (CLMS)

Below is a **model SRS excerpt** suitable for a KTU 14-mark answer. Each subsection is annotated with the marks-relevant emphasis.

### 3.3.1 Section 1 — Introduction

**1.1 Purpose**
> This SRS describes the functional and non-functional requirements of the **College Library Management Software (CLMS)**. It is intended for use by the development team, the QA team, the Library Committee, and the University IT Cell.

**1.2 Scope**
> The CLMS will be a web-based application that automates book acquisition, cataloguing, circulation (issue/return), reservation, fine calculation, and report generation. The system will serve **approximately 3,000 students and 50 staff members** of the college.

> *In-Scope* — Book search, issue/return, reservations, fine calculation, user management, report generation.
> *Out-of-Scope* — Procurement of physical books, inter-library loan with external universities.

**1.3 Definitions & Acronyms**

| Acronym | Definition |
|---|---|
| CLMS | College Library Management Software |
| ISBN | International Standard Book Number |
| SSO | Single Sign-On |
| NFR | Non-Functional Requirement |
| SLA | Service Level Agreement |
| GUI | Graphical User Interface |

**1.4 References**
- IEEE Std 830-1998 — Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications
- University IT Security Policy v3.2

### 3.3.2 Section 2 — Overall Description

**2.1 Product Perspective**
> CLMS is a standalone web application. It interfaces with:
> - The **University SSO** for authentication.
> - The **existing MySQL database** of the Library.
> - A **barcode scanner** for ISBN scanning at the circulation desk.

**2.2 User Characteristics**

| User Class | Skill Level | Frequency of Use |
|---|---|---|
| Student | Basic computer literacy | 2–5 times/week |
| Librarian | Proficient | Daily, full working hours |
| Library Admin | Advanced | Weekly configuration |

**2.3 Constraints**
- Must be developed using **Java + Spring Boot** (per University IT mandate).
- Must be deployable on the existing **on-premise Linux server**.
- Must comply with **University Data Privacy Policy 2024**.

**2.4 Assumptions & Dependencies**
- Stable internet connectivity in the Library.
- All books carry valid barcodes.

### 3.3.3 Section 3 — Specific Requirements

**3.1 External Interface Requirements**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{INT-UI-001} &: \text{Web-based GUI accessible via Chrome 100+, Firefox 95+, Edge 100+.} \\
\text{INT-API-001} &: \text{RESTful API endpoint POST /api/v1/reserve returning JSON.} \\
\text{INT-HW-001} &: \text{USB-connected barcode scanner emulating keyboard input.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3.2 Functional Requirements**

| ID | Requirement | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| REQ-FN-001 | The system shall allow students to log in via University SSO. | High |
| REQ-FN-002 | The system shall allow librarians to add a new book record (Title, Author, ISBN, Category, Copies). | High |
| REQ-FN-003 | The system shall allow students to search books by Title, Author, ISBN, or Category. | High |
| REQ-FN-004 | The system shall allow a librarian to issue a book to a student for a default period of **14 days**. | High |
| REQ-FN-005 | The system shall compute a fine of **₹5 per day** for overdue returns. | High |
| REQ-FN-006 | The system shall allow a student to reserve a book that is currently issued. | Medium |
| REQ-FN-007 | The system shall send an email reminder **2 days before** the due date. | Medium |
