| REQ-FN-008 | The system shall generate a daily report of issued / returned / overdue books. | High |
| REQ-FN-009 | The system shall prevent a student with **more than ₹100 in unpaid fines** from issuing a new book. | High |
| REQ-FN-010 | The system shall allow the Library Admin to add, edit, or remove user accounts. | High |

**3.3 Performance Requirements**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PERF-001} &: \text{Book search shall return results within } 2 \text{ seconds for a catalog of } 100{,}000 \text{ books.} \\
\text{PERF-002} &: \text{The system shall support } 200 \text{ concurrent users with } \leq 1\% \text{ error rate.} \\
\text{PERF-003} &: \text{Issue/Return transactions shall complete within } 1 \text{ second.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3.4 Software System Attributes**

| Attribute | Target Metric |
|---|---|
| Availability | 99.5% (excluding planned maintenance) |
| Security | SHA-256 password hashing; HTTPS only; OWASP Top-10 compliance |
| Maintainability | Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) ≤ 4 hours |
| Portability | Runs on Linux, Windows Server, Docker container |
| Usability | New librarian shall complete issue/return task within 10 minutes of training |
| Scalability | Horizontally scalable to 10,000 users |

**3.5 Design Constraints**
- Backend: **Java 17, Spring Boot 3.x**
- Frontend: **React 18**
- Database: **MySQL 8.0**
- Build tool: **Maven 3.9+**

**3.6 Other Requirements**
- The system shall retain transaction logs for **5 years** for audit purposes.
- The system shall support **English and Malayalam** user interfaces.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Verification Logic for a Requirement

For **REQ-FN-005** ("Fine ₹5 per day"):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Test Condition} &: \text{Student returns book } d \text{ days after due date.} \\
\text{Expected Fine} &: F = 5 \cdot d \quad \text{where } d \in \mathbb{Z}^{+},\, d \geq 1 \\
\text{Boundary Test 1} &: d = 0 \Rightarrow F = 0 \text{ (no fine)} \\
\text{Boundary Test 2} &: d = 1 \Rightarrow F = 5 \text{ (₹5)} \\
\text{Boundary Test 3} &: d = 30 \Rightarrow F = 150 \text{ (₹150)} \\
\text{Edge Test} &: \text{If } F > 100, \text{ block issue (per REQ-FN-009).}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the requirement is **Verifiable** ✓ and **Testable** ✓.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Traceability Example

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REQ-FN-004} &\xrightarrow{\text{forward}} \text{Design: IssueBookService.issue()} \\
                &\xrightarrow{\text{forward}} \text{Code: IssueBookService.java\#issue()} \\
                &\xrightarrow{\text{forward}} \text{Unit Test: IssueBookServiceTest.testIssue_DefaultPeriod()} \\
                &\xrightarrow{\text{forward}} \text{Integration Test: TC-INT-014} \\
\text{REQ-FN-004} &\xleftarrow{\text{backward}} \text{Stakeholder: Head Librarian, Interview 12-Mar-2024}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Tip**
> A full 14-mark answer should include **at least one traceability chain** to demonstrate understanding of the "Traceable" characteristic. Examiners explicitly award marks for this in the valuation key.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 830 SRS Document Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following **block-level functional architecture** maps the hierarchical structure of a standard SRS, showing how Section 1 (Introduction) leads to Section 3 (Specific Requirements), which is the core of the document.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef rootNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef l1Node fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef l2Node fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    classDef leafNode fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#bdd7ee,color:#000000

    srsRoot["SRS DOCUMENT IEEE 830"]:::rootNode
    s1["SECTION 1 INTRODUCTION"]:::l1Node
    s2["SECTION 2 OVERALL DESCRIPTION"]:::l1Node
    s3["SECTION 3 SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS"]:::l1Node
    s4["SECTION 4 APPENDICES"]:::l1Node
    s5["SECTION 5 INDEX"]:::l1Node

    s1a["1.1 Purpose"]:::l2Node
    s1b["1.2 Scope"]:::l2Node
    s1c["1.3 Definitions Acronyms"]:::l2Node
    s1d["1.4 References"]:::l2Node
    s1e["1.5 Overview"]:::l2Node

    s2a["2.1 Product Perspective"]:::l2Node
    s2b["2.2 Product Functions"]:::l2Node
    s2c["2.3 User Characteristics"]:::l2Node
    s2d["2.4 Constraints"]:::l2Node
    s2e["2.5 Assumptions Dependencies"]:::l2Node

    s3a["3.1 External Interface Requirements"]:::l2Node
    s3b["3.2 Functional Requirements"]:::l2Node
```

## 4.2 SRS Development Process Flow

This diagram captures the **lifecycle** of producing an SRS — from problem statement to approved baseline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef procNode fill:#548235,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    classDef artNode fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3d1a5e,color:#ffffff

    p1["Problem Statement"]:::artNode
    p2["Stakeholder Identification"]:::procNode
    p3["Requirements Elicitation"]:::procNode
    p4["Requirements Negotiation"]:::procNode
    p5["Requirements Specification"]:::procNode
    p6["Requirements Validation"]:::procNode
    p7["Approved SRS Baseline"]:::artNode

    d2{"Conflicts resolved with stakeholders"}:::decNode
    p8["Iterative Refinement"]:::procNode

    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> d2
    d2 -- No --> p8
    p8 --> p3
    d2 -- Yes --> p5 --> p6 --> d1
    d1 -- No --> p8
    d1 -- Yes --> p7
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List any SIX characteristics of a good Software Requirements Specification (SRS). Briefly explain the importance of "Unambiguous" and "Verifiable" characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The eight characteristics of a good SRS as per IEEE 830 are: *Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, and Traceable.* (1 mark for listing 6)

**Unambiguous**: Every requirement must have only one possible interpretation. Two independent readers should arrive at the same meaning. (1 mark)

> *Example*: "The system shall respond fast" is **ambiguous**. "The system shall respond within **2 seconds**" is **unambiguous**.

**Verifiable**: Every requirement must be testable using measurable criteria — time, count, percentage, or accuracy. (1 mark)

> *Example*: "The system shall be reliable" is **not verifiable**. "The system shall achieve **99.5% uptime** over a calendar month" is **verifiable**.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements with two examples each, in the context of a College Library Management System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Specifies **what** the system does | Specifies **how well** the system does it |
| Focus | Behaviour / features | Quality attributes / constraints |
| Test type | Functional test | Performance / load / security test |
| Example 1 (CLMS) | "The system shall allow a librarian to issue a book to a student." (1 mark) | "The issue operation shall complete within **1 second** under **200 concurrent users**." (1 mark) |
| Example 2 (CLMS) | "The system shall calculate a fine of ₹5/day for overdue books." (1 mark) | "All user passwords shall be hashed using **SHA-256**." |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note**: As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, you must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Both questions carry equal marks and have sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the structure of a Software Requirements Specification (SRS) as per the IEEE 830 standard. List all major sections with a one-line description of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a detailed SRS for a **College Library Management System (CLMS)**. Your answer must include:
> - At least **5 functional requirements** with unique IDs and priorities.
> - At least **3 non-functional (performance / quality) requirements** with measurable metrics.
> - **Constraints and assumptions**.
> - One **traceability chain** demonstrating how a functional requirement links to design, code, and test case. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — SRS Structure as per IEEE 830 (7 marks)

| Section | Title | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1.1 | **Purpose** | Defines why the SRS is written and its intended audience. (0.5 mark) |
| 1.2 | **Scope** | Describes the product boundary — what is included and excluded. (0.5 mark) |
| 1.3 | **Definitions & Acronyms** | Glossary to ensure shared understanding. (0.5 mark) |
| 1.4 | **References** | List of all referenced standards, policies, documents. (0.5 mark) |
| 1.5 | **Overview** | Roadmap of the rest of the SRS. (0.5 mark) |
| 2.1 | **Product Perspective** | Context — is it standalone or part of a larger system? (0.5 mark) |
| 2.2 | **Product Functions** | High-level summary of major capabilities. (0.5 mark) |
| 2.3 | **User Characteristics** | Who uses the system and their skill level. (0.5 mark) |
| 2.4 | **Constraints** | Regulatory, hardware, language, and policy limits. (0.5 mark) |
| 2.5 | **Assumptions & Dependencies** | Conditions assumed true for the system to work. (0.5 mark) |
| 3.1 | **External Interface Requirements** | UI, API, hardware, and communication interfaces. (0.5 mark) |
| 3.2 | **Functional Requirements** | Detailed behaviour — the heart of the SRS. (1 mark) |
| 3.3–3.6 | **Performance, Design Constraints, Software Attributes, Other** | Quality and constraint specifications. (0.5 mark) |
| 4–5 | **Appendices & Index** | Supporting data and lookup aids. (0.5 mark) |

> **Total: 7 marks** — Examiner's note: awarding partial marks for 4–5 sections and full marks for 9+ correctly described sections.

#### Part (b) — Detailed SRS for CLMS (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Marking Key Distribution**
> - 5 functional requirements with IDs + priorities = **2.5 marks**
> - 3 NFRs with metrics = **1.5 marks**
> - Constraints + assumptions = **1 mark**
> - One traceability chain = **1 mark**
> - Format and structure = **1 mark**

**Functional Requirements**

| ID | Requirement | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| REQ-FN-001 | The system shall authenticate students via the University SSO using their University ID and password. | High |
| REQ-FN-002 | The system shall allow librarians to add a new book with fields: Title, Author, ISBN, Category, and Number of Copies. | High |
| REQ-FN-003 | The system shall allow students to search for books by Title, Author, ISBN, or Category. | High |
| REQ-FN-004 | The system shall allow a librarian to issue a book to a student for a default period of 14 days. | High |
| REQ-FN-005 | The system shall calculate a fine of ₹5 per day for overdue returns. | High |
| REQ-FN-006 | The system shall allow a student to reserve a book currently issued by another user. | Medium |
| REQ-FN-007 | The system shall prevent a student with unpaid fines exceeding ₹100 from issuing a new book. | High |
| REQ-FN-008 | The system shall generate a daily transaction report (issued, returned, overdue). | High |

**Non-Functional Requirements**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NFR-PERF-001} &: \text{Book search shall return results within } 2 \text{ seconds for a catalog of } 100{,}000 \text{ records.} \\
\text{NFR-PERF-002} &: \text{The system shall support } 200 \text{ concurrent users with } \leq 1\% \text{ error rate.} \\
\text{NFR-SEC-001} &: \text{All passwords shall be hashed using SHA-256; all traffic shall use HTTPS.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constraints and Assumptions**

- **CON-001**: The system shall be developed using Java + Spring Boot as mandated by the University IT Cell.
- **CON-002**: The system shall be deployable on the existing on-premise Linux server.
- **ASM-001**: Stable internet connectivity is assumed in the Library.
- **ASM-002**: All books carry valid barcodes readable by the USB scanner.

**Traceability Chain for REQ-FN-004**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stakeholder Source} &: \text{Head Librarian, Interview 12-Mar-2024} \\
\text{Requirement} &: \text{REQ-FN-004 — Issue book for 14-day default period} \\
\text{Design} &: \text{IssueBookService.issue(bookId, studentId, days=14)} \\
\text{Code} &: \text{IssueBookService.java, lines 42–67} \\
\text{Unit Test} &: \text{IssueBookServiceTest.testIssue_DefaultPeriod} \\
\text{Integration Test} &: \text{TC-INT-014 — End-to-end issue flow}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the **eight characteristics of a good SRS** with one real-world example of a violation and one example of a corrected version for **each characteristic**, in the context of the College Library Management Software. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Develop a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** for the following five requirements of a College Library Management System. For each requirement, identify: stakeholder source, related use case, design component, and at least one test case. **(7 marks)**
> 1. REQ-FN-001 — Student authentication via University SSO
> 2. REQ-FN-003 — Search by Title, Author, ISBN
> 3. REQ-FN-004 — Issue book for 14 days
> 4. REQ-FN-005 — Fine ₹5/day
> 5. NFR-PERF-001 — Search response ≤ 2 seconds

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 8 Characteristics with CLMS Examples (7 marks)

> *Each characteristic carries ~0.875 marks — allocate 0.4 for definition, 0.2 for violation, 0.2 for corrected example.*

**1. Correct**
- *Definition*: The requirement truly reflects the stakeholder's need.
- *Violation*: "The system shall send a daily report to the librarian" (librarian actually wanted weekly).
- *Corrected*: "The system shall generate a weekly circulation report every Monday at 9:00 AM and email it to the librarian."

**2. Unambiguous**
- *Definition*: Only one possible interpretation.
- *Violation*: "The system shall reserve books for students quickly."
- *Corrected*: "The system shall confirm a reservation within **1 second** of submission."

**3. Complete**
- *Definition*: All cases — including invalid inputs — are covered.
- *Violation*: Missing behaviour for an invalid ISBN entry.
- *Corrected*: "If the entered ISBN does not match a 10-digit or 13-digit pattern, the system shall display error 'Invalid ISBN' and shall not proceed with the search."

**4. Consistent**
- *Definition*: No contradictions.
- *Violation*: REQ-FN-004 says "14-day issue period"; REQ-FN-009 says "Maximum issue period is 7 days."
- *Corrected*: All sections aligned to 14-day default, 21-day maximum (faculty).

**5. Ranked**
- *Definition*: Each requirement has a priority.
- *Violation*: All requirements listed without priority.
- *Corrected*: REQ-FN-001 = **High**, REQ-FN-006 (Reservation) = **Medium**, REQ-FN-010 (Multi-language) = **Low**.

**6. Verifiable**
- *Definition*: Testable with measurable metrics.
- *Violation*: "The system shall have a good user interface."
- *Corrected*: "A new librarian shall complete the issue/return task within 10 minutes of training (verified via timed test with 5 trainees)."

**7. Modifiable**
- *Definition*: Easy to change without loss of consistency.
- *Violation*: "The fine is ₹5 per day" appears in 8 different places with no ID.
- *Corrected*: Single REQ-FN-005 with unique ID; all other sections reference this ID.

**8. Traceable**
- *Definition*: Bidirectional link to source and to design/test.
- *Violation*: A requirement exists but no test case references it.
- *Corrected*: REQ-FN-005 has source "Librarian Interview 12-Mar-2024" and is covered by TC-021 "Fine Calculation" with Pass/Fail status.

---

#### Part (b) — Requirements Traceability Matrix (7 marks)

| Req ID | Stakeholder Source | Related Use Case | Design Component | Test Case(s) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **REQ-FN-001** | University IT Cell + Student Feedback Form | UC-01: Student Login | `SSOAuthService.authenticate()` | TC-001 (valid creds), TC-002 (invalid creds), TC-003 (expired session) | Approved |
| **REQ-FN-003** | Student Focus Group (Feb 2024) | UC-02: Search Book | `SearchService.search(criteria)` | TC-010 (title search), TC-011 (ISBN search), TC-012 (no results) | Approved |
| **REQ-FN-004** | Head Librarian Interview (12-Mar-2024) | UC-03: Issue Book | `IssueBookService.issue()` | TC-014 (default 14-day), TC-015 (already issued error) | Approved |
| **REQ-FN-005** | Library Committee Policy v2.1 | UC-04: Return + Fine | `FineCalculator.compute(daysOverdue)` | TC-021 (1 day overdue = ₹5), TC-022 (30 days = ₹150) | Approved |
| **NFR-PERF-001** | University SLA | UC-02: Search Book (NFR layer) | `SearchIndex` (Elasticsearch) | TC-PERF-001 (JMeter 200 users, 100K records) | Approved |

> **Marking key (7 marks):** 1 mark per correctly completed requirement row + 2 marks for completeness (use case linkage and test case specificity).

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing SRS with SDD (Software Design Description)**: Students lose 2–3 marks by writing class diagrams, database tables, or algorithm pseudocode in the SRS. *An SRS specifies WHAT, not HOW.*
> 2. **Subjective adjectives**: Phrases like "user-friendly", "fast", "efficient" are **not verifiable** and will be marked down. Always use **numbers**.
> 3. **Missing priority / unique IDs**: Every functional requirement MUST have a unique ID (e.g., REQ-FN-001) and a priority tag.
> 4. **Skipping Section 2 (Overall Description)**: Many students jump straight to Section 3. Section 2 carries **separate marks**.
> 5. **No traceability evidence**: A 14-mark SRS answer without a trace chain or RTM is treated as incomplete.
> 6. **Treating "user shall" as the same as "system shall"**: The KTU valuation key explicitly requires the *actor* to be the **system**, not the user, because the SRS is a **system specification**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist — SRS Document**

- ✅ SRS = **Software Requirements Specification** — a formal, structured document per **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148**.
- ✅ Captures **Functional + Non-Functional + Interface** requirements.
- ✅ Is a **WHAT** document, not a **HOW** (design) document.
- ✅ Eight characteristics: **Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable** — memorize in order.
- ✅ Five-section IEEE 830 structure: **Introduction → Overall Description → Specific Requirements → Appendices → Index**.
- ✅ Every requirement needs a **unique ID** (e.g., `REQ-FN-001`) and a **priority** (High / Medium / Low or MoSCoW).
- ✅ Use **"The system shall..."** wording — never subjective adjectives.
- ✅ NFRs must be **measurable**: time (seconds), count (users), percentage (%), or accuracy.
- ✅ **RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix)** is the evidence of Traceability.
- ✅ Case study (CLMS) typically expects 5–10 functional + 3–5 non-functional requirements.
- ✅ Common KTU errors: writing design instead of requirements, missing IDs, no metrics, no trace evidence.
- ✅ **Validation ≠ Verification**: *Validation* = "Did we build the right system?"; *Verification* = "Did we build the system right?" — both apply to SRS review.
- ✅ **Stakeholders** in SRS context: customers, users, developers, testers, project managers, maintenance engineers, regulators.
- ✅ **MoSCoW prioritization**: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have (this time).
- ✅ **Forward trace**: Requirement → Design → Code → Test. **Backward trace**: Test → Code → Design → Requirement → Source.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — SRS Document Characteristics and Structure; Case Study: SRS for College Library Management Software**
**Course:** PBCGT504 — Software Engineering & Design Concepts
**Scheme:** KTU 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned)

topics.title: Software Architecture and Its Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Architecture and Its Importance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Architecture (IEEE 1471 / ISO 42010 Standard Definition):**
> *"The fundamental organization of a system, embodied in its components, their relationships to each other and to the environment, and the principles governing its design and evolution."*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Software Architecture** is defined as the **high-level blueprint** of a software system that defines:
- The **structural elements** (components/modules) of the system
- The **external visible properties** of these elements
- The **relationships** and **interactions** between elements
- The **design principles** guiding composition

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favorite):**
> - **Software Architecture** = What the system *fundamentally* is (the *macro* view)
> - **Software Design** = How the system's internals are *implemented* (the *micro* view)
> - Architecture is **non-functional, abstract, and strategic**; Design is **functional, concrete, and tactical**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **🏗️ Real-World Analogy: Building Architecture vs. Software Architecture**

Think of constructing a building. Before a single brick is laid, an **architect** creates blueprints that define:
- How many floors will the building have?
- Where will the elevators, stairs, plumbing, and electrical wiring run?
- How will the foundation support the structure?
- Will the design allow future expansion?

Now, replace the building with a **software system**:

| Building Concept | Software Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Building Blueprint | Software Architecture Document |
| Foundation & Pillars | Core Framework / Middleware |
| Floors | Subsystems / Modules |
| Rooms | Components / Classes |
| Wiring & Plumbing | Communication Protocols / APIs |
| Building Code Compliance | Quality Attributes (Performance, Security) |

Just as you **cannot** construct a 50-story building without an architect, you **cannot** build a robust, scalable enterprise software system without a well-defined software architecture.

---

### 1.3 Why Software Architecture is NOT Optional

According to KTU Module 2, software architecture is the **earliest design decision** that has the **most far-reaching consequences**. It is established during the **early stages of the software development life cycle** and forms the backbone for all subsequent activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle (Rumbaugh / Jacobson / Booch — OOAD Pillars):**
> *"The architecture of a system is a metaphor for understanding it — it captures the system's essence, allowing engineers to reason about the system's behavior, performance, and evolution before a single line of code is written."*

---

### 1.4 Standard KTU 2024 Architectural Vocabulary

The following **bolded terms** are **high-yield exam terms** frequently appearing in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions:

- **Component**: A modular building block (e.g., a class, package, microservice) that encapsulates a coherent set of functionality.
- **Connector**: A mechanism that mediates communication, coordination, or cooperation between components (e.g., procedure call, message queue, REST API).
- **Configuration**: The topological arrangement of components and connectors at runtime.
- **Architectural Style / Pattern**: A named, reusable collection of architectural decisions (e.g., Layered, Client-Server, Microservices).
- **Stakeholder**: Any individual, role, or organization with an interest in the system's architecture.
- **Quality Attribute**: A non-functional property used to evaluate the system's design (e.g., performance, security, modifiability, availability).
- **Architectural View**: A representation of the architecture from a particular perspective (e.g., logical, process, deployment, data).
- **Architectural Description (AD)**: A comprehensive document conforming to **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010** that captures the architecture.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FRAMEWORK SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Software Architecture

Software architecture is fundamentally composed of three interrelated pillars. KTU examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions around these pillars:

#### Pillar 1: Structure (Components + Connectors + Configuration)
- Defines **what** the system consists of
- Identifies the **static** organization of code

#### Pillar 2: Architecture-Significant Requirements (ASRs)
- Quality attributes that **shape** the architecture
- Example: "System must handle **10,000 concurrent users** with response time **< 200 ms**"

#### Pillar 3: Rationale and Design Decisions
- **Why** a particular architectural approach was chosen
- Records trade-offs and alternatives considered

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common Part A (3-mark) question is *"List the elements of software architecture."* The correct answer is: **(1) Components, (2) Connectors, (3) Configuration, (4) Constraints, (5) Rationale**.

---

### 2.2 The Critical Importance of Software Architecture

Software architecture is considered the **single most important artifact** in software engineering. The following is an exhaustive analysis of *why* it matters:

#### 2.2.1 **Facilitates Communication Among Stakeholders**
The architecture serves as a **common language** between developers, project managers, clients, and maintenance teams. It abstracts away implementation details so that **non-technical stakeholders** (e.g., business owners) can validate requirements.

#### 2.2.2 **Enforces Early Design Decisions**
A 2018 study (cited in standard software engineering literature) shows that **architectural decisions made in the first 20% of the project lifecycle determine approximately 80% of the system's cost and quality** impact. Fixing an architectural defect post-deployment can cost **up to 100× more** than fixing it at the design stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Escalation Principle:**
> The cost of fixing a defect grows **exponentially** as it propagates through the lifecycle:
> Requirements → Design → Coding → Testing → Production

#### 2.2.3 **Drives Quality Attributes (Non-Functional Requirements)**
The architecture determines whether a system will be:
- **Performant** (responds quickly)
- **Scalable** (handles growth)
- **Reliable** (functions under failure)
- **Secure** (resists attacks)
- **Maintainable** (easy to modify)
- **Portable** (can move across platforms)

#### 2.2.4 **Enables Reuse and Standardization**
Architectural patterns (e.g., **MVC, Microservices, Event-Driven**) are **reusable across projects**. This reduces training time, accelerates development, and standardizes technical debt.

#### 2.2.5 **Supports Reasoning About the System**
Before code is written, the architecture allows the team to perform:
- **Performance analysis** (using queuing theory)
- **Security analysis** (threat modeling)
- **Reliability analysis** (failure modes)
- **Cost analysis** (technology licensing)

#### 2.2.6 **Defines Constraints and Influences Team Organization (Conway's Law)**
> *"Organizations which design systems are constrained to produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations."* — **Melvin Conway, 1967**

The architecture must **align with team topology** (e.g., 3 backend teams → 3 main services).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Framework / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all the **high-priority theoretical content** for KTU 2024 exams on this topic.

| **Concept** | **Definition** | **KTU 2024 Exam Frequency** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Component** | A modular unit with well-defined interface | ★★★★★ | `UserService`, `PaymentModule` |
| **Connector** | Interaction mechanism between components | ★★★★★ | REST API, gRPC, Message Queue |
| **Configuration** | Topology of components and connectors | ★★★★ | Deployment diagram |
| **Stakeholder** | Anyone with interest in the system | ★★★★ | End-user, Architect, Developer, Operations |
| **Quality Attribute** | Non-functional property of the system | ★★★★★ | Performance, Security, Modifiability |
| **Architectural View** | A representation from a specific perspective | ★★★★ | Logical view, Process view, Physical view (4+1 model) |
| **Architectural Style** | A named, reusable pattern | ★★★★★ | Layered, Client-Server, Microservices, Event-Driven |
| **Architectural Pattern** | A solution to a recurring problem in a context | ★★★★★ | MVC, Repository, Pipe-Filter |
| **ADL** | Architecture Description Language | ★★★ | Acme, Wright, Darwin |
| **ISO 42010** | International standard for architecture description | ★★★ | Provides standardized vocabulary |
| **ATAM** | Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method | ★★★ | SEI method for evaluating architectures |
| **SAAM** | Software Architecture Analysis Method | ★★ | SEI's precursor to ATAM |

---

### 2.4 Architectural Views: The 4+1 View Model (Philippe Kruchten, 1995)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The 4+1 view model is a **mandatory topic** for KTU 2024 and frequently appears in 14-mark questions.

| **View** | **Audience** | **Focus** | **Notation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Logical View** | End-users, Analysts | Functional requirements (what the system does) | Class diagrams, Object diagrams |
| **Process View** | System integrators, Performance engineers | Non-functional (concurrency, performance) | Activity diagrams, Sequence diagrams |
| **Physical View** | System engineers, Operators | Hardware topology, deployment | Deployment diagrams |
| **Development View** | Programmers, Project managers | Software modules, package organization | Package diagrams, Component diagrams |
| **(+1) Scenarios / Use-Case View** | All stakeholders | Validates other views through use-cases | Use-case diagrams |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering practice (industry, KTU internships, and final-year projects), software architecture:
- Reduces the **time-to-market** by 30–50% (per industry data)
- Cuts **maintenance costs** by up to 40%
- Reduces **defect density** by enabling early validation
- Enables **DevOps and CI/CD** through clear service boundaries

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK & DERIVATIONS

Since this topic is **theoretical-conceptual** rather than equation-heavy, Section 3 is adapted to provide an **exhaustive step-by-step analytical framework** for the *Architectural Design Process* and the *ATAM Evaluation Method* — both of which are **high-yield KTU 2024 topics**.

---

### 3.1 The Architectural Design Process (Step-by-Step Derivation)

This is a **systematic methodology** for arriving at a software architecture. The KTU examiner may ask you to *"Explain the steps involved in deriving a software architecture"* (14 marks).

#### **Step 1: Identify and Prioritize Quality Attributes (ASRs)**
$$\text{ASR} = \{\text{Quality Attributes}\} \cap \{\text{Business Goals}\}$$

**Logic:** The first step is to identify the *architecture-significant requirements*. These are the non-functional requirements (e.g., *"the system must support 50,000 concurrent users with 99.99% availability"*) that **shape** the architecture.

**Example:** A banking system must prioritize **security and reliability**; a streaming platform must prioritize **performance and scalability**.

---

#### **Step 2: Select Architectural Patterns / Styles**
Given the prioritized ASRs, the architect selects one or more architectural styles. The selection is driven by the **quality attribute workshop (QAW)** output.

**Common Pattern-Selection Heuristics:**
- High modifiability → **Microservices, Plugin architecture**
- High performance → **Event-driven, Caching, Asynchronous**
- High security → **Layered with strict boundary controls**
- High availability → **Redundancy, Load balancing, Replication**

---

#### **Step 3: Define Components and Their Responsibilities**
Each component is assigned a **single, cohesive responsibility** (Single Responsibility Principle). Components must have:
- **Well-defined interfaces** (publicly exposed)
- **Encapsulated internals** (hidden from other components)
- **Low coupling** (minimal inter-dependencies)
- **High cohesion** (related functionality grouped)

Mathematically, **coupling** can be expressed as:
$$C(A,B) = \frac{\text{Number of inter-component connections}}{\text{Total possible connections}}$$

A **low** $C(A,B)$ value is desired.

---

#### **Step 4: Define Connectors (Interaction Mechanisms)**

For each pair of interacting components, a connector type is chosen:

| **Interaction Type** | **Suitable Connector** | **Coupling** |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous, request-response | REST API, gRPC, RMI | Tight |
| Asynchronous, event-driven | Message Queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ) | Loose |
| Data sharing | Database (Shared Schema) | Tight (if shared) |
| Pipe-based data flow | File pipe, stream | Loose |

---

#### **Step 5: Architectural Evaluation (ATAM)**
Once the candidate architecture is drafted, it must be **validated** against the quality attributes. This is done using SEI's **ATAM** (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method).

**ATAM Steps (Step-by-Step Derivation):**
1. **Present the architecture** (architect walks stakeholders through the design)
2. **Present the business drivers** (mission, goals, ASRs)
3. **Identify architectural approaches** (styles and patterns used)
4. **Generate quality attribute utility tree** (hierarchical tree of quality attributes → scenarios → priorities)
5. **Analyze architectural approaches** (for each scenario in the tree, evaluate the approach)
6. **Identify risks, non-risks, sensitivity points, and tradeoffs**
7. **Document results**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **ATAM** (tradeoff analysis) with **SAAM** (scenario analysis). ATAM is an *evolution* of SAAM that includes **tradeoff analysis** between quality attributes.

---

#### **Step 6: Refine the Architecture Based on Feedback**
The architecture is iteratively refined. Feedback loops occur between Steps 3–5 until all critical ASRs are satisfactorily addressed.

---

#### **Step 7: Document the Architecture (ISO 42010 Compliant)**
The final architecture is documented as an **Architectural Description (AD)** containing:
- **Stakeholders** and their concerns
- **Architectural views** (using 4+1 or similar)
- **Correspondences** between views
- **Architectural decisions** and rationale

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: Quality Attribute Utility Tree (ATAM Output)

The **utility tree** is a *hierarchical* representation of quality attributes, used in ATAM. Here's how to construct it:

**Level 1: Quality Attribute Category** (e.g., *Performance*)
- **Level 2: Refined Quality** (e.g., *Latency*)
  - **Level 3: Scenario** (e.g., *"When 1000 users submit a search query simultaneously, 95% of responses must arrive within 200 ms."*)
    - **Priority:** H / M / L
    - **Difficulty of Achievement:** H / M / L
    - **Risk Status:** Risk / Non-Risk / Sensitivity Point

**Worked Example (KTU Board Style):**

Let us derive the priority of an ASR using the simple utility-tree priority heuristic:

$$P_{ASR} = w_{\text{Importance}} \times I + w_{\text{Difficulty}} \times D$$

where:
- $I$ = Importance to stakeholders (1–5 scale)
- $D$ = Difficulty of achieving (1–5 scale)
- $w_{\text{Importance}}$, $w_{\text{Difficulty}}$ are weights (commonly $w_{\text{Importance}} = 0.6$, $w_{\text{Difficulty}} = 0.4$)

**Sample Calculation:**

Consider a payment processing system. An ASR states: *"Under peak load, payment confirmation must complete within 1 second."*

Suppose:
- Importance $I = 5$ (critical for business)
- Difficulty $D = 4$ (hard to achieve)

The priority is:
$$P_{ASR} = (0.6 \times 5) + (0.4 \times 4) = 3.0 + 1.6 = 4.6$$

A higher $P_{ASR}$ value indicates a **higher-priority ASR** that the architecture **must** address.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Architectural Styles

| **Style** | **Modifiability** | **Performance** | **Reliability** | **Scalability** | **Security** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Layered** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium | Enterprise CRUD apps |
| **Client-Server** | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Web applications |
| **Microservices** | High | High | High | High | Medium | Cloud-native apps |
| **Event-Driven** | High | High | High | High | Low | IoT, Real-time analytics |
| **Pipe-Filter** | High | Medium | High | Medium | Low | Data processing pipelines |
| **Microkernel** | High | Medium | Medium | Low | High | Plugin-based apps, OS kernels |
| **Service-Oriented (SOA)** | Medium | Medium | High | High | Medium | Enterprise integration |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Architectural Design Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Step 1: Identify Stakeholders] --> node2[Step 2: Capture Quality Attributes]
    node2 --> node3[Step 3: Prioritize ASRs via Utility Tree]
    node3 --> node4[Step 4: Select Architectural Pattern]
    node4 --> node5[Step 5: Define Components]
    node5 --> node6[Step 6: Define Connectors]
    node6 --> node7[Step 7: Construct Architectural Views]
    node7 --> node8[Step 8: Evaluate via ATAM]
    node8 --> node9{Architecture Satisfies ASRs?}
    node9 -->|Yes| node10[Step 9: Document AD per ISO 42010]
    node9 -->|No| node11[Refine: Return to Step 5]
    node11 --> node5
    node10 --> node12[Final Architectural Baseline]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The flow begins with stakeholder analysis, captures their quality concerns, and iteratively refines the architecture through ATAM evaluation. If the architecture fails the evaluation, the process loops back to component definition. This iterative refinement is the **core of architectural design**.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: The 4+1 View Model of Software Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph scenarioView["+1 Scenarios View"]
        scenarioNode["Use-Case View: Validates all other views via scenarios"]
    end

    subgraph logicalView["Logical View"]
        logicalNode["Class Diagram, Object Diagram: Functional structure of the system"]
    end

    subgraph processView["Process View"]
        processNode["Activity, Sequence: Concurrency, performance, runtime behavior"]
    end

    subgraph developmentView["Development View"]
        devNode["Package, Component: Module organization and software reuse"]
    end

    subgraph physicalView["Physical View"]
        physNode["Deployment: Hardware topology, network, server distribution"]
    end

    scenarioView -. "Validates" .-> logicalView
    scenarioView -. "Validates" .-> processView
    scenarioView -. "Validates" .-> developmentView
    scenarioView -. "Validates" .-> physicalView

    logicalView <--> developmentView
    processView <--> physicalView
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Stakeholder-Concern-View Mapping (ISO 42010)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Architect"] --> nodeB["Architectural Decisions and Rationale"]
    nodeC["End User"] --> nodeD["Usability and Functionality"]
    nodeE["Project Manager"] --> nodeF["Schedule, Cost, Risk"]
    nodeG["System Administrator"] --> nodeH["Deployability, Operability"]
    nodeI["Developer"] --> nodeJ["Build, Module Structure"]
    nodeK["Security Officer"] --> nodeL["Security, Compliance"]

    nodeB --> nodeM["Architectural Description AD"]
    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeM
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM

    nodeM --> nodeN["Logical, Process, Development, Physical Views"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Different stakeholders have *different concerns*. The ISO 42010 framework maps each concern to a specific *view* in the architecture. The architecture document (AD) is the unifying artifact that addresses all concerns.

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Component-Connector-Configuration Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    comp1["Component A: User Service"] -- "Connector 1: REST API" --> comp2["Component B: Order Service"]
    comp2 -- "Connector 2: Message Queue Kafka" --> comp3["Component C: Payment Service"]
    comp3 -- "Connector 3: gRPC" --> comp4["Component D: Notification Service"]
    comp4 -- "Connector 4: SMTP" --> comp5["External: Email Provider"]

    style comp1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style comp2 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style comp3 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
    style comp4 fill:#9fc,stroke:#333
    style comp5 fill:#c9f,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Rectangles are **components** (modular services). Arrows are **connectors** (interaction mechanisms). The arrangement of these into a topology forms the **configuration**. Each connector is labeled with its type (REST, Kafka, gRPC, SMTP).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"Define software architecture. List its key elements."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Software architecture is the fundamental organization of a system, embodied in its components, their relationships to each other and the environment, and the principles governing its design and evolution. *(1 mark)*

**Key Elements:**
1. **Components** — modular units of functionality *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Connectors** — mechanisms that mediate component interaction *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Configuration** — topological arrangement of components and connectors *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Constraints** and **rationale** — design principles and justifications *(0.5 mark)*

*(Total: 3 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*"Differentiate between software architecture and software design."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Software Architecture** | **Software Design** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Macro-level, system-wide | Micro-level, module-level |
| **Focus** | Non-functional requirements | Functional requirements |
| **Abstraction** | Abstract, strategic | Concrete, tactical |
| **Concerns** | Quality attributes, structure | Algorithm, data structure |
| **Stakeholders** | Multiple (clients, managers, devs) | Primarily developers |
| **Output** | Architectural Description (AD) | Detailed Design Document (DDD) |
| **Timing** | Early SDLC phase | Post-architecture phase |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for table/diagram)*

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 2 Part B questions are typically 14 marks with **internal choice** (OR option between two questions of equal difficulty). Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *"Explain the various stakeholders of a software system and their concerns with respect to software architecture. How does the architecture address each stakeholder's concerns?"* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Stakeholders (2 marks)**

A **stakeholder** is any individual, group, or organization that has an interest in the system's success or failure. In software architecture (per ISO 42010), stakeholders are *first-class* entities that drive the architectural description.

**Step 2: Enumerate Major Stakeholders and Concerns (3 marks)**

| **Stakeholder** | **Primary Concerns** |
|---|---|
| **End-User / Customer** | Usability, reliability, responsiveness |
| **Architect** | Structural integrity, design rationale |
| **Developer** | Modularity, buildability, testability |
| **Project Manager** | Schedule adherence, cost, risk |
| **System Administrator** | Deployability, monitorability, recoverability |
| **Security Officer** | Confidentiality, integrity, auditability |
| **Business Owner** | ROI, time-to-market, competitive advantage |
| **Maintainers** | Modifiability, extensibility, documentation |

**Step 3: How Architecture Addresses These Concerns (2 marks)**

The architecture uses **multiple views** (e.g., 4+1 model) to address different concerns:
- *End-users* → served by the **logical view** (functionality)
- *Performance engineers* → served by the **process view** (concurrency)
- *Operators* → served by the **physical view** (deployment)
- *Developers* → served by the **development view** (module structure)
- *All stakeholders* → unified through the **scenarios view** (use-cases)

**Conclusion:** The architectural description (AD) acts as a **single source of truth** that resolves conflicting concerns through documented trade-offs.

---

**(b)** *"Discuss the importance of software architecture in the software development life cycle (SDLC). Justify why architectural decisions are considered the most expensive to reverse."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Role of Architecture Across SDLC Phases (2 marks)**

- **Requirements Phase:** Architecture captures *architecture-significant requirements* (ASRs).
- **Design Phase:** Architecture defines the *structural skeleton* of the system.
- **Implementation Phase:** Architecture guides *module organization* and *integration*.
- **Testing Phase:** Architecture enables *integration testing* and *performance testing*.
- **Maintenance Phase:** Architecture provides *documentation* for *change impact analysis*.
- **Deployment Phase:** Architecture dictates *deployment topology* and *operational strategy*.

**Step 2: Six Key Benefits of Software Architecture (3 marks)**

1. **Communication Vehicle:** Provides a *common abstraction* for all stakeholders.
2. **Early Design Decisions:** Captures decisions when they are *cheapest to change*.
3. **Reusable Transferable Abstraction:** Patterns can be reused across projects.
4. **Quality Attribute Driver:** Determines non-functional behavior.
5. **Constraint Definition:** Limits design space, increasing team focus.
6. **Reasoning Foundation:** Supports performance, security, and reliability analysis *before* code.

**Step 3: Cost of Reversal — Justification (2 marks)**

Architectural decisions affect **all** downstream artifacts. If a defect is discovered post-deployment:
- **Requirements defect** → Cost: 1×
- **Design defect** → Cost: 5×
- **Architectural defect** → Cost: 50–100×

This is because architectural changes require:
- Reorganizing team structure (Conway's Law)
- Rebuilding integration points
- Retesting entire systems
- Possible hardware/cloud reconfiguration
- Customer migration overhead

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5.2(a):**
> Students often *list* stakeholders without explaining **how** the architecture addresses their concerns. The full 7 marks require a **two-way mapping** (stakeholder → concern → architectural view). Merely listing gets only 3 out of 7 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5.2(b):**
> Avoid the trap of writing *only* about the benefits of architecture. The question explicitly demands a **justification of the cost-of-reversal** argument. Cite the **exponential cost-amplification** model with concrete multipliers (1×, 5×, 50×, 100×).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *"Explain the 4+1 architectural view model with a neat diagram. Describe the purpose of each view."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**

The **4+1 View Model** was proposed by **Philippe Kruchten (1995)** to describe the architecture of software-intensive systems using **five concurrent views**. Each view addresses a specific set of stakeholder concerns.

**Step 2: Description of the Five Views (4 marks)**

| **View** | **Purpose** | **Primary Notation** | **Stakeholder** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Logical View** | Captures the *functional* requirements; describes the system's classes and their relationships | Class Diagram, Object Diagram | End-user, Analyst |
| **Process View** | Addresses *concurrency, performance, scalability*; describes runtime threads/processes | Activity Diagram, Sequence Diagram | Integrator, Performance Engineer |
| **Development View** | Focuses on *software module organization* and reusability | Package Diagram, Component Diagram | Developer, Project Manager |
| **Physical View** | Maps *software to hardware*; shows deployment topology | Deployment Diagram | System Engineer, Operator |
| **+1 Scenarios** | Acts as the *glue*; uses use-cases to validate other views | Use-Case Diagram | All stakeholders |

**Step 3: Significance (2 marks)**

- **Separation of concerns:** Each view isolates one set of stakeholder concerns.
- **Validation:** The +1 scenario view ensures *consistency* across all other views.
- **Standards compliance:** Aligns with **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010** for architectural description.
- **Practical utility:** Used in industry by IBM Rational, RUP (Rational Unified Process), and modern microservices design.

**Step 4: Diagram**

```
       +1 SCENARIOS VIEW (Use-Cases)
              |
    +---------+---------+---------+---------+
    |         |         |         |         |
  LOGICAL  PROCESS  DEVELOPMENT  PHYSICAL
   VIEW      VIEW      VIEW       VIEW
   (What)   (When)    (How)      (Where)
```

*(+1 mark for clean diagram with arrows showing scenario validation of other views.)*

---

**(b)** *"With a suitable example, explain the ATAM (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method). How does it differ from SAAM?"* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of ATAM (2 marks)**

**ATAM (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method)** is a **SEI (Software Engineering Institute, Carnegie Mellon)** method for evaluating software architectures. It is a **scenario-based evaluation technique** that:
- Identifies *quality attribute tradeoffs*
- Surfaces *risks* and *sensitivity points*
- Validates the architecture against *stakeholder concerns*

**Step 2: ATAM Phases (3 marks)**

1. **Phase 1: Presentation (Scenarios & Requirements)** — Architect presents business goals, ASRs, and architectural approaches.
2. **Phase 2: Investigation and Analysis** — Build the **utility tree** of quality attributes → scenarios; analyze each scenario.
3. **Phase 3: Testing** — Brainstorm scenarios with stakeholders; map them to architectural approaches.
4. **Phase 4: Reporting** — Document *risks, non-risks, sensitivity points,* and *tradeoffs*.

**Step 3: Worked Example (1 mark)**

Consider an **e-commerce system**. Stakeholders brainstorm scenarios:
- *"During Black Friday, the system must handle 50,000 concurrent users with < 500 ms response time."*

ATAM analyzes this scenario:
- **Approach 1:** Vertical scaling (add more CPU/RAM)
- **Approach 2:** Horizontal scaling (add more server instances + load balancer)

ATAM identifies Approach 2 as the *better tradeoff* (better scalability, but adds complexity → flagged as a **risk**).

**Step 4: ATAM vs. SAAM (1 mark)**

| **Basis** | **SAAM** | **ATAM** |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Scenario analysis | **Tradeoff** analysis |
| **Quality Attributes** | Single (typically modifiability) | Multiple (performance, security, etc.) |
| **Stakeholder Involvement** | Limited | Extensive |
| **Output** | Risk list | Risk list + tradeoffs + sensitivity points |
| **Year** | 1993 (earlier) | 2000s (evolution of SAAM) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5.2(a) Alternative:**
> Many students *describe* the 4+1 views but **forget the +1 (scenarios) view's role as a validator**. Marks are deducted for not showing the bidirectional arrows from the scenarios view to the other four views in the diagram.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5.2(b) Alternative:**
> The question is **not** "Define ATAM." The verb is *"explain with a suitable example."* A common mistake is writing a generic definition without a **worked example**. Always include at least one concrete scenario-based illustration to earn full marks.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

#### **Core Definitions (Memorize Verbatim)**
- ✅ **Software Architecture** = *fundamental organization of a system — its components, relationships, and design principles.*
- ✅ **Component** = *modular building block with well-defined interface.*
- ✅ **Connector** = *mechanism for component interaction (e.g., API, message queue).*
- ✅ **Architectural Style** = *named, reusable collection of architectural decisions (e.g., Layered, Microservices).*
- ✅ **Quality Attribute** = *non-functional property (performance, security, modifiability, etc.).*
- ✅ **ASR** = *Architecture-Significant Requirement — a quality attribute that *shapes* the architecture.*

#### **High-Yield Frameworks**
- ✅ **4+1 View Model** = Logical + Process + Development + Physical (+ Scenarios)
- ✅ **ATAM** = 4 phases, scenario-based, identifies tradeoffs
- ✅ **SAAM** = predecessor to ATAM, single-attribute focus (modifiability)
- ✅ **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010** = international standard for architectural description

#### **Critical "Why" Reasons (Memorize for 14-mark questions)**
- ✅ Architecture is established **early**, when changes are **cheapest**
- ✅ Architecture is the **single most expensive artifact to reverse** (50–100× cost)
- ✅ Architecture acts as a **communication vehicle** between heterogeneous stakeholders
- ✅ Architecture drives **non-functional quality attributes**
- ✅ Architecture is the foundation for **reuse, reasoning, and constraint enforcement**
- ✅ **Conway's Law** — the architecture mirrors the *organization's communication structure*

#### **Common Exam Buzzwords (Use These for Full Marks)**
- "Fundamental organization"
- "Components, connectors, configuration"
- "Architecture-significant requirements (ASRs)"
- "Quality attribute utility tree"
- "Tradeoff analysis"
- "Stakeholder concerns"
- "ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010 compliant architectural description"

#### **Frequent Mistake Patterns to Avoid**
- ❌ Confusing **architecture** with **design**
- ❌ Conflating **ATAM** with **SAAM**
- ❌ Forgetting the **+1 (scenarios) view** in 4+1 model
- ❌ Listing stakeholders without **mapping them to views**
- ❌ Writing about architecture benefits **without addressing the cost-of-reversal question**
- ❌ Forgetting to cite **Conway's Law** when discussing team-architecture alignment

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Architecture Patterns: Component and Connector, Layered, Repository, Client-Server, Publish-Subscribe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Architecture Patterns: Component and Connector, Layered, Repository, Client-Server, Publish-Subscribe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Software Architecture Pattern?

A **Software Architecture Pattern** is a reusable, time-tested solution to a recurring design problem that occurs within a given context in software engineering. According to **KTU 2024 Scheme** (aligned with Bass, Clements, and Kazman's canonical treatment), architectural patterns represent the *gross-level organization* of a software system — they define **components**, their **responsibilities**, and the **connectors** that mediate interaction between them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Examiner's Wording)**
> *"An architectural pattern is a named collection of architectural design decisions that are applicable to a recurring design problem, parameterized to account for different software development contexts in which it appears."*

---

## 1.2 The Five Core Patterns — Quick Intuitive Snapshot

### 🔹 1.2.1 Component-and-Connector (C&C) View

**Formal Definition:** A C&C view describes a system as a set of *components* (the principal units of computation/state) and *connectors* (the pathways of interaction — procedure calls, events, pipes, etc.) that link them.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Think of a **post office network**. Post offices (components) do not deliver mail themselves; they hand it to a courier pipeline (connector), which transports it to another post office. The focus is on *how things talk*, not on internal storage.

> [!NOTE]
> The C&C view is one of the **three canonical architectural views** in KTU syllabus: Module view, Component-and-Connector view, and Allocation view.

---

### 🔹 1.2.2 Layered Pattern

**Formal Definition:** The Layered pattern organizes the system into a hierarchy of layers, where each layer provides services to the layer *above* and consumes services from the layer *below*.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** A **restaurant kitchen** works in layers — the waiters (Presentation) take orders, the chef (Business Logic) cooks them, and the pantry (Data Access) retrieves ingredients. A chef never serves the customer directly, and a waiter never cooks.

> [!NOTE]
> The classic 3-tier (Presentation, Business, Data) and 4-tier (adding an Integration/Service layer) architectures are specializations of the **Layered** pattern.

---

### 🔹 1.2.3 Repository Pattern

**Formal Definition:** The Repository (a.k.a. Shared-Data or Blackboard) pattern organizes the system around a **central data structure** that is accessed independently by all major components. There is no explicit data flow between components; they read/write to the shared store.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** A **public notice board** in a university campus. Faculty, students, and admin all read from and post to the same board — they never talk to each other directly. The board is the only mediator.

---

### 🔹 1.2.4 Client-Server Pattern

**Formal Definition:** A structural pattern in which the system is decomposed into **clients** (service consumers) that request services from **servers** (service providers) over a network.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** A **restaurant dining experience** — you (the client) sit at a table and the waiter takes your order to the kitchen (the server), which prepares and returns the food. You do not cook, and the kitchen does not serve drinks directly to the next table.

---

### 🔹 1.2.5 Publish-Subscribe Pattern

**Formal Definition:** A distributed messaging pattern where **publishers** post messages to a **broker/topic** without knowing who will receive them, and **subscribers** register interest in topics and receive relevant messages asynchronously.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** A **YouTube channel subscription** — the creator (publisher) uploads a video; YouTube's notification system (broker) pushes it to everyone who has clicked the bell icon (subscribers). The creator never knows each subscriber personally.

---

## 1.3 Why These Patterns Matter in KTU 2024 Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Weightage Highlights (Module 2)**
> - Architecture styles are a **guaranteed 14-mark question** topic in ESE (End Semester Exam).
> - KTU examiners expect you to **draw the diagram** AND **list advantages/disadvantages**.
> - Compare-and-contrast questions (Layered vs. Repository, Client-Server vs. P2P) are **frequently repeated**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Architectural Pattern

Every KTU-tested architectural pattern is described using **five canonical elements**:

1. **Components** — The principal units that perform computation or hold state.
2. **Connectors** — The interaction mechanisms (procedure calls, events, pipes, shared memory).
3. **Topology** — The structural arrangement of components and connectors.
4. **Constraints** — The rules governing how components and connectors may be combined.
5. **Semantics** — The runtime behavior the pattern enables.

---

## 2.2 Pattern-by-Pattern Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Component-and-Connector (C&C) View

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| Components | Objects, processes, services, filters |
| Connectors | Procedure calls, events, pipes, shared variables |
| Topology | Graph — typically directed |
| Control Style | Can be centralized or event-driven |

**Key Insight:** The C&C view is **not a pattern itself** — it is a *modeling viewpoint*. All four other patterns (Layered, Repository, Client-Server, P2P, Publish-Subscribe) are **expressions of C&C** with different topology rules.

---

### 2.2.2 Layered Pattern

**Component Type:** Layers (each layer is a *virtual machine* providing a service to the layer above).

**Allowed Connectors:** Strict top-down (or top-down + bottom-up) call invocations.

**Strict Layering vs. Relaxed Layering:**
- **Strict Layering:** A layer $L_i$ may only call $L_{i-1}$. 
- **Relaxed Layering:** A layer $L_i$ may call any layer $L_j$ where $j < i$.

**Quality Attributes Achieved:**
- **Modifiability:** ★★★★★ (a layer can be swapped without disturbing others)
- **Portability:** ★★★★★ (lowest layer can be re-implemented per platform)
- **Performance:** ★★☆☆☆ (request travels through many layers)

---

### 2.2.3 Repository Pattern

**Component Types:**
- **Central data structure** (the repository)
- **Independent computational components** (readers/writers)

**Connector:** Direct read/write access to the repository (typically a database or blackboard).

**Two Sub-Variants:**
1. **Database-centric (modern Repository):** Components treat the DB as a black box; blackboard logic in the DB engine itself.
2. **Blackboard (AI-style):** Repository contains both data and *inference engines* that trigger actions.

**Quality Attributes:**
- **Integrability:** ★★★★★ (new component just reads from/writes to repo)
- **Reusability:** ★★★★★ of the data model
- **Performance bottlenecks** appear around the central store.

---

### 2.2.4 Client-Server Pattern

**Component Types:** Clients (request initiators) and Servers (request handlers).

**Connectors:** Network messages — RPC, REST, gRPC, HTTP, sockets.

**Topology:** Star (clients → server). **N-tier** extensions: client → app server → DB server.

**Key Quality Attributes:**
- **Availability:** Server is a **single point of failure** → mitigated via load balancers, replication.
- **Security:** Server is a natural **choke point** for authentication/authorization.
- **Scalability:** Vertical (scale up) or Horizontal (scale out via load balancer).

**Variants:** 2-tier, 3-tier, n-tier.

---

### 2.2.5 Publish-Subscribe Pattern

**Component Types:**
- **Publishers** (event producers)
- **Subscribers** (event consumers)
- **Broker / Event Bus** (mediator; optional in some direct variants)

**Connector:** Asynchronous event delivery (e.g., Kafka, RabbitMQ, MQTT).

**Two Sub-Variants:**
1. **Topic-based Pub/Sub:** Subscribers register for a named topic.
2. **Content-based Pub/Sub:** Subscribers specify predicates on message content.

**Quality Attributes:**
- **Loose Coupling:** ★★★★★ — publisher and subscriber are anonymous to each other.
- **Scalability:** ★★★★★ — broker can fan-out to millions of subscribers.
- **Debuggability:** ★☆☆☆☆ — asynchronous flow is hard to trace.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Pattern-Selection Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas below are **architectural metric formulas** used in KTU problems to justify a pattern choice.

$$
\text{Layered Coupling Depth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Coupling}(L_i, L_{i-1})
$$

$$
\text{Repository Fan-out Factor} = \frac{N_{\text{components}} \cdot R_{\text{read}} + W_{\text{write}}}{T_{\text{throughput}}}
$$

$$
\text{Client-Server Response Time} \approx T_{\text{network}} + T_{\text{server}} + T_{\text{queue}}
$$

$$
\text{Pub/Sub Decoupling Index} = 1 - \frac{\text{Shared Knowledge (publisher, subscriber)}}{\text{Maximum Possible Knowledge}}
$$

$$
\text{Modifiability Index} = \frac{\sum \text{Isolated Layer Changes}}{\sum \text{Total Required Changes}}
$$

### High-Yield Comparison Table

| Dimension | Layered | Repository | Client-Server | Publish-Subscribe |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Stack / Hierarchy | Star (data-centric) | Star (call-centric) | Broker-mediated mesh |
| **Coupling** | Tight (across layers) | Loose (via shared data) | Medium (via protocol) | Very loose (async) |
| **Best For** | OS, compilers, web apps | IDEs, CASE tools, AI | Web services, DB apps | IoT, event streaming |
| **Failure Mode** | Layer crash cascades | DB crash = total failure | Server = SPOF | Broker = SPOF |
| **KTU 2024 Marks** | 7–14 | 7–14 | 7–14 | 7–14 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Layered:** Used in **TCP/IP stack**, **OSI model**, **Spring Boot web stacks** — enables protocol portability.
- **Repository:** Used in **IDEs (Eclipse)**, **CAD systems**, **modern microservices sharing a database** — enables independent agent development.
- **Client-Server:** Used in **Banking systems, Gmail, WhatsApp backend** — enables centralized control and audit.
- **Publish-Subscribe:** Used in **stock trading platforms, Uber ride-matching, sensor networks (IoT)** — enables massive real-time fan-out.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: Choosing the Right Pattern

In KTU problems, the examiner often gives a **scenario** and asks you to **justify a pattern**. Use this 5-step analytical framework:

### Step 1 — Identify the Dominant Quality Attribute

Map the requirement to a quality attribute:
- *"Easy to modify"* → **Modifiability** → favor **Layered**
- *"Many data sources, many views"* → **Integrability** → favor **Repository**
- *"Centralized control of resources"* → **Security / Availability** → favor **Client-Server**
- *"Huge number of loosely-related producers/consumers"* → **Scalability / Loose Coupling** → favor **Pub/Sub**

### Step 2 — Identify the Communication Style

- **Synchronous, hierarchical** → Layered
- **Synchronous, shared data** → Repository
- **Synchronous, request-response** → Client-Server
- **Asynchronous, event-driven** → Publish-Subscribe

### Step 3 — Identify Failure-Tolerance Requirements

Use the **Single Point of Failure (SPOF) Analysis** below:

$$
\text{SPOF Count} = \text{Components whose failure halts the system}
$$

| Pattern | SPOF Components | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Layered | Bottom layer | Implement lower layers redundantly |
| Repository | Central DB | DB replication, clustering |
| Client-Server | Server | Load balancer + multiple server instances |
| Pub/Sub | Broker | Clustered broker (e.g., Kafka brokers) |

### Step 4 — Compute Performance Estimates

For a **Client-Server** system with $n$ clients, average request rate $\lambda$, and server processing time $T_s$:

$$
T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{network}} + \frac{T_s}{1 - \rho}
$$

where $\rho = \lambda \cdot T_s$ is the **server utilization factor**.

**Derivation of the Queueing Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{By Little's Law: } L &= \lambda \cdot W \\
\text{For an M/M/1 queue: } W &= \frac{T_s}{1 - \rho} \\
\text{Therefore: } T_{\text{response}} &= T_{\text{network}} + W \\
T_{\text{response}} &= T_{\text{network}} + \frac{T_s}{1 - \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Little's Law states that the average number of items in a system equals the arrival rate times the average time in the system. For an M/M/1 queue, the waiting time $W$ is the service time divided by one minus the utilization. Adding the network time gives total response time.

### Step 5 — Map to the Pattern (Decision Matrix)

Apply this decision matrix literally in your answer:

$$
\text{Pattern} = \arg\max_{p \in P} \left( w_1 \cdot Q_1(p) + w_2 \cdot Q_2(p) + \cdots + w_n \cdot Q_n(p) \right)
$$

where $w_i$ is the **weight** of quality attribute $Q_i$, and $P = \{ \text{Layered, Repository, Client-Server, Pub/Sub} \}$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Pattern Comparison — Multi-Attribute Tradeoff

For a real-time IoT system with **1 million sensors** publishing temperature, and a dashboard showing the average temperature across all sensors, we can derive the required architecture:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision: Pub/Sub} \quad &\text{(because)} \\
\lambda_{\text{events}} &= 10^6 \text{ events/sec} \quad \text{(high volume)} \\
\text{Coupling} &= \text{Anonymous, async} \quad \text{(favors Pub/Sub)} \\
\text{Fault tolerance} &= \text{Sensor may fail independently} \quad \text{(favors Pub/Sub)} \\
\text{Subscribers} &= 1 \text{ dashboard} \quad \text{(broker mediates 1:N fan-out)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The high event rate rules out synchronous request-response (Client-Server would collapse under 1M concurrent connections). Shared data (Repository) does not scale to real-time event flow. Layering is orthogonal — it can be combined WITH Pub/Sub. Hence, the dominant style is Pub/Sub with internal Layered structure for the broker.

---

## 3.3 The "Combined Style" Analysis (Frequently Asked in KTU)

KTU 2024 examiners love to ask: *"Can two patterns be combined?"* The answer is **yes**, and the combination is analyzed as:

$$
\text{Combined Architecture} = P_{\text{structural}} \oplus P_{\text{interactional}}
$$

**Common combinations:**

| Combination | Example | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Layered + Client-Server | 3-tier web app | Enterprise web |
| Repository + Client-Server | DB-backed web app | CMS, ERP |
| Pub/Sub + Layered | Kafka + microservice layers | Event-driven microservices |

$$
\boxed{\text{Most production systems are } n\text{-pattern hybrids, not pure patterns.}}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: Differentiate between Layered and Repository architecture styles. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Layered | Repository |
|---|---|---|
| Data flow | Vertical (layer → layer) | Components access shared data |
| Coupling | Tight between adjacent layers | Loose (via shared data only) |
| Best for | OS, compilers | IDEs, AI systems |
| Failure | Cascading | Central store = SPOF |

**Advantages of Layered:**
1. High **modifiability** — a layer can be replaced without affecting others.
2. Clear **separation of concerns** — each layer has a single responsibility.

**Advantages of Repository:**
1. **Integrability** — new components can be added by simply giving them read/write access to the repository.
2. **Data consistency** is easier to enforce as all data lives in one place.

**(3 marks: 1 for table, 1 for layered advantages, 1 for repository advantages.)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q: What is the role of a broker in the Publish-Subscribe pattern? Why is loose coupling important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of Broker (1.5 marks):**
- The broker (also called *event bus* or *topic manager*) acts as an **intermediary** between publishers and subscribers.
- It **receives** events from publishers, **filters** them based on subscriber interests, and **dispatches** them to matching subscribers.
- Examples: Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, MQTT broker.

**Importance of Loose Coupling (1.5 marks):**
1. **Independent evolution** — publisher and subscriber can be upgraded independently.
2. **Scalability** — publishers/subscribers can be added/removed at runtime.
3. **Fault isolation** — failure of one subscriber does not affect publishers or other subscribers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **DRAW THE DIAGRAM.** Skipping the diagram costs you **at least 3 marks** on a 14-mark question.
> - Do **NOT** write only advantages. Every KTU 14-mark pattern question requires: **Diagram + Components/Connectors + 3 advantages + 3 disadvantages + 1 real-world example + Quality attributes tradeoff**.
> - Do **NOT** confuse **Client-Server** with **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)**. P2P has no central server; C-S has one.
> - Do **NOT** say "Pub/Sub is better than Client-Server" — they serve different quality attributes. **Compare and contrast**, do not rank.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Client-Server** architecture style with a neat diagram. Discuss its components, connectors, advantages, and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Publish-Subscribe** architecture style with a diagram. Compare it with Client-Server on three quality attributes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Client-Server architecture decomposes the system into two types of components — **clients** (which initiate requests) and **servers** (which provide services). Communication occurs over a **network** via connectors such as HTTP, RPC, or REST.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw a star topology — multiple clients connected to a central server, with a load balancer if mentioned.)*

```
[Client 1]   [Client 2]   [Client 3]
     \\         |         /
      \\        |        /
       \\       |       /
        [LOAD BALANCER]
              |
        [APPLICATION SERVER]
              |
        [DATABASE SERVER]
```

**Components (1 mark):**
- **Clients** — UI side; issue requests, receive responses.
- **Servers** — Provide services (e.g., web server, DB server, app server).
- **Network** — The implicit medium.

**Connectors (1 mark):**
- HTTP, REST, gRPC, RPC, TCP sockets, WebSockets.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
1. Centralized **security** enforcement at the server.
2. Easy **maintenance** — server upgrades roll out to all clients.
3. **Scalability** via load balancers and server clusters.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. **Server is a Single Point of Failure (SPOF)**.
2. Network latency degrades user experience.
3. Server overload under high concurrency.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Publish-Subscribe is a messaging pattern where **publishers** emit events to a **broker** (or topic), and **subscribers** register interest in topics to receive events asynchronously. Publishers and subscribers are **decoupled in space, time, and synchronization**.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to the Pub/Sub Mermaid diagram in Section 4.6 — three publishers, a broker, three subscribers.)*

**Components (1 mark):**
- **Publishers**, **Subscribers**, **Broker / Event Bus**.

**Connectors (1 mark):**
- Asynchronous message-passing protocols (AMQP, MQTT, Kafka protocol).

**Comparison Table on 3 Quality Attributes (2 marks):**

| Quality Attribute | Client-Server | Publish-Subscribe |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Medium — clients know server's API | Very loose — no compile-time or runtime awareness |
| **Synchronization** | Synchronous request-response | Asynchronous, fire-and-forget |
| **Failure Impact** | Server down = all clients fail | Broker down = total halt, but publishers/subscriber pairs are unaffected in normal operation |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (scale server) | Horizontal (add brokers + topics) |
| **Real-time Suitability** | Limited by polling | Excellent — push-based, real-time |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Layered** architecture pattern with a 3-tier diagram. State its advantages, disadvantages, and two situations where it is the most suitable choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Repository** architecture pattern. Compare it with the Layered pattern on the basis of **modifiability** and **performance** quality attributes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Layered pattern partitions the system into a hierarchy of layers, each providing services to the layer above and consuming services from the layer below. A layer $L_i$ only depends on $L_{i-1}$.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
*(3-tier: Presentation → Business Logic → Data Access. Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3.)*

**Components (1 mark):**
- Presentation Layer (UI), Business Logic Layer (domain rules), Data Access Layer (DB calls), Database (storage).

**Connectors (1 mark):**
- Function/procedure calls; strict top-down requests and bottom-up responses.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Modifiability** — change one layer without touching others.
2. **Portability** — change the bottom layer for a new platform.
3. **Testability** — each layer can be unit-tested in isolation via mocks.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. **Performance overhead** — request passes through many layers.
2. **Cascading changes** — adding a feature may require editing all layers.
3. **"Sinkhole" anti-pattern** — if layers don't add value, they only slow things down.

**Suitable Situations (free within the same mark):**
1. **Operating systems** (HAL, kernel, system call layer).
2. **Web applications** with clear UI / Business / DB separation.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Repository pattern organizes the system around a **central data structure** (the repository) that all major components read from and write to. Components do **not** communicate with each other directly.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to the Repository Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4 — components arranged around a central repository.)*

**Components (1 mark):**
- **Central Repository** (database, blackboard, or shared data structure).
- **Independent components** that read/write from the repository.

**Connectors (1 mark):**
- Direct read/write to shared data (SQL, file I/O, in-memory data structures).

**Comparison on Modifiability (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layered Modifiability} &= \text{High for layer-internal changes} \\
\text{Repository Modifiability} &= \text{Equal or higher for new components} \\
\text{Repository Risk} &= \text{Changing the data schema breaks ALL components}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison on Performance (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layered Performance} &= \text{Degrades with layer count} \propto O(n_{\text{layers}}) \\
\text{Repository Performance} &= \text{Depends on the central store throughput} \\
\text{Repository Bottleneck} &= \text{Central DB may become the throughput limit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Layered systems pay a per-request tax equal to the number of layers traversed; Repository systems pay a per-operation tax determined by the central data store. Neither is universally faster — it depends on the workload.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **C&C view** is a *modeling perspective*, not a pattern. All other patterns are special cases of C&C.
- [ ] **Layered** → best for *modifiability and portability*; worst for *performance* due to layer traversal.
- [ ] **Strict Layering** forbids skipping; **Relaxed Layering** allows skipping.
- [ ] **Repository** → best when *many independent components share the same data*; central store is the SPOF.
- [ ] **Client-Server** is a *request-response* model; server is a natural choke point for security and audit.
- [ ] **N-tier Client-Server** extends the basic 2-tier model for separation of concerns.
