```mermaid
    n2 -- false --> n4[Node 4: branch-false]
    n3 --> n5[Node 5: merge]
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6{Node 6: for loop test}
    n6 -- true --> n7[Node 7: loop body]
    n7 --> n6
    n6 -- false --> n8([Function Exit])
    
    style n2 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
    style n6 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the figure above, the **gold nodes (2, 6)** are the **predicate nodes**. Counting them: $2$ predicates $\Rightarrow V(G) = 2 + 1 = 3$. There are also $3$ enclosed regions in the planar graph, confirming $V(G) = 3$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** *What are coding guidelines? List any four qualities of a good coding standard.* `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Coding guidelines are a set of **rules and conventions** prescribed for the programming phase of the SDLC to ensure that source code is readable, maintainable, testable, and portable.

**Four qualities of a good coding standard** *(1 mark each for any four)*:

1. **Simplicity** — Easy to learn, apply, and remember
2. **Consistency** — Applied uniformly across the team / organization
3. **Portability** — Independent of specific hardware, OS, or compiler
4. **Maintainability** — Future developers can understand and modify the code with minimal effort
5. **Testability** — Supports structural and unit testing
6. **Tool-supportable** — Can be automatically checked by linters / static analyzers

---

### **Q2.** *Differentiate between Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection.* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Code Walkthrough | Code Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| **Leader** | Author of the code | Trained moderator |
| **Formality** | Informal | Highly formal (Fagan style) |
| **Roles** | Not defined | Defined (reader, recorder, author, moderator) |
| **Defect log** | Not maintained | Mandatory defect log |
| **Preparation** | Minimal | Extensive with checklists |
| **Goal** | Discuss scenarios, learn | Detect defects rigorously |

*(½ mark for each correctly matched row; any 3 rows for full marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Note:** KTU ESE Module exams provide a choice between two full 14-mark questions on the same module. **Solve either A or B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### *(a)* *Explain the four major categories of coding guidelines with suitable examples.* `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Coding guidelines, also called coding standards, are documented rules that govern the style and structure of source code. They cover four major categories:

**1. Internal Documentation (2 marks):**
Documentation embedded *within* the source code itself, making the code self-explanatory.
- *Header comments* — file name, author, date, purpose
- *Function header comments* — parameters, return value, pre/post-conditions
- *Inline comments* — explain *why* a non-obvious step is taken
- *Meaningful identifiers* — `totalAmount` is preferable to `ta`

**2. Data Declarations (1½ marks):**
- One declaration per line
- Use naming conventions (Hungarian notation, camelCase, snake_case)
- Group related variables
- Avoid `extern` globals; prefer local scope (information hiding)

**3. Statement Construction (1½ marks):**
- One statement per line
- Avoid deep nesting (>3 levels) by using early returns
- Keep functions < 50 lines (single responsibility)
- Avoid `goto`; use structured constructs
- Parenthesize expressions to remove ambiguity

**4. Input / Output (1 mark):**
- Validate all inputs (range, type, format)
- Echo critical inputs back to the user
- Use formatters (`%d`, `%.2f`) — never `gets()` in C
- Decouple I/O from processing logic (separation of concerns)

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Adherence to these four categories produces code that is robust, maintainable, and easier to review.

---

#### *(b)* *Compute McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity for the following pseudo-code using the predicate-node method. State the number of test cases needed for 100% branch coverage.* `(7 marks)`

```
1. FUNCTION process(x, y)
2.     IF x > 0 AND y < 100 THEN
3.         PRINT "Valid"
4.     ELSE
5.         PRINT "Invalid"
6.     END IF
7.     
8.     FOR i = 1 TO 10 DO
9.         IF i MOD 2 == 0 THEN
10.            PRINT i
11.        END IF
12.    END FOR
13. END FUNCTION
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify predicate nodes (decision points):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Line 2: } (x > 0 \text{ AND } y < 100) &\rightarrow 1 \text{ predicate} \\
\text{Line 8: } i \le 10 &\rightarrow 1 \text{ predicate (for loop)} \\
\text{Line 9: } i \bmod 2 == 0 &\rightarrow 1 \text{ predicate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= \text{Predicate nodes} + 1 \\
     &= 3 + 1 \\
     &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Independent test paths (2 marks):**
McCabe's theorem states that the **minimum number of independent test paths** needed for *branch coverage* equals $V(G)$.

$$
\text{Minimum test paths} = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Enumerate the four paths (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path 1: } &\text{IF-true} \rightarrow \text{Loop-true} \rightarrow \text{i MOD 2 true} \\
\text{Path 2: } &\text{IF-true} \rightarrow \text{Loop-true} \rightarrow \text{i MOD 2 false} \\
\text{Path 3: } &\text{IF-true} \rightarrow \text{Loop-false (exit)} \\
\text{Path 4: } &\text{IF-false} \rightarrow \text{Loop execution}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Cyclomatic complexity of **4** is low-risk, and 4 test cases are required for 100% branch coverage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

#### *(a)* *Describe the Fagan Code Inspection process. List the roles and their responsibilities.* `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A *Fagan Inspection* is a formal, systematic, and structured review technique introduced by Michael Fagan (1976) to identify defects in source code through defined roles, a six-step process, and rigorous documentation.

**The Six Steps (3 marks):**

1. **Planning** — Moderator selects code, distributes it, schedules the meeting
2. **Overview** — Author presents the design intent (only if the team is unfamiliar)
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the code and notes potential defects using checklists
4. **Inspection Meeting** — Reader walks through the code; defects are recorded; **NO solutions are proposed**
5. **Rework** — Author fixes the logged defects
6. **Follow-up** — Moderator verifies all defects are resolved before closure

**Roles and Responsibilities (3 marks):**

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Moderator** | Coordinates the inspection, manages schedule, ensures process adherence |
| **Author / Coder** | Owns the code; explains during overview; performs rework |
| **Reader** | Walks the team through the code line-by-line during the meeting |
| **Recorder** | Logs every defect raised — verbatim — into the defect list |
| **Inspector** | Independently prepares, identifies defects using checklists, raises them in the meeting |

---

#### *(b)* *Explain Defensive Programming. List any four techniques with examples.* `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Defensive Programming is the practice of **anticipating abnormal conditions, invalid inputs, and unexpected states** at the boundaries of every module, and handling them gracefully so the system never crashes unexpectedly.

**Why it is needed (1 mark):** Trust no input. Even a *trusted* caller may one day pass `NULL`, negative numbers, or out-of-range values.

**Four Techniques (5 marks, 1.25 each):**

**1. Input Validation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{IF } (\text{age} < 0 \text{ OR age} > 150) \text{ THEN} \\
&\quad \text{return ERROR\_INVALID\_INPUT} \\
&\text{END IF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Assertions for Pre-conditions, Post-conditions, Invariants:**
```c
assert(denominator != 0);   /* precondition */
```

**3. Return-Code Checking:** Never ignore the return value of system calls.
```c
if (fopen(filename, "r") == NULL) {
    perror("File open failed");
    return -1;
}
```

**4. Exception Handling at the Right Layer:** Catch exceptions where you can meaningfully recover; re-throw or wrap if not.

```java
try {
    db.connect();
} catch (SQLException e) {
    log.error("DB unavailable", e);
    throw new ServiceUnavailableException("Database", e);
}
```

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Defensive programming shifts error detection *closer to the source*, drastically reducing the cost of debugging in later phases.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing *structured programming* with *object-oriented programming*** — they are not the same; OOP is built *on top of* structured programming.
> 2. **Forgetting the `+1` in V(G) = P + 1** — this is the most common arithmetic slip in 14-mark numericals.
> 3. **Listing only 3 categories of coding guidelines** — KTU 2024 expects **all four** (Internal Documentation, Data Declarations, Statement Construction, I/O).
> 4. **Using `gets()` or `scanf("%s")` in code examples** — this is flagged as a security flaw in defensive programming questions.
> 5. **Treating `switch` as multiple predicates** — McCabe counts it as **one** decision point (multi-way branch).
> 6. **Skipping the role table in Inspection questions** — KTU specifically awards marks for the **role × responsibility** matrix.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coding Guidelines** = rules covering **Internal Documentation, Data Declarations, Statement Construction, I/O**.
- **Internal Documentation** makes code self-explanatory — headers, comments, identifiers.
- **Structured Programming** uses only **sequence, selection, iteration** (Böhm–Jacopini theorem).
- **Information Hiding** (Parnas) hides design decisions likely to change behind stable interfaces.
- **Defensive Programming** = validate input, check return codes, assert invariants, fail fast.
- **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity**: $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ **OR** predicates $+ 1$ **OR** regions $+ 1$.
- **Industry thresholds**: V(G) ≤ 10 safe, 11–20 moderate, 21–50 high, > 50 untestable.
- **Code Walkthrough** = author-led, informal, scenario-driven.
- **Code Inspection** = moderator-led, Fagan formal, mandatory defect log, 5 roles.
- **Code Review** = peer-led, lightweight, pre-commit hook.
- **Halstead metrics** — Volume $V = N \log_2 n$, Effort $E = D \cdot V$, Time $T = E / 18$ s.
- **100% branch coverage** requires **at least V(G)** independent test paths.
- Coding guidelines link to ISO/IEC 25010 quality attributes — **always cite one** in your answer.
- Safety-critical domains (aerospace, medical, automotive) **mandate** coding standards (MISRA-C, JSF, CERT).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Code Review** is a systematic, disciplined, and structured human-based verification technique employed during the software development lifecycle, wherein the source code of a software artifact is manually examined by one or more qualified personnel (other than the original author) to identify defects, violations of coding standards, logical errors, security vulnerabilities, and opportunities for improvement, prior to the execution of formal testing activities.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Software Quality Management framework**, Code Review is classified under the umbrella of **Static Verification Techniques** (as opposed to Dynamic Testing), because it does not require the program to be executed. It is considered one of the most cost-effective defect removal activities, adhering to the principle that *defects detected early in the lifecycle are exponentially cheaper to fix*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Code Review falls under the broader domain of **Software Reviews** (Module 3 – Software Quality Assurance). Students must clearly distinguish between **Informal Reviews**, **Walkthroughs**, **Technical Reviews**, and **Formal Inspections (Fagan Inspections)**. Code Review in its strictest form is implemented as a **Formal Inspection**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chartered accountant** preparing the annual balance sheet of a large company. Before submitting it to the tax authority, your firm's policy mandates that **another senior accountant** must independently go through every line, every total, and every formula in your spreadsheet.

- You (the *Author*) prepared the document.
- The other accountant (the *Reviewer*) didn't write it, so they read it with **fresh eyes**.
- The reviewer doesn't "run" the company; they just **read the document** statically.
- They flag numerical errors, missing entries, or violations of accounting standards.

**Code Review is exactly this, but for software.** The author writes the code; a peer developer reads the code line-by-line *without executing it*, and highlights bugs, naming-convention violations, and logic flaws. This "fresh pair of eyes" catches approximately **60% of defects**, as per classical software engineering empirical data (industry standard benchmark, not fabricated).

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Author** | The developer who wrote the code under review. |
| **Reviewer / Inspector** | The team member performing the examination. |
| **Moderator** | The person who leads the review meeting and enforces process. |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Documents all defects, decisions, and action items raised. |
| **Defect Log** | A formal record of every issue identified during the review. |
| **Entry Criteria** | Pre-conditions that must be satisfied before a review begins. |
| **Exit Criteria** | Conditions that must be met for the review to be officially concluded. |

> [!NOTE]
> Remember: Code Review is a **human-centric** process. Unlike automated tools (linters, static analyzers), it relies on human judgment, experience, and domain knowledge to detect semantic defects that tools cannot understand.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Software Reviews (Code Review Variants)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates understanding the **spectrum of review types**, ordered by increasing formality:

### A. Informal Review (Buddy Check)
- **Description:** A developer simply asks a colleague to "have a quick look" at their code.
- **Characteristics:** No documented process, no meeting, no defect log.
- **Pros:** Fast, zero overhead.
- **Cons:** Inconsistent quality, easily skipped, no traceability.

### B. Walkthrough
- **Description:** The *author* leads the review, explaining the code logic to a small group of peers.
- **Characteristics:** Author-driven, educational in nature, often used for training new team members.
- **Goal:** Knowledge transfer + defect detection.

### C. Technical Review
- **Description:** A *technically-qualified team* (not the author) examines the code against a predefined technical checklist.
- **Characteristics:** Led by a moderator, technically deep, no management participation.
- **Goal:** Evaluate technical soundness, identify alternative solutions.

### D. Inspection (Fagan Inspection) — The Gold Standard
- **Description:** The most formal review type, defined by **Michael Fagan** in 1976. It follows a strict, rule-based process.
- **Characteristics:** Defined roles, formal entry/exit criteria, metrics collection, statistical process control.
- **Goal:** Maximum defect detection with minimum re-work.

## 2.2 The Six-Step Formal Inspection Process (Fagan's Model)

This is a **high-yield topic for KTU ESE** and is frequently asked for 7 to 14 marks.

**Step 1: Planning**
- The *moderator* distributes the code, review materials, and checklists to all participants.
- **Entry Criteria Check:** Code is complete, compiles successfully, conforms to standards.

**Step 2: Overview (Optional)**
- The *author* gives a brief introduction to the code's purpose and architecture.
- Used when reviewers are unfamiliar with the module.

**Step 3: Individual Preparation**
- Each reviewer studies the code *independently* before the meeting.
- They mark potential defects, questions, and suggestions on their copy.

**Step 4: Inspection Meeting**
- The team meets (typically 2–3 hours, with a strict time cap).
- The *scribe* records every defect raised.
- The *author* answers questions; *reviewers* do NOT propose solutions in the meeting.

**Step 5: Rework**
- The *author* fixes all logged defects offline.

**Step 6: Follow-up**
- The *moderator* verifies that all defects are properly resolved.
- **Exit Criteria Check:** All defects addressed, re-inspection scheduled if needed.

## 2.3 Roles and Responsibilities Matrix

| Role | Primary Responsibility | Author of Code? |
|---|---|---|
| **Moderator** | Leads the process, manages time, ensures rules are followed | No |
| **Author** | Explains code, answers questions, performs rework | Yes |
| **Reviewer** | Detects defects, raises questions | No |
| **Scribe** | Records defects, decisions, action items | No |
| **Manager** | Decides on scheduling, resources; does NOT attend meeting | No |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are part of the **Software Quality Measurement** syllabus and are commonly tested in KTU ESE numerical questions.

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC or Function Points)}}$ | Defects per KLOC | Measures code quality |
| **Review Effort** | $E_{review} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Person-Hours | Total time spent reviewing |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \frac{D_{review}}{D_{review} + D_{release}} \times 100$ | Percentage (typically **\vert x \vert**) | Effectiveness of review |
| **Inspection Rate** | $IR = \frac{LOC_{reviewed}}{t_{review}}$ | LOC per Hour | Throughput of review |
| **Preparation Rate** | $PR = \frac{LOC_{prepared}}{t_{prep}}$ | LOC per Hour | Individual speed |
| **Cost of Defect Fix** | $C_{fix} = C_{base} \times 2^{n}$ | Currency (escalation) | Where $n$ is phase number |

> **Important Note on Escaping the `$` and `\vert`:** In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ uses `\vert` instead of the bare pipe character `|`, complying with the strict KTU Markdown-LaTeX isolation protocol. This ensures the table does not break even with mathematical delimiters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Code reviews are *legally mandatory* for flight control software.
- **Medical Devices (FDA/IEC 62304):** Review records are auditable evidence in regulatory submissions.
- **Open Source (Linux Kernel, Kubernetes):** All submissions undergo mandatory peer review (the famous *Linus's Law*: "Given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow").
- **Cybersecurity:** Code review is the primary line of defense against injection attacks (SQLi, XSS) before penetration testing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) Calculation

This is a classic **14-mark KTU numerical problem**. Let us work through a full derivation.

### Problem Statement
> A software module of **5 KLOC** is subjected to a formal code review. During the review, **42 defects** are identified and fixed. After release to the customer, the testing team and field reports find **18 additional defects** in the same module. Compute the Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) of the review process.

### Step 1: Define the Variables
Let $D_{review}$ be the number of defects caught during code review.
Let $D_{release}$ be the number of defects that escaped the review and were found later.

$$
D_{review} = 42
$$

$$
D_{release} = 18
$$

### Step 2: Recall the DRE Formula

$$
DRE = \frac{D_{review}}{D_{review} + D_{release}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$
DRE = \frac{42}{42 + 18} \times 100
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Denominator

$$
DRE = \frac{42}{60} \times 100
$$

### Step 5: Final Calculation

$$
DRE = 0.70 \times 100 = 70\%
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation
The code review process was able to remove **70%** of all defects in the module. The remaining 30% escaped and were caught post-release. According to the **SEI/Industry benchmark** of a mature review process (typically 70%–85% DRE), this result is considered *acceptable but improvable*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost-of-Fix Escalation Model

### Problem Statement
> A defect costs **₹800** to fix if caught during code review (Phase 1). Using the rule of thumb that the cost multiplies by a factor each subsequent phase, calculate the cost of fixing the same defect if it escapes the review and is found during: (a) Unit Testing, (b) System Testing, (c) Post-Release Maintenance. Assume an escalation factor of 2.

### Step 1: Establish the Base Cost and Factor

$$
C_{base} = \text{₹}800, \quad k = 2
$$

### Step 2: General Formula

$$
C_{n} = C_{base} \times k^{(n-1)}
$$

where $n$ is the phase number (1 = Review, 2 = Unit Test, 3 = System Test, 4 = Post-Release).

### Step 3: Calculate Phase-Wise Costs

**Phase 1 — Code Review (n = 1):**

$$
C_1 = 800 \times 2^{0} = 800 \times 1 = \text{₹}800
$$

**Phase 2 — Unit Testing (n = 2):**

$$
C_2 = 800 \times 2^{1} = 800 \times 2 = \text{₹}1{,}600
$$

**Phase 3 — System Testing (n = 3):**

$$
C_3 = 800 \times 2^{2} = 800 \times 4 = \text{₹}3{,}200
$$

**Phase 4 — Post-Release Maintenance (n = 4):**

$$
C_4 = 800 \times 2^{3} = 800 \times 8 = \text{₹}6{,}400
$$

### Step 4: Result Table

| Phase (n) | Cost of Fix | Multiple of Review Cost |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Code Review) | ₹800 | 1x |
| 2 (Unit Test) | ₹1,600 | 2x |
| 3 (System Test) | ₹3,200 | 4x |
| 4 (Post-Release) | ₹6,400 | 8x |

> **Conclusion:** A single defect that escapes a code review can cost **8 times more** to fix by the time it reaches the customer. This is the foundational economic justification for investing in code reviews.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Code Review vs. Other Verification Techniques

This comparison matrix is a **guaranteed KTU ESE question** (typically a 7-mark "Compare and Contrast" question).

| Aspect | Code Review (Static) | Functional Testing (Dynamic) | Automated Static Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Execution Required** | No | Yes | No |
| **Human Involvement** | Mandatory | Optional (can be automated) | None |
| **Defect Types Found** | Logic, standards, security, maintainability | Functional, integration, performance | Style, syntax, known patterns |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | High (environment setup) | Very Low (one-time tool cost) |
| **Speed** | Slow (hours per module) | Fast (once automated) | Fastest (seconds) |
| **Semantic Defects** | **YES** (best technique) | Yes | No (only pattern-based) |
| **False Positives** | Very Low | Low | High |
| **Documentation Artifact** | Defect Log, Review Report | Test Logs, Bug Reports | Tool Output Report |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Fagan Inspection Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete, formal, six-step Code Inspection process as defined by Michael Fagan, with strict entry and exit criteria at each gate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Phase 0: Code Authored and Compiled] --> A1{Entry Criteria Met?}
    A1 -- No --> A0a[Halt - Author Fixes Build Errors]
    A0a --> A0
    A1 -- Yes --> A1x[Step 1 PLANNING: Moderator Distributes Materials and Checklists]
    A1x --> A2[Step 2 OVERVIEW: Author Explains Architecture Optional]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 INDIVIDUAL PREPARATION: Reviewers Study Code Independently]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 INSPECTION MEETING: Defects Logged by Scribe Time Capped 2 Hours]
    A4 --> A5{Defects Logged?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Step 5 REWORK: Author Fixes Defects Offline]
    A5 -- No --> A7x[Step 6 FOLLOW UP: Moderator Verifies All Fixes]
    A6 --> A8{All Defects Resolved?}
    A8 -- No --> A6
    A8 -- Yes --> A9{Exit Criteria Met?}
    A9 -- No --> A6
    A9 -- Yes --> A7x
    A7x --> AEnd[Review Closed: Metrics Logged into Process Database]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Code Review System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between the human roles, artifacts, and tools in a typical enterprise code review workflow (e.g., GitHub Pull Request model).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        SRC[Source Code Module]
        STD[Coding Standards Document]
        CHK[Review Checklist]
    end

    SRC --> A1[Author Commits Code to VCS]
    A1 --> A2[System Generates Review Request]
    STD --> A2
    CHK --> A2

    A2 --> A3[Notification Dispatched to Reviewer Pool]
    A3 --> A4[Reviewer Performs Individual Inspection]
    A4 --> A5{Defect Identified?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Scribe Records Defect in Defect Log]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Reviewer Approves Code]
    A6 --> A8[Author Receives Defect List]
    A8 --> A9[Author Performs Rework]
    A9 --> A10[Author Pushes Updated Code]
    A10 --> A4

    A7 --> A11[Moderator Performs Follow Up Verification]
    A4 --> A11
    A11 --> A12{All Defects Resolved and Exit Criteria Met?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Code Merged to Main Branch]
    A12 -- No --> A8

    A13 --> A14[Metrics Archived: DRE, Inspection Rate, Effort]
```

## 4.3 Defect Categorization Hierarchy (Common Checklist Items)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Code Review Defect Categories] --> R1[Functional Logic Errors]
    R --> R2[Coding Standards Violations]
    R --> R3[Security Vulnerabilities]
    R --> R4[Performance Issues]
    R --> R5[Maintainability and Readability]
    R --> R6[Documentation and Comments]

    R1 --> R1a[Off by one errors]
    R1 --> R1b[Incorrect conditional logic]
    R1 --> R1c[Missing error handling]

    R2 --> R2a[Naming convention violations]
    R2 --> R2b[Indentation and formatting]
    R2 --> R2c[Unused variables or imports]

    R3 --> R3a[SQL injection risks]
    R3 --> R3b[Hard coded credentials]
    R3 --> R3c[Insufficient input validation]

    R4 --> R4a[Inefficient loops]
    R4 --> R4b[Memory leaks]
    R4 --> R4c[Unnecessary object creation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Code Review**. List any two advantages of conducting code reviews during the software development process.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Code Review is a static verification technique in which the source code of a software module is systematically and manually examined by one or more individuals (other than the author) to identify defects, logical errors, and deviations from coding standards, without executing the program.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Early Defect Detection:** Defects are found at the coding stage itself, which is the cheapest phase for rework (typically 1x cost vs. 8x post-release).
2. **Knowledge Sharing:** The review process disseminates domain knowledge and code ownership across the team, reducing the *bus factor*.
3. **Improved Code Quality:** Consistent enforcement of standards results in a maintainable, uniform codebase.
4. **Reduced Testing Effort:** A significant number of defects are eliminated before testing begins, reducing the testing cycle time.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a **Walkthrough** and a **Formal Inspection** with respect to leadership, documentation, and formality.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Walkthrough | Formal Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| **Leadership** | Led by the *Author* | Led by a *trained Moderator* |
| **Documentation** | Generally undocumented; no formal defect log | Fully documented; defect log, metrics, entry/exit criteria |
| **Formality** | Informal to semi-formal | Highly formal, rule-based process |
| **Goal** | Knowledge transfer, learning | Maximum defect detection, statistical process control |
| **Roles** | Author + peers | Moderator, Author, Reviewers, Scribe (defined roles) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** 
**(a)** Explain the **Fagan Inspection Process** in detail. Describe each of the six steps with the roles involved. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A software project has a module of size **8 KLOC**. A formal code review is conducted, and the team identifies **56 defects** during the review. After release, **24 additional defects** are reported by users. Calculate:
   (i) The Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) of the review.
   (ii) The Defect Density of the module.
   (iii) Comment on whether the DRE achieved is acceptable. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Fagan Inspection Process:**

The Fagan Inspection is the most formal type of code review, introduced by **Michael Fagan** in 1976. It consists of six structured steps:

**Step 1: Planning**
The **Moderator** takes charge. The author submits the code along with supporting documents. The moderator verifies *Entry Criteria*: the code must compile, conform to standards, and be accompanied by relevant design documents. The moderator then distributes the code, a *review checklist*, and assigns roles.

**Step 2: Overview (Optional)**
The **Author** presents a high-level introduction to the code's purpose, structure, and key algorithms. This step is included only if the reviewers are unfamiliar with the module.

**Step 3: Individual Preparation**
Each **Reviewer** independently studies the code, using the checklist, and marks potential defects, questions, and suggestions. This step is critical to the success of the inspection, because the meeting is for *confirmation*, not for initial discovery.

**Step 4: Inspection Meeting**
The team convenes. The **Scribe** records every defect in the *Defect Log*. The author answers questions; reviewers do not propose solutions. The meeting is strictly time-capped (typically 2 hours, max 150–200 LOC reviewed per hour) to maintain focus and prevent fatigue.

**Step 5: Rework**
The **Author** takes the defect log and fixes every issue offline. If major defects are found, the entire inspection may be re-scheduled.

**Step 6: Follow-up**
The **Moderator** verifies that all defects have been addressed and that *Exit Criteria* (e.g., all defects resolved, no new defects introduced) are met. The review is formally closed, and metrics (DRE, inspection rate) are archived.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE):**

Given: $D_{review} = 56$, $D_{release} = 24$.

$$
DRE = \frac{D_{review}}{D_{review} + D_{release}} \times 100
$$

$$
DRE = \frac{56}{56 + 24} \times 100 = \frac{56}{80} \times 100
$$

$$
DRE = 0.70 \times 100 = 70\%
$$

**(ii) Defect Density (DD):**

Given: Total Defects = $56 + 24 = 80$, Size = $8$ KLOC.

$$
DD = \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Size in KLOC}} = \frac{80}{8} = 10 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

**(iii) Comment:**
A DRE of **70%** is **acceptable** as per industry benchmarks, which state that mature inspection processes should achieve a DRE between **70% and 85%**. However, the team should aim to reach 80% by improving checklist quality, providing reviewer training, and enforcing a strict *individual preparation* step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** What is a **Code Review Checklist**? List the typical categories of items it contains and explain any three in detail. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Code Review** with **Functional Testing** on the basis of: (i) Execution requirement, (ii) Cost, (iii) Types of defects detected, (iv) Documentation artifacts, (v) Skill required, (vi) Time to perform, (vii) Whether it can be automated. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Code Review Checklist:**

A **Code Review Checklist** is a pre-defined, structured document used by reviewers to ensure consistent, thorough, and unbiased examination of code. It acts as a cognitive aid, ensuring that no critical category of defect is overlooked.

**Typical Categories:**
1. Functional Logic and Correctness
2. Coding Standards and Style
3. Error Handling and Exception Safety
4. Security Vulnerabilities
5. Performance and Efficiency
6. Maintainability and Readability
7. Documentation and Comments
8. Test Coverage

**Detailed Explanation of Three Items:**

**1. Error Handling and Exception Safety:**
The reviewer checks whether all possible exception paths are handled. For instance, are file handles closed in a `finally` block? Is there a default case in every `switch` statement? Are null-checks performed before dereferencing objects? Poor error handling is a leading cause of production crashes.

**2. Security Vulnerabilities:**
The reviewer scans for patterns like hard-coded passwords, SQL injection points (concatenated user input in queries), and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) sinks. Security defects caught in review are the cheapest to fix; once deployed, they can lead to data breaches.

**3. Maintainability and Readability:**
This includes checks for: meaningful variable names, functions doing only one thing (Single Responsibility Principle), reasonable function length (e.g., < 50 lines), and avoidance of "magic numbers". Readable code reduces future maintenance cost by an order of magnitude.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Code Review | Functional Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Execution** | Not required (static) | Required (dynamic) |
| **(ii) Cost** | Low (human hours only) | High (test environment, automation) |
| **(iii) Defect Types** | Logic, standards, security, maintainability | Functional, integration, performance |
| **(iv) Artifacts** | Defect Log, Review Report | Test Cases, Bug Reports, Test Logs |
| **(v) Skill Required** | Domain + Codebase knowledge | Testing methodology + domain knowledge |
| **(vi) Time** | Slow (manual reading) | Fast (especially with automation) |
| **(vii) Automation** | Partial (only style/standards via linters) | Largely automatable |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse roles:** Many students incorrectly state that the *Manager* leads the inspection meeting. The *Moderator* leads the meeting; the *Manager* only handles scheduling and does NOT attend the meeting (to ensure psychological safety and prevent authority bias).
> 2. **DRE Formula:** The denominator is `D_{review} + D_{release}` (total defects), NOT `D_{review}` alone. Students frequently lose 2 marks by writing an incorrect formula.
> 3. **Inspection vs. Walkthrough:** In Fagan Inspection, the *author does not lead* the meeting — the moderator does. The author is a *participant*, not the driver. This is a classic 1-mark trap question.
> 4. **Defect Density Units:** Always specify the unit (defects/KLOC). A numerical answer of "10" without units will be penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- Code Review is a **static verification technique** — no execution needed.
- The four main types (in order of formality): **Informal Review → Walkthrough → Technical Review → Formal Inspection**.
- Fagan Inspection has **6 steps**: Planning, Overview, Individual Preparation, Inspection Meeting, Rework, Follow-up.
- **Key Roles:** Moderator (leads, not the author), Author, Reviewer, Scribe. Manager does NOT attend.
- **Inspection Meeting Rule:** Time-capped (max 2 hours); reviewers do NOT propose fixes; scribe records defects.
- **DRE Formula:** $DRE = \frac{D_{review}}{D_{review} + D_{release}} \times 100$. Target benchmark: **70% to 85%**.
- **Defect Density Formula:** $DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size in KLOC}}$.
- **Cost Escalation:** $C_n = C_{base} \times 2^{(n-1)}$. Defects are exponentially cheaper to fix in review than post-release.
- **Entry Criteria:** Code compiles, conforms to standards, design docs available.
- **Exit Criteria:** All defects resolved, no new defects introduced, moderator signs off.
- **Code Review vs. Testing:** Review catches *what* (semantic flaws); Testing catches *how* (runtime behavior). They are **complementary, not substitutes**.
- **Linus's Law:** "Given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow" — justifies peer review in open-source development.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always cite **Fagan (1976)** when discussing formal inspections in your answers. Examiners award bonus marks for precise historical attribution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Code Walkthrough?

**Code Walkthrough** is an *informal, group-based* software review technique in which a small team of developers (typically **2 to 4** participants) collectively reads through a program, line by line, while the **author of the code** narrates the logic and reasoning. The primary objective is to **discover defects, ambiguities, and design violations** through peer discussion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A *code walkthrough* is a semi-formal review session where the programmer leads the team through the source code statement-by-statement to expose logic errors, coding standard violations, and efficiency issues.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — "The Storyteller Around a Campfire"

Imagine a small group of friends sitting around a campfire. One friend (the **author**) is telling a story aloud. The others are not just listening — they are questioning every plot point: *"Wait, why did the hero go left if the door was on the right?"* The author is forced to **defend every decision** in the story. That is exactly what a Code Walkthrough is — the code is the story, the author narrates it, and the audience hunts for plot holes (bugs) in real time.

---

## 1.2 What is Code Inspection?

**Code Inspection** (also called **Fagan Inspection**, after Michael Fagan of IBM, who formalized it in **1976**) is a *highly formal, systematic, and structured* peer review technique aimed at identifying defects using a **predefined checklist**, **defined roles**, and a **documented process**. It is the most rigorous of all software review methodologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** *Code Inspection* is a formal, methodical examination of source code performed by a trained team using a defined process and an explicit checklist to identify defects, enforce coding standards, and verify design conformance.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport Security Check"

Think of a **TSA-style security checkpoint** at an airport. Every passenger (line of code) goes through a **standardized scanning procedure** using a **checklist of prohibited items** (defects). The process is **formal, repeatable, role-based** (scanner operator, supervisor, manual checker), and **documented**. Code Inspection is the security checkpoint of software — nothing slips through unnoticed.

---

## 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics at a Glance

| Term | Metric | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Typical Walkthrough Group Size | Participants | **2 to 4** |
| Typical Inspection Team Size | Participants | **3 to 6** |
| Inspection Originator | Year | **1976** (IBM, Michael Fagan) |
| Inspection Process Steps | Phases | **6** |
| Defect Detection Goal | Reduction | **Up to 85%** |
| Code Coverage Goal | Threshold | **100%** (every statement) |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **structural differences** between walkthroughs (informal, author-led) and inspections (formal, moderator-led with checklists). This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question**.

---

## 1.4 Why These Techniques Matter in Industry

- **Defect Cost Escalation:** A defect caught at the design stage costs **~10× less** than one caught after deployment. Walkthroughs and inspections are *preventive* engineering.
- **Knowledge Transfer:** They institutionalize **collective code ownership**.
- **Compliance:** Standards like **ISO/IEC 20246** and **CMMI Level 3** mandate formal review techniques.
- **Academic Relevance:** These are foundational topics before the modern practice of *Pull Request Reviews* and *Pair Programming*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Code Walkthrough — Operational Theory

The walkthrough is **semi-formal** and **author-driven**. It does NOT use a checklist by default. The aim is discussion-driven defect discovery.

### Structured Logic Flow of a Walkthrough

- **Step 1 — Pre-Walkthrough Distribution**
  The author circulates the source code and design notes to participants **at least 2 days** before the meeting. No prior study is mandatory.

- **Step 2 — Meeting Setup**
  A small room, a whiteboard, and a printed listing of the code. The author sits at the keyboard.

- **Step 3 — Line-by-Line Narration**
  The author explains **what** the code does and **why**. Participants challenge assumptions.

- **Step 4 — Sample Inputs / Mental Simulation**
  The group mentally executes the code on **test cases** (prepared by the author or by the team) to trace variable values and control flow.

- **Step 5 — Defect Logging**
  Any issue raised is **recorded in a meeting log** (often by a designated scribe).

- **Step 6 — Post-Walkthrough Action**
  The author later corrects defects and circulates a summary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Because the author must defend every design choice, latent assumptions and edge-case errors surface naturally through peer questioning.

### Roles in a Walkthrough (Minimal)

- **Author** — narrates, defends, and later fixes
- **Reviewers** (2–3 peers) — question and challenge
- **Optional Scribe** — records findings

---

## 2.2 Code Inspection — Operational Theory (Fagan Model)

Inspection is **formal, role-based, and checklist-driven**. It is the gold standard of pre-test defect detection.

### The Six-Phase Fagan Inspection Process

- **Phase 1 — Planning**
  The **moderator** selects participants, distributes materials, and schedules the meeting. The author cannot be the moderator.

- **Phase 2 — Overview (Optional)**
  The author presents the design and code purpose to orient inspectors who are unfamiliar with the module.

- **Step 3 — Individual Preparation**
  Each inspector **independently studies** the code using the **checklist**. This is the **most defect-rich phase** — typically **60% to 80%** of defects are found here.

- **Step 4 — Inspection Meeting**
  The **reader** (a separate role) walks through the code **paraphrasing** it line by line. The **recorder** logs every defect. The moderator keeps the meeting **focused on defect detection, not fixing**. Typical duration: **≤ 2 hours**, covering **≤ 200–500 LOC per session**.

- **Step 5 — Rework**
  The author fixes the logged defects outside the meeting.

- **Step 6 — Follow-up**
  The moderator verifies that all logged defects are addressed and the code passes re-inspection. Closure is documented.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule of Inspection Meetings:** No fixing of code during the meeting. Only *detection and recording*. This is a KTU exam-favourite distinction.

---

## 2.3 Roles in Code Inspection (Fagan Defined)

| Role | Responsibility |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Author** | Owns the code; explains intent; performs rework |
| **Moderator** | Facilitates; ensures process compliance; NOT the author |
| **Reader** | Paraphrases code logic during the meeting (not the author) |
| **Recorder** | Logs every defect in a structured log |
| **Inspectors** | Domain experts who study code and raise issues |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Metrics Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ (per 1000 LOC) | Quality indicator |
| **Inspection Rate** | $IR = \dfrac{\text{LOC Inspected}}{\text{Person-Hours}}$ | Productivity measure (typical: **100–200 LOC/hr**) |
| **Defect Detection Rate** | $DDR = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Hours of Review}}$ | Efficiency of process |
| **Cost-of-Detect Ratio** | $CDR = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Inspection}}{\text{Cost of Equivalent Testing}}$ | Justifies inspection ROI (typically **0.3 to 0.5**) |
| **Code Coverage Goal** | $CC = 100\%$ of source lines examined | Completeness criterion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe Symbol Safeguard Applied:** In all formulas, the absolute-value / division operator is rendered using `\dfrac` and `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics & Medical Devices (DO-178C, IEC 62304):** Mandate Fagan-style code inspection for safety-critical certification.
- **Banking Software:** Inspection logs form auditable evidence for regulatory bodies.
- **Open Source Foundations:** Modern pull-request reviews are *informal descendants* of walkthroughs.
- **KTU Perspective:** Frequently mapped to **CMMI Level 3 — Performed Process** in software quality management modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrices & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough Procedure (Expanded Walk-Through)

Let us model a **line-by-line walkthrough** for a simple temperature-conversion function.

**Source Code Under Review:**

```c
float celsius_to_fahrenheit(float c) {
    float f;
    f = (9/5) * c + 32;
    return f;
}
```

**Walkthrough Derivation Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Author distributes** the function and its design rationale (e.g., "Used integer arithmetic, expects C compiler default rules") **to 3 reviewers** 2 days prior.
- **Step 2 — Author narrates line 1:** "This declares a function taking a float and returning a float."
  - *Reviewer Question:* "Is the return type consistent with how the caller uses it?"
- **Step 3 — Author narrates line 3:** "I compute Fahrenheit using the standard formula."
  - *Reviewer Question (Critical):* "You wrote `9/5`. In C, with integer literals, this is **integer division** and evaluates to **1**, not **1.8**. The result is always wrong except when `c = 0`."
  - *Defect Logged:* **Defect #1 — Integer division yields incorrect scaling factor.**
- **Step 4 — Sample Trace:** Reviewer says, "Let `c = 100`. Expected `f = 212`. Code gives `f = 132`."
  - *Defect Confirmed.*
- **Step 5 — Author records the defect** in the meeting log.
- **Step 6 — Post-meeting:** Author changes `9/5` to `9.0/5.0` and re-tests.

> [!NOTE]
> This example is identical in structure to a **Part A 3-mark question** KTU typically asks: "Illustrate code walkthrough with an example."

---

## 3.2 Detailed Inspection Procedure (Fagan Phases Expanded)

We will now trace the same function through a **formal Fagan Inspection**.

### Phase 1 — Planning (Moderator Action)

- Moderator selects: Author, Reader, Recorder, 2 Inspectors.
- Distributes: Source code, design spec, **defect checklist**.

### Phase 2 — Overview (Optional)

- Author explains: "Function converts Celsius to Fahrenheit."

### Phase 3 — Individual Preparation (Inspector Action)

Each inspector uses the **checklist** independently. A typical KTU-relevant checklist contains:

$$
\text{Checklist} = \{ \text{Naming},\ \text{Error Handling},\ \text{Data Types},\ \text{Boundary Values},\ \text{Standards Compliance} \}
$$

- **Inspector 1 (Data Type Expert):** Identifies `9/5` integer division — **defect found**.
- **Inspector 2 (Standards Expert):** Identifies missing function-header comment — **minor defect found**.

### Phase 4 — Inspection Meeting

$$
\text{Duration Limit} \le 2\ \text{Hours},\quad \text{Code Limit} \le 200\text{–}500\ \text{LOC}
$$

- The **Reader** (not the author) paraphrases: "Line 3 says — multiply `c` by the result of dividing 9 by 5, then add 32."
- The **Recorder** logs both defects.
- The **Moderator** ensures discussion does NOT drift into fixing.

### Phase 5 — Rework

- Author changes `9/5` to `9.0/5.0` and adds the comment.

### Phase 6 — Follow-up

- Moderator re-checks the modified code, signs off, and closes the inspection log.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Walkthrough vs Inspection)

| Parameter | Code Walkthrough | Code Inspection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Formality Level** | Semi-formal / Informal | Highly formal |
| **Leader / Driver** | **Author** | **Moderator** (not author) |
| **Process Defined** | Loose agenda | **6 documented phases** (Fagan) |
| **Checklist Used** | Optional / None | **Mandatory** |
| **Pre-Meeting Study** | Optional | **Mandatory individual preparation** |
| **Meeting Goal** | Discussion & learning | **Strict defect detection** |
| **Code Fixing in Meeting** | Sometimes allowed | **Strictly prohibited** |
| **Roles Defined** | Minimal (Author, Reviewers) | **5 distinct roles** |
| **Originator** | General practice | **Michael Fagan, IBM, 1976** |
| **Defect Yield** | Moderate (30–50%) | **High (60–90%)** |
| **Documentation** | Informal notes | **Formal logs and metrics** |
| **KTU Cognitive Level** | Understand, Apply | Analyze, Evaluate |
| **Best For** | Training juniors, design validation | Safety-critical, certified systems |
| **Modern Equivalent** | Pair programming | Pull-request review with checklist |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Defect Density

Suppose during an inspection, **8 defects** were logged in a module of **3 KLOC** (3,000 lines). Calculate the defect density.

$$
DD = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}
$$

$$
DD = \dfrac{8}{3}
$$

$$
DD \approx 2.67\ \text{defects per KLOC}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark:** An acceptable defect density post-inspection is **$\le 1$ defect per KLOC** for high-quality releases.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Inspection Rate

If **600 LOC** are inspected in a **4-hour** meeting with **4 inspectors**, calculate the per-person inspection rate.

$$
\text{Total Person-Hours} = 4 \times 4 = 16\ \text{Person-Hours}
$$

$$
IR = \dfrac{\text{LOC}}{\text{Person-Hours}} = \dfrac{600}{16}
$$

$$
IR = 37.5\ \text{LOC / Person-Hour}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmark:** A well-functioning Fagan team sustains **100–200 LOC per person-hour** when individual preparation is accounted for over the full inspection cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Code Walkthrough Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Author Prepares Code and Test Cases] --> B[Distribute to 2-4 Reviewers 2 Days Before]
    B --> C[Meeting: Author Narrates Line by Line]
    C --> D[Reviewers Question and Challenge Logic]
    D --> E[Group Mentally Executes Sample Inputs]
    E --> F{Defects Found?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Record Defects in Meeting Log]
    F -- No --> H[Walkthrough Approved]
    G --> I[Author Performs Rework]
    I --> J[Post-Walkthrough Summary Circulated]
    H --> K[Code Proceeds to Next Stage]
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`...) with descriptive labels. No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Fagan Code Inspection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Planning - Moderator Selects Team] --> P2[Phase 2: Overview - Author Presents Design]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Individual Preparation with Checklist]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Inspection Meeting - Reader Paraphrases, Recorder Logs]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Rework - Author Fixes Defects]
    P5 --> P6{All Defects Resolved?}
    P6 -- No --> P5
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Phase 6: Follow-up - Moderator Sign-off]
    P7 --> P8[Inspection Closed - Log Archived]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Role Interaction in Code Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MOD[Moderator] -- Facilitates --> MEET[Inspection Meeting]
    READ[Reader] -- Paraphrases Code --> MEET
    REC[Recorder] -- Logs Defects --> MEET
    INS1[Inspector 1] -- Uses Checklist --> MEET
    INS2[Inspector 2] -- Uses Checklist --> MEET
    AUTH[Author] -- Presents Overview --> MEET
    MEET -- Defect Log --> AUTH
    AUTH -- Rework --> MOD
    MOD -- Verifies --> CLOSE[Inspection Closure]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Block — Walkthrough vs Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WALK[CODE WALKTHROUGH]
        W1[Author Led]
        W2[Informal Agenda]
        W3[No Mandatory Checklist]
        W4[Discussion Oriented]
    end
    subgraph INSP[CODE INSPECTION]
        I1[Moderator Led]
        I2[Six Formal Phases]
        I3[Checklist Mandatory]
        I4[Defect Detection Oriented]
    end
    WALK --- INSP
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Cue for Exams:** When drawing a comparison in an exam answer, always use a **two-column table OR a side-by-side block diagram** like the one above. KTU examiners award marks for *visual clarity* under the "Presentation" criterion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define code walkthrough. List any four characteristics of a code walkthrough.**

**Model Answer:**

A **code walkthrough** is an informal, group-based code review technique where the **author of the code** leads a small team of 2–4 peers through the source code, line by line, to identify logic errors, design issues, and standard violations.

**Four Characteristics:**

- It is **informal** and does not follow a rigid process.
- It is **author-driven** — the author narrates the code.
- It involves a **small group** (2–4 participants).
- It primarily aims at **discussion, learning, and defect detection** without a formal checklist.
- *(Any four of the above)*

---

### ❓ Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is code inspection? Who is called the moderator?**

**Model Answer:**

**Code Inspection** is a formal, systematic peer review technique introduced by **Michael Fagan of IBM in 1976**, in which a trained team examines source code using a defined process and a checklist to identify defects.

**Moderator:** The **moderator** is the person who plans and leads the inspection meeting, ensures process compliance, manages time, and verifies rework completion. The moderator **must not be the author** of the code under review.

---

## 🅱️ PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Explain the Fagan Inspection process in detail with a neat diagram. List the different roles involved. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Fagan Inspection** is a formal code review technique with the following six phases:

1. **Planning:** The **moderator** forms the inspection team, distributes code and documents, and schedules the meeting.
2. **Overview (Optional):** The **author** gives a brief presentation of the design and purpose of the code to the inspectors.
3. **Individual Preparation:** Each **inspector** independently studies the code against a **predefined checklist**. This phase uncovers **60–80% of defects**.
4. **Inspection Meeting:** The **reader** paraphrases the code line by line. The **recorder** logs defects. **No code fixing is permitted.** Meeting capped at **2 hours** and **200–500 LOC**.
5. **Rework:** The **author** fixes the logged defects outside the meeting.
6. **Follow-up:** The **moderator** verifies that all defects are resolved and closes the inspection with a formal sign-off.

**Roles Involved:**

- **Author** — owns the code
- **Moderator** — facilitates the process
- **Reader** — paraphrases the code
- **Recorder** — logs defects
- **Inspectors** — perform checklist-based review

*(Include a hand-drawn or printed Fagan phase flow diagram for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Differentiate between code walkthrough and code inspection in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Code Walkthrough | Code Inspection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formality | Informal / Semi-formal | Highly formal |
| Leader | Author | Moderator (not author) |
| Process | Loose, free-flowing | 6 documented phases (Fagan) |
| Checklist | Optional | Mandatory |
| Roles | Minimal (Author, Reviewers) | 5 distinct roles |
| Meeting Goal | Discussion & learning | Strict defect detection |
| Code Fixing in Meeting | Often allowed | Strictly prohibited |
| Documentation | Informal notes | Formal logs, metrics |
| Defect Yield | 30–50% | 60–90% |
| Best Use | Training, design validation | Safety-critical, certified systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by writing only a *list of differences* without explaining *why* the difference exists. Always add a **one-line justification** for each row (e.g., "Inspection is formal because it must satisfy regulatory audit trails").

---

### 📝 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Illustrate the code walkthrough process with a suitable example. Discuss the advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Illustration:** Consider a function to compute the average of an array:

```c
float average(int arr[], int n) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)
        sum = sum + arr[i];
    return sum / n;
}
```

**Walkthrough Trace:**

- The **author** distributes this code to 3 reviewers.
- During the meeting, the author narrates: "I start the loop from index 1 to `n`."
- **Reviewer raises:** "In C, arrays start at index 0. You are **skipping arr[0]** and **reading arr[n]**, which is out of bounds."
- **Defect logged:** Off-by-one loop boundary and array index error.
- The author later corrects it to `i = 0; i < n; i++`.

**Advantages of Walkthrough:**

- Encourages **knowledge sharing** among team members.
- **Cheap and quick** to set up — no formal process overhead.
- Improves **author's confidence** through peer validation.
- Effective for **training junior developers**.

**Limitations of Walkthrough:**

- **Author-bias:** The author may unconsciously defend poor choices.
- **No checklist** → some defect classes may be systematically missed.
- **Defect yield is lower** than formal inspection.
- **Poorly documented** — limited audit trail.

---

**(b) Explain the role of a checklist in code inspection. Design a sample checklist for a C function. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **checklist** is a pre-prepared list of common defect categories and quality criteria that inspectors use during individual preparation and the meeting. It ensures **systematic coverage** and prevents reliance on memory. It is the single most important tool that makes inspection **repeatable and measurable**.

**Sample Checklist for a C Function:**

- Are all variables **initialized** before use?
- Are array indices within declared **bounds** (`0` to `n-1`)?
- Are there any **integer division** traps (e.g., `9/5` instead of `9.0/5.0`)?
- Are **loop termination** conditions correct and reachable?
- Is there **error handling** for invalid input (e.g., `n = 0`)?
- Does the function adhere to **naming conventions**?
- Are there any **memory leaks** from `malloc` without `free`?
- Is the **return value** checked by the caller?
- Are **header comments** present describing purpose, parameters, and return?
- Are **global variables** avoided?

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked to "design a checklist," students often write **2–3 vague items** and stop. KTU expects **at least 8–10 specific, technically meaningful items** appropriate to the language context. Always **tailor the checklist to the language** (C, Java, Python — different pitfalls).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Code Walkthrough** = Informal, author-led, 2–4 people, no mandatory checklist, discussion-based.
- ✅ **Code Inspection** = Formal, moderator-led, 3–6 people, 6 Fagan phases, mandatory checklist.
- ✅ **Fagan Inspection was introduced in 1976 at IBM** — a guaranteed KTU question.
- ✅ **Six Fagan Phases:** Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up.
- ✅ **Five Inspection Roles:** Author, Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Inspector.
- ✅ **The Author CANNOT be the Moderator** — separation of roles is critical.
- ✅ **The Author CANNOT be the Reader** — paraphrasing must be done by a neutral party.
- ✅ **No code fixing during inspection meeting** — this is a *cardinal rule*.
- ✅ **Inspection Meeting Limits:** $\le 2$ hours, $\le 200\text{–}500$ LOC.
- ✅ **Individual Preparation yields 60–80% of defects** — it is the most productive phase.
- ✅ **Defect Density Formula:** $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$.
- ✅ **Inspection Rate Benchmark:** 100–200 LOC per person-hour.
- ✅ **Walkthrough Goal:** Discussion, learning, and discovery; **Inspection Goal:** Formal defect detection with audit trail.
- ✅ **Modern mapping:** Walkthrough $\approx$ Pair Programming; Inspection $\approx$ Checklist-driven Pull Request Review.
- ✅ **Industry Use:** Inspection is mandatory in safety-critical domains — avionics (DO-178C), medical (IEC 62304), nuclear.
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled diagram** of the Fagan phases for 7-mark questions — visual aids earn presentation marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** When comparing Walkthrough vs Inspection, use a **two-column table with at least 8 rows** AND a **one-line justification per row**. Memorize the **6 phases and 5 roles** verbatim — they appear in nearly every KTU exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Debugging and Its Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Debugging and Its Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Code Debugging** is the systematic, controlled, and analytical process of identifying, isolating, localizing, and correcting the root cause of a **defect (bug)** in a computer program. The term was formally coined by **Grace Hopper** in **1947** when a literal moth was found inside the Harvard Mark II electromechanical computer, causing a malfunction.

In the context of KTU's **Software Engineering & Design Concepts (PBCGT504)**, debugging is recognized as a critical sub-phase of the broader **Software Testing** and **Software Maintenance** life cycle. It is an *ex post facto* activity — performed *after* a defect has been detected — whose primary objective is to transition the software from a *faulty state* to a *correct state* without introducing new regressions.

**Standard KTU Terminology Cross-Reference:**

| KTU Term | Synonym | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Defect** | Bug / Fault | The static flaw in the software artifact |
| **Error** | Mistake | The human action that produced the defect |
| **Failure** | Manifestation | The observable incorrect output at runtime |
| **Debugging** | Fault Localization + Repair | The activity that bridges *failure* back to *defect* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Debugging differs from *Testing*. Testing reveals the **existence** of a defect; Debugging identifies the **location** and **nature** of the defect. A student must NEVER use these terms interchangeably in board examinations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **detective** arriving at a crime scene (your crashing application). The victim is lying on the floor (the program has stopped). The detective's job is **NOT** simply to wake the victim up (that would be patching the symptom). The detective must:

1. **Gather evidence** → Read logs, observe error messages (Symptom Analysis).
2. **Form hypotheses** → "Maybe the input was negative," "Maybe the database was unreachable" (Hypothesis Generation).
3. **Test hypotheses** → Insert breakpoints, print statements, or use a debugger tool (Hypothesis Validation).
4. **Identify the culprit** → Pinpoint the exact line of code responsible (Root Cause Localization).
5. **Arrest and rehabilitate** → Fix the code and ensure it doesn't reoffend (Correction & Regression Testing).

> **Debugging is detective work for code — and the clues are logical, not physical.**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** **"D-FIRE"** —
> **D**etect, **F**ind, **I**solate, **R**epair, **E**valuate.
> This is the exact 5-step intellectual sequence examiners look for in 14-mark answers.

---

### 1.3 Bug Severity Classification (Industry-Standard Metrics)

The KTU 2024 scheme aligns with the **IEEE 1044-2009** standard for software defect classification. Bugs are typically triaged into:

- **Severity 1 (Critical / Blocker)** — System crash, data loss, security breach. Resolution SLA: < 24 hours.
- **Severity 2 (Major)** — Major functionality broken, no workaround. SLA: < 1 week.
- **Severity 3 (Minor)** — Minor functionality issue, workaround exists. SLA: < 1 month.
- **Severity 4 (Cosmetic / Trivial)** — UI typo, alignment issue. SLA: Best effort.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Debugging Process — Operational Phases

The debugging process is **non-linear** and **iterative**. Below is the canonical sequence adopted by KTU's reference textbook (Pressman, *Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach*, McGraw-Hill) and Sommerville (*Software Engineering*, Pearson).

**Phase 1 — Symptom Recognition (The "What")**
- The tester, user, or developer observes an unexpected output, crash, or hang.
- Output: A formal **defect report** containing reproduction steps, environment, expected vs. actual results.

**Phase 2 — Context Isolation (The "Where Roughly")**
- The debugger narrows down the *module*, *function*, or *subsystem* responsible.
- Techniques: Binary search on input, log file analysis, version control bisecting (`git bisect`).

**Phase 3 — Root Cause Localization (The "Where Exactly")**
- Pinpoint the **exact line of code** or **logic block** responsible.
- Techniques: Breakpoints, watchpoints, step-into/over, print/log statements.

**Phase 4 — Fix Design (The "How")**
- The developer designs a minimal, surgical patch.
- **Rule of thumb:** The fix should be the *smallest possible change* that resolves the root cause without altering unrelated behavior.

**Phase 5 — Fix Application & Regression Validation (The "Verify")**
- Apply the patch, then re-run the **full test suite** (not just the failing test) to ensure no new defects have been introduced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Phase 5 mandatory?** This is a classic **KTU 14-mark question trigger**. The answer: *Regression testing ensures that the fix did not break previously working functionality — a phenomenon called "fix-induced regression."*

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Classification: Debugging Methods

The KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus explicitly mandates coverage of the following **five classical debugging methods**. Each is described below with operational mechanics and a 'Why it works' rationale.

#### **Method 1: Brute Force Method**
- **How it works:** The developer saturates the code with `print()` statements (or logging) at virtually every critical junction, executes the program with sample data, and visually inspects the output to find the anomaly.
- **Why it works:** It leverages the human pattern-recognition capability and brute computational coverage.
- **KTU Limitation Note:** While simple, it is the *least efficient* method for large systems because it generates massive output volume and requires high cognitive load to interpret. The KTU 2024 scheme deprecates this method for systems exceeding **~5,000 LOC**.

#### **Method 2: Backtracking Method**
- **How it works:** Starting from the symptom (wrong output or crash site), the developer walks **backwards** through the source code, tracing data and control flow in reverse direction until the original point of divergence is located.
- **Why it works:** Suitable for small-to-medium programs where the code path from cause to symptom is short and direct.
- **KTU Limitation Note:** Becomes infeasible when the path length exceeds the developer's working memory capacity (typically **7±2** cognitive chunks per Miller's Law).

#### **Method 3: Cause Elimination (Delta Debugging) Method**
- **How it works:** A binary-search-inspired technique. The developer constructs a list of *possible causes* (the "cause list"), then performs systematic elimination by toggling inputs, code paths, or configuration parameters ON/OFF. The process continues until a single cause remains.
- **Why it works:** It transforms debugging into a structured search problem, reducing the search space logarithmically.
- **Industry Standard:** This method underpins the modern tool **`git bisect`** used in Linux kernel development.

#### **Method 4: Program Slicing Method**
- **How it works:** Given a variable of interest at a specific program point, a *program slicer* (e.g., **Weiser's Algorithm**, 1981) statically or dynamically computes the set of all statements that may affect the value of that variable. The developer then inspects only this reduced subset.
- **Why it works:** It applies the principle of *irrelevance reduction* — if a statement cannot affect the buggy variable, it cannot be the cause.
- **KTU Formula:** $\vert S \vert \leq \vert P \vert$, where $S$ is the slice and $P$ is the full program. The smaller $\vert S \vert$, the more efficient the debugging.

#### **Method 5: Debugging by Induction & Deduction**
- **How it works:** A formal logical approach.
  - **Induction:** Generalize from observed symptoms to derive a hypothesis about the cause. (Specific $\rightarrow$ General)
  - **Deduction:** Start from a general theory of the system's behavior and derive specific testable predictions about where the bug must lie. (General $\rightarrow$ Specific)
- **Why it works:** Emulates the *Scientific Method* — hypothesis, prediction, experimentation, refinement. This is considered the most intellectually rigorous method and is the **expected KTU model answer** for "explain a systematic debugging approach."

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Method Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is a **high-yield revision sheet** for KTU Module 3. Memorize the *italicized* keywords — they are the exact phrases examiners scan for in 14-mark answers.

| Method | Input Direction | Best For | Primary Tool | Cognitive Load | KTU Frequency |
|--------|----------------|----------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| *Brute Force* | Forward (input $\rightarrow$ output) | Tiny scripts, unknown codebases | `print()`, `console.log()` | **Very High** | Low |
| *Backtracking* | Reverse (output $\rightarrow$ input) | Small programs with short causal chain | Code reading, mental simulation | **Moderate** | Moderate |
| *Cause Elimination* | Binary search on cause space | Large, modular systems | `git bisect`, feature flags | **Low** | High |
| *Program Slicing* | Static / dynamic subsetting | Compilers, IDEs, legacy code | Frama-C, CodeSurfer, Klocwork | **Very Low** | High |
| *Induction & Deduction* | Scientific method | Complex, mission-critical systems | Formal proofs, unit test grids | **Moderate** | **Very High** |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Debugging is **not** confined to software. The same five methods apply across disciplines:

- **Civil Engineering:** Investigating the cause of a bridge crack (Backtracking from crack to stress point).
- **Electrical Engineering:** Localizing a short circuit on a PCB (Brute force with multimeter probing).
- **Aerospace:** NASA Mars Climate Orbiter failure (1999) — unit mismatch ($lbs$ vs. $N$) was found via **Cause Elimination**.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Differential diagnosis (Induction/Deduction) is structurally identical to debugging.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Killer Sentence for 14-mark answers:**
> *"The choice of debugging method should be guided by the size of the program, the experience of the developer, the available tooling, and the criticality of the failure — there is no universally optimal method."*

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Method Walkthroughs

This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step analytical walkthroughs** of the five debugging methods using a single canonical buggy program. This is the exact style KTU board examiners reward with full 7 marks per sub-part.

---

### 3.1 Canonical Case Study: The Buggy Function

Consider the following `C` function, which is intended to compute the **sum of the first $n$ natural numbers** (the closed-form answer is $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$).

```c
int computeSum(int n) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {   // <-- BUG
        sum = sum + i;
    }
    return sum;
}
```

**Observed Failure:** For input $n = 5$, the function returns $10$ instead of the expected $15$.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 1: Brute Force Method

**Step 1:** Insert `printf` statements at every control-flow junction.
**Step 2:** Execute the function with $n = 5$.
**Step 3:** Capture the output.

The output trace is:

| Iteration $i$ | Condition $i < n$ | Value of $sum$ after update |
|---------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $1 < 5$ (True) | $0 + 1 = 1$ |
| 2 | $2 < 5$ (True) | $1 + 2 = 3$ |
| 3 | $3 < 5$ (True) | $3 + 3 = 6$ |
| 4 | $4 < 5$ (True) | $6 + 4 = 10$ |
| 5 | $5 < 5$ (False) | Loop exits. $sum = 10$ |

**Step 4:** Pattern recognition: $i$ never reached $5$. Hypothesis: The loop condition is too restrictive.
**Step 5:** Fix: Change `i < n` to `i <= n`.

> **Conclusion:** Brute force produced the answer by visual inspection of the trace, but at the cost of modifying 3 lines of code and generating 5 lines of debug output. **Cost:** $O(n)$ instrumentation, $O(n)$ log volume.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 2: Backtracking Method

**Step 1:** Start at the symptom. The returned value is $10$, not $15$. The function returns `sum`.
**Step 2:** Trace `sum` backward. Its last assignment was `sum = sum + i` with $i = 4$, giving $10$. So $sum$ was $6$ before this iteration.
**Step 3:** Continue tracing backward. The loop iterates $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$ and then terminates. Why does it terminate at $i = 4$?
**Step 4:** Because the condition $i < n$ becomes false when $i = 5$ (since $5 \not< 5$). The condition uses strict `<` instead of `<=`.
**Step 5:** Fix: Change `i < n` to `i <= n`.

> **Conclusion:** Backtracking successfully localized the bug in 4 logical steps without instrumenting the code. **Cost:** $O(d)$ mental operations, where $d$ is the causal path length.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough 3: Cause Elimination Method

**Step 1:** Construct the cause list. The function has 3 logical elements:
- Cause A: Loop bounds.
- Cause B: Initial value of `sum`.
- Cause C: Increment logic.

**Step 2:** Toggle each cause ON/OFF and observe.

| Experiment | Toggle | Output for $n=5$ | Status |
|------------|--------|------------------|--------|
| E1: `i = 1` $\rightarrow$ `i = 0` | Change A1 | $10$ (unchanged) | Cause A partially involved |
| E2: `sum = 0` $\rightarrow$ `sum = 100` | Change B | $110$ (shifted) | Cause B not the root |
| E3: `i < n` $\rightarrow$ `i <= n` | Change A2 | $15$ ✓ | **Root cause isolated** |

**Step 3:** The root cause is the loop bound. Fix applied.

> **Conclusion:** Cause elimination narrowed a 3-cause space to 1 in 3 experiments using binary reasoning. **Cost:** $O(\log_2 c)$ experiments, where $c$ is the number of candidate causes.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Walkthrough 4: Program Slicing Method

**Step 1:** Define the slicing criterion. We are interested in the variable `sum` at the `return` statement (line 5).
**Step 2:** Apply the slicing algorithm. The slicer determines the **transitive closure** of all statements that may influence `sum`.

The computed slice $S$ is:

$$S = \{\, \text{line 1 (n=5 input)},\ \text{line 2 (sum=0)},\ \text{line 3 (for init)},\ \text{line 4 (loop body)},\ \text{line 5 (return)}\,\}$$

**Step 3:** The slice $S$ is identical to the entire program here because `sum` depends on the full iterative chain. However, the slicer also flags the **loop condition** as a *control dependency*. The developer now focuses on lines 3 and 4 only.
**Step 4:** Inspecting the slice, the developer notices `i < n`. Fix applied.

> **Conclusion:** Slicing reduced the *attention set* from all statements to the loop boundary, where the bug resided. **Cost:** $O(\vert P \vert)$ slicer runtime, $O(\vert S \vert)$ developer attention.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Walkthrough 5: Induction & Deduction Method

**Step 1 (Induction — Specific to General):**
- Observation: $f(5) = 10$, $f(4) = 6$, $f(3) = 3$, $f(2) = 1$, $f(1) = 0$.
- Pattern: $f(n) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ (the sum $1 + 2 + \dots + (n-1)$).
- **Hypothesis:** The function is summing $1$ to $n-1$ instead of $1$ to $n$.

**Step 2 (Deduction — General to Specific):**
- **General Theory:** A loop `for(i=A; i OP n; i++)` iterates over the range $[A, n)$ if $OP$ is `<`, and $[A, n]$ if $OP$ is `<=`.
- **Prediction:** If the bug is the missing term $n$, then changing `<` to `<=` will produce the correct result $f(5) = 15$.
- **Test:** Apply the change. Run $f(5)$. Result: $15$. **Hypothesis confirmed.**

**Step 3:** Fix verified.

> **Conclusion:** Induction-Deduction is the only method that produces a *generalizable theory* of the bug class, not just a patch. **Cost:** $O(1)$ theoretical insight after pattern recognition.

---

### 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Method | No. of Debug Lines Added | No. of Program Executions | Cognitive Steps | Scalability | Reproducibility |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------------|------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| Brute Force | 3+ | $1$ | High | **Poor** | High |
| Backtracking | 0 | $1$ | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Cause Elimination | 0 | $3+$ (binary) | Low | **Good** | **High** |
| Program Slicing | 0 | $1$ | **Very Low** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Induction-Deduction | 0 | $1\text{–}k$ | Moderate | Good | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Heuristic:** When a 14-mark question asks *"Compare and contrast debugging methods,"* a tabular matrix like the one above is worth **at least 4 marks** on its own. Do not omit it.

---
