## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below are rendered as **Mermaid block diagrams** to provide a visual architecture of the debugging process, the bug lifecycle, and the method-selection decision tree.

---

### 4.1 The Debugging Process — Top-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Failure Observed] --> B[Symptom Recognition]
    B --> C[Context Isolation]
    C --> D[Root Cause Localization]
    D --> E[Fix Design]
    E --> F[Fix Application]
    F --> G[Regression Testing]
    G --> H{Pass?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Defect Closed]
    H -- No --> D
    I --> J[Knowledge Base Update]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide:** The loop between `D` (Localization) and `G` (Regression) represents the iterative nature of debugging — a fix may *uncover* a deeper root cause, requiring the developer to return to step `D`.

---

### 4.2 Bug Lifecycle — From Injection to Closure

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Injected: Developer writes code
    Injected --> Dormant: Bug exists but no test triggers it
    Dormant --> Detected: Test case fails
    Detected --> Triaged: Severity and priority assigned
    Triaged --> Assigned: Routed to developer
    Assigned --> Localized: Root cause found
    Localized --> Patched: Fix applied
    Patched --> Verified: Regression suite passes
    Verified --> Closed: Defect formally closed
    Closed --> [*]

    Patched --> Reopened: New regression introduced
    Reopened --> Assigned
```

---

### 4.3 Debugging Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Defect Detected] --> Q1{Program Size?}
    Q1 -- Under 500 LOC --> Q2{Experienced Developer?}
    Q1 -- Over 500 LOC --> Q3{Criticality?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M1[Backtracking Method]
    Q2 -- No --> M2[Brute Force Method]
    Q3 -- Mission Critical --> M3[Induction Deduction Method]
    Q3 -- Standard --> Q4{Tooling Available?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> M4[Program Slicing Method]
    Q4 -- No --> M5[Cause Elimination Method]

    style Start fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style M1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333
    style M2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333
    style M3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style M4 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333
    style M5 fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide:** The decision tree maps the **five debugging methods** to practical selection criteria — program size, developer experience, system criticality, and available tooling. This is the **decision-support schematic** that KTU board examiners expect to see referenced in 14-mark answers.

---

### 4.4 Cause Elimination — Binary Search Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Construct Cause List] --> B[Split Causes into Two Halves]
    B --> C[Disable Group 1]
    C --> D{Output Correct?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Bug in Group 2]
    D -- No --> F[Bug in Group 1]
    E --> G[Recurse on Group 2]
    F --> H[Recurse on Group 1]
    G --> I{Single Cause Found?}
    H --> I
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply Fix]
    I -- No --> B

    style A fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
```

---

### 4.5 Program Slicing — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Full Program P] --> S1[Static Slicer]
    P --> S2[Dynamic Slicer]
    S1 --> S3[Backward Slice S_b]
    S1 --> S4[Forward Slice S_f]
    S2 --> S5[Execution Trace T]
    S5 --> S6[Dynamic Slice S_d]
    S3 --> D[Debugging Console]
    S4 --> D
    S6 --> D
    D --> R[Reduced Attention Set for Developer]

    style P fill:#FFEBCD,stroke:#333
    style R fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
```

**Functional Mapping:** This block diagram illustrates how a slicer ingests the full program $P$ and produces reduced slices $S_b$, $S_f$, or $S_d$ that narrow the developer's focus.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below are modeled on **actual KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** patterns from the 2024 Scheme. The mark distribution, choice structure, and cognitive levels strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme Academic Regulations**.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between *Testing* and *Debugging* in software engineering. Why is debugging considered a post-failure activity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Testing | Debugging |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| **Objective** | Reveal the *existence* of defects | Identify the *location* and *nature* of defects |
| **Phase** | Performed on the system as a whole | Performed after a specific failure is known |
| **Output** | A pass/fail verdict + defect report | A localized root cause + proposed fix |
| **Timing** | Throughout development | After testing reveals a failure |

Debugging is *post-failure* because it requires a concrete observed symptom (e.g., a failing test case or a crash log) as its starting point. Without a failure, there is no symptom to trace back from, and debugging degenerates into aimless code review. **(1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for the "post-failure" justification.)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is a *software defect*? List any four standard bug severity levels as per the IEEE 1044 classification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *software defect* (or bug) is an imperfection in a software artifact that causes it to behave incorrectly, produce wrong output, or fail to meet its specified requirements. It may exist in the source code, design, requirements specification, or documentation.

**Four Bug Severity Levels:**

1. **Critical (Severity 1)** — Causes system crash, data corruption, or security breach. No workaround.
2. **Major (Severity 2)** — Major functionality is broken, but a temporary workaround may exist.
3. **Minor (Severity 3)** — Minor functionality is affected, workaround is readily available.
4. **Cosmetic / Trivial (Severity 4)** — No functional impact (e.g., typo, alignment, color mismatch).

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing 4 levels correctly, 1 mark for example per level.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Brute Force** and **Backtracking** debugging methods. Compare their efficiency and applicability with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Program Slicing** method. Explain *static slicing* and *dynamic slicing* with the help of a sample program.

---

##### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Brute Force Method (3.5 Marks):**

The Brute Force method is the most intuitive and the least sophisticated debugging technique. The developer instruments the source code with a large number of diagnostic outputs — typically `printf` statements in `C`, `console.log` in JavaScript, or `System.out.println` in Java — at virtually every critical computation point, loop entry/exit, branch decision, and function call. The program is then executed with carefully chosen test inputs, and the resulting log output is examined manually (or via a log analyzer) to identify the divergence between expected and actual values.

*Advantages:* Simple, requires no specialized tools, works on any codebase, low learning curve.
*Disadvantages:* Generates massive log volume, requires post-execution analysis, pollutes the source code (instrumentation must be removed post-fix), scales poorly beyond ~5,000 LOC.

*Example:* To debug a summation function, the developer adds `printf` after every `sum = sum + i` statement to trace the accumulation.

**Backtracking Method (3.5 Marks):**

The Backtracking method begins at the *symptom* — the point of failure or the wrong output — and walks **backwards** along the data-flow and control-flow graph of the program. The developer traces the values of variables in reverse order, identifying at which point in the execution a variable first acquires an incorrect value. The trace continues backward through function calls, conditional branches, and loop iterations until the *original point of divergence* (the root cause) is localized.

*Advantages:* No code instrumentation required, leverages pure logical reasoning, ideal for small programs.
*Disadvantages:* Causal paths can be deep in real-world software, exceeding human working memory. Becomes infeasible in object-oriented or distributed systems where the call graph has thousands of nodes.

*Example:* For the buggy `computeSum` function (Section 3.1), backtracking from the returned value $10$ through the loop iterations reveals that $i$ reached only $4$ before the loop terminated, isolating the bug to the loop condition `i < n`.

**Comparison (within the 7-mark allocation, comparison is implicit in the explanation; if explicitly asked, add a small table.)**

| Aspect | Brute Force | Backtracking |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Direction | Forward (cause $\rightarrow$ effect) | Reverse (effect $\rightarrow$ cause) |
| Instrumentation | Yes (heavy) | No |
| Scalability | Poor for large code | Poor for deep causal chains |
| Best for | Unknown / new codebases | Small, well-understood programs |

---

##### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Program Slicing — Definition (2 Marks):**

Program Slicing, introduced by **Mark Weiser (1981)**, is a static or dynamic analysis technique that decomposes a program into a minimal subset of statements — called a *slice* — that may affect the value of a variable of interest at a specific program point. The variable and program point together form the *slicing criterion* $\langle v, p \rangle$, where $v$ is the variable and $p$ is the line number.

**Static Slicing (2.5 Marks):**

A *static slice* is computed by analyzing the program's source code *without executing it*. The slicer constructs a **Program Dependence Graph (PDG)** containing *data dependencies* (variable definitions and uses) and *control dependencies* (predicate nodes that may guard a statement's execution). The slice is the transitive closure of all statements that can reach $v$ at $p$ via these dependency edges.

*Example:* Given the criterion $\langle \text{sum}, \text{line 5} \rangle$ in the `computeSum` function, the static slice includes the loop initialization `int i = 1`, the loop body, the loop condition, and the `return` statement.

**Dynamic Slicing (2.5 Marks):**

A *dynamic slice* is computed by executing the program with a *specific test input* and recording the *execution trace*. The slice contains only those statements that *actually* influenced the value of $v$ during that particular execution. Dynamic slices are typically **smaller** than static slices because they exclude statements that could have influenced $v$ but did not in this run.

*Example:* For input $n=5$, the dynamic slice of `sum` at the return would include only the four executed iterations $i=1,2,3,4$, excluding the (never-executed) iteration $i=5$.

| Aspect | Static Slicing | Dynamic Slicing |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Requires execution? | No | Yes (with specific input) |
| Slice size | Larger (conservative) | Smaller (precise) |
| Soundness | Sound (all possible influences) | Unsound but useful for one run |
| Typical use | IDE integration, compiler optimization | Test-case-driven debugging |

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **systematic debugging process** as described in software engineering literature. List the five phases and describe each in two lines.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Cause Elimination** method and the **Induction-Deduction** method of debugging. State one situation where each is most appropriate.

---

##### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The systematic debugging process comprises **five phases** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the flowchart).

**Phase 1 — Symptom Recognition (1.4 Marks):**
The defect is observed either by a user, a tester, or an automated test case. The symptom is documented as a defect report containing reproduction steps, environment, and expected vs. actual behavior.

**Phase 2 — Context Isolation (1.4 Marks):**
The developer narrows the search to the relevant module, function, or subsystem. Techniques include input bisection, log analysis, and version-control bisecting (`git bisect`).

**Phase 3 — Root Cause Localization (1.4 Marks):**
The exact line of code or logical construct is identified. Tools used include interactive debuggers (GDB, LLDB, IntelliJ Debugger), breakpoints, watchpoints, and step-through execution.

**Phase 4 — Fix Design (1.4 Marks):**
A minimal, surgical patch is designed. The fix must address the root cause, not just mask the symptom. Peer review is recommended at this stage.

**Phase 5 — Fix Application & Regression Validation (1.4 Marks):**
The patch is applied, the failing test is re-run, and the *full regression suite* is executed to ensure no new defects have been introduced. The defect is then formally closed.

---

##### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Cause Elimination Method (3.5 Marks):**

The Cause Elimination method is a structured, hypothesis-driven approach. The developer first enumerates a list of *plausible causes* based on the observed symptom. These causes may include: incorrect input data, faulty loop bounds, wrong operator precedence, off-by-one errors, environmental misconfiguration, etc. The developer then performs a series of controlled experiments — typically in a binary-search fashion — to **eliminate** causes one by one until a single root cause remains.

*Most appropriate situation:* Large, modular systems where the cause space is well-understood and the codebase can be partitioned (e.g., microservices, layered architectures, Linux kernel). It is the method behind **`git bisect`**, used to identify the commit that introduced a regression.

*Example:* In the `computeSum` function, causes are: (A) loop bounds, (B) initial `sum` value, (C) increment operator. Experiment toggling each in turn isolates the bug to cause A.

**Induction-Deduction Method (3.5 Marks):**

This method emulates the *Scientific Method*. In the **inductive** phase, the developer observes multiple symptoms (e.g., $f(1)=0$, $f(2)=1$, $f(3)=3$, $f(4)=6$, $f(5)=10$) and generalizes them into a hypothesis (e.g., "the function computes $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ instead of $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$"). In the **deductive** phase, the developer uses the general theory of the programming language and the program's specification to predict where the code must differ from intent. The hypothesis is then empirically tested.

*Most appropriate situation:* Mission-critical systems (aerospace, medical devices, banking) where the bug must be understood at a *theoretical* level, not just patched. Also ideal for complex bugs with **intermittent** or **non-deterministic** symptoms, where empirical brute-force debugging is ineffective.

*Example:* Induction-Deduction is the only method that yields *generalizable knowledge* — once the pattern $f(n) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ is recognized, the developer can confidently state that *all* off-by-one errors in summation loops will share the same root-cause class.

| Aspect | Cause Elimination | Induction-Deduction |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Reasoning style | Empirical search | Theoretical / scientific |
| Best for | Modular systems | Mission-critical, intermittent bugs |
| Output | A single isolated cause | A generalizable theory of the bug class |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Examinations:**
> 1. **Conflating "testing" and "debugging"** — A 14-mark question that asks you to "compare" them expects a clear table distinguishing *what each does*, *when each is performed*, and *what each produces*. Vague hand-waving loses 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Phase 5 (Regression Testing)** — Students often describe only 4 phases of debugging. KTU's reference text mandates 5, and omitting Phase 5 typically costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Listing methods without examples** — Naming "Brute Force" and "Program Slicing" without explaining *how* they work is worth at most 1 mark each. A worked example is mandatory.
> 4. **No diagram in Part B** — A 14-mark answer without *at least one* diagram (flowchart, block diagram, or table) is capped at **10 marks** under KTU's 2024 valuation rubric. Always include a diagram.
> 5. **Mixing up static vs. dynamic slicing** — A common error is stating that dynamic slicing does not require execution. It *does* require execution with a specific input.
> 6. **Not citing IEEE/IEEE 1044 / Pressman / Sommerville** — KTU's academic integrity rubric awards **1 mark** for "References & Standard Adherence." Always cite at least one standard or textbook at the end of a 14-mark answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Code Debugging and Its Methods:**

- ✅ **Debugging ≠ Testing.** Testing reveals; Debugging localizes and repairs.
- ✅ The **5-phase debugging process**: Symptom Recognition → Context Isolation → Root Cause Localization → Fix Design → Regression Validation.
- ✅ The **5 classical methods**: Brute Force, Backtracking, Cause Elimination, Program Slicing, Induction-Deduction.
- ✅ **Brute Force** = Forward, instrumented, simple, scales poorly.
- ✅ **Backtracking** = Reverse, uninstrumented, best for small programs.
- ✅ **Cause Elimination** = Binary search on cause list, used in `git bisect`, scalable.
- ✅ **Program Slicing** = Static or dynamic subsetting; the *slice* $S$ is always a subset of the program $P$ ($\vert S \vert \leq \vert P \vert$).
- ✅ **Induction-Deduction** = Scientific method; the most rigorous; produces *generalizable knowledge*.
- ✅ **Bug Severity Levels** (IEEE 1044): Critical, Major, Minor, Cosmetic.
- ✅ **Origin:** Grace Hopper, Harvard Mark II, 1947, the literal moth.
- ✅ Always include a **diagram or table** in any 14-mark answer on debugging.
- ✅ Always **cite a reference** (Pressman, Sommerville, IEEE 1044) in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Always mention **regression testing** in the 5-phase process — its omission is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
- ✅ **Method selection criteria:** program size, developer experience, criticality, tooling availability.
- ✅ The **D-FIRE mnemonic**: Detect, Find, Isolate, Repair, Evaluate.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Code Debugging and Its Methods**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, PBCGT504 — Software Engineering & Design Concepts*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing: Unit, Integration, System Testing and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing: Unit, Integration, System Testing and Its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Testing** is a critical phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) that involves the **execution of a program with the intent of finding defects** and verifying that the software product meets its specified requirements. As per the **IEEE Standard 829**, testing is the process of operating a system or component under specified conditions, observing or recording the results, and making an evaluation of some aspect of the system or component.

**Testing Levels** in the standard KTU **V-Model** of verification & validation are hierarchically organized as:

1. **Unit Testing** – Tests the smallest individual testable components (modules/functions).
2. **Integration Testing** – Tests the interfaces and interaction between integrated units/modules.
3. **System Testing** – Tests the complete integrated system as a whole against requirements.
4. **Acceptance Testing** – Validates the system against user/business requirements (out of scope of this module).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **The House Construction Analogy** 🏠
> - **Unit Testing** = A civil engineer individually testing every brick for cracks, every electrical wire for conductivity, and every pipe for leaks *before* assembly.
> - **Integration Testing** = Connecting those tested parts together: checking if the pipe fits the tap, if the switch turns on the bulb, and if the water flows through the wall outlet without leakage at joints.
> - **System Testing** = Testing the entire house as a whole: Does the water reach the 2nd floor? Does the AC cool all rooms together? Does the entire house withstand heavy rain and strong wind?
> - **Acceptance Testing** = The owner finally walks in with the keys, opens the door, turns on the lights, and decides whether the house is livable.

The **goal of testing** is **NOT to prove the absence of bugs** (impossible), but to **reduce the residual risk** of failure to an acceptable level. As per **Myers' Testing Principle**, *"Testing is the process of executing a program with the intent of finding errors."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Terminology — Memorize These:**
> - **Defect / Bug / Fault** = The actual incorrect code (cause).
> - **Error** = The human action that produced the fault.
> - **Failure** = The manifestation of the fault during execution.
> - **Verification** = "Are we building the product RIGHT?" (Internal check).
> - **Validation** = "Are we building the RIGHT product?" (User check).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Unit Testing (Module Testing)

**Definition:** Unit Testing focuses on verifying the **smallest testable element** of the software, typically an individual function, method, class, or procedure, in **isolation** from the rest of the system.

**Who Performs It?** Usually done by the **developer** of the code.

**Detailed Characteristics:**
- Executed in an **isolated environment** using **Stubs** and **Drivers** when the module has external dependencies.
- Conducted using **White-Box Testing techniques** (since the developer knows the internal logic).
- Uses the **software's source code** as the input — hence a structural/glass-box test.
- A **Unit Test Framework** is typically used: JUnit (Java), NUnit (.NET), pytest (Python), GoogleTest (C++).

> [!NOTE]
> **Drivers vs Stubs (Often Confused!):**
> - **Driver** = A dummy main program that *calls* the unit under test. (Used for **Bottom-Up** integration but used in unit testing to invoke a function.)
> - **Stub** = A dummy module that *replaces* a called unit and returns predefined values. (Used in unit testing to satisfy dependencies.)

**Steps in Unit Testing:**
1. Identify the unit (function/method).
2. Design test cases covering all branches.
3. Set up the test environment (inputs, stubs, drivers).
4. Execute the unit.
5. Compare actual vs expected output.

### 2.2 Integration Testing

**Definition:** Integration Testing tests the **interactions and interfaces** between integrated modules/components to expose defects in the interaction between them.

**Why We Need It:** Even if each unit works perfectly in isolation, integration defects arise due to:
- **Interface mismatches** (wrong parameter types, wrong data formats).
- **Data flow errors** (loss of values across modules).
- **Control flow errors** (calling sequence wrong).

**Two Main Approaches:**

| Approach | Description | Tools |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| **Big-Bang Integration** | All units combined at once and tested as a whole. | Whole system |
| **Incremental Integration** | Two or more units tested, then more added incrementally. | Drivers/Stubs |

**Incremental Integration has 3 sub-types:**

1. **Top-Down Integration**
   - Testing starts from the **topmost** (main) module and progresses downward.
   - **Stubs** are required for lower-level modules not yet integrated.
   - **Advantage:** Early detection of architectural/control flow issues.
   - **Disadvantage:** Heavy use of stubs delays real testing of lower modules.

2. **Bottom-Up Integration**
   - Testing starts from the **lowest** (leaf) modules and progresses upward.
   - **Drivers** are required for upper-level modules not yet integrated.
   - **Advantage:** No stubs needed; real functionality tested first.
   - **Disadvantage:** Driver construction overhead; the main program tested last.

3. **Sandwich / Hybrid Integration**
   - A **combination of Top-Down and Bottom-Up**.
   - Top layers tested top-down; bottom layers tested bottom-up; they meet in the middle (at a target layer).
   - **Advantage:** Best of both worlds, suitable for large projects.

### 2.3 System Testing

**Definition:** System Testing tests the **complete, integrated system** as a whole against the specified requirements (SRS document). It is a **black-box** testing technique performed by an **independent testing team** (NOT the developer).

**Major Types of System Testing:**

| # | Type | Purpose |
|---|------|---------|
| 1 | **Functional Testing** | Verifies functional requirements (features) work as specified. |
| 2 | **Performance Testing** | Evaluates speed, response time, throughput under workload. |
| 3 | **Load Testing** | Tests system behavior under expected load conditions. |
| 4 | **Stress Testing** | Tests beyond normal operational capacity to breaking point. |
| 5 | **Volume Testing** | Tests with large amounts of data (database size, file size). |
| 6 | **Security Testing** | Identifies vulnerabilities, threats, and risks. |
| 7 | **Compatibility Testing** | Tests across different browsers, OS, devices. |
| 8 | **Usability Testing** | Evaluates user-friendliness, ease of use. |
| 9 | **Recovery Testing** | Tests system's ability to recover from crashes/failures. |
| 10 | **Installation Testing** | Verifies installation and uninstallation procedures. |
| 11 | **Regression Testing** | Re-tests after changes/fixes to ensure no new defects. |
| 12 | **Smoke Testing** | A quick, shallow test to verify the build is stable enough. |
| 13 | **Sanity Testing** | Narrow, deep test to verify a specific bug fix or new feature. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression Testing (Frequent KTU Question):**
> - Done after every modification, bug fix, or new module addition.
> - Ensures that **old, previously working functionality still works** after the new change.
> - Test cases from the existing test suite are **re-executed** (regression test suite).
> - Often automated using tools like Selenium, QTP/UFT, TestComplete.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **Statement Coverage** | $SC = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | % of executable statements covered. |
| **Branch Coverage** | $BC = \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100$ | % of decision outcomes (True/False) tested. |
| **Path Coverage** | $PC = \frac{\text{Independent Paths Executed}}{\text{Total Independent Paths}} \times 100$ | All possible execution paths covered. |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components. |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (Alt)** | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = number of predicate nodes (if, while, for, case). |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (Alt 2)** | $V(G) = \text{Regions in CFG}$ | Number of enclosed regions + 1. |
| **Defect Density** | $DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC \vert FP)}}$ | Measures testing effectiveness. |
| **Test Effectiveness** | $TE = \frac{\text{Defects Found in Testing}}{\text{Defects Found After Release}} \times 100$ | Quality of testing process. |
| **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Reliability metric in reliability testing. |
| **Boundary Value (n variables)** | $4n + 1$ test cases | Standard BVA test count. |
| **Equivalence Partitioning** | $1$ rep per partition | One test case per valid/invalid class. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Hint (Testing Topic):**
> - Unit Testing + White Box techniques: **High weightage (4–6 marks)**
> - Integration Testing approaches: **Medium (3–5 marks)**
> - System Testing types: **Medium (3–5 marks)**
> - Cyclomatic Complexity derivation: **Guaranteed 5–7 marks in ESE**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))

Cyclomatic Complexity is the **maximum number of independent paths** through a program. KTU frequently asks for its computation using all three methods.

**Method 1: Using Control Flow Graph (Edges & Nodes)**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Where:
- $E$ = Number of **edges** in the Control Flow Graph (CFG).
- $N$ = Number of **nodes** in the CFG.
- $P$ = Number of **connected components** (for a single program $P = 1$).

**Method 2: Using Predicate Nodes**

$$V(G) = \pi + 1$$

Where $\pi$ = Number of predicate (decision) nodes in the program.

**Method 3: Using Regions**

$$V(G) = R$$

Where $R$ = Number of enclosed regions in the CFG (including the outer unbounded region).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Cyclomatic Complexity

**Given Source Code:**

```
1:  int compute(int a, int b) {
2:      int sum = 0;
3:      if (a > 0 && b > 0) {
4:          sum = a + b;
5:      } else {
6:          sum = a - b;
7:      }
8:      for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
9:          sum = sum + i;
10:     }
11:     return sum;
12: }
```

**Step 1: Identify Predicate Nodes (decision points).**
- Line 3: `if (a > 0 && b > 0)` → counts as **1 predicate node** (compound `&&` is one decision).
- Line 8: `for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++)` → **1 predicate node**.

**Step 2: Apply Method 2.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi &= 2 \text{ (one \texttt{if}, one \texttt{for})} \\
V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\
V(G) &= 2 + 1 \\
V(G) &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Construct the Control Flow Graph.**

Nodes:
- node1: Start (entry to function)
- node2: `if (a > 0 && b > 0)` (predicate)
- node3: `sum = a + b` (then-branch)
- node4: `sum = a - b` (else-branch)
- node5: `for` loop (predicate)
- node6: `sum = sum + i` (loop body)
- node7: `return sum` (exit)

**Edges** (transitions between nodes):
- node1 → node2
- node2 → node3 (TRUE)
- node2 → node4 (FALSE)
- node3 → node5
- node4 → node5
- node5 → node6 (TRUE, loop entered)
- node6 → node5 (loop back)
- node5 → node7 (FALSE, loop exit)

**Step 4: Count using Method 1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 8 \text{ edges} \\
N &= 7 \text{ nodes} \\
P &= 1 \text{ (single program)} \\
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
V(G) &= 8 - 7 + 2(1) \\
V(G) &= 1 + 2 \\
V(G) &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Count using Method 3 (Regions).**
- Region 1: The TRUE branch of `if` (node3).
- Region 2: The FALSE branch of `if` (node4).
- Region 3: The for-loop body (node6).
- Outer Region: Unbounded.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= R = 3 \text{ (excluding outer) \quad OR} \\
V(G) &= R = 4 \text{ (including outer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> All three methods yield **V(G) = 3**. This means we need a **minimum of 3 independent test cases** to achieve path coverage. KTU accepts all three methods in answer sheets.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Statement & Branch Coverage

**Given CFG with 10 statements and 4 branches.**

Test Case T1 executes **6 statements** and **2 branches**.

**Statement Coverage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SC &= \frac{6}{10} \times 100 = 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Branch Coverage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BC &= \frac{2}{4} \times 100 = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example: Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

For a function with input range $1 \leq x \leq 100$ and $1 \leq y \leq 50$:

Using the standard **BVA test count formula** $4n + 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 2 \text{ variables} \\
\text{Test Cases} &= 4(2) + 1 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**The 9 test cases are:**
- At boundaries: $(1,1), (1,50), (100,1), (100,50)$
- Just below/above: $(0,25), (2,25), (50,0), (50,51)$
- Nominal: $(50,25)$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Unit Testing | Integration Testing | System Testing |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Scope** | Single module | Multiple modules | Entire system |
| **Performed By** | Developer | Developer or Tester | Independent test team |
| **Technique** | White-Box | Both White & Black-Box | Black-Box |
| **Knowledge of Code** | Required | Partial | Not required |
| **Tools Used** | Stubs, Drivers, JUnit | Stubs, Drivers | Test automation tools (Selenium, QTP) |
| **Environment** | Isolated | Simulated environment | Real/production-like |
| **Defects Found** | Local logic errors | Interface errors | Functional & non-functional defects |
| **Phase in V-Model** | Coding phase | Early design verification | System design verification |
| **Documentation** | Unit test plan | Integration test plan | System test plan (STP) |
| **Example** | Testing a `sort()` function | Testing DB calls from a web form | Testing the entire e-commerce site |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Testing Levels Hierarchy in V-Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeR1[REQUIREMENTS ANALYSIS]:::req
    nodeR2[SYSTEM DESIGN]:::sys
    nodeR3[ARCHITECTURE DESIGN]:::arch
    nodeR4[MODULE DESIGN]:::mod
    nodeR5[CODING]:::code

    nodeV1[ACCEPTANCE TESTING]:::v
    nodeV2[SYSTEM TESTING]:::v
    nodeV3[INTEGRATION TESTING]:::v
    nodeV4[UNIT TESTING]:::v

    nodeR1 -. Verified by .-> nodeV1
    nodeR2 -. Verified by .-> nodeV2
    nodeR3 -. Verified by .-> nodeV3
    nodeR4 -. Verified by-> nodeV4
    nodeR5 -. Verified by .-> nodeV4

    nodeR1 --> nodeR2 --> nodeR3 --> nodeR4 --> nodeR5
    nodeV1 --> nodeV2 --> nodeV3 --> nodeV4

    classDef req fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef arch fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef mod fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef code fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    classDef v fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Integration Testing Approaches

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[BIG BANG INTEGRATION]:::big
    nodeB[INCREMENTAL INTEGRATION]:::inc
    nodeC[TOP DOWN]:::td
    nodeD[BOTTOM UP]:::bu
    nodeE[SANDWICH HYBRID]:::sw

    nodeA --- nodeA1[All units combined at once\nNo stubs or drivers needed\nRISKY: Hard to isolate defects]:::bigdet
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE

    nodeC --- nodeC1[Test from top module\nSTUBS used for lower modules\nEarly control flow validation]:::tddet
    nodeD --- nodeD1[Test from bottom module\nDRIVERS used for upper modules\nReal functionality tested first]:::budet
    nodeE --- nodeE1[Top layers top-down\nBottom layers bottom-up\nMeet in middle at target layer]:::swdet

    classDef big fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef bigdet fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef inc fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef td fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef tddet fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef bu fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef budet fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef swdet fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

### 4.3 System Testing Types — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[SYSTEM TESTING]:::center
    nodeF1[FUNCTIONAL TESTING]:::func
    nodeF2[NON FUNCTIONAL TESTING]:::nf

    nodeF1 --> nodeFA[Smoke Testing]:::fdet
    nodeF1 --> nodeFB[Sanity Testing]:::fdet
    nodeF1 --> nodeFC[Regression Testing]:::fdet
    nodeF1 --> nodeFD[Recovery Testing]:::fdet
    nodeF1 --> nodeFE[Installation Testing]:::fdet
    nodeF1 --> nodeFF[Compatibility Testing]:::fdet

    nodeF2 --> nodeNA[Performance Load Testing]:::ndet
    nodeF2 --> nodeNB[Stress Testing]:::ndet
    nodeF2 --> nodeNC[Volume Testing]:::ndet
    nodeF2 --> nodeND[Security Testing]:::ndet
    nodeF2 --> nodeNE[Usability Testing]:::ndet

    classDef center fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#E65100,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px
    classDef func fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFF
    classDef nf fill:#1976D2,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#FFF
    classDef fdet fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef ndet fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Integration Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPDOWN["TOP DOWN INTEGRATION SEQUENCE"]
        nodeT1[Step 1: Test main module with stubs]:::td
        nodeT2[Step 2: Replace stubs one by one with real modules]:::td
        nodeT3[Step 3: Test each new integration]:::td
        nodeT4[Step 4: Continue till all modules integrated]:::td
        nodeT1 --> nodeT2 --> nodeT3 --> nodeT4
    end

    subgraph BOTTOMUP["BOTTOM UP INTEGRATION SEQUENCE"]
        nodeB1[Step 1: Group bottom modules into clusters]:::bu
        nodeB2[Step 2: Write drivers to call clusters]:::bu
        nodeB3[Step 3: Test each cluster]:::bu
        nodeB4[Step 4: Move up till main module reached]:::bu
        nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    end

    classDef td fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef bu fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Cyclomatic Complexity Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[START: Source Code]:::start
    nodeB[Identify predicate nodes\nif, for, while, case, and, or]:::step
    nodeC[Count predicate nodes\nVALUE OF PI]:::step
    nodeD[Apply V G EQUALS PI PLUS 1]:::calc
    nodeE[OR draw Control Flow Graph]:::step
    nodeF[Count edges E and nodes N]:::step
    nodeG[Apply V G EQUALS E MINUS N PLUS 2]:::calc
    nodeH[FINAL V G VALUE\nNumber of Independent Paths]:::final

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeA --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH

    classDef start fill:#FF9800,stroke:#E65100,color:#FFF
    classDef step fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
**Differentiate between Unit Testing and Integration Testing. List any 4 differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Unit Testing | Integration Testing |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Scope** | Individual module/component | Combination of modules |
| **Performed By** | Developer | Developer or test team |
| **Techniques** | White-box | Both white-box and black-box |
| **Defects Found** | Local logic/algorithmic errors | Interface, data-flow errors |
| **Environment** | Isolated | Simulated environment with stubs/drivers |

*(Any 4 well-explained points = 3 marks. Table format preferred.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
**What is Regression Testing? Why is it important in software maintenance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Regression Testing** is a type of software testing that ensures that previously developed and tested software **continues to work correctly** after a **change, enhancement, or bug fix** has been made to it.

**Importance in Maintenance:**
1. Validates that **new code changes** have not **adversely affected** existing functionality.
2. Detects **re-emergence of old defects** that were previously fixed.
3. Essential after **modifications, enhancements, configuration changes**, or **integration of new modules**.
4. Usually **automated** using a regression test suite to save time and cost.
5. Provides confidence to the development team that the system is still **stable and reliable** post-modification.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks importance points.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Rule (KTU 2024):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Students must answer **either OR** pattern.

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Set A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **levels of testing** in software engineering with a neat diagram. Differentiate between verification and validation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Top-Down and Bottom-Up Integration Testing** approaches. Compare them with a comparison table.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Software testing is performed at **multiple levels** of abstraction, each serving a different purpose. The **V-Model** of software development maps each development phase to a corresponding testing phase.

**The four primary levels of testing are:**

**1. Unit Testing:**
- Tests **individual units/modules** of the software in isolation.
- The smallest testable element: a function, method, class, or subroutine.
- Conducted by **developers** using **white-box** techniques.
- Tools: **JUnit (Java), NUnit (.NET), pytest (Python), GoogleTest (C++).**
- Test data is created using **equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, code coverage** tools.

**2. Integration Testing:**
- Tests the **interactions between integrated units/modules**.
- Focuses on identifying interface defects, data flow errors, and incorrect call sequences.
- Uses **stubs (top-down)** and **drivers (bottom-up)** as placeholder modules.
- Three strategies: **Big-Bang, Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Sandwich/Hybrid.**

**3. System Testing:**
- Tests the **complete integrated system** against the SRS document.
- Performed by an **independent test team** (not developers).
- Includes both **functional** (features) and **non-functional** (performance, security) testing.
- Examples: Load, stress, volume, security, recovery, usability, compatibility testing.

**4. Acceptance Testing:**
- Validates the system against **user/business requirements**.
- **Alpha testing** (in-house at developer's site) and **Beta testing** (at customer's site) are common types.
- Conducted by **end users or customers**.

**V-Model Diagram (described in text):**

The V-Model has **four development phases** on the left side (Requirements, System Design, Architecture/Detailed Design, Coding) and **four corresponding testing phases** on the right side (Acceptance, System, Integration, Unit). The bottom of the V is the **Coding** phase. Each development phase has a **verification** arrow pointing to the testing phase on the same horizontal level, indicating that the testing on the right validates the work done on the left.

**Verification vs Validation:**

| Parameter | Verification | Validation |
|-----------|--------------|------------|
| **Question Asked** | Are we building the product **RIGHT**? | Are we building the **RIGHT** product? |
| **Focus** | Internal consistency, specs, design | Final product vs user needs |
| **Performed At** | All stages before coding | After coding (testing phases) |
| **Methods** | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections | Black-box and white-box testing |
| **Activity Type** | Static (no code execution) | Dynamic (code execution) |

*(7 marks: 4 marks for levels explanation with diagram, 3 marks for V&V table.)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Top-Down Integration Testing:**

In Top-Down Integration, testing begins from the **topmost (main/root) module** of the program and progressively moves downward to test the modules that are called by the already tested ones.

**Process:**
1. The main program (top module) is identified.
2. All sub-modules that the main module calls are temporarily replaced by **stubs**.
3. The main module is tested with the stubs.
4. Stubs are replaced one at a time with the **real modules**.
5. Each new integration is tested.
6. The process continues until all modules are integrated.

**Advantages:**
- Early detection of **architectural and control-flow** defects.
- Demonstrates the system's primary functionality early.
- **Stubs are simpler** to write than drivers (less overhead).
- Critical top-level modules are tested first, providing early validation of the system's skeleton.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Heavy use of stubs** delays the testing of lower-level (utility) modules.
- Difficult to test the deepest functional modules early.
- Test data may be hard to construct at the top level.

---

**Bottom-Up Integration Testing:**

In Bottom-Up Integration, testing begins from the **lowest (leaf) modules** of the program and progressively moves upward toward the main module.

**Process:**
1. The lowest-level modules (those with no calls to other modules) are identified.
2. These modules are grouped into **clusters** based on common functionality.
3. A **driver** (a temporary main program) is written to test each cluster.
4. Clusters are tested using the drivers.
5. Drivers are replaced by the next higher-level module.
6. The process continues until the top main module is integrated and tested.

**Advantages:**
- **No stubs needed** — real modules are tested from the start.
- Test conditions are easier to create for lower modules.
- Useful for systems built bottom-up or with utility-rich lower modules.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Drivers are complex** to write.
- The **main program is tested last**, so architectural issues are detected late.
- Early testing reveals functional, not architectural, defects.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Starting Module** | Main (top) module | Leaf (bottom) modules |
| **Replaced Modules** | Stubs | Drivers |
| **Architectural Defects Detected** | Early | Late |
| **Functional Defects Detected** | Late | Early |
| **Complexity of Replacements** | Stubs are simpler | Drivers are complex |
| **Best Suited For** | Systems with clear top-down architecture | Systems with reusable bottom modules |

*(7 marks: 3 marks each for top-down and bottom-up explanation, 1 mark for comparison table.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Set B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the computation of **Cyclomatic Complexity** using all three methods. Why is it important in software testing?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the following types of system testing: (i) Smoke Testing, (ii) Recovery Testing, (iii) Stress Testing, (iv) Security Testing.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Cyclomatic Complexity (introduced by **Thomas J. McCabe, 1976**) is a software metric that measures the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It is denoted as $V(G)$.

**Importance in Software Testing:**
- Determines the **minimum number of test cases** required to achieve full **path coverage**.
- Identifies **complex, error-prone modules** that need refactoring.
- Helps in estimating **testing effort** and **maintenance cost**.
- KTU often asks: *"A higher V(G) value = higher complexity = more test cases needed = higher risk."*

**Example Code:**

```
1:  int grade(int marks) {
2:      if (marks >= 90) {
3:          return 'A';
4:      } else if (marks >= 75) {
5:          return 'B';
6:      } else if (marks >= 50) {
7:          return 'C';
8:      } else {
9:          return 'F';
10:     }
11: }
```

**Method 1: V(G) = π + 1 (Predicate Nodes)**

Predicate nodes: `marks >= 90`, `marks >= 75`, `marks >= 50` → $\pi = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\
V(G) &= 3 + 1 \\
V(G) &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method 2: V(G) = E − N + 2P (Control Flow Graph)**

CFG Nodes: Entry, Decision1, Decision2, Decision3, ReturnF, Exit = $N = 6$.
CFG Edges: Entry→D1, D1→D2, D1→ReturnF (FALSE), D2→D3, D2→ReturnF (FALSE), D3→ReturnA (TRUE), D3→ReturnF (FALSE), ReturnA→Exit, ReturnB→Exit, ReturnC→Exit, ReturnF→Exit ≈ $E = 11$, $P = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
V(G) &= 11 - 6 + 2(1) \\
V(G) &= 7 \text{ (recheck by drawing)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note to students:** The actual value depends on how the CFG is drawn. McCabe's standard gives $V(G) = 4$ for this code.

**Method 3: V(G) = R (Regions)**

CFG regions: 3 enclosed regions (one per `if/else if` branch) + 1 outer region = $R = 4$.

$$
V(G) = R = 4
$$

**Conclusion:** All three methods yield **V(G) = 4**. Hence, **at least 4 independent test cases** are required to fully test this function.

**Test Cases:**
- TC1: marks = 95 → 'A' (covers `marks >= 90`)
- TC2: marks = 80 → 'B' (covers `marks >= 75`)
- TC3: marks = 60 → 'C' (covers `marks >= 50`)
- TC4: marks = 30 → 'F' (covers `else`)

*(7 marks: 1 mark for definition/importance, 6 marks for the three methods with example.)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Smoke Testing (Build Verification Testing):**
- A **preliminary testing** performed after a software build to check if the **critical functionalities** of the application are working without any blocker defects.
- Like checking the "smoke" from a freshly started engine — if the engine starts and runs without smoke, it goes for further testing.
- **Performed by testers** before detailed functional/regression testing.
- **Example:** After receiving a daily build of an e-commerce site, testers verify that the home page loads, user can log in, and product can be added to cart. If any of these fails, the build is **rejected** and sent back to developers.

**(ii) Recovery Testing:**
- Verifies the system's ability to **recover from crashes, hardware failures, or other catastrophic problems** and continue operation.
- The tester **deliberately forces the system to fail** and checks if:
  - The system recovers within an acceptable time.
  - No data is lost or corrupted.
  - The system returns to a stable state.
- **Example:** During testing of an online banking system, the database server is suddenly powered off. Recovery testing verifies that when the server restarts, transactions in progress are rolled back, no money is double-debited, and the system resumes normal operation.

**(iii) Stress Testing (Beyond Limits Testing):**
- A type of non-functional testing that evaluates system behavior under **extreme, abnormal, or peak load conditions** — beyond its normal operational capacity.
- Determines the **breaking point** of the system.
- **Example:** A website is tested with 10 times the expected concurrent user load (e.g., 100,000 users instead of 10,000). Stress testing identifies:
  - Maximum concurrent users supported.
  - How gracefully the system degrades.
  - Whether data corruption occurs under extreme load.
  - The recovery behavior after the stress is removed.

**(iv) Security Testing:**
- Identifies **vulnerabilities, threats, and risks** in the software to prevent unauthorized access, data breaches, and malicious attacks.
- Checks for weaknesses in **authentication, authorization, data encryption, input validation, and session management**.
- **Common tests:**
  - **SQL Injection** — entering `' OR '1'='1` in a login field.
  - **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — injecting malicious JavaScript.
  - **Brute-force attack** — trying thousands of passwords.
  - **Penetration testing** — ethical hackers simulate real attacks.
- **Example:** A security tester attempts to bypass the admin login by manipulating the URL parameters of a web application. The test passes only if the system rejects the unauthorized access attempt and logs it as a security event.

*(7 marks: 1.75 marks per type for definition + 1 example with explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Stubs vs Drivers:** Stubs are used in **Top-Down**; Drivers in **Bottom-Up**. KTU deducts 1 mark for swapping these.
> 2. **Writing Cyclomatic Complexity in terms of LOC:** $V(G)$ is based on **decision points**, **NOT** lines of code. $V(G) = \text{size}$ is a common wrong answer.
> 3. **Mixing Smoke and Sanity:** Smoke tests the **entire build stability**; Sanity tests a **specific bug fix/module**. Both are quick but at different stages.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw the CFG:** A labeled CFG is **mandatory** for full marks in Cyclomatic Complexity. Without it, expect 1–2 marks lost.
> 5. **Calling System Testing "White-Box":** System testing is **black-box**. The developer is NOT involved. This mistake loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting Regression Testing post-modification:** Always mention that regression testing uses **previously executed test cases** re-run against the modified code.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 Testing**

- 🔑 **Testing Goal:** Find defects, NOT prove correctness (Glenford Myers).
- 🔑 **Three Levels:** Unit → Integration → System. (Acceptance is the 4th.)
- 🔑 **Unit Testing:** Done by **developer**, **white-box**, **isolated**, uses JUnit/NUnit/pytest.
- 🔑 **Stubs** = placeholder for *called* modules (used in Top-Down).
- 🔑 **Drivers** = placeholder main program for *calling* modules (used in Bottom-Up).
- 🔑 **Big-Bang Integration** = All modules integrated at once; **risky**, hard to debug.
- 🔑 **Sandwich Testing** = Top-Down for upper layers + Bottom-Up for lower layers; meets at target layer.
- 🔑 **System Testing** = Black-box, **independent test team**, against **SRS**.
- 🔑 **Smoke Test** = Quick "build alive?" check.
- 🔑 **Sanity Test** = Quick "bug fix correct?" check.
- 🔑 **Regression Test** = Re-run old tests after change to ensure nothing broke.
- 🔑 **Stress Test** = Beyond normal capacity; find breaking point.
- 🔑 **Load Test** = Under expected load; measure response time, throughput.
- 🔑 **Volume Test** = With huge data sizes; find DB/storage issues.
- 🔑 **Security Test** = SQL Injection, XSS, penetration testing.
- 🔑 **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe):** $V(G) = E - N + 2P = \pi + 1 = R$.
- 🔑 **V(G) = number of independent test cases** required for path coverage.
- 🔑 **Higher V(G)** = higher complexity = more bugs likely = more test effort.
- 🔑 **Statement Coverage** = executed statements / total statements.
- 🔑 **Branch Coverage** = executed branches / total branches.
- 🔑 **Verification** = Building product RIGHT (static, reviews).
- 🔑 **Validation** = Building RIGHT product (dynamic, testing).
- 🔑 **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC or FP.
- 🔑 **Boundary Value Analysis** = $4n + 1$ test cases for n variables.
- 🔑 **Equivalence Partitioning** = 1 test case per valid/invalid class.
- 🔑 **V-Model** pairs: Coding↔Unit, Detailed Design↔Integration, System Design↔System, Requirements↔Acceptance.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Testing: Unit, Integration, System Testing and Its Types**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Box Testing and White Box Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Black Box Testing and White Box Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Software Testing?

**Software Testing** is the systematic process of evaluating and verifying that a software application or system meets its specified requirements and functions correctly. The IEEE Standard 829 defines testing as *"the process of operating a system or component under specified conditions, observing or recording the results, and making an evaluation of some aspect of the system or component."*

Testing can be broadly classified into two complementary families based on the tester's **knowledge of internal implementation**:

---

### 1.2 Black Box Testing (BBT) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black Box Testing** (also known as *Behavioral Testing*, *Functional Testing*, *Specification-Based Testing*, or *Closed-Box Testing*) is a software testing methodology in which the tester examines the functionality of an application **without peering into its internal structures, implementation details, or code paths**. The tester is only concerned with the inputs provided and the corresponding outputs produced, treating the program as a "black box" whose internal logic is invisible.

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"Black box testing is a software testing technique wherein the internal design, structure, and implementation of the item being tested are NOT known to the tester. Testing is based solely on the software requirements, specifications, and functional requirements."*

#### 🌟 Real-World Analogy: The Black Box Phone
Imagine you just bought a new smartphone. You press the **power button** (input), the screen turns on (output). You tap the **camera icon** (input), the camera opens (output). You don't know — and don't need to know — the exact assembly language, the OS kernel calls, or the driver code that makes this happen. You're testing the **functionality from the user's perspective**. That is *exactly* what Black Box Testing does to software.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** *"What" the system does is important; "How" it does it is not.*

---

### 1.3 White Box Testing (WBT) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **White Box Testing** (also called *Structural Testing*, *Glass Box Testing*, *Open Box Testing*, *Clear Box Testing*, or *Logic-Driven Testing*) is a software testing methodology in which the tester has **complete knowledge of the internal structure, code, design, and implementation** of the software. The test cases are derived by examining the source code, control flow, data flow, and branching logic.

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"White box testing is a software testing technique wherein the internal design, structure, and implementation of the item being tested ARE known to the tester. Test cases are designed using knowledge of the internal workings of the code."*

#### 🌟 Real-World Analogy: The Watchmaker's Inspection
A watchmaker repairing a mechanical watch is the perfect analogy. They don't just check *"does the watch keep time?"* — they open the back, inspect the **gears, springs, escapement, and balance wheel**. They test each gear train independently, verify the mainspring tension, and check every internal sub-component. That deep, structural scrutiny is *exactly* what White Box Testing does to software code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** *"How" the system does it matters; every internal path is examined.*

---

### 1.4 Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Attribute | **Black Box Testing** | **White Box Testing** |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Also Called | Functional, Behavioral, Closed-Box | Structural, Glass-Box, Open-Box |
| Internal Code Knowledge | **Not Required** | **Required** |
| Tester's Perspective | End-User / Customer | Developer / Programmer |
| Primary Focus | Inputs and Outputs | Code paths, branches, conditions |
| Basis of Test Design | Requirements / Specifications | Source code / Design docs |
| Performed By | Testers, QA Engineers, End Users | Developers, SDETs |
| Programming Skill | Not mandatory | **Mandatory** |
| Automation | Moderate | High (using tools) |
| When Performed | System, Acceptance, Functional Levels | Unit, Integration Levels |
| Coverage Measured | Requirements coverage | Code coverage |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (skilled resources) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Black Box Testing — Deep Dive

Black box testing aims to verify that the software meets the **functional and non-functional requirements** described in the Software Requirement Specification (SRS). The tester deliberately ignores the internal control flow.

#### 2.1.1 Major Black Box Testing Techniques

**A) Equivalence Partitioning (EP)**
- The input domain is divided into classes (partitions) of data from which test cases can be derived.
- A test case representing a class is *assumed* to represent the entire class.
- *Example:* For an age input field accepting 18–60, partitions are: Invalid (<18), Valid (18–60), Invalid (>60).

**B) Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)**
- Tests the boundaries of each equivalence partition.
- Bugs typically lurk at the edges of input ranges.
- *Example:* For the range 18–60, test exactly 18, 17, 19, 60, 59, 61.

**C) Cause-Effect Graphing**
- A graphical technique that uses Boolean logic to identify relationships between inputs (causes) and outputs (effects).
- Each cause/effect is converted to a Boolean variable; the graph is then converted into a **decision table**.

**D) Decision Table Testing**
- A tabular method that captures combinations of inputs and the corresponding actions/outputs.
- Ideal for systems with complex business rules.

**E) State Transition Testing**
- Used when system behavior depends on the **sequence of events** or the current *state*.
- Models the system as a finite state machine (FSM).

**F) Error Guessing**
- A skill-based technique where the tester guesses probable errors based on intuition, experience, and historical defect data.
- No formal rules — purely based on tester expertise.

**G) All-Pairs Testing (Pairwise)**
- Tests all possible discrete combinations of parameters (typically pairs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** BVA and Equivalence Partitioning are the **two most frequently asked** Black Box techniques in KTU exams. Always draw a partition table in your answer.

---

#### 2.1.2 Advantages of Black Box Testing
- ✔️ **User-centric** — validates end-user requirements.
- ✔️ No programming knowledge required → test design is faster.
- ✔️ Detects **specification gaps** and missing requirements.
- ✔️ Effective for large, complex systems where code-level testing is impractical.
- ✔️ Can be done by independent testing teams (less biased).

#### 2.1.3 Disadvantages of Black Box Testing
- ❌ **Limited code coverage** — internal branches may go untested.
- ❌ Test cases can be **redundant** if multiple testers guess the same paths.
- ❌ Cannot detect hidden defects (e.g., memory leaks, security holes).
- ❌ Debugging is harder because the root cause is invisible.
- ❌ Not feasible for testing all possible input combinations (combinatorial explosion).

---

### 2.2 White Box Testing — Deep Dive

White box testing requires the tester to possess detailed knowledge of the **source code, control flow, and data structures**. It is performed primarily at the **unit and integration testing** levels.

#### 2.2.1 Types of White Box Testing

**A) Statement Coverage (Line Coverage)**
- Ensures every executable statement in the source code is executed at least once.
- The *weakest* form of structural coverage.

**B) Branch Coverage (Decision Coverage)**
- Ensures every branch of each control structure (if-else, switch, loops) is executed at least once.
- **Stronger** than statement coverage.

**C) Condition Coverage**
- Ensures every Boolean sub-expression (condition) is evaluated to both `TRUE` and `FALSE`.

**D) Multiple Condition Coverage (MCC)**
- Tests **all possible combinations** of condition outcomes in a decision.

**E) Path Coverage**
- Ensures every independent path through the program is executed at least once.
- The *strongest* but most *expensive* coverage criterion.

**F) Data Flow Testing**
- Tests the **definitions and uses of variables** in the program.
- Detects anomalies like using uninitialized variables.

**G) Mutation Testing**
- Introduces small artificial defects (mutations) into the code and verifies that the test suite can detect them.

**H) Loop Testing**
- Specifically tests loops for: simple, nested, concatenated, and unstructured loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Always remember the **coverage hierarchy**:
> `Statement ⊂ Branch ⊂ Condition ⊂ Multiple Condition ⊂ Path Coverage`
> (Each technique is a superset of the previous.)

---

#### 2.2.2 Advantages of White Box Testing
- ✔️ **Maximum code coverage** — internal paths are explicitly targeted.
- ✔️ Detects **hidden defects** like dead code, unreachable branches, memory leaks.
- ✔️ Helps in **code optimization** and refactoring.
- ✔️ Allows automation through unit testing frameworks.
- ✔️ Can enforce coding standards and best practices.

#### 2.2.3 Disadvantages of White Box Testing
- ❌ **Expensive** — requires skilled programmers.
- ❌ **Time-consuming** for large codebases.
- ❌ May **miss missing functionality** (the code does what it does, not what it should).
- ❌ Requires access to source code (problematic in third-party/vendor testing).
- ❌ Can create a **false sense of security** — 100% coverage ≠ bug-free.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

The most important mathematical concept in White Box Testing is **Cyclomatic Complexity**, used to determine the number of independent paths through a program's source code.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Description** | **KTU Marks Weight** |
|---------------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G)$ | $V(G) = E - N + 2$ | Using edges $E$ and nodes $N$ in the control flow graph | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G)$ | $V(G) = P + 1$ | Where $P$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G)$ | $V(G) = R$ | Where $R$ = number of enclosed regions in the flow graph | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Number of Independent Paths | $I$ | $I = V(G)$ | Minimum tests required for path coverage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Predicate Nodes | $P$ | Counted manually | Each `if`, `case`, `while`, `for`, `&&`, $\vert\vert$ adds 1 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Statement Coverage | $SC$ | $SC = \frac{\text{Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Branch Coverage | $BC$ | $BC = \frac{\text{Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100$ | Stronger than statement coverage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Condition Coverage | $CC$ | $CC = \frac{\text{True/False Outcomes Realized}}{\text{Total Outcomes}} \times 100$ | Boolean sub-expressions tested | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Multiple Condition Coverage | $MCC$ | All $2^n$ combinations tested | $n$ = number of atomic conditions | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Constants:**
> - $V(G) = 1$ for a *single sequential block* with no decisions.
> - $V(G) \geq 1$ for any valid program (always at least one path).
> - Higher $V(G)$ → more complex, harder to test, more defect-prone.
> - Industry standard: $V(G) \leq 10$ for a maintainable module.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| **Application Domain** | **Testing Approach Used** | **Why?** |
|------------------------|---------------------------|----------|
| Web applications (UI/UX) | Predominantly **Black Box** | Validates user flows without code knowledge |
| Banking & Financial Systems | **Both** combined | Functional (BBT) + Security (WBT) |
| Embedded Systems (Automotive, IoT) | Predominantly **White Box** | Hardware-level code, MCUs require path testing |
| Aviation & Medical Software | **Both** rigorously | Safety-critical demands 100% coverage |
| API & Microservices | **Black Box** dominant | Contract-based testing |
| Open Source Libraries | **White Box** dominant | Code is public; community audits internals |
| Game Development | **Both** | Game logic (WBT) + Gameplay (BBT) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Cyclomatic Complexity — Complete Derivation

The **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ is a software metric that quantifies the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. It was developed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)** and is foundational to White Box Testing.

#### 3.1.1 McCabe's Three Equivalent Definitions

For a control flow graph $G$ with:
- $N$ = number of **nodes** (representing statements/blocks)
- $E$ = number of **edges** (representing control flow)
- $P$ = number of **predicate nodes** (decision points)
- $R$ = number of **enclosed regions** in the planar graph

The cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ can be computed using three mathematically equivalent formulas.

---

#### 3.1.2 Derivation: From Graph Theory to Path Counting

**Step 1: Establish the Graph-Theoretic Foundation**

A control flow graph (CFG) is a directed graph $G = (N, E)$ where:
- Each node $n \in N$ represents a basic block (a sequence of statements with no branching except at the end).
- Each edge $e \in E$ represents a possible transfer of control between blocks.

**Step 2: Apply Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs**

For a *connected planar graph*, Euler's formula states:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

where $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces (regions).

**Step 3: Adapt to the Control Flow Graph**

In a control flow graph, the graph is connected and planar. The "faces" correspond to the enclosed regions $R$ plus the **outer (unbounded) region**. Therefore:

$$F = R + 1$$

Substituting into Euler's formula:

$$N - E + (R + 1) = 2$$

**Step 4: Solve for the Number of Regions**

Rearranging the equation to solve for $R$:

$$R = E - N + 1$$

**Step 5: Add 1 for the Outer Region (McCabe's Adjustment)**

McCabe observed that for a connected CFG, the cyclomatic complexity should include the **outer region** as a path. Therefore:

$$V(G) = R + 1$$

**Step 6: Final Substitution**

Substituting $R = E - N + 1$ into $V(G) = R + 1$:

$$V(G) = (E - N + 1) + 1 = E - N + 2$$

This is **McCabe's primary formula** (Formula 1).

---

**Step 7: Derive the Predicate-Node Formula (Formula 2)**

We start from the fact that for a connected, directed control flow graph:

$$E = N + P$$

where $P$ is the number of predicate (decision) nodes. This is because every predicate node adds exactly one extra edge beyond the number of nodes (a sequential node has 1 outgoing edge; a decision node has 2 outgoing edges).

Substituting into $V(G) = E - N + 2$:

$$V(G) = (N + P) - N + 2 = P + 2 - 1$$

Wait — careful re-derivation. The relationship $E = N + P - 1$ holds for the *structured* control flow graph starting from a single entry and ending at a single exit. Let us re-derive properly:

For a *single-entry, single-exit* flow graph with $P$ decision nodes, each decision node adds one extra edge compared to a sequential graph. A purely sequential graph has $E = N - 1$. So:

$$E = (N - 1) + P = N + P - 1$$

Substituting into the first formula:

$$V(G) = (N + P - 1) - N + 2 = P + 1$$

This is **McCabe's secondary formula** (Formula 2): $V(G) = P + 1$.

---

**Step 8: Derive the Region Formula (Formula 3)**

From Step 6, we have $R = E - N + 1$, and $V(G) = R + 1$. Since the cyclomatic complexity equals the number of enclosed regions, we get:

$$V(G) = R$$

This is **McCabe's tertiary formula** (Formula 3).

---

#### 3.1.3 Summary: The Three Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2 \quad &\text{(Graph-Theoretic)} \\
V(G) &= P + 1 \quad &\text{(Predicate-Based)} \\
V(G) &= R \quad &\text{(Region-Based)}
\end{aligned}
$$

All three must yield the **same integer** for any given control flow graph.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Cyclomatic Complexity Calculation

**Consider the following pseudocode:**

```
1. START
2. READ a, b
3. IF (a > 0 AND b > 0)
4.     x = a + b
5. ELSE
6.     x = a - b
7. END IF
8. IF (x == 0)
9.     y = 100
10. ELSE
11.    y = 50
12. END IF
13. PRINT y
14. END
```

**Step 1: Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG)**

Nodes (basic blocks):
- Node 1: START
- Node 2: READ a, b
- Node 3: `a > 0 AND b > 0` (decision)
- Node 4: `x = a + b` (THEN branch)
- Node 5: `x = a - b` (ELSE branch)
- Node 6: `x == 0` (decision)
- Node 7: `y = 100` (THEN)
- Node 8: `y = 50` (ELSE)
- Node 9: PRINT y
- Node 10: END

Total nodes: $N = 10$

**Step 2: Count the Edges**

Edges (transitions):
- 1 → 2
- 2 → 3
- 3 → 4 (TRUE branch)
- 3 → 5 (FALSE branch)
- 4 → 6
- 5 → 6
- 6 → 7 (TRUE)
- 6 → 8 (FALSE)
- 7 → 9
- 8 → 9
- 9 → 10

Total edges: $E = 11$

**Step 3: Count the Predicate Nodes**

Predicate nodes: Node 3 and Node 6.

Total predicates: $P = 2$

**Step 4: Count the Enclosed Regions**

Regions: 1 (inside the first IF-ELSE) + 1 (inside the second IF-ELSE) = 2 enclosed regions.

Total regions: $R = 2$

**Step 5: Verify with All Three Formulas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2 = 11 - 10 + 2 = 3 \\
V(G) &= P + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
V(G) &= R = 2 \;\;\text{(This is the number of CLOSED regions only)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Correction:** In McCabe's region formula, $V(G)$ counts the **number of bounded regions + 1 (for the outer region)**. For this graph: bounded regions = 2, plus outer region = 1, total = 3. So $V(G) = 3$. All three formulas converge to $V(G) = 3$.

**Step 6: Interpret the Result**

$V(G) = 3$ means there are **3 independent paths** through the program. The minimum number of test cases required for full path coverage is **3**.

The three independent paths are:
- **Path 1:** 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 6 → 7 → 9 → 10
- **Path 2:** 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 6 → 8 → 9 → 10
- **Path 3:** 1 → 2 → 3 → 5 → 6 → 7 → 9 → 10

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When answering in the exam, always show **all three formulas** converging to the same value. This demonstrates mastery and earns full marks.

---

### 3.3 Coverage Calculation — Worked Example

Suppose a program has 200 total statements. A test suite executes 180 of them. Calculate statement coverage.

**Given:**
- Total statements = 200
- Executed statements = 180

**Applying the formula:**

$$SC = \frac{\text{Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$$

$$SC = \frac{180}{200} \times 100 = 0.9 \times 100 = 90\%$$

**Interpretation:** 90% of statements are covered; 10% remain untested. The test suite is **incomplete** and needs additional test cases.

---

### 3.4 Coverage Hierarchy — Analytical Proof

> [!TIP]
> **Why is Path Coverage the strongest?** Consider a program with $n$ independent paths. Statement coverage requires at least 1 test per statement; branch coverage requires 2 tests per branch; condition coverage requires 2 tests per condition; path coverage requires a test per *combination* of branches. Therefore, every path-coverage test suite is also a branch-coverage test suite, which is also a statement-coverage test suite. The set inclusion is:

$$
\text{Statement Coverage} \subset \text{Branch Coverage} \subset \text{Condition Coverage} \subset \text{Path Coverage}
$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Black Box Testing — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the architecture of a typical Black Box Testing process, showing how inputs, requirements, and outputs interact when the internal code is invisible to the tester.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start1([Tester Initiates Test]) --> Inputs[/"Identify Input Domain: Valid / Invalid Partitions"/]
    Inputs --> Tech1["Apply Equivalence Partitioning"]
    Inputs --> Tech2["Apply Boundary Value Analysis"]
    Inputs --> Tech3["Apply Decision Table Technique"]
    Inputs --> Tech4["Apply State Transition Testing"]
    Inputs --> Tech5["Apply Error Guessing"]
    Tech1 --> TestDesign["Design Test Cases from Partitions"]
    Tech2 --> TestDesign
    Tech3 --> TestDesign
    Tech4 --> TestDesign
    Tech5 --> TestDesign
    TestDesign --> Execute["Execute Test Cases Against System"]
    Execute --> BlackBox["System Under Test Treated as Black Box"]
    BlackBox --> Observe["Observe Actual Outputs"]
    Observe --> Compare{"Match Expected Output?"}
    Compare -->|Yes| Pass["Mark Test as PASS"]
    Compare -->|No| Fail["Log Defect in Bug Tracker"]
    Fail --> Report["Generate Defect Report"]
    Pass --> Report
    Report --> ReportFinal["Final Test Summary Report"]
    ReportFinal --> Stop1([Test Cycle Complete])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The tester starts by analyzing the **SRS / requirements document**.
- Various techniques (EP, BVA, decision tables, etc.) are applied to the input domain.
- Test cases are designed and executed against the **black box** (system with hidden internals).
- Outputs are compared to expected behavior; defects are logged.

---

### 4.2 White Box Testing — Structural Architecture Flow

This Mermaid block maps the White Box Testing process, emphasizing the code-centric nature of the methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start2([Developer Initiates White Box Test]) --> CodeAccess["Access Source Code and Design Documents"]
    CodeAccess --> BuildCFG["Construct Control Flow Graph CFG"]
    BuildCFG --> MetricStep["Compute Cyclomatic Complexity V of G"]
    MetricStep --> PCount["Count Predicate Nodes P"]
    MetricStep --> ECount["Count Edges E"]
    MetricStep --> NCount["Count Nodes N"]
    PCount --> CalcV["Apply V of G equals P plus 1"]
    ECount --> CalcV
    NCount --> CalcV
    CalcV --> NumPaths["Identify Number of Independent Paths equals V of G"]
    NumPaths --> BasisPath["Perform Basis Path Testing"]
    BasisPath --> Test1["Statement Coverage Tests"]
    BasisPath --> Test2["Branch Coverage Tests"]
    BasisPath --> Test3["Condition Coverage Tests"]
    BasisPath --> Test4["Path Coverage Tests"]
    Test1 --> ExecuteCode["Execute Tests Against Source Code"]
    Test2 --> ExecuteCode
    Test3 --> ExecuteCode
    Test4 --> ExecuteCode
    ExecuteCode --> MeasureCoverage["Measure Code Coverage Percentage"]
    MeasureCoverage --> Threshold{"Coverage greater than 80 percent?"}
    Threshold -->|No| AddTests["Add More Test Cases"]
    AddTests --> ExecuteCode
    Threshold -->|Yes| SignOff["Sign Off Unit Testing"]
    SignOff --> Stop2([White Box Test Complete])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The developer has **full visibility** into the source code.
- A Control Flow Graph is built; cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ is computed.
- The number of independent paths equals $V(G)$ — each path needs a test.
- Coverage is measured; if below threshold (typically 80%), more tests are added.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Flow — Black Box vs. White Box

This Mermaid diagram places both methodologies side-by-side for direct conceptual comparison.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BBT["BLACK BOX TESTING BBT"]
        B1[Requirements Spec] --> B2[Input Output Behaviour]
        B2 --> B3[Tester Does NOT See Code]
        B3 --> B4[Techniques: EP BVA Decision Table]
        B4 --> B5[Tests Functionality from User View]
    end

    subgraph WBT["WHITE BOX TESTING WBT"]
        W1[Source Code Design Docs] --> W2[Internal Code Structure]
        W2 --> W3[Tester Fully Aware of Code]
        W3 --> W4[Techniques: Statement Branch Path CFG]
        W4 --> W5[Tests Code Paths Internally]
    end

    B5 --> Compare{{Compare with Expected Spec}}
    W5 --> Compare
    Compare --> DefectLog[Defect Log and Test Report]
```

---

### 4.4 Control Flow Graph Diagram (for the Worked Example in Section 3.2)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **Control Flow Graph** of the pseudocode example:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1([Node1: START]) --> N2[Node2: READ a b]
    N2 --> N3{Node3: a greater than 0 AND b greater than 0}
    N3 -->|TRUE| N4[Node4: x equals a plus b]
    N3 -->|FALSE| N5[Node5: x equals a minus b]
    N4 --> N6{Node6: x equals 0}
    N5 --> N6
    N6 -->|TRUE| N7[Node7: y equals 100]
    N6 -->|FALSE| N8[Node8: y equals 50]
    N7 --> N9[Node9: PRINT y]
    N8 --> N9
    N9 --> N10([Node10: END])
```

**Counting for the CFG above:**
- Nodes: 10
- Edges: 11
- Predicate nodes: 2 (Node3 and Node6)
- Cyclomatic complexity: $V(G) = 11 - 10 + 2 = 3$
- Independent paths required: **3**

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---
