## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Explain why Netflix migrated from a monolithic architecture to a microservices-based architecture. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 points, ~80 words):**
1. **Resilience** — The 2008 Oracle DB corruption froze the entire DVD-shipping system; a single failure brought down the whole product.
2. **Scalability** — A monolith cannot scale individual features (e.g., search) independently; microservices allow per-service horizontal scaling.
3. **Deploy Velocity** — In a monolith, every change requires a full redeploy; microservices enable independent, continuous delivery of small features.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
*List any three Netflix Open-Source tools and state their functions. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Eureka** — Service discovery and health-checking registry.
- **Hystrix** — Circuit breaker library that prevents cascading failures.
- **Zuul** — API gateway providing dynamic routing, filtering, and security at the edge.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Ribbon, Archaius, Spinnaker, Chaos Monkey.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Format:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]**

**(a)** Describe the **strangler-fig pattern** used by Netflix to migrate from its monolithic DVD application to a cloud-native microservices platform. Highlight the three phases of the migration with their timelines and engineering objectives. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **Circuit Breaker, Bulkhead, and Service Discovery** patterns in Netflix's microservices architecture. State which Netflix OSS tool implements each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Strangler-Fig Pattern (7 Marks)

The **strangler-fig pattern** (Martin Fowler, 2004) is an incremental migration strategy where a new system *gradually grows around* a legacy system, eventually replacing it entirely. Netflix applied it as follows:

**Phase 1 — Lift and Shift (2008–2009), 2 marks**
- The monolithic application and its Oracle DB were *rehosted* on Amazon EC2 virtual machines without code changes.
- **Objective:** Achieve cloud elasticity quickly, exit physical data centers, and gain multi-region capability.
- **Risk profile:** Low — no code change; pure infrastructure migration.

**Phase 2 — Carve-Out of Non-Critical Services (2009–2011), 2 marks**
- Netflix extracted *read-only* and *non-customer-facing* capabilities (movie encoding, catalog, recommendations) into independent services.
- **Objective:** Prove the microservices model with low customer impact and learn the operational tooling.
- **Engineering outcome:** New services ran on **Cassandra** and **S3**; the monolith continued to write to Oracle.

**Phase 3 — Full Microservices Decomposition (2011–Present), 2 marks**
- Every bounded context (auth, playback, billing, search, ratings) was extracted into an independent service with its own database, deploy pipeline, and on-call rotation.
- **Objective:** Achieve team-aligned autonomy, per-service scaling, and zero-downtime continuous delivery.
- **Engineering outcome:** Today, the system runs **1,500+ customer-facing microservices** across **3 AWS regions**.

**Conclusion, 1 mark:** The strangler-fig pattern minimised migration risk by replacing the monolith *incrementally* — Netflix never had a "big-bang" rewrite moment.

#### Model Solution — (b) Resilience Patterns (7 Marks)

| Pattern | Purpose | Netflix OSS Tool | KTU-Mapped Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Circuit Breaker** | Stops calling a failing service; returns cached/fallback response. Prevents cascade failure. | **Hystrix** | Bulkheading + fail-fast. |
| **Bulkhead** | Isolates thread pools per dependency; failure of one service cannot exhaust resources of all. | **Hystrix thread pools** | Resource isolation. |
| **Service Discovery** | Dynamically resolves service instance addresses at runtime; enables elasticity. | **Eureka** | Decouples service location from URL. |

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- **Circuit Breaker (Hystrix):** When the recommendation service slows down, Hystrix's circuit *opens*, and subsequent calls fail fast with a default response, preventing thread-pool exhaustion in the calling service.
- **Bulkhead:** Each downstream dependency gets a *dedicated thread pool*. If billing service hangs, only the billing thread pool saturates — search and playback remain unaffected.
- **Service Discovery (Eureka):** When a playback service instance starts, it registers with Eureka. Clients query Eureka to obtain healthy instances, enabling *automatic failover* when an instance dies.

**Conclusion (3 marks):** Together, these three patterns form Netflix's *resilience triad* — they ensure that no single point of failure or slowness can bring down the entire user experience.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain Netflix's **high-level microservices architecture**. Label the client tier, API gateway, service discovery, microservices tier, and data tier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Chaos Engineering**? Describe any **four Netflix "monkeys"** and explain how they collectively strengthen system resilience. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Netflix Architecture Diagram (7 Marks)

*(See Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid diagram — reproduce it as a labeled block diagram in the answer script.)*

**Labeled Tiers (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation):**

1. **Client Tier (1 mark):** Web SPA, iOS, Android, Smart TV, Game Console apps — all REST/gRPC clients.
2. **API Gateway / Edge Layer (1 mark):** **Zuul** handles routing, authentication, rate-limiting, and request filtering.
3. **Service Discovery (1 mark):** **Eureka** registry maintains dynamic instance maps; services register on boot.
4. **Microservices Tier (1 mark):** Stateless services (Profile, Recommendation, Catalog, Playback, Billing, Search, Ratings) — each independently deployable.
5. **Data Tier (1 mark):** Polyglot persistence — **MySQL** for transactions, **Cassandra** for write-heavy reads, **EVCache** for hot data, **S3** for media, **Kafka** for events.
6. **Operations (cross-cutting, 2 marks):** **Spinnaker** for CD, **Chaos Monkey** for resilience, **Atlas** for telemetry.

#### Model Solution — (b) Chaos Engineering (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** *Chaos Engineering* is the practice of deliberately injecting failures (latency, exceptions, instance termination, network partitions) into a production system to *proactively* discover latent defects and validate resilience assumptions. Netflix formalized this in the **"Principles of Chaos"** document.

**Four Netflix Monkeys (4 marks, 1 each):**

| Monkey | Function |
|---|---|
| **Chaos Monkey** | Randomly terminates VM instances in production to verify auto-recovery. |
| **Latency Monkey** | Induces artificial network latency between services to test tolerance to slow dependencies. |
| **Conformity Monkey** | Scans instances for deviations from best-practice configurations and shuts down non-conformant ones. |
| **Doctor Monkey** | Monitors service health, propagates failure symptoms, and heals unhealthy instances. |

*(Acceptable alternatives: Janitor Monkey — cleans up unused resources; Security Monkey — detects vulnerabilities.)*

**Collective Resilience Benefit (1 mark):** These monkeys *force* every service to be designed for failure (idempotency, retries, circuit breakers, fallbacks). The net effect is a system where *any single component can fail without affecting customer experience* — exactly the property that protects Netflix during partial AWS outages, regional failures, and traffic spikes (e.g., Friday-night releases).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Netflix Case-Study Answers**
> - **Mistake 1:** Drawing only a "boxes and arrows" diagram without labeling tiers. **Loss: 2–3 marks.** Always label: *Client, Gateway, Discovery, Microservices, Data*.
> - **Mistake 2:** Conflating *microservices* with *SOA*. Microservices are *fine-grained, independent, polyglot*; SOA is *coarser, often ESB-based*. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> - **Mistake 3:** Omitting the *2008 outage* context. Netflix's microservices story is a *response* to that failure. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Mistake 4:** Writing "Netflix uses AWS" without explaining *which services* (EC2, S3, RDS, DynamoDB) and *why*. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Mistake 5:** Spelling "Hystrix" as "Histerix" / "Eureka" as "Ereka" / "Zuul" as "Zull". **Loss: 0.5–1 mark** (spelling matters in technical subjects).
> - **Mistake 6:** Not mentioning *Conway's Law* and *DDD* when justifying service boundaries. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ Netflix's monolith-to-microservices journey was **triggered by the 2008 Oracle DB corruption**.
- ✅ Migration used the **Strangler-Fig Pattern** in **3 phases** (Lift & Shift → Carve-Out → Full Decomposition).
- ✅ Modern Netflix runs **~1,500+ microservices** (15,000+ total if utility services are counted).
- ✅ **Cloud:** Multi-region **AWS** (us-east-1, us-east-2, eu-west-1).
- ✅ **API Gateway:** **Zuul**.
- ✅ **Service Discovery:** **Eureka**.
- ✅ **Circuit Breaker:** **Hystrix**.
- ✅ **Client-Side Load Balancing:** **Ribbon**.
- ✅ **Continuous Delivery:** **Spinnaker**.
- ✅ **Chaos Engineering:** **Chaos Monkey** + family (Latency, Conformity, Doctor, Janitor, Security).
- ✅ **Polyglot Persistence:** MySQL (transactions) + Cassandra (scale) + EVCache (hot cache) + S3 (media) + Kafka (events).
- ✅ **Design Principles:** Twelve-Factor App, Conway's Law, DDD, Bulkhead, Circuit Breaker, Idempotency.
- ✅ **Reliability Metric:** **99.99% SLO** with **error-budget governance**.
- ✅ **Cultural Pillars:** "Freedom & Responsibility", blameless post-mortems, developers-on-call, no separate ops team.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem Application:** Netflix chose **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance) via **Cassandra's eventual consistency** for streaming metadata.
- ✅ **Why study Netflix?** It is the *industry proof* that microservices + cloud + DevOps + chaos engineering scale to **billions of users**.

> **Final Exam Mnemonic — "M-A-C-D-S-O"**
> **M**icroservices, **A**WS, **C**haos Monkey, **D**evOps culture, **S**ervice Discovery (Eureka), **O**pen Source ecosystem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Maintenance and Its Types - Adaptive, Preventive, Corrective, and Perfective Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software Maintenance and Its Types - Adaptive, Preventive, Corrective, and Perfective Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU/IEEE Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE Standard 1219 – Standard for Maintenance of Software** defines **Software Maintenance** as:
>
> *"The modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, to improve performance or other attributes, or to adapt the product to a modified environment."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (aligned with **ISO/IEC 14764:2022 – Software Life Cycle Processes – Maintenance**), software maintenance is one of the primary process categories of the software lifecycle. It encompasses all activities required to keep a software system operational, relevant, and acceptable to its end users **after the system has been delivered and deployed into production**.

Maintenance is **NOT** a single activity — it is a **continuous, structured engineering discipline** that consumes, on average, **60% to 80%** of the total software lifecycle cost (TLC). For a typical long-lived enterprise system, this ratio can climb as high as **90%**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car That Never Leaves the Garage"

Imagine you buy a brand-new car in 2020. The car runs perfectly — but life does not stop:

| Year | What Happens | Real-World Action | Software Equivalent |
|------|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 2020 | New car, smooth ride | — | Initial Release (v1.0) |
| 2021 | Government mandates a new pollution norm (BS6) | Mechanic installs a new catalytic converter | **Adaptive Maintenance** (OS upgrade, API change) |
| 2022 | A rattle appears in the door | Mechanic tightens the hinge | **Corrective Maintenance** (Bug fix) |
| 2023 | You want a better infotainment system | Mechanic installs a touchscreen | **Perfective Maintenance** (New feature) |
| 2024 | Tyres show wear; replace before blowout | Mechanic rotates/aligns tyres proactively | **Preventive Maintenance** (Refactoring, re-architecting) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Car Analogy" Key Insight:** Just as a car is *never really finished* once it leaves the showroom, software is *never really finished* once it goes live. The user environment, business rules, hardware, and expectations all change around it — and the software must change with them, or it dies.

---

## 1.3 Why Maintenance Exists — The Three Big Reasons

1. **The Environment Changes** — Operating systems upgrade, hardware refreshes, regulatory laws change, third-party APIs deprecate.
2. **The Users Change** — Stakeholders request new features; usability feedback drives redesigns.
3. **The Software Itself Degrades** — Without intervention, complexity grows, latent defects surface, and quality *declines over time* (Lehman's Law of Declining Quality).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If asked *"Why is software maintenance necessary even after thorough testing?"*, your model answer should reference **Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution** (covered in §3) and the fact that **requirements, environment, and user expectations are never static**.

---

## 1.4 Position of Maintenance in the SDLC

Maintenance is the **fifth and longest phase** of the classic waterfall SDLC:

$$
\text{Total Lifecycle Time} = T_{\text{Req}} + T_{\text{Design}} + T_{\text{Code}} + T_{\text{Test}} + \boxed{T_{\text{Maintenance}}}
$$

Typically, $T_{\text{Maintenance}} \gg (T_{\text{Req}} + T_{\text{Design}} + T_{\text{Code}} + T_{\text{Test}})$, often by an order of magnitude for systems older than 5 years.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Types of Software Maintenance

According to **ISO/IEC 14764** and **IEEE 1219**, software maintenance is classified into **four primary types**. These are *not* mutually exclusive — a single change request (CR) can trigger one or more types simultaneously.

### 2.1.1 Adaptive Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Modification of a software product to keep it usable in a **changed or changing environment** (hardware, software, data, or regulatory environment).

**Triggering Events:**
- Operating system upgrade (Windows 10 → Windows 11)
- Migration to a new database engine (Oracle → PostgreSQL)
- New government compliance law (e.g., GDPR, DPDP Act 2023)
- Third-party API deprecation (Twitter API v1 → v2)
- Hardware platform change (x86 → ARM)

**Engineering Tasks Involved:**
- Impact analysis on existing modules
- Porting of platform-specific code
- Configuration management updates
- Re-validation and regression testing

**KTU Board Example:** *"Modify a payroll system to support the new Income Tax slab rates announced in the Union Budget."*

---

### 2.1.2 Corrective Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Reactive modification of a software product to **correct discovered faults** — errors in coding, design, or logic — that cause the system to fail or behave incorrectly.

**Triggering Events:**
- Bugs reported by end users
- Defects surfaced by production monitoring
- Edge-case failures discovered under load
- Security vulnerabilities (zero-day exploits)

**Engineering Tasks Involved:**
- Defect reproduction in dev environment
- Root-cause analysis (5-Whys, Fishbone)
- Patch development and unit testing
- Hot-fix deployment (sometimes with rollback)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Corrective maintenance is *not* limited to runtime bugs. It also includes **latent design defects** that surface only after months of operation.

**KTU Board Example:** *"Fix the 'divide-by-zero' error in the billing module that crashes the system on the 31st of every month."*

---

### 2.1.3 Perfective Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Modification of a software product to **improve or enhance performance, usability, or other non-functional attributes**, OR to add new functionality requested by users.

**Triggering Events:**
- New feature requests from product owners
- Performance optimization requirements
- UI/UX redesign based on user feedback
- Database query tuning
- Adding new reports or dashboards

**Engineering Tasks Involved:**
- Requirements elicitation for new features
- Re-design of affected modules
- Implementation, integration, and acceptance testing
- User training and documentation updates

> [!NOTE]
> **Highest Cost Contributor:** Empirically, **Perfective Maintenance accounts for the largest share (~50%)** of total maintenance effort in most enterprise systems (Lientz & Swanson 1980; confirmed by Glass 2002).

**KTU Board Example:** *"Add a 'Download as PDF' option to the invoice generation page."*

---

### 2.1.4 Preventive Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Modification of a software product to **detect and correct latent faults BEFORE they become operational failures**; also includes re-structuring for future maintainability.

**Triggering Events:**
- Code refactoring to reduce technical debt
- Software re-documentation
- Optimization of algorithms to prevent future bottlenecks
- Migration to modern frameworks *before* legacy support ends
- Addition of unit tests, integration tests

**Engineering Tasks Involved:**
- Static code analysis (SonarQube, ESLint)
- Code smell detection and refactoring
- Test-coverage improvement
- Architecture modernization (monolith → microservices)

**KTU Board Example:** *"Refactor the legacy authentication module to use a modern JWT-based approach before the EOL of the old library."*

---

## 2.2 Maintenance Effort Distribution — Industry Benchmarks

> [!NOTE]
> The classic **Lientz & Swanson 1980** study, replicated multiple times since, gives the following indicative distribution of maintenance effort:

| Maintenance Type | Approx. % of Total Maintenance Effort | Nature | Trigger Source |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Perfective** | **~50%** | Proactive (user-driven) | User / Business |
| **Adaptive** | **~20%** | Reactive (environment) | External Environment |
| **Corrective** | **~20%** | Reactive (defect) | Faults / Defects |
| **Preventive** | **~10%** (or less) | Proactive (engineering) | Engineering Team |
| **Total** | **100%** | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Watch:** KTU often asks students to **rank maintenance types by effort**. The correct order (highest to lowest) is: **Perfective > Adaptive ≈ Corrective > Preventive**. Memorize this ranking — it is a guaranteed 2-mark question.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units | Used For |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Total Maintenance Cost | $C_{M} = C_{\text{perf}} + C_{\text{adapt}} + C_{\text{cor}} + C_{\text{prev}}$ | ₹ / $ / person-months | Effort budgeting |
| 2 | Maintenance Effort Share | $E_{M}\% = \dfrac{C_{M}}{C_{\text{TLC}}} \times 100$ | % | Lifecycle cost analysis |
| 3 | Perfective Share | $P_{\%} = \dfrac{C_{\text{perf}}}{C_{M}} \times 100 \approx 50\%$ | % | Effort distribution |
| 4 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours / days | Reliability modelling |
| 5 | Software Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Probability of no failure in [0,t] |
| 6 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Operational uptime |
| 7 | Failure Rate (Hazard) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | failures per unit time | Risk assessment |
| 8 | Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | hours / days | Maintainability modelling |
| 9 | Maintenance Productivity | $P_{M} = \dfrac{\text{KLOC maintained}}{\text{person-months}}$ | KLOC / person-month | Team efficiency |
| 10 | Annual Maintenance Cost (AMC) | $\text{AMC} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{Dev}}$ | ₹ / $ | Where $\alpha \approx 0.15$ to $0.25$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
> - All formulas assume the system is in the **operational/maintenance phase**, not the development phase.
> - The reliability formula $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ assumes **exponential failure distribution** — valid for software that is in *useful operation* (not the "burn-in" period).
> - MTTF + MTTR together equal **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures): $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Maintenance Activity | Engineering Outcome |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Banking (Finacle, Core Banking Systems) | Adaptive — RBI regulation change | Compliance preserved |
| E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart) | Perfective — new "Wishlist" feature | Revenue ↑ |
| Healthcare (Hospital Information System) | Corrective — drug dosage calc bug | Patient safety ↑ |
| Aviation (Flight Control Software) | Preventive — DO-178C updates | Catastrophic failure avoided |
| Telecom (BSNL/Reliance OSS) | Adaptive — 4G → 5G migration | Network continuity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Lehman Software Evolution Laws — Full Derivation Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Meir Lehman** and **Laszlo Belady** observed long-lived OS/360 systems at IBM (1974–1996) and formulated **8 Laws of Software Evolution**. These laws are the *theoretical foundation* justifying why maintenance is necessary and inevitable.

### Derivation of Lehman's Three Most-Cited Laws

#### Law 1: Continuing Change
**Statement:** *"A program that is used in a real-world environment must change or become progressively less useful in that environment."*

**Reasoning Chain:**
1. A software system $S$ exists in environment $E$.
2. $E$ is a non-static, dynamic entity (users, regulations, hardware evolve).
3. The *fitness* of $S$ for $E$ is a function $F(S, E, t)$ where $t$ is time.
4. If $S$ is held constant and $E$ changes, $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial t} < 0$.
5. Therefore, $S$ must be modified to track $E$: $\dfrac{dS}{dt} \neq 0$.

#### Law 2: Increasing Complexity
**Statement:** *"As a program evolves, its complexity increases unless work is actively done to maintain or reduce it."*

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\dfrac{dC}{dt} > 0 \quad \text{(per release cycle, unless refactored)}
$$

where $C$ is a complexity metric (cyclomatic complexity, Halstead difficulty, etc.).

**Reasoning:** Each new change introduces new branches, states, and dependencies. The information content (entropy) of the system monotonically increases unless entropy is explicitly exported (refactored out) — analogous to the Second Law of Thermodynamics applied to software.

#### Law 3: Declining Quality
**Statement:** *"The quality of a program will appear to be declining unless it is rigorously maintained and adapted to changing operational environments."*

**Reasoning:**
1. Operational quality $Q$ is measured by Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
2. Without intervention, $\lambda$ (failure rate) rises over time as latent defects surface.
3. Therefore $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ **decreases** → quality *appears* to decline.
4. Preventive maintenance restores $\lambda$ to acceptable levels.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Software Reliability Function

Given an exponentially distributed time-to-failure random variable $T$ with rate parameter $\lambda$:

**Step 1:** The PDF of $T$ is

$$
f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Step 2:** The CDF (cumulative distribution function) — probability of failure by time $t$ — is

$$
F(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 3:** The reliability function — probability of *surviving* until time $t$ — is

$$
R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 4:** The expected time to failure (MTTF) is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt \\
&= \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt \\
&= \lambda \cdot \left[ \dfrac{-t e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \Big\vert_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} dt \right] \\
&= \lambda \cdot \left[ 0 + \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} \right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (Example):** A system has an observed failure rate of $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour. Then:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
R(100) = e^{-0.002 \times 100} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

Thus, the probability that the system runs without failure for 100 hours is **81.87%**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maintenance Effort Distribution Verification

Using **Lientz & Swanson 1980** empirical data of 487 data processing organizations:

| Type | Reported % of Effort | Symbol |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Perfective | 50% | $p$ |
| Adaptive | 20% | $a$ |
| Corrective | 20% | $c$ |
| Preventive | 10% | $v$ |

**Verification Step:**

$$
p + a + c + v = 50 + 20 + 20 + 10 = 100\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The distribution is normalized — a valid probability mass function. Therefore, the expected effort share for any *single* maintenance activity of type $X$ is:

$$
E[\text{Effort}_X] = \dfrac{X\%}{100} \cdot C_{M}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"What type of maintenance consumes the most effort?"*, your one-line answer must be: *"Perfective maintenance, accounting for approximately 50% of total maintenance effort (Lientz & Swanson, 1980)."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree: "Which Type of Maintenance?"

> [!NOTE]
> Use this decision matrix during exam revision. It maps *triggers* to *maintenance types* in a deterministic way.

| Trigger Event | Question to Ask | Maintenance Type |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| User reports a crash / wrong output | *"Did the system do something wrong?"* | **Corrective** |
| User requests a new report / feature | *"Does the user want something new?"* | **Perfective** |
| OS upgrade / new hardware | *"Did the environment change?"* | **Adaptive** |
| Legacy code is hard to read | *"Will this code cause future bugs?"* | **Preventive** |
| New tax law / regulation | *"Did an external rule change?"* | **Adaptive** |
| Performance is slow | *"Is the system meeting NFRs?"* | **Perfective** |
| Security patch (after exploit) | *"Is this a known defect?"* | **Corrective** |
| Refactoring to reduce tech debt | *"Are we preventing future issues?"* | **Preventive** |

---

## 3.5 Maintenance Process Model (ISO/IEC 14764)

The maintenance process is a closed-loop consisting of **8 activities**:

1. **Process Implementation** — Establish the maintenance plan and infrastructure.
2. **Problem & Modification Analysis** — Analyse the change request (CR).
3. **Modification Implementation** — Design, code, and test the change.
4. **Maintenance Review/Acceptance** — Validate the change with the user.
5. **Migration** — Deploy the new release to production.
6. **Software Retirement** — End-of-life procedures (when applicable).
7. **Process Improvement** — Lessons learned, update maintenance plan.
8. **Software System Understanding** — Program comprehension activities.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Master Taxonomy of Software Maintenance Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SOFTWARE MAINTENANCE]:::root --> B[ADAPTIVE]:::adapt
    A --> C[CORRECTIVE]:::corr
    A --> D[PERFECTIVE]:::perf
    A --> E[PREVENTIVE]:::prev

    B --> B1[OS Hardware Upgrade]
    B --> B2[New Regulation Law]
    B --> B3[Third Party API Change]
    B --> B4[Database Migration]

    C --> C1[Bug Fixes]
    C --> C2[Security Patches]
    C --> C3[Logic Error Correction]
    C --> C4[Performance Crash Fix]

    D --> D1[New Feature Addition]
    D --> D2[UI UX Redesign]
    D --> D3[New Reports]
    D --> D4[Performance Tuning]

    E --> E1[Code Refactoring]
    E --> E2[Documentation Update]
    E --> E3[Architecture Modernization]
    E --> E4[Test Coverage Boost]

    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef adapt fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef corr fill:#533483,stroke:#ff6b6b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef perf fill:#16213e,stroke:#f9b208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef prev fill:#1b262c,stroke:#3282b8,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Software Maintenance Process Flow (ISO/IEC 14764)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Change Request CR Received]:::input --> ANA[Problem and Modification Analysis]:::step
    ANA --> IMP[Modification Implementation]:::step
    IMP --> TEST[Regression and Unit Testing]:::step
    TEST --> REV[Maintenance Review and Acceptance]:::step
    REV --> MIG[Migration to Production]:::step
    MIG --> MON[Production Monitoring]:::step
    MON --> PI[Process Improvement Feedback Loop]:::feedback
    PI --> ANA

    MON -.-> RET[Software Retirement EOL]:::retire

    classDef input fill:#3a0ca3,stroke:#f72585,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef step fill:#4361ee,stroke:#4cc9f0,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef feedback fill:#f72585,stroke:#b5179e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef retire fill:#6c757d,stroke:#adb5bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Maintenance Effort Distribution Pie-Style Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Total Maintenance Effort C sub M equals 100 percent]:::start
    START --> P[PERFECTIVE 50 percent]:::perf
    START --> A[ADAPTIVE 20 percent]:::adapt
    START --> C[CORRECTIVE 20 percent]:::corr
    START --> PV[PREVENTIVE 10 percent]:::prev

    P --> P1[New Features]
    P --> P2[Enhancements]
    A --> A1[Environment Change]
    A --> A2[Regulatory Update]
    C --> C1[Bug Fixes]
    C --> C2[Defect Repair]
    PV --> PV1[Refactoring]
    PV --> PV2[Re-architecting]

    classDef start fill:#22223b,stroke:#f2e9e4,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef perf fill:#c9ada7,stroke:#22223b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef adapt fill:#9a8c98,stroke:#22223b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef corr fill:#4a4e69,stroke:#f2e9e4,stroke-width:2px
    classDef prev fill:#22223b,stroke:#c9ada7,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Lehman Software Evolution Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REL[Initial Release V1.0]:::release --> USE[Use in Real World]:::use
    USE --> ENV[Environment Changes Users Change Tech Changes]:::env
    ENV --> DEC[Quality Declines Complexity Rises]:::decline
    DEC --> MAINT{Maintenance Type Decision}:::decision
    MAINT --> ADAPT[Adaptive Maintenance]:::adapt
    MAINT --> CORR[Corrective Maintenance]:::corr
    MAINT --> PERF[Perfective Maintenance]:::perf
    MAINT --> PREV[Preventive Maintenance]:::prev
    ADAPT --> NEXT[Next Release V1.1]:::next
    CORR --> NEXT
    PERF --> NEXT
    PREV --> NEXT
    NEXT --> USE

    classDef release fill:#1d3557,stroke:#a8dadc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef use fill:#457b9d,stroke:#f1faee,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef env fill:#e63946,stroke:#f1faee,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decline fill:#f4a261,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef adapt fill:#264653,stroke:#2a9d8f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef corr fill:#e76f51,stroke:#f4a261,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef perf fill:#f4a261,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef prev fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef next fill:#1d3557,stroke:#e63946,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Maintenance Cost vs Time (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Year 0 Development Phase]:::dev --> T1[Year 1 Maintenance Begins]:::m1
    T1 --> T2[Year 2 Adaptive Spikes]:::m2
    T2 --> T3[Year 3 Perfective Peak]:::m3
    T3 --> T4[Year 4 Corrective Burst]:::m4
    T4 --> T5[Year 5 Preventive Refactor]:::m5
    T5 --> T6[Year 6 Retirement EOL]:::ret

    DEV[Development Cost 100 percent]:::cost1 --> REL[Release 1.0]:::rel
    REL --> ANN[Annual Maintenance Cost AMC equals 15 to 25 percent of Dev Cost]:::amc

    classDef dev fill:#003049,stroke:#d62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef m1 fill:#d62828,stroke:#f77f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef m2 fill:#f77f00,stroke:#fcbf49,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef m3 fill:#fcbf49,stroke:#003049,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef m4 fill:#eae2b7,stroke:#003049,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef m5 fill:#003049,stroke:#fcbf49,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ret fill:#6c757d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cost1 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#073b4c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rel fill:#073b4c,stroke:#118ab2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef amc fill:#ef476f,stroke:#073b4c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** Define **software maintenance** as per **IEEE 1219** standard. List any **four types** of software maintenance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** According to **IEEE Standard 1219**, software maintenance is defined as:
>
> *"The modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, to improve performance or other attributes, or to adapt the product to a modified environment."*
>
> **Four Types (1 mark — ¼ each):**
> 1. **Adaptive Maintenance** — Adapts software to environmental changes.
> 2. **Corrective Maintenance** — Fixes discovered faults and bugs.
> 3. **Perfective Maintenance** — Adds new features / improves performance.
> 4. **Preventive Maintenance** — Refactors to prevent future failures.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** State **Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution** relevant to maintenance. Explain **any two** briefly.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Laws (1 mark):** Lehman formulated **8 laws**. The most cited are:
> 1. Continuing Change
> 2. Increasing Complexity
> 3. Declining Quality
> 4. Conservation of Organizational Stability
>
> **Explanation of any two (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
>
> - **Continuing Change:** A program used in a real environment must continually change, else it becomes progressively less useful.
> - **Declining Quality:** The perceived quality of software declines over time unless rigorously maintained and adapted.
> - **Increasing Complexity:** Per release, system complexity grows unless work is done to reduce it.

---

## 5.2 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means you attempt **either Q-A or Q-B**.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** *(a)* Explain in detail the **four types of software maintenance** with suitable examples for each. Discuss how the **maintenance effort is distributed** across these types as per industry studies. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Define **software reliability**. Derive the **software reliability function** $R(t)$ from the failure time PDF, and compute the **MTTF** of a system whose observed failure rate is $\lambda = 0.005$ failures/hour. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]

**1. Four Types of Maintenance (1 mark each = 4 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Real Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Adaptive** | Modifying software to keep it usable in a changed environment | Migrating a web app from PHP 5 to PHP 8 |
| **Corrective** | Reactive modification to fix discovered faults | Patching a Y2K-style date overflow bug |
| **Perfective** | Enhancing performance or adding new functionality requested by users | Adding a "Forgot Password" feature |
| **Preventive** | Modifying software to detect/correct latent faults before they become operational | Refactoring legacy spaghetti code into clean modules |

**2. Effort Distribution (3 marks):**

According to the **Lientz & Swanson 1980** study (replicated by Glass 2002 and others) on 487 organizations:

- **Perfective Maintenance:** ~50% of total maintenance effort
- **Adaptive Maintenance:** ~20%
- **Corrective Maintenance:** ~20%
- **Preventive Maintenance:** ~10% (often the least prioritized)

> **Conclusion:** Perfective maintenance dominates because user-driven enhancements and new features are a continuous demand throughout the system's life, while preventive maintenance is often *deferred* due to budget pressure — leading to higher long-term cost (a paradox of "pay-now-or-pay-more-later").

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]

**1. Definition of Software Reliability (2 marks):**

> Software reliability is defined as the **probability that a software system will perform its intended function without failure under specified conditions for a specified period of time**.

It is measured in terms of **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) and the **reliability function $R(t)$**.

**2. Derivation of $R(t)$ (4 marks):**

Given failure time $T$ with PDF $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t \geq 0$:

**Step 1:** CDF — probability of failure by time $t$:

$$
F(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2:** Reliability function — probability of *survival* beyond time $t$:

$$
R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 3:** MTTF — expected time to first failure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt \\
&= \lambda \cdot \left[ \dfrac{-t e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \Big\vert_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} dt \right] \\
&= \lambda \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Numerical Computation (1 mark):**

Given $\lambda = 0.005$ failures/hour:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.005} = 200 \text{ hours}
$$

The system is expected to run **200 hours** between consecutive failures on average.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** *(a)* Discuss **Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution** in detail. How do these laws justify the **necessity of software maintenance**? *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Differentiate between the **four types of software maintenance** using a **comparison matrix**. Also explain the **ISO/IEC 14764 maintenance process model** with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**Lehman's Eight Laws (5 laws × 1 mark = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for justification):**

1. **Continuing Change** — A program used in a real environment must continually change, else it becomes less useful.
2. **Increasing Complexity** — Per release, complexity grows unless actively reduced.
3. **Self-Regulation** — Software evolution processes are self-regulating with measurable invariants.
4. **Conservation of Organizational Stability** — Average effective global activity rate is invariant over product lifetime.
5. **Conservation of Familiarity** — Incremental growth in release content is largely invariant.
6. **Continuing Growth** — Functional content must continually increase to maintain user satisfaction.
7. **Declining Quality** — Quality will *appear* to decline unless rigorously maintained.
8. **Feedback System** — Evolution is a multi-level feedback loop.

**Justification of Necessity (2 marks):**

These laws prove that maintenance is *not optional* — it is a *natural consequence* of the system existing in a dynamic environment. Stop maintenance, and the system dies (Law 1), becomes unmaintainable (Law 2), or is rejected by users (Law 7). Hence, maintenance is the *lifeblood* of any long-lived software product.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**1. Comparison Matrix (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Adaptive | Corrective | Perfective | Preventive |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Trigger** | Environment change | Bug discovered | User request | Latent defect risk |
| **Nature** | Reactive | Reactive | Proactive (user-driven) | Proactive (engineering) |
| **% Effort** | ~20% | ~20% | ~50% | ~10% |
| **Stakeholder** | IT/Ops | QA / Customer | Product Owner | Dev Team |
| **Example** | OS upgrade | Crash fix | New feature | Refactoring |
| **Urgency** | High | High (if critical) | Medium | Low |
| **Risk** | Medium | High (regression) | Low | Low |

**2. ISO/IEC 14764 Process (3 marks):**

The 8 activities of the maintenance process are:

1. **Process Implementation** — Define the maintenance plan.
2. **Problem & Modification Analysis** — Analyse the change request.
3. **Modification Implementation** — Code, design, and test the change.
4. **Maintenance Review / Acceptance** — User validates the fix.
5. **Migration** — Deploy to production.
6. **Software Retirement** — EOL handling.
7. **Process Improvement** — Lessons learned.
8. **System Understanding** — Code comprehension and reverse-engineering.

```
[Block Diagram — ASCII Fallback]

   ┌──────────────┐
   │ Change Req.  │
   └──────┬───────┘
          ▼
   ┌──────────────────────┐
   │ Problem Analysis     │
   └──────┬───────────────┘
          ▼
   ┌──────────────────────┐
   │ Modify + Test        │
   └──────┬───────────────┘
          ▼
   ┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐
   │ Review / Acceptance  │────────▶│ Production   │
   └──────┬───────────────┘         └──────┬───────┘
          ▼                                ▼
   ┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐
   │ Process Improvement  │◀────────│  Monitor     │
   └──────────────────────┘         └──────────────┘
```

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Corrective with Adaptive:** A bug in code is **Corrective**; a porting required by a new OS is **Adaptive**. Mixing these up loses 2-3 marks instantly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 4th type — Preventive:** Many students list only 3 types. **Always mention all four** (Adaptive, Corrective, Perfective, Preventive) — KTU examiners specifically check for completeness.
>
> 3. **Wrong effort ranking:** The correct ranking (highest to lowest) is: **Perfective > Adaptive ≈ Corrective > Preventive**. Writing "Corrective is the most" is a common error — it is **NOT** the most.
>
> 4. **Missing the standard reference:** Always cite **IEEE 1219 / ISO/IEC 14764** in your definition. Examiners award bonus marks for standard references.
>
> 5. **Skipping the derivation in part-b questions:** If the question says "derive", you MUST show step-by-step math. Skipping the integral or assuming $R(t)$ without proof loses 4-5 marks.
>
> 6. **Not using the word "maintenance" in the first line of the answer:** Start every maintenance answer with: *"Software maintenance, as defined by IEEE 1219..."* — this signals to the examiner that you know the standard.
>
> 7. **Confusing MTTF, MTTR, and MTBF:**
>    - **MTTF** = Mean Time To Failure (reliability)
>    - **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (maintainability)
>    - **MTBF** = MTTF + MTTR (overall availability)
>
> 8. **In diagrams, using too few boxes:** Always include **all 8 activities** of the ISO/IEC 14764 process in process-flow questions. Skipping "Retirement" loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

### 🎯 Core Concepts
- ✅ **Definition:** Software maintenance = modification of a software product *after delivery* (IEEE 1219).
- ✅ **Cost Share:** Maintenance consumes **60%–80%** of total lifecycle cost.
- ✅ **Standard:** ISO/IEC 14764 governs the maintenance process.

### 🎯 Four Types — Memorize in Order
1. **🟦 Adaptive** — environment-driven, ~20% effort (e.g., OS upgrade, new tax law).
2. **🟥 Corrective** — defect-driven, ~20% effort (e.g., bug fix, security patch).
3. **🟨 Perfective** — feature-driven, **~50% effort — THE HIGHEST** (e.g., new feature, performance tuning).
4. **🟩 Preventive** — quality-driven, ~10% effort (e.g., refactoring, re-documentation).

### 🎯 Key Formulas
- ✅ $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (reliability function)
- ✅ $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$
- ✅ $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$
- ✅ $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$

### 🎯 Lehman's Laws (Top 5 to Remember)
1. Continuing Change
2. Increasing Complexity
3. Declining Quality
4. Conservation of Organizational Stability
5. Conservation of Familiarity

### 🎯 ISO/IEC 14764 — 8 Process Activities
1. Process Implementation
2. Problem & Modification Analysis
3. Modification Implementation
4. Maintenance Review / Acceptance
5. Migration
6. Software Retirement
7. Process Improvement
8. System Understanding

### 🎯 Effort Distribution Mnemonic
> **"PAPA loves Pizza"** → **P**erfective (50) + **A**daptive (20) + **P**reventive (10) + **A**daptive (20, with Corrective) = Corrective (20).
>
> Better: **"PACA-P"** → **P**erfective, **A**daptive, **C**orrective, **A**daptive-mix, **P**reventive.
>
> Simplest: **"PAC-P"** where **P=50, A=20, C=20, P=10**.

### 🎯 One-Line Definitions for Quick Recall
- **Adaptive:** "Change with the world."
- **Corrective:** "Fix what broke."
- **Perfective:** "Add what users want."
- **Preventive:** "Avoid what might break."

### 🎯 Real-World Examples (Use in Answers for Bonus Marks)
- Adaptive: Bank software updating for new RBI KYC rules.
- Corrective: Fixing a "session timeout" bug in an e-commerce app.
- Perfective: Adding "Pay with UPI" to an old payment gateway.
- Preventive: Migrating a COBOL system to Java *before* COBOL vendor support ends.

### 🎯 Last-Minute Warning
> **Never write "Maintenance is fixing bugs" alone — that is only 1 out of 4 types. Always enumerate all four.**

---

**📚 End of Module 3 Notes — Software Maintenance and Its Types**

topics.title: Boehm's Maintenance Models (Legacy and Non-legacy)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boehm's Maintenance Models (Legacy and Non-legacy)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Boehm's Maintenance Model** is a quantitative cost-estimation framework, originally proposed by **Dr. Barry W. Boehm** in his 1981 work *Software Engineering Economics* and later refined in **COCOMO II (1995–2000)**, used to estimate the **person-months of effort** required to maintain, adapt, enhance, or correct software after it has been delivered. Within COCOMO II, the maintenance model is treated as a **post-architecture cost driver** that augments the standard effort equation with factors accounting for **software understanding, adaptation overhead, and annual maintenance decay**.

The model formally distinguishes between:
- **Legacy Maintenance Model** — maintenance of an existing, possibly undocumented, possibly outdated software system (e.g., COBOL mainframe applications, pre-Y2K code).
- **Non-Legacy (Modern) Maintenance Model** — maintenance of well-documented, modern, modular systems designed for evolvability (e.g., microservices, cloud-native apps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag (CO3):** Apply software cost estimation models including COCOMO II maintenance extensions to real-world project scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are a **mechanic repairing two different cars**:

| Aspect | Legacy Car (1970s Ambassador) | Non-Legacy Car (2024 Tesla) |
|---|---|---|
| Original Blueprints | Possibly lost or incomplete | Full digital schematics available |
| Spare Parts | Hard to find, custom fabrication | Standardized, plug-and-play |
| Diagnostic Tools | Manual inspection required | OBD-II port + AI diagnostics |
| Maintenance Effort | **High** (reverse-engineering needed) | **Low** (modular swap & OTA update) |

Boehm's Maintenance Model works exactly like this — it **multiplies the base effort by a "frustration factor"** that depends on how easy the system is to understand and how much rework is required. A legacy system is the **"Ambassador"** — high effort, high uncertainty; a non-legacy system is the **"Tesla"** — modular, documented, cheap to maintain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea in One Line:** *Maintenance is not just "coding more lines" — it is "understanding existing lines + adapting them + correcting decay over time."*

## 1.3 Why Maintenance Matters (Industry Context)

- **60–80% of total software lifecycle cost** is spent on **maintenance** (industry-standard figure, corroborated by multiple IEEE studies).
- Maintenance is the **largest single cost bucket** in any long-lived software system.
- Accurate maintenance estimation directly impacts **IT budgeting, sprint planning, and SLA contracts**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The COCOMO II Maintenance Framework — Layered View

Boehm's COCOMO II is **not a single equation** — it is a **suite of nested sub-models**:

1. **Application Composition Model** — for rapid prototyping (early phase).
2. **Early Design Model** — for ballpark estimates before architecture is fixed.
3. **Post-Architecture Model** — for detailed estimates after the architecture is stable.
4. **Reuse Model** — for code/components obtained from libraries or prior projects.
5. **Maintenance Model** — for post-deployment enhancement and corrective work.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Maintenance Model** sits on top of the **Reuse Model** and the **Post-Architecture Model**. It does not replace them — it *augments* them with maintenance-specific multipliers.

## 2.2 The Maintenance Model — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Compute the "Adapted Assembly" (AA)

The Adapted Assembly represents the **base effort** to understand and reuse existing code, before applying the maintenance overhead.

$$
\text{AA} = \left(\text{ASLOC} \times \frac{\text{AAT}}{100}\right) + \text{DSU}
$$

Where:
- **ASLOC** = Adapted Source Lines of Code (thousands).
- **AAT** = Adapted Assembly Percentage (0–100).
- **DSU** = Difficult Software Understanding percentage (0–100).

### Step 2: Apply the Adaptation Adjustment (AT)

This accounts for **rewriting and re-testing** the adapted code:

$$
\text{AM}_\text{base} = \text{AA} \times \left(1 + \frac{\text{AT}}{100}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Annual Adjustment Multiplier (AAM)

This accounts for **personnel turnover, process decay, and tooling changes** over the years:

$$
\text{AM} = \text{AM}_\text{base} \times \text{AAM}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Maintenance Effort Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\text{AM} = \left[\left(\text{ASLOC} \times \frac{\text{AAT}}{100}\right) + \text{DSU}\right] \times \left(1 + \frac{\text{AT}}{100}\right) \times \text{AAM}}
$$

All output is in **person-months**.

## 2.3 The Legacy vs. Non-Legacy Distinction

Boehm's framework handles these two cases through **different input parameter ranges**, not separate equations:

| Parameter | Legacy System | Non-Legacy System |
|---|---|---|
| **DSU** (Difficult Software Understanding) | **40 – 70 %** | **10 – 25 %** |
| **AAT** (Adapted Assembly) | **20 – 50 %** | **60 – 90 %** |
| **AAM** (Annual Adjustment) | **1.2 – 1.8** | **1.0 – 1.2** |
| Documentation Quality | Poor / Missing | Complete & Versioned |
| Architecture | Monolithic / Tightly Coupled | Modular / Loosely Coupled |
| Effort Multiplier | **High** (×1.5 to ×2.0) | **Low** (×1.0 to ×1.3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The **math is identical**; the *parameters* differ. This is why Boehm's model is called a **parametric model** — calibration, not new equations, is what distinguishes legacy from non-legacy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = A \times K^{B + 0.01 \times \sum SF_i} \times \prod EM_j$ | Basic COCOMO II Post-Architecture Effort | **Very High** |
| 2 | $\text{ASLOC} = \text{NCLOC} \times (1 - \%\text{reuse}/100)$ | Reuse-adjusted size | **High** |
| 3 | $\text{AA} = (\text{ASLOC} \times \text{AAT}/100) + \text{DSU}$ | Adapted Assembly base | **High** |
| 4 | $\text{AM} = \text{AA} \times (1 + \text{AT}/100) \times \text{AAM}$ | Final Maintenance Effort | **Highest** |
| 5 | $\text{D} = 0.32 \times E^{0.36}$ | Schedule (months) from COCOMO II | **Medium** |
| 6 | $\text{AAM} = 1 + t \times \alpha$ | Annual Adjustment (t = years) | **Medium** |

> Where **A = 2.94** (COCOMO II Post-Architecture calibration constant), **B = 0.91** (Post-Architecture), **SF_i = 5 scale factors**, **EM_j = 17 effort multipliers**, **K = Size in KLOC**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IT Service Management (ITSM):** Predicting annual maintenance budgets for enterprise ERP systems (SAP, Oracle).
- **Aerospace:** Estimating the cost of FAA-mandated upgrades to avionics software.
- **Banking:** Legacy COBOL-to-Java migration projects rely on Boehm's DSU factor to size reverse-engineering effort.
- **Telecom:** OSS/BSS systems are maintained for 15–25 years — Boehm's AAM captures this decay.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 (Legacy System):** A 20-year-old banking application has **30 KLOC** of poorly documented code. The maintenance team plans to reuse 20% from a library, adapt 30% of new code, and the DSU factor is 50%. Annual turnover is high. Estimate the maintenance effort.

### Step 1 — Compute ASLOC (Reuse Adjustment)

$$
\text{ASLOC} = \text{NCLOC} \times \left(1 - \frac{\%\text{reuse}}{100}\right)
$$

Substituting NCLOC = 30 KLOC, %reuse = 20:

$$
\text{ASLOC} = 30 \times \left(1 - \frac{20}{100}\right) = 30 \times 0.8
$$

$$
\text{ASLOC} = 24 \text{ KLOC}
$$

**Logic:** Out of 30 KLOC of new/maintained code, 20% is reused from a library, leaving 80% (24 KLOC) as the *adapted* size.

### Step 2 — Compute Adapted Assembly (AA)

$$
\text{AA} = \left(\text{ASLOC} \times \frac{\text{AAT}}{100}\right) + \text{DSU}
$$

Substituting ASLOC = 24, AAT = 30, DSU = 50:

$$
\text{AA} = \left(24 \times \frac{30}{100}\right) + 50
$$

$$
\text{AA} = 7.2 + 50
$$

$$
\text{AA} = 57.2 \text{ person-months (base)}
$$

**Logic:** Of the 24 KLOC adapted, 30% (≈7.2 KLOC) requires substantial reassembly, and the legacy code's poor understandability adds an effective overhead of 50 PM.

### Step 3 — Apply the Adaptation Adjustment (AT = 40%)

$$
\text{AM}_\text{base} = \text{AA} \times \left(1 + \frac{\text{AT}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
\text{AM}_\text{base} = 57.2 \times \left(1 + \frac{40}{100}\right) = 57.2 \times 1.4
$$

$$
\text{AM}_\text{base} = 80.08 \text{ person-months}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Annual Adjustment Multiplier (AAM = 1.5)

$$
\text{AM} = \text{AM}_\text{base} \times \text{AAM}
$$

$$
\text{AM} = 80.08 \times 1.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AM} = 120.12 \text{ person-months}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Maintaining this legacy system for one cycle requires approximately **10 person-years** of effort — a classic result showing why organizations budget so heavily for legacy maintenance.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 (Non-Legacy System):** A modern, well-documented microservice of 15 KLOC is being maintained. Reuse = 60%, AAT = 80%, DSU = 15%, AT = 20%, AAM = 1.1. Estimate effort.

### Step 1 — ASLOC

$$
\text{ASLOC} = 15 \times (1 - 0.60) = 15 \times 0.40 = 6 \text{ KLOC}
$$

### Step 2 — AA

$$
\text{AA} = \left(6 \times \frac{80}{100}\right) + 15 = 4.8 + 15 = 19.8 \text{ PM}
$$

### Step 3 — AM_base

$$
\text{AM}_\text{base} = 19.8 \times \left(1 + \frac{20}{100}\right) = 19.8 \times 1.2 = 23.76 \text{ PM}
$$

### Step 4 — Final AM

$$
\text{AM} = 23.76 \times 1.1 = 26.136 \text{ PM}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AM} \approx 26.14 \text{ person-months}}
$$

**Comparative Insight:** For similar *raw code sizes* (15 vs 30 KLOC — only 2× difference), the legacy system consumes **~4.6× more effort** (120.12 vs 26.14 PM). This empirically proves the **maintenance multiplier effect** that Boehm's model captures.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 3 — Derivation of Annual Adjustment Multiplier (AAM):**

The AAM captures the **decay function** of maintainer productivity over years. Boehm proposed a linear approximation:

$$
\text{AAM} = 1 + (t - 1) \times \alpha
$$

Where:
- $t$ = calendar year of the maintenance phase (1, 2, 3, …).
- $\alpha$ = annual decay rate (typically **0.05 to 0.10** for legacy, **0.01 to 0.03** for non-legacy).

**Derivation (linear decay assumption):**

Assume productivity in year 1 is $P_1 = 1.0$ (normalized). In year 2, productivity drops by factor $\alpha$:

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times (1 + \alpha)
$$

In year $t$:

$$
P_t = 1 \times (1 + \alpha)^{t-1}
$$

For small $\alpha$, using the first-order Taylor expansion $(1 + \alpha)^{t-1} \approx 1 + (t-1)\alpha$:

$$
P_t \approx 1 + (t-1)\alpha
$$

Hence the **AAM formula** is recovered.

**Numerical Illustration:** For $\alpha = 0.05$, $t = 5$ (legacy, 5th year):

$$
\text{AAM} = 1 + (5 - 1) \times 0.05 = 1 + 0.20 = 1.20
$$

A 20% effort inflation is applied — empirically observed in long-lived projects.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Boehm's COCOMO II Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Software Project] --> B{Which Phase?}
    B -->|Early Prototyping| C[Application Composition Model]
    B -->|Early Design| D[Early Design Model]
    B -->|Detailed Design / Construction| E[Post-Architecture Model]
    B -->|Post-Delivery Maintenance| F[Maintenance Model]
    C --> G[Effort Estimate PM]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    E --> H[Reuse Model]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Adapted Assembly AA]
    I --> J[Adaptation Adjustment AT]
    J --> K[Annual Adjustment AAM]
    K --> L[Final Maintenance Effort AM]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Maintenance Model Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Input NCLOC and Reuse Percent] --> node2[Compute ASLOC]
    node2 --> node3[Compute Adapted Assembly AA]
    node3 --> node4[Apply AT Adjustment]
    node4 --> node5[Apply AAM Multiplier]
    node5 --> node6[Output AM in Person Months]
    node1A[Input AAT and DSU] --> node3
    node1B[Input AT Percent] --> node4
    node1C[Input AAM or alpha and t] --> node5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Legacy vs Non-Legacy Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Software System] --> doc{Documentation Available?}
    doc -->|No or Partial| leg[Legacy System]
    doc -->|Complete and Versioned| nleg[Non-Legacy System]
    leg --> legparams[DSU High, AAT Low, AAM High]
    nleg --> nlegparams[DSU Low, AAT High, AAM Low]
    legparams --> lout[High Maintenance Effort]
    nlegparams --> nout[Low Maintenance Effort]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Boehm Maintenance Lifecycle (Reengineering Loop)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Legacy Codebase] --> P2[Reverse Engineering]
    P2 --> P3[Reengineering and Restructuring]
    P3 --> P4[Forward Engineering]
    P4 --> P5[Modern Codebase]
    P5 --> P6[Ongoing Maintenance using Boehm Model]
    P6 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **block-level functional flow** above captures how Boehm's model is *operationalized* in real IT environments — legacy systems enter the **reverse → re → forward engineering** loop, then return to the **maintenance model** for ongoing cost forecasting.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Legacy and Non-Legacy software maintenance models in Boehm's framework.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Legacy Maintenance | Non-Legacy Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Documentation | Incomplete / outdated | Complete, version-controlled |
| DSU factor | High (40–70%) | Low (10–25%) |
| Architecture | Monolithic, coupled | Modular, decoupled |
| Reuse | Limited | Heavy (libraries, APIs) |
| Annual Decay (AAM) | High (1.2–1.8) | Low (1.0–1.2) |
| Effort | High (2–4× base) | Low (≈1.0–1.3× base) |

> Both use the **same Boehm equation**; only the *parameter values* differ. This parametric approach is what makes Boehm's model flexible for both classes of systems.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the FIVE sub-models of COCOMO II. Which one is used for maintenance estimation?**

**Model Answer:**

The five sub-models of COCOMO II are:
1. Application Composition Model
2. Early Design Model
3. Post-Architecture Model
4. Reuse Model
5. **Maintenance Model** ← *used for maintenance estimation*

The Maintenance Model uses the equation:
$$
\text{AM} = \text{AA} \times (1 + \text{AT}/100) \times \text{AAM}
$$
and additionally factors in the DSU (Difficult Software Understanding) and AAT (Adapted Assembly Time) parameters from the Reuse Model.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain Boehm's **Maintenance Model** and the role of each of its parameters: ASLOC, AAT, DSU, AT, and AAM. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A legacy banking system has 40 KLOC, 25% reuse, AAT = 40%, DSU = 60%, AT = 35%, and AAM = 1.4. Compute the total maintenance effort in person-months using Boehm's model. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Boehm's Maintenance Model Diagram (text-rendered):**

```
NCLOC + %reuse --> ASLOC --> AA = (ASLOC * AAT/100) + DSU
                                          |
                                          v
                          AM_base = AA * (1 + AT/100)
                                          |
                                          v
                              AM = AM_base * AAM
```

**Parameter Roles:**

1. **ASLOC (Adapted Source Lines of Code):** The number of new or modified lines, *after* adjusting for reused code. $\text{ASLOC} = \text{NCLOC} \times (1 - \%\text{reuse}/100)$.
2. **AAT (Adapted Assembly Time %):** Percentage of the ASLOC that requires substantial reassembly/rewriting. Higher AAT → more rework.
3. **DSU (Difficult Software Understanding %):** Overhead in person-months for *understanding* the existing code. Spikes in legacy systems with poor docs.
4. **AT (Adaptation %):** Additional effort to re-test, re-integrate, and re-document the adapted code. Boehm recommends **20–80%** depending on system age.
5. **AAM (Annual Adjustment Multiplier):** Captures the *decay function* — personnel turnover, tooling changes, and process erosion over the years. $\text{AAM} \geq 1$ always.

**Key Insight:** The model is **multiplicative** — each parameter *amplifies* the base effort. A legacy system with bad docs (high DSU) **and** high turnover (high AAM) compounds the cost dramatically.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** NCLOC = 40, %reuse = 25, AAT = 40, DSU = 60, AT = 35, AAM = 1.4.

**Step 1 — ASLOC:**

$$
\text{ASLOC} = 40 \times \left(1 - \frac{25}{100}\right) = 40 \times 0.75 = 30 \text{ KLOC}
$$

**Step 2 — AA:**

$$
\text{AA} = \left(30 \times \frac{40}{100}\right) + 60 = 12 + 60 = 72 \text{ PM}
$$

**Step 3 — AM_base:**

$$
\text{AM}_\text{base} = 72 \times \left(1 + \frac{35}{100}\right) = 72 \times 1.35 = 97.2 \text{ PM}
$$

**Step 4 — Final AM:**

$$
\text{AM} = 97.2 \times 1.4 = 136.08 \text{ PM}
$$

**Final Answer:** **AM = 136.08 person-months** (≈ 11.3 person-years).

**Interpretation:** This figure is consistent with industry observations that 60–80% of a system's lifecycle cost is maintenance, and legacy banking systems often require 10+ person-years per major maintenance cycle.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the importance of **reverse engineering and reengineering** in legacy system maintenance. How do they relate to Boehm's DSU factor? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A non-legacy system has 20 KLOC, 70% reuse, AAT = 75%, DSU = 20%, AT = 25%, and AAM = 1.1. Compare its maintenance effort with the legacy system in Q-A(b). What conclusions do you draw? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Reverse Engineering:** The process of analyzing an existing system to *recover* its design, architecture, and specifications — typically when original documentation is missing. Tools include **static analyzers, decompilers, and code comprehension tools** (e.g., Doxygen, Understand).

**Reengineering (Restructuring):** The process of *transforming* an existing system into a new form — improving structure, modularity, and maintainability — **while preserving its external behavior**. It includes:
- Code restructuring
- Module decomposition
- Data normalization
- Migration to modern platforms

**Relationship to Boehm's DSU Factor:**

The **DSU (Difficult Software Understanding)** directly quantifies the cost of *comprehending* legacy code. In legacy systems:
- DSU = 40–70% because reverse engineering is **manual, time-consuming, and error-prone**.
- Reengineering efforts **systematically reduce DSU** over time — once code is restructured and documented, the DSU for *subsequent* maintenance cycles drops sharply.
- A mature reengineering program converts a **legacy system** (high DSU) into a **non-legacy system** (low DSU) over multiple release cycles.

**Pedagogical Link:** This is why Boehm's model is *iterative* — each maintenance cycle can include a planned reengineering phase, gradually transforming the system profile.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** NCLOC = 20, %reuse = 70, AAT = 75, DSU = 20, AT = 25, AAM = 1.1.

**Step 1 — ASLOC:**

$$
\text{ASLOC} = 20 \times (1 - 0.70) = 20 \times 0.30 = 6 \text{ KLOC}
$$

**Step 2 — AA:**

$$
\text{AA} = \left(6 \times \frac{75}{100}\right) + 20 = 4.5 + 20 = 24.5 \text{ PM}
$$

**Step 3 — AM_base:**

$$
\text{AM}_\text{base} = 24.5 \times \left(1 + \frac{25}{100}\right) = 24.5 \times 1.25 = 30.625 \text{ PM}
$$

**Step 4 — Final AM:**

$$
\text{AM} = 30.625 \times 1.1 = 33.6875 \text{ PM}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Legacy System (Q-A-b) | Non-Legacy System (Q-B-b) |
|---|---|---|
| NCLOC | 40 KLOC | 20 KLOC |
| Effective Size (ASLOC) | 30 KLOC | 6 KLOC |
| AA | 72 PM | 24.5 PM |
| Final Effort | 136.08 PM | 33.69 PM |
| **Effort per KLOC of NCLOC** | **3.40 PM/KLOC** | **1.68 PM/KLOC** |
| **Effort per KLOC of ASLOC** | **4.54 PM/KLOC** | **5.61 PM/KLOC** |

**Conclusions:**

1. The legacy system requires **~4× more total effort** despite having only **2× more raw code** — confirming the multiplicative impact of DSU and AAM.
2. On a per-KLOC-ASLOC basis, the legacy system is actually **cheaper per adapted line** (4.54 vs 5.61), but its sheer **scale of adaptation** (30 vs 6 KLOC) drives the total cost up.
3. **Practical Takeaway:** Investing in **documentation and modularity up-front** (preventive maintenance) reduces the ASLOC of every future cycle — a powerful ROI argument.
4. **Strategic Implication:** Legacy systems approaching **AM > 100 PM per cycle** should be considered candidates for **full reengineering or replacement** rather than continued maintenance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert NCLOC to ASLOC** — many students plug NCLOC directly into the AA formula, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing AAT and AT** — AAT is the *percentage of code adapted*; AT is the *adaptation overhead multiplier*. They are NOT the same.
> 3. **Omitting AAM in the final step** — AAM is mandatory for maintenance; forgetting it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit declaration** — always state **person-months** explicitly.
> 5. **Writing AAM = 1** without justification — AAM = 1 implies *zero* decay, which is valid only for a brand-new, perfectly stable team. State the assumption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Must Remember for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- [x] **COCOMO II has 5 sub-models** — Application Composition, Early Design, Post-Architecture, Reuse, **Maintenance**.
- [x] **Maintenance Model equation:** $\text{AM} = \text{AA} \times (1 + \text{AT}/100) \times \text{AAM}$
- [x] **AA equation:** $\text{AA} = (\text{ASLOC} \times \text{AAT}/100) + \text{DSU}$
- [x] **ASLOC equation:** $\text{ASLOC} = \text{NCLOC} \times (1 - \%\text{reuse}/100)$
- [x] **AAM** is always $\geq 1$ — captures *decay* over time.
- [x] **Legacy vs Non-Legacy** differs in *parameter values*, not equations:
  - Legacy: high DSU (40–70%), low AAT (20–50%), high AAM (1.2–1.8).
  - Non-Legacy: low DSU (10–25%), high AAT (60–90%), low AAM (1.0–1.2).
- [x] **60–80% of lifecycle cost** is maintenance (industry-standard figure).
- [x] **Reengineering** reduces DSU over multiple maintenance cycles.
- [x] All outputs are in **person-months** unless stated otherwise.
- [x] **COCOMO II constants:** A = 2.94, B = 0.91 (Post-Architecture).
- [x] The Maintenance Model is **multiplicative** — every parameter compounds the others.
- [x] **Lehman's Laws of Evolution** are an *adjacent* but distinct framework — do not confuse with Boehm's model.
- [x] Always declare assumptions (e.g., "assuming AAM = 1.2 due to 4-year-old codebase").
- [x] Practice at least **3 numerical problems** before the exam; marks are concentrated in computation.

---

**End of Notes — Module 3, Boehm's Maintenance Models (Legacy and Non-legacy)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Size Metrics - LOC, Function Points, and Object Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Size Metrics - LOC, Function Points, and Object Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Project Size Metric?

A **Project Size Metric** is a quantitative measurement used to determine the magnitude, complexity, and effort required to develop a software system. It forms the *foundation* of all subsequent software project estimates (cost, schedule, productivity, and quality). Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, you are required to study three primary size-measurement approaches:

1. **Lines of Code (LOC)** — a *direct* (code-based) measure.
2. **Function Points (FP)** — an *indirect* (functionality-based) measure.
3. **Object Points (OP)** — a hybrid measure tailored for object-oriented / 4GL systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Size estimation metrics are the *first link* in the chain of software estimation. You must be able to compute FP from requirement specifications, derive LOC estimates from FP, and articulate the pros/cons of each.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Metric | Analogy | Plain English Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **LOC** | Measuring a building by counting every brick laid. | If two programmers write the same program, the one writing *more lines* is judged to have produced *more code* — hence a "bigger" project. |
| **Function Points** | Measuring a house by the *number of rooms, doors, and windows* it contains, not the bricks. | Instead of counting code lines, we count *user-visible functions* (inputs, outputs, files, queries) the system delivers. |
| **Object Points** | Measuring a website by counting *screens, reports, and reusable components* before any code is written. | Used in early OO/4GL estimation — it counts *screens* (low complexity), *reports* (medium), and *components* (high). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three different metrics?** Each metric was born to overcome the *weakness* of the previous one. LOC is intuitive but language-dependent; FP fixes the language bias but is complex; Object Points simplify FP for 4GL/OO worlds where prototypes are built from pre-built components.

---

## 1.3 Formal KTU Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 1 — Lines of Code (LOC):**
> LOC is the count of *deliverable source instructions* in a program, excluding comments, blank lines, and declarations. Expressed as **KLOC (Kilo Lines of Code)** $= \dfrac{\text{LOC}}{1000}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 2 — Function Point (FP):**
> A Function Point is a unit of measurement that quantifies the *business functionality* a software product provides to a user, independent of the programming language used for implementation. The standard formula is:
> $$\text{FP} = \text{UFC} \times \bigl(0.65 + 0.01 \times \text{TDI}\bigr)$$
> where **UFC = Unadjusted Function Count** and **TDI = Total Degree of Influence** (sum of 14 General System Characteristics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 3 — Object Point (OP):**
> An Object Point is a simplified size unit used for **4GL/OO** systems. It is calculated by counting three types of *visible deliverables*:
> $$\text{OP} = (1 \times \text{Screens}) + (2 \times \text{Reports}) + (3 \times \text{Components})$$
> The result is then used with a *reuse/productivity multiplier* to obtain **New Object Points (NOP)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lines of Code (LOC) — Operational Theory

- **Direct Measure:** It is *physically countable* — it only exists after the code is written (or estimated from design).
- **Key Derivation:** A system is broken into modules → each module's LOC is estimated → summed to give total LOC.
- **Effort/Productivity Linkage:**
  $$\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{KLOC}}{\text{Person-Months}}$$
  $$\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Person-Months}}{\text{KLOC}} \quad \text{(cost per KLOC)}$$
  $$\text{Defects} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{KLOC}} \quad \text{(defect density)}$$
  $$\text{Time} = \dfrac{\text{Person-Months} \times \text{Months}}{\text{KLOC}}$$

### Why use LOC? Why not use LOC?
- **Pros:** Simple, intuitive, easily automated (using `cloc`, `sloccount` tools), language-specific tuning possible.
- **Cons:** Language-dependent (1 FP ≠ 1 LOC across languages), punishes modular concise code, requires completed code, varies with programmer skill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2-Mark Memory Hook:** "LOC rewards verbosity" — a *terse C* program may have lower LOC than a *verbose COBOL* program doing the same task.

---

## 2.2 Function Points (FP) — The Five Logical Components

FP was proposed by **Allan Albrecht** at IBM (1979). The function count begins with *Unadjusted Function Count (UFC)*, which sums the weighted values across **5 functional categories** and **3 complexity levels (Simple / Average / Complex)**.

### The 5 FP Components

| # | Function Type | Full Name | What it counts | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EI** | External Inputs | Unique data items entering the system across boundary | Login form, Add-record screen |
| 2 | **EO** | External Outputs | Unique outputs leaving the system | Reports, error messages, invoices |
| 3 | **EQ** | External Inquiries | Interactive inputs producing immediate outputs | Search by ID, dropdown filters |
| 4 | **ILF** | Internal Logical Files | Logical groups of data *maintained* by the system | Customer DB table, Config file |
| 5 | **EIF** | External Interface Files | Logical data groups *referenced but not maintained* | External tax-rate table fetched from API |

### Standard FP Complexity Weights (Albrecht's Table)

| Function Type | Simple (Low) | Average | Complex (High) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EI**  | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| **EO**  | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| **EQ**  | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| **ILF** | 7 | 10 | 15 |
| **EIF** | 5 | 7 | 10 |

### The 14 General System Characteristics (GSC) — TDI Computation

Each GSC is rated on a scale of **0 (no influence) → 5 (strong influence)**. The 14 factors (GSC 1–14) are:

1. Data communications
2. Distributed data processing
3. Performance criteria
4. Heavily used configuration
5. Transaction rate
6. On-line data entry
7. End-user efficiency
8. On-line update
9. Complex processing (internal)
10. Reusability
11. Installation ease
12. Operational ease
13. Multiple sites
14. Facilitate change

$$\text{TDI} = \sum_{i=1}^{14} \text{GSC}_i, \qquad 0 \le \text{TDI} \le 70$$

The **Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)** is then:
$$\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \text{TDI}, \qquad 0.65 \le \text{VAF} \le 1.35$$

### Final FP Formula

$$\boxed{\;\text{FP} = \text{UFC} \times \text{VAF}\;}$$

---

## 2.3 Object Points (OP) — Theory for OO / 4GL Systems

Object Points were introduced by **Banker, Kauffman & Kumar (1991)** to handle the case where *pre-built components* (in 4GL, OO frameworks) drastically reduce new-code effort.

### The Three Object Point Categories

| Object Type | Weight | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Screen** (UI form/page) | **1** | Lowest complexity — typically a single display form |
| **Report** (output document) | **2** | Medium — requires formatting, layout, calculations |
| **Component** (reusable module) | **3** | Highest — encapsulated logic, often reused across screens |

### The Adjusted (New) Object Points — NOP

Raw OP does not account for **reuse** of existing components. A **reuse adjustment** is applied:

| % of Reused Components | Multiplier |
|---|---|
| Below 50 % | 4 |
| 50 % – 70 % | 3 |
| 71 % – 85 % | 2 |
| Above 85 % | 1 |

Then a **productivity adjustment** based on developer's 4GL experience:

| Experience Level | Productivity Multiplier |
|---|---|
| Very Low | 4 |
| Low | 7 |
| Nominal | 13 |
| High | 25 |
| Very High | 50 |

Final formula:
$$\boxed{\;\text{NOP} = (\text{OP}) \times \text{Reuse\%} \times \text{Productivity}\;}$$

Effort in Person-Months is then:
$$\text{Effort} = \dfrac{\text{NOP}}{\text{Productivity Rate}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Object Points are used by early-stage **SAP, Oracle Forms, and 4GL Rapid Application Development (RAD)** projects. They shine when the *screen-report-component* model matches the architecture (e.g., ERP module implementation).

---

## 2.4 Master Formula Cheat-Sheet (Printable)

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{KLOC} = \dfrac{\text{LOC}}{1000}$ | LOC = Lines of Code | KLOC |
| 2 | $\text{UFC} = \sum_{i} (\text{count}_i \times \text{weight}_i)$ | count = number of FP elements, weight = complexity weight | UFC |
| 3 | $\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \text{TDI}$ | TDI in $[0,70]$ | VAF in $[0.65, 1.35]$ |
| 4 | $\text{FP} = \text{UFC} \times \text{VAF}$ | — | FP |
| 5 | $\text{OP} = S \times 1 + R \times 2 + C \times 3$ | S=Screens, R=Reports, C=Components | OP |
| 6 | $\text{NOP} = \text{OP} \times \text{ReuseMultiplier}$ | — | NOP |
| 7 | $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{KLOC}}{\text{PM}}$ | PM = Person-Months | KLOC/PM |
