Members vote: **Approve / Reject / Defer / Request more info**. - Decision is recorded in the **CR log**. **Step 3.4 — Implementation of Approved Change** - Developer implements the change. - New version is committed to VCS with a clear commit message. - Build is regenerated. - Regression tests are executed. **Step 3.5 — Verification and Closure** - Tester/auditor verifies the change meets the original CR. - CR is marked as **Closed**. - Configuration status log is updated. --- ## Phase 4: Version Control (Configuration Tracking) **Step 4.1 — Repository Setup** - Central repository (e.g., GitHub repo, Azure DevOps project) is initialized. - Branching strategy is defined (e.g., GitFlow, trunk-based development). **Step 4.2 — Branching for Parallel Work** - Create branches for features, bug-fixes, or experiments. - Branches are isolated and merged only after review. **Step 4.3 — Commit and History** - Every change is committed with a unique hash (Git) or revision number (SVN). - History log provides an **audit trail** of who changed what, when, and why. **Step 4.4 — Tagging and Releases** - Tag a commit as `v1.0.0`, `v1.1.0`, etc. - Tags are immutable markers for released versions. **Step 4.5 — Merging and Conflict Resolution** - When parallel branches are integrated, conflicts (overlapping changes) are resolved manually. - Continuous integration (CI) tools auto-build and auto-test after each merge. --- ## Phase 5: Configuration Status Accounting **Step 5.1 — Define the Status Report Structure** - Each CI report must contain: ID, version, status, owner, date, change references. **Step 5.2 — Maintain a Status Log** - Updated on every change event (CR closure, new baseline, etc.). **Step 5.3 — Generate Reports** - Periodic reports (weekly/monthly) to project management. - Reports summarize: total CIs, pending changes, recently baselined items, outstanding CRs. **Step 5.4 — Traceability Matrix** - A **traceability matrix** is maintained linking: - Requirements ↔ Design ↔ Code ↔ Tests ↔ CIs. - This is critical for impact analysis and audit. --- ## Phase 6: Configuration Auditing **Step 6.1 — Plan the Audit** - Auditor defines audit scope, schedule, and criteria. **Step 6.2 — Functional Configuration Audit (FCA)** - Verifies the software **performs all functions** described in the SRS. - Test cases are re-executed against the current build. - Outcome: pass/fail report on functional compliance. **Step 6.3 — Physical Configuration Audit (PCA)** - Verifies the **build matches its documentation** (README, release notes, design). - Confirms that what is shipped to the customer corresponds to what was approved. **Step 6.4 — Report Findings** - Audit report lists discrepancies, non-conformities, and corrective actions. - Findings are tracked to closure. **Step 6.5 — Certification** - Upon successful audit, the baseline is **formally certified** as a deliverable. --- ## Phase 7: Release and Build Management **Step 7.1 — Build Automation** - Define build scripts (e.g., `Makefile`, `pom.xml`, `Jenkinsfile`). - Builds are reproducible — same source, same output. **Step 7.2 — Release Packaging** - Bundle the build with version metadata, release notes, license, and checksums. **Step 7.3 — Deployment to Environments** - Promote build across **Dev → QA → Staging → Production**. - Each promotion is logged in the SCM system. **Step 7.4 — Rollback Strategy** - Maintain previous version's tagged release for quick rollback if issues are found. --- ## 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Phase vs. Output vs. Tool > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is a **high-yield exam asset** — it directly maps to how KTU questions are framed for 7-mark and 14-mark answers. | Phase | Primary Output | Common Tool / Artifact | Who Owns It | |-------|----------------|------------------------|-------------| | Planning | SCM Plan | IEEE 828 template | Configuration Manager | | Identification | CI list, Baseline definitions | Naming scheme document | CM + Dev Team | | Change Control | Approved CRs, change history | CR forms, CCB minutes | CCB | | Version Control | Version history, tagged releases | Git, SVN, Mercurial | Developers | | Status Accounting | Status reports, traceability matrix | Dashboard, log DB | CM | | Auditing | Audit reports, certifications | FCA/PCA documents | Independent Auditors | | Build Management | Executable artifacts | Jenkins, Maven, Docker | Build/Release Engineer | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master SCM Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid) > [!NOTE] > The following diagram maps the **complete end-to-end SCM workflow** as a Mermaid flow. This is the single most important visual for KTU 14-mark answers on SCM. ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Project Initiation]) --> planNode[SCM Planning] planNode --> identifyNode[Configuration Identification] identifyNode --> baselineNode[Establish Baselines] baselineNode --> developNode[Software Development Activity] developNode --> crNode{Change Required?} crNode -- No --> auditNode[Status Accounting] crNode -- Yes --> submitCR[Submit Change Request] submitCR --> impactNode[Impact Analysis] impactNode --> ccbNode[CCB Review] ccbNode -- Approved --> implementNode[Implement Change] ccbNode -- Rejected --> crNode implementNode --> vcsNode[Commit to Version Control] vcsNode --> buildNode[Build and Test] buildNode --> auditNode auditNode --> reportNode[Generate Status Report] reportNode --> fcaNode[Functional Configuration Audit] fcaNode --> pcaNode[Physical Configuration Audit] pcaNode --> releaseNode{Release Approved?} releaseNode -- Yes --> deployNode[Release and Deploy] releaseNode -- No --> crNode deployNode --> endNode([Baseline Certified and Delivered]) classDef phase fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#01579B classDef decision fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100 classDef terminal fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 class planNode,identifyNode,baselineNode,impactNode,implementNode,vcsNode,buildNode,reportNode,deployNode phase class crNode,ccbNode,releaseNode decision class startNode,endNode terminal ``` ## 4.2 SCM Functional Pillars (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR scmHub((Software Configuration
Management System)) --> pillar1[Configuration
Identification] scmHub --> pillar2[Configuration
Change Control] scmHub --> pillar3[Version
Control] scmHub --> pillar4[Status
Accounting] scmHub --> pillar5[Configuration
Auditing] scmHub --> pillar6[Build and
Release Management] pillar1 --> ciDB[(CI Database)] pillar2 --> crLog[(CR Log)] pillar3 --> vcsRepo[(VCS Repository)] pillar4 --> statusLog[(Status Reports)] pillar5 --> auditLog[(Audit Reports)] pillar6 --> buildLog[(Build Artifacts)] classDef core fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1 classDef store fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C classDef hub fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#33691E class pillar1,pillar2,pillar3,pillar4,pillar5,pillar6 core class ciDB,crLog,vcsRepo,statusLog,auditLog,buildLog store class scmHub hub ``` ## 4.3 Baseline Evolution Across the SDLC ```mermaid flowchart LR phase1[Requirements Phase] -->|Approved| fb[Functional Baseline] fb --> phase2[Design Phase] phase2 -->|Approved| ab[Allocated Baseline] ab --> phase3[Implementation and Testing] phase3 -->|Approved| pb[Product Baseline] pb --> phase4[Deployment and Maintenance] classDef phase fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#311B92 classDef baseline fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#BF360C class phase1,phase2,phase3,phase4 phase class fb,ab,pb baseline ``` ## 4.4 Change Control Workflow Detail ```mermaid flowchart TD reqStart([Need for Change Identified]) --> crForm[Fill Change Request Form] crForm --> analysis[Technical Impact Analysis] analysis --> ccbSession{CCB Review} ccbSession -- Approved --> impl[Schedule Implementation] ccbSession -- Rejected --> reqStart ccbSession -- Deferred --> backlog[Backlog for Future] impl --> dev[Developer Codes Change] dev --> test[Testing Team Verifies] test --> audit[QA Audit] audit -- Pass --> baseline[Update Baseline and Close CR] audit -- Fail --> dev baseline --> endNode([CR Closed and Logged]) classDef proc fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#01579B classDef dec fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100 classDef term fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20 class crForm,analysis,impl,dev,test,audit,baseline proc class ccbSession dec class reqStart,endNode term ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Define Software Configuration Management. List any four Configuration Items.** ### Model Answer (3 Marks) **Definition (2 marks):** Software Configuration Management (SCM) is a software engineering discipline that tracks, controls, and manages the changes made to the artifacts of a software system throughout its lifecycle. It ensures consistency, traceability, and integrity of the product as it evolves. **Four Configuration Items (1 mark – 0.25 each):** 1. **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** document. 2. **Source code** files. 3. **Test plans and test cases**. 4. **User manuals** or release notes. > **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **What is a baseline? Explain the three types of baselines.** ### Model Answer (3 Marks) **Definition (1 mark):** A **baseline** is a formally approved, frozen snapshot of configuration items that serves as a reference point for further development; it can only be changed through formal change-control procedures. **Three Baselines (2 marks):** 1. **Functional Baseline** – established after the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is formally approved. It captures the *what* of the system. 2. **Allocated Baseline** – established after the **architectural and detailed design** are approved. It captures the *how* (technical allocation of requirements). 3. **Product Baseline** – established after the system has passed **system testing** and is ready for release. It captures the *final as-built* product. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **Note:** As per KTU ESE pattern, students answer **either Question A or Question B**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a) Explain the various phases of Software Configuration Management in detail. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: The Software Configuration Management process consists of **six core phases**: **Phase 1: SCM Planning (1 mark)** - The project team develops an **SCM Plan** that defines the SCM activities, tools, schedule, and responsibilities. - The plan is typically based on **IEEE 828 standard**. - Roles are defined: Configuration Manager, CCB members, developers, auditors. **Phase 2: Configuration Identification (1 mark)** - All software artifacts are identified and uniquely labeled as **Configuration Items (CIs)**. - CIs include: requirements docs, design models, source code, test cases, build scripts, user manuals. - A **naming convention** and **versioning scheme** (e.g., semantic versioning) is established. - Initial **baselines** are defined for key milestones. **Phase 3: Configuration Change Control (1.5 marks)** - A formal **Change Control Process** governs all modifications to baselined CIs. - Steps: Change Request → Impact Analysis → CCB Review → Implementation → Verification → Closure. - The **Configuration Control Board (CCB)** acts as the gatekeeper. **Phase 4: Version Control (1 mark)** - Tools like **Git, SVN, Mercurial** track every change to every CI. - **Branching and merging** enable parallel development. - **Tagging** marks release points. - A complete history log is maintained. **Phase 5: Configuration Status Accounting (1 mark)** - A **status log** records the lifecycle state of each CI: version, owner, status, change history. - **Periodic reports** are generated for project managers and stakeholders. - A **traceability matrix** links requirements to design, code, and tests. **Phase 6: Configuration Auditing (1.5 marks)** - **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA):** verifies the software performs per the SRS. - **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA):** verifies the build matches the approved design and documentation. - Successful audits result in **formal certification** of the baseline. **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the Configuration Change Control Process. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Introduction (1 mark):** Configuration Change Control is the structured process of managing changes to baselined CIs. It ensures that **only approved, reviewed, and tested changes** are integrated into the software baseline. **Steps of the Change Control Process (4.5 marks):** 1. **Change Request (CR) Submission** – A stakeholder raises a formal CR specifying the desired change, justification, urgency, and affected CIs. 2. **Impact Analysis** – A technical lead evaluates the cost, time, risk, and dependency implications of the change. 3. **CCB Review** – The Configuration Control Board examines the impact analysis and **approves, rejects, defers, or requests more information**. 4. **Implementation** – On approval, the developer implements the change in a separate branch, commits it to the VCS, and documents the change. 5. **Verification** – The QA team tests the change; regression testing ensures no other features are broken. 6. **Baseline Update and CR Closure** – The change is merged into the main baseline, and the CR is closed with a complete audit trail in the configuration status log. **Neat Diagram (1.5 marks):** ``` [Change Request] → [Impact Analysis] → [CCB Review] | ┌───────────┼───────────┐ ↓ ↓ ↓ Approved Rejected Deferred ↓ ↓ ↓ [Implementation] [Backlog] [Backlog] ↓ [Verification] ↓ [Baseline Update & CR Closure] ``` **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** This structured process preserves baseline integrity, prevents unauthorized modifications, and ensures all changes are traceable and auditable. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a) What is a Configuration Item (CI)? Explain with examples how CIs are identified and organized in a software project. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Configuration Item (CI)** is any artifact — such as a document, source file, library, or tool — that is placed under formal configuration control and treated as a single manageable entity. It is the atomic unit of SCM. **Identification of CIs (3 marks):** - CIs are identified by analyzing the **work products** of each phase of the SDLC. - Common criteria: an artifact is a CI if it has **multiple versions, multiple stakeholders, or needs formal change control**. - A **Configuration Item Identification Document** is prepared, listing each CI with: - **Unique ID** (e.g., `SRC-PAY-001`). - **Name and description**. - **Owner / Responsible person**. - **Version scheme**. - **Baseline membership**. **Organization of CIs (2 marks):** - CIs are grouped into **logical baselines** aligned with SDLC milestones: - *Functional Baseline:* SRS, use-case specs, requirement traceability matrix. - *Allocated Baseline:* Architecture documents, design specs, interface definitions. - *Product Baseline:* Source code, executables, build scripts, test reports, user manuals. - CIs are stored in a **central repository** (VCS) with controlled access rights. **Examples (0.5 mark):** For a banking software project, CIs include the *Account Module source code*, the *Loan Approval SRS*, the *Database schema scripts*, the *Test Case Suite for Transactions*, and the *End-User Manual*. **(b) Discuss the importance of version control and configuration auditing in SCM. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Version Control (3.5 marks):** - **Definition:** Version control is the practice of tracking and managing changes to a CI across its lifetime. - **Importance:** - Enables **parallel development** through branching and merging. - Provides a **complete history** of who changed what, when, and why (audit trail). - Supports **rollback** to any previous state in case of defects. - Enables **tagging** of releases (e.g., `v1.0.0`, `v2.0.1`). - Facilitates **collaboration** among distributed teams. - **Tools:** Git, Subversion, Mercurial, CVS, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps Repos. **Configuration Auditing (3.5 marks):** - **Definition:** Configuration auditing is the independent verification that the software product conforms to its specifications, standards, and contractual requirements. - **Two types:** - **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA)** – confirms the product performs all functions described in the SRS. - **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA)** – confirms the build matches the approved design, code, and documentation. - **Importance:** - **Detects drift** between the documented baseline and the actual software. - **Ensures compliance** with regulatory and contractual standards (e.g., ISO, FDA, DO-178C). - **Certifies the baseline** as a deliverable for release. - **Reduces risk** of shipping incomplete or wrong software. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently lose marks by **listing CIs without categorizing them into baselines** — always organize CIs under functional/allocated/product baselines. > - In version-control answers, students often **describe Git commands instead of explaining the *concept* and *importance***. KTU expects conceptual depth, not tool-specific tutorials. > - For auditing questions, students sometimes **skip distinguishing FCA from PCA**. Clearly define both — this is a **2-mark differentiator** in 7-mark sub-questions. > - Avoid one-liner Part-A answers. Each 3-mark question expects a **definition + 2–3 supporting points** with proper examples. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before KTU Exam:** - ✅ **SCM** = the discipline of tracking, controlling, and managing software changes across the life cycle. - ✅ The **six core SCM functions** are: Identification, Change Control, Version Control, Status Accounting, Auditing, Build/Release Management. - ✅ A **Configuration Item (CI)** is the atomic unit of SCM; examples include SRS, source code, test cases, manuals, and build scripts. - ✅ A **Baseline** is a frozen, formally approved snapshot — only changeable via formal change control. - ✅ The **three baselines** are: Functional (post-SRS), Allocated (post-design), Product (post-system test). - ✅ **Change Control** flows: CR → Impact Analysis → CCB Review → Implementation → Verification → Closure. - ✅ **CCB (Configuration Control Board)** is the governing body for approving/rejecting change requests. - ✅ **Version Control** uses tools like Git, SVN; provides history, branching, merging, and tagging. - ✅ **Status Accounting** = maintaining logs and traceability matrices for all CIs. - ✅ **FCA (Functional Configuration Audit)** checks performance vs. SRS. - ✅ **PCA (Physical Configuration Audit)** checks build vs. approved design. - ✅ **IEEE 828** is the standard that defines SCM plans and processes. - ✅ **Semantic versioning** format: `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` (e.g., `2.4.1`). - ✅ **Build management** ensures reproducibility of software artifacts. - ✅ SCM is governed by the **SCM Plan**, typically part of the SPMP. - ✅ KTU 14-mark answers must include **diagrams** (change control flow, baseline evolution). - ✅ For Part A, structure every 3-mark answer as: **Definition + 2/3 key points + Example**.

topics.title: Software Quality Management - ISO 9000, Six Sigma for Software Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Quality Management - ISO 9000, Six Sigma for Software Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Software Quality Management (SQM) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Quality Management (SQM)** is a systematic, planned set of coordinated activities that **direct, control, and assure** the software development process and its products to meet specified requirements, standards, and customer expectations. It encompasses three integrated sub-processes: **Quality Planning**, **Quality Assurance (QA)**, and **Quality Control (QC)**.

**Key SQM Sub-Processes (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate):**

| Sub-Process | Strategic Focus | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Planning** | Selecting applicable standards & metrics | Software Quality Management Plan (SQMP) |
| **Quality Assurance** | Process-oriented, prevention-driven | Audit reports, process checklists |
| **Quality Control** | Product-oriented, defect detection | Test reports, defect logs, RCA documents |

### 1.2 ISO 9000 — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 9000** is a family of international standards developed by the *International Organization for Standardization (ISO)* that define the requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**. For software, the relevant sub-standard is **ISO 9001:2015** (generic) supported by **ISO/IEC 90003:2014** (software-specific guidance).

### 1.3 Six Sigma — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma** is a **data-driven, statistical methodology** for eliminating defects in any process. The target is **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**, corresponding to a process operating at **±6 standard deviations (±6σ) from the mean**. The flagship methodology is **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳

Imagine you own a five-star restaurant:

- **ISO 9000** is like the **standardized recipe book and hygiene certification** displayed on your kitchen wall. It tells every chef *what* quality means, *which* steps to follow, and an *external auditor* visits to certify that your kitchen follows the rules. Certification reassures customers.
- **Six Sigma** is like the **statistical taste-testing lab** at the back. Every day, you measure the salt content of 1,000 soup bowls in parts-per-million, plot a normal distribution curve, and ensure the saltiness never drifts beyond the **6σ tolerance band**. If it drifts, you tweak the process (more salt in the base stock).
- **SQM** is the *overall restaurant management system* that combines the recipe book (ISO), the lab (Six Sigma), the inspector (QA), and the taster (QC) into a single framework.

This is precisely how a software firm ensures: a project is **planned well** (ISO clauses), **measured statistically** (Six Sigma), **audited** (QA), and **tested** (QC).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ISO 9000 Family — Eight Quality Management Principles

The ISO 9000:2015 family is built on **seven quality management principles** (formerly eight, refactored):

1. **Customer Focus** — Understand and meet customer needs.
2. **Leadership** — Establish unity of direction.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered workforce.
4. **Process Approach** — Manage activities as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** — Continual improvement is a permanent objective.
6. **Evidence-based Decision Making** — Decisions based on analysis of data.
7. **Relationship Management** — Manage relationships with interested parties.

### 2.2 ISO 9001:2015 — Clause Structure (KTU High-Yield)

ISO 9001:2015 follows the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** with **10 clauses**:

- **Clauses 1–3**: Scope, Normative references, Terms & definitions
- **Clause 4**: Context of the organization
- **Clause 5**: Leadership
- **Clause 6**: Planning
- **Clause 7**: Support
- **Clause 8**: Operation (core — Plan-Do-Check-Act)
- **Clause 9**: Performance evaluation
- **Clause 10**: Improvement

### 2.3 Six Sigma — DMAIC Methodology (Deep Dive)

| Phase | Objective | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|
| **D — Define** | Identify the problem, customer (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality), and project scope | Project Charter, SIPOC diagram, Voice of Customer (VoC) |
| **M — Measure** | Quantify current process performance; baseline metrics | Defect logs, DPMO, Process Sigma, Pareto charts |
| **A — Analyze** | Identify root causes of defects | Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, 5-Whys, FMEA, Regression |
| **I — Improve** | Implement solutions to remove root causes | Pilot runs, Design of Experiments (DoE), Poka-Yoke |
| **C — Control** | Sustain the improvements via statistical process control | Control charts, SOPs, training, dashboards |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all exam-relevant formulas** for Software Quality Management. Memorize these before stepping into the exam hall.

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{D}{K}$ | Defects per KLOC (thousand lines of code) |
| 2 | **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | $DPMO = \dfrac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}$ | $D$ = Defects, $N$ = Units, $O$ = Opportunities per unit |
| 3 | **Process Sigma Level** | $\sigma_{level} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5$ | The **1.5σ shift** accounts for long-term drift |
| 4 | **Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{N - D}{N} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of defect-free units |
| 5 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$ | Compound yield across $n$ process steps |
| 6 | **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{N}$ | For non-repairable components |
| 7 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | For repairable systems |
| 8 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | System uptime percentage |
| 9 | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ = capable |
| 10 | **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum W_{i} \times S_{i}}{\sum W_{i}}$ | Weighted satisfaction score |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In KTU ESE problems, when you compute *Process Sigma*, always remember to **add the 1.5σ shift** to convert *short-term* capability to *long-term* performance — this is the most commonly missed step in board evaluations.

### 2.5 Six Sigma — DPMO to Sigma Conversion Reference Table

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Cost of Poor Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.15% | 25–40% of sales |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32% | 15–25% of sales |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | 5–15% of sales |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | 1–5% of sales |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.9997%** | **< 1% of sales** |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISO 9001 certification** is a *mandatory prerequisite* for bidding on government and defense software contracts in India.
- **Six Sigma** is integrated into **CMMI Level 5** practices (Optimizing) — overlapping with KTU Module 4's quality themes.
- Major IT firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) operate dedicated **Six Sigma Black Belt** programs for process excellence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Process Sigma from Defect Data

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A banking software team processes 12,000 transaction modules in a quarter. A module has, on average, **35** opportunities for defects. During testing, the team logs **120** defects. Compute: (a) DPMO, (b) Yield, (c) Process Sigma Level.

**Given:**
- Units produced, $N = 12{,}000$
- Total defects, $D = 120$
- Opportunities per unit, $O = 35$

#### Step (a) — DPMO Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= N \times O \\
&= 12{,}000 \times 35 \\
&= 420{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= \frac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{120}{420{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.000285714 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 285.71 \approx 286 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Roughly 286 defects occur per million opportunities — a fairly mature process.

#### Step (b) — Yield Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \frac{N - D}{N} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{12{,}000 - 120}{12{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{11{,}880}{12{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.99 \times 100\% = 99.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (c) — Process Sigma Level

We need to compute the **z-score** corresponding to the *defect rate* in a normal distribution, then add the **1.5σ shift**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rate} &= 1 - Y = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01 \\
\text{Proportion good} &= 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the inverse standard normal:
$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{short} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.99) \approx 2.326
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the long-term shift of 1.5σ:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{level} &= z_{short} + 1.5 \\
&= 2.326 + 1.5 \\
&= 3.826 \approx 3.83\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** The banking transaction module is operating at a **~3.83σ quality level** — adequate for a non-safety-critical app, but well below Six Sigma excellence. The team should target the **Improve** phase of DMAIC.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

> **Problem:** A software build pipeline has 4 stages: Coding (95% yield), Unit Testing (92%), Integration Testing (90%), and System Testing (97%). Calculate the RTY and the total defect escape rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 \times 0.97
\end{aligned}
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.95 \times 0.92 &= 0.8740 \\
0.8740 \times 0.90 &= 0.7866 \\
0.7866 \times 0.97 &= 0.7630
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{RTY = 76.30\%}
$$

The **defect escape rate** is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - RTY &= 1 - 0.7630 = 0.2370 = 23.70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how a *seemingly* 95%+ pipeline compounds into a 76% RTY — a hidden cost of *Hickam's dictum* ("the patient can have more than one disease"). Six Sigma targets **RTY ≥ 99.9997%** for mission-critical systems.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)

> **Problem:** A response-time metric has specification limits LSL = 100 ms, USL = 300 ms. The mean $\mu = 180$ ms, and standard deviation $\sigma = 25$ ms. Compute $C_{pk}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{300 - 180}{3 \times 25},\; \frac{180 - 100}{3 \times 25}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{120}{75},\; \frac{80}{75}\right) \\
&= \min(1.60,\; 1.067) \\
&= 1.067
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** $C_{pk} \approx 1.07$ — the process is **borderline capable** (industry threshold: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$). The process is drifting toward the **lower specification limit**, signaling asymmetry in the response-time distribution.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ISO 9000 vs. Six Sigma

| Dimension | ISO 9000 / ISO 9001 | Six Sigma |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Quality management standards body (ISO, 1987) | Motorola (Bill Smith, 1986); popularized by GE/Jack Welch |
| **Primary Focus** | Process documentation & conformance | Defect reduction via statistical methods |
| **Approach** | Prescriptive (do *these* things) | Analytical (measure *this* variable) |
| **Output** | Certification (3-year cycle) | Quantified process capability (sigma level) |
| **Mandatory for** | Government/defense tenders | Continuous improvement programs |
| **Tools** | PDCA cycle, audit checklists, SOPs | DMAIC, DPMO, control charts, FMEA |
| **Cost** | Moderate (consultancy + audit) | High (training Black/Green Belts) |
| **Software Tailoring** | ISO/IEC 90003:2014 | CMMI integration; DPMO on KLOC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Six Sigma DMAIC Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeD["D DEFINE\nIdentify CTQ\nProject Charter\nSIPOC"] 
    nodeM["M MEASURE\nBaseline metrics\nDPMO calculation\nPareto chart"]
    nodeA["A ANALYZE\nRoot cause analysis\nFishbone Ishikawa\n5 Whys FMEA"]
    nodeI["I IMPROVE\nPilot solution\nDoE Poka Yoke\nKaizen events"]
    nodeC["C CONTROL\nSOP update\nControl charts\nTraining sustain"]

    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeC -.feedback.-> nodeD
```

### 4.2 ISO 9001:2015 Process Map (PDCA-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ctx["Clause 4 Context of Organization"]
        nodeSWOT["SWOT Analysis\nStakeholder Needs"]
    end
    subgraph Plan["Clause 6 PLANNING"]
        nodePlan["Quality Objectives\nRisk & Opportunity"]
    end
    subgraph Do["Clause 8 DO Operation"]
        nodeDo["Software Lifecycle\nDevelopment & Test"]
    end
    subgraph Check["Clause 9 CHECK"]
        nodeCheck["Internal Audit\nManagement Review"]
    end
    subgraph Act["Clause 10 ACT Improvement"]
        nodeAct["CAPA\nCorrective Action"]
    end
    nodeSWOT --> nodePlan --> nodeDo --> nodeCheck --> nodeAct
    nodeAct -.feedback.-> nodePlan
```

### 4.3 Software Quality Management Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SQMP["Software Quality Management Plan SQMP"]
        nodeQPlan["Quality Planning\nMetrics Selection"]
        nodeQA["Quality Assurance\nProcess Audits"]
        nodeQC["Quality Control\nTesting & Reviews"]
    end
    subgraph Inputs["Inputs"]
        nodeReq["Software Requirements\nSRS Document"]
    end
    subgraph Standards["External Standards"]
        nodeISO["ISO 9001 QMS"]
        node6S["Six Sigma DMAIC"]
        nodeCMMI["CMMI Level 1 to 5"]
    end
    subgraph Output["Output Deliverable"]
        nodeQualProd["Quality Software Product\nMeets Customer CTQs"]
    end
    nodeReq --> nodeQPlan
    nodeQPlan --> nodeQA --> nodeQC
    nodeISO --> nodeQPlan
    node6S --> nodeQA
    nodeCMMI --> nodeQC
    nodeQA --> nodeQualProd
    nodeQC --> nodeQualProd
```

### 4.4 DPMO → Sigma Conversion Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start: Collect Defect Data D N O"]
    nodeCalc["Compute DPMO = D / NxO x 10^6"]
    nodeYield["Compute Yield Y = 1 - DPMO / 10^6"]
    nodeInv["Apply Inverse Normal Phi inverse Y"]
    nodeShift["Add 1.5 sigma shift for long term"]
    nodeResult["Report Final Process Sigma Level"]
    nodeBench["Benchmark vs 6 sigma 3.4 DPMO"]

    nodeStart --> nodeCalc --> nodeYield --> nodeInv --> nodeShift --> nodeResult --> nodeBench
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Software Quality Management. List its three main activities.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Software Quality Management (SQM) is the coordinated set of activities that direct and control an organization with regard to software quality, ensuring products meet stated requirements.

**Three main activities:**
1. **Quality Planning** — selection of standards, metrics, and processes.
2. **Quality Assurance** — process-oriented preventive activities.
3. **Quality Control** — product-oriented detective activities (testing, reviews).

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + ½ mark × 3 = 1.5 marks for activities + 0.5 mark for correct terminology.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between ISO 9000 and Six Sigma. State one similarity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ISO 9000 | Six Sigma |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Process conformance via documentation | Defect reduction via statistical analysis |
| Approach | Prescriptive "what to do" | Analytical "how much" |
| Outcome | Certification | Quantified sigma level |

**Similarity:** Both aim to improve customer satisfaction, emphasize process orientation, and require management commitment for successful implementation.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for two differences + 1 mark similarity.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

> **MODULE 4 — INTERNAL CHOICE QUESTION (A)**

**Q.A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **ISO 9001:2015 quality management principles** in detail. How do they apply to a software development organization? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A healthcare software firm processed 25,000 patient-record modules last quarter. Each module has 20 defect opportunities. Quality logs show 250 defects. Calculate: (i) DPMO, (ii) Yield, (iii) Process Sigma Level. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

The seven ISO 9001:2015 quality management principles, applied to a software organization:

1. **Customer Focus (1 mark):** The software organization must identify CTQs (Critical-to-Quality characteristics) such as response time, availability, and data integrity. Voice of Customer (VoC) techniques like interviews and surveys drive the requirements phase.

2. **Leadership (1 mark):** Senior management establishes the quality policy, allocates resources for testing tools, and fosters a quality-first culture. In agile firms, the Scrum Master and Product Owner embody this.

3. **Engagement of People (1 mark):** Competent developers, testers, and QA engineers are empowered to halt production on detecting critical defects. Certifications (ISTQB, CSTE) validate competence.

4. **Process Approach (1 mark):** The SDLC (Requirements → Design → Code → Test → Maintenance) is managed as a set of interrelated processes with defined inputs, outputs, and KPIs.

5. **Improvement (1 mark):** CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) processes drive continuous improvement. Each release retrospective feeds lessons learned into the next iteration.

6. **Evidence-based Decision Making (1 mark):** Defect density, MTBF, code coverage, and customer satisfaction scores inform release readiness decisions rather than gut-feel.

7. **Relationship Management (1 mark):** The firm manages relationships with vendors (component suppliers), partners (cloud providers), and customers (healthcare clients) to ensure end-to-end quality.

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $N = 25{,}000$ units, $O = 20$ opportunities/unit, $D = 250$ defects.

#### (i) DPMO Computation (3 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= 25{,}000 \times 20 = 500{,}000 \\
DPMO &= \frac{250}{500{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.0005 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 500 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (ii) Yield Computation (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \frac{25{,}000 - 250}{25{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{24{,}750}{25{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 99.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (iii) Process Sigma Level (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{short} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.99) \approx 2.326 \\
\sigma_{level} &= 2.326 + 1.5 = 3.83\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The healthcare software is operating at ~3.83σ — adequate but improvable. To reach 6σ (3.4 DPMO), the team must target **MTBF improvements** and **defect prevention in the design phase**.

---

> **MODULE 4 — INTERNAL CHOICE QUESTION (B)**

**Q.B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe the **Six Sigma DMAIC methodology** in detail with a software-engineering example for each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** with its formula. For a memory-leak metric, $LSL = 0$ MB, $USL = 50$ MB, $\mu = 15$ MB, $\sigma = 8$ MB. Compute $C_{pk}$ and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

**DMAIC Phases for Software Engineering:**

1. **D — Define (1.5 marks):** Identify the problem. *Example:* "Our mobile app crashes 4.5 times per 1,000 sessions, breaching the SLA of ≤ 2 crashes." CTQ = crash rate. Output: Project Charter + SIPOC (Suppliers: cloud APIs; Inputs: session data; Process: app session lifecycle; Outputs: crash logs; Customers: end users).

2. **M — Measure (1.5 marks):** Quantify baseline. *Example:* Collect 30 days of crash logs → DPMO calculation, Pareto chart (80% of crashes from 3 modules). Baseline sigma level established.

3. **A — Analyze (1.5 marks):** Root cause analysis. *Example:* Fishbone diagram reveals three root causes — null-pointer exceptions, network timeouts, uninitialized variables. Confirmed by 5-Whys technique on the top defect.

4. **I — Improve (1.5 marks):** Implement solutions. *Example:* Add null checks, retry-on-timeout middleware, static-analysis linter. Pilot run shows crash rate drops to 1.2 per 1,000 sessions.

5. **C — Control (1 mark):** Sustain improvements. *Example:* Add automated regression suite, configure dashboards with control limits, train developers on coding standards.

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Process Capability Index (3 marks explanation + 4 marks calculation):**

The **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** quantifies how well a process can produce output within specification limits. It accounts for the process **mean's position** relative to the limits.

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Given:** $LSL = 0$ MB, $USL = 50$ MB, $\mu = 15$ MB, $\sigma = 8$ MB.

**Step 1 — Upper Capability:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{upper} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{50 - 15}{3 \times 8} = \frac{35}{24} \approx 1.458
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Lower Capability:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{lower} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{15 - 0}{24} = \frac{15}{24} = 0.625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final $C_{pk}$:**
$$
\boxed{C_{pk} = \min(1.458,\; 0.625) = 0.625}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** $C_{pk} = 0.625 < 1.00$ — the process is **NOT capable**. The lower capability (0.625) is the bottleneck, indicating the process mean is too close to the **lower specification limit** (memory leak near zero). The team should either shift the mean upward or reduce $\sigma$ via better memory management (object pooling, garbage-collection tuning).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Software Quality Management**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift** in Process Sigma calculation → loses 2 marks. KTU examiners specifically test for *long-term* sigma, not *short-term* z-score.
> 2. **Confusing DPMO with Defect Density** — DPMO uses *opportunities*, DD uses *KLOC*. They are NOT interchangeable.
> 3. **Mixing up MTTF vs. MTBF** — MTTF is for non-repairable items; MTBF is for repairable systems. Examiner flags this consistently.
> 4. **Skipping units in the DPMO formula** — Always show "DPMO = ... = 500 DPMO" with the unit label.
> 5. **Writing ISO 9000:2008 clauses in a 2024-scheme paper** — Always use the **ISO 9001:2015** ten-clause HLS structure.
> 6. **In DMAIC answers, omitting a software-specific example** — Generic manufacturing examples cost 1–2 marks; a software illustration (e.g., crash rate, defect density) is mandatory.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: SQM / ISO / Six Sigma**

- **SQM triad:** Quality Planning + Quality Assurance + Quality Control. Memorize the difference: *QA prevents, QC detects*.
- **ISO 9001:2015** uses a **10-clause HLS** and **7 quality management principles**. Certification is a 3-year cycle with annual surveillance audits.
- **ISO/IEC 90003:2014** is the *software-specific guidance document* for applying ISO 9001 to software — often asked in 3-mark questions.
- **Six Sigma target:** 3.4 DPMO over the long term, achieved via the **DMAIC** methodology and the **1.5σ shift** rule.
- **Critical formulas** (must memorize): DPMO, Yield, RTY, $C_{pk}$, Process Sigma, MTBF, Availability.
- **RTY compounds multiplicatively** — a 95% × 95% × 95% × 95% pipeline drops to 81.45% RTY, not 80%.
- **Six Sigma belts** (hierarchy): White → Yellow → Green → Black → Master Black. Black Belts lead DMAIC projects.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** = capable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ = excellent (Six Sigma level).
- **Six Sigma vs. ISO 9000 are complementary**, not competing — ISO tells you *what* to do, Six Sigma tells you *how good* you are.
- **PDCA cycle (Deming Wheel)** underpins both frameworks: Plan-Do-Check-Act.
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** uses control charts (X-bar, R-charts) to detect out-of-control conditions in software metrics.
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a key Six Sigma analysis tool — assigns Risk Priority Numbers (RPN = S × O × D).
- **Software Quality Metrics** include defect density, MTBF, MTTR, customer-reported defects, code coverage, and cyclomatic complexity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggested Project Topics: College Library Management System, Online Movie Ticket Reservation System, Online Food Delivery App, Home Automation using IoT, Smart Parking System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCGT504 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING & DESIGN CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggested Project Topics: College Library Management System, Online Movie Ticket Reservation System, Online Food Delivery App, Home Automation using IoT, Smart Parking System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Suggested Project Topics" in KTU Software Engineering?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Software Engineering & Design Concepts (PBCGT504)*, **Suggested Project Topics** are a curated set of mini-to-mid scale real-world problem statements used to translate the theoretical Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) into a **practical, end-to-end engineering deliverable**. Each project acts as a *capstone-style laboratory* in which students must demonstrate the application of:

- Requirements Engineering (Elicitation, Analysis, Specification, Validation)
- System Modeling (UML — Use Case, Class, Sequence, Activity, State, Component, Deployment)
- Architectural Design (Monolithic, Client–Server, N-Tier, MVC, Microservices, Event-Driven)
- Design Engineering (GRASP, SOLID Principles, Design Patterns)
- Testing Strategies (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance)
- Project Management (Scheduling with Gantt/PERT/CPM, Effort Estimation using **COCOMO** or **FP**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** These projects are not just coding exercises. They are designed to evaluate **Course Outcomes (CO4–CO6)** specifically — *Design, Modeling, and Testing* competencies. A student is expected to deliver a **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)**, **Design Document (SDD)**, **Test Plan**, and a **working prototype**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Construction" Intuition

Imagine a software project as **building a house**:

| Construction Phase | Software Engineering Equivalent | Project Topic Example |
|---|---|---|
| Understanding what the family needs | Requirements Gathering (SRS) | "Students need to reserve books online" → Library System |
| Blueprint by an architect | UML Modeling + Architecture Design | Class diagrams, Deployment diagrams |
| Laying foundation & walls | Core module implementation (Auth, DB) | Login system, Database schema |
| Electrical wiring, plumbing | Cross-cutting concerns (security, logging) | OAuth 2.0, exception handling |
| Painting, furnishing | UI/UX polish | Responsive web/mobile UI |
| Inspection by municipal engineer | Testing (Unit, Integration, UAT) | Test cases, defect reports |
| Handover keys to family | Deployment & Maintenance | CI/CD, cloud hosting |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as you cannot pour concrete before drawing a blueprint, you cannot write code before designing. **Design precedes implementation** — this is the cardinal rule these projects will test.

---

## 1.3 The Five Official Project Topics — One-Line Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition Block (Use these verbatim in your exams for full credit):**

1. **College Library Management System (CLMS):** A centralized, multi-user information system that automates the cataloging, issuing, returning, and tracking of library resources (books, journals, e-books) for a collegiate institution, while generating analytical reports for librarians.

2. **Online Movie Ticket Reservation System (OMTRS):** A web/mobile application that enables users to browse currently running films, view show timings, select seats in real-time from a virtual auditorium layout, make electronic payments, and receive digital tickets with QR codes.

3. **Online Food Delivery App (OFDA):** A three-sided marketplace platform connecting Customers, Restaurants, and Delivery Partners, supporting menu browsing, order placement, real-time tracking, payment processing, and ratings/reviews.

4. **Home Automation using IoT (HA-IoT):** A cyber-physical system integrating sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, and cloud services to enable remote monitoring and control of household appliances (lights, fans, doors, temperature) via smartphone or voice commands.

5. **Smart Parking System (SPS):** An IoT-enabled urban solution that detects vehicle occupancy in parking slots using sensors, displays availability on digital signage/mobile apps, and enables reservation and cashless payment to reduce search-time congestion.

---

## 1.4 Common Vocabulary All Five Projects Share

| Term | Meaning in Project Context | Example (Library System) |
|---|---|---|
| **Actor** | External entity interacting with system | Student, Librarian, Admin |
| **Use Case** | A goal-directed interaction | *Issue Book*, *Search Catalog* |
| **Entity Class** | Persistent data object | `Book`, `Member`, `Transaction` |
| **Boundary Class** | UI / API interface | `LoginPage`, `REST_Controller` |
| **Controller Class** | Business logic orchestrator | `IssueBookController` |
| **NFR** | Non-Functional Requirement | Response time **< 2 seconds** |
| **Stakeholder** | Anyone affected by system | College management, Students |
| **CRUD** | Create, Read, Update, Delete | Add/View/Edit/Delete a book |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Unified Software Engineering Process (USEP) for All Five Projects

Regardless of which project you choose, KTU expects you to walk through the **same five-phase engineering process**. The projects differ only in **domain logic**, not in the engineering methodology applied.

### Phase 1 — Inception & Requirements Engineering
- Identify **stakeholders** (primary, secondary, tertiary).
- Elicit requirements via interviews, questionnaires, observation, and use case workshops.
- Classify into **Functional Requirements (FR)** and **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)**.
- Produce a formal **SRS document** following the **IEEE 830-1998** standard structure.

### Phase 2 — System Modeling (UML 2.5)
- Build a **Use Case Diagram** to capture functional scope.
- Build **Class Diagrams** for static structure (entities + relationships).
- Build **Sequence/Communication Diagrams** for dynamic behavior.
- Build **State Chart Diagrams** for object lifecycle (e.g., Order state machine).
- Build **Activity Diagrams** for workflows and business processes.
- Build **Component & Deployment Diagrams** for architectural realization.

### Phase 3 — Architectural Design
- Choose a **pattern** (MVC for web apps, Layered for IoT, Pub-Sub for real-time).
- Identify **subsystems** and their responsibilities.
- Define **interfaces** between layers.
- Address **cross-cutting concerns**: security, logging, caching, error handling.

### Phase 4 — Detailed Design & Implementation
- Apply **SOLID** and **GRASP** principles.
- Select appropriate **Design Patterns** (Factory, Observer, Singleton, Strategy).
- Use version control (**Git**), issue tracking, and CI/CD.

### Phase 5 — Testing & Quality Assurance
- Write **test cases** for each use case (positive + negative).
- Apply **equivalence partitioning** and **boundary value analysis**.
- Track defects in a bug-tracking system.
- Measure code coverage, performance, and usability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** In the 14-mark questions, the examiner allocates roughly **4 marks for SRS**, **6 marks for UML diagrams (any 3)**, and **4 marks for architecture/testing**. Memorize this distribution.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Project Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | CLMS | OMTRS | OFDA | HA-IoT | SPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Domain** | Education | Entertainment | E-Commerce | Cyber-Physical | Smart City |
| **Architecture Style** | Layered (MVC) | 3-Tier + Realtime | Microservices | Edge-Cloud Hybrid | Event-Driven + Cloud |
| **Database Type** | RDBMS (MySQL) | RDBMS + Cache (Redis) | RDBMS + NoSQL | Time-Series (InfluxDB) | RDBMS + Time-Series |
| **Real-Time Req.** | Low | **High** (seat locking) | **High** (tracking) | **High** (sensor data) | **High** (slot updates) |
| **Key NFR** | Concurrency control | Atomic seat reservation | Low latency tracking | Latency **< 200 ms** | Availability **99.9 %** |
| **External HW/SW** | Barcode scanner | Payment gateway, QR | Maps API, GPS, SMS | Arduino/ESP32, sensors | IR/Ultrasonic sensors, LED |
| **Security Focus** | Role-based access | PCI-DSS payment | OAuth, JWT, encryption | TLS, device auth | Anonymized data |
| **Core Algorithm** | Search indexing, fine calc. | Pessimistic/Optimistic locking | Route optimization (Dijkstra) | MQTT pub-sub | Slot allocation |
| **Scale (Users)** | 100s–1000s | 1000s–Millions | 1000s–Millions | 10s per home | City-wide |
| **Testing Emphasis** | Concurrency, regression | Concurrency, load, security | Load, security, UX | Hardware-in-loop, latency | Sensor accuracy, load |

---

## 2.3 Effort Estimation Reference (COCOMO II) for Mini-Projects

For mini-projects in KTU (typically 3–4 member teams over one semester), the **COCOMO II** post-architecture model is the examiner's favourite:

$$
\text{Effort} = A \times \text{Size}^{E} \times \prod_{i=1}^{17} \text{EM}_i
$$

$$
\text{Schedule} = C \times \text{Effort}^{F}
$$

Where:
- $A$ = calibrated productivity constant (typical **2.94**)
- $E$ = exponent derived from 5 scale factors
- $\text{Size}$ = KLOC (Kilo Lines of Code) or Function Points
- $\text{EM}_i$ = Effort Multipliers (17 cost drivers)
- $C$, $F$ = schedule constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Indian IT companies (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) use the COCOMO II family for bid estimation. Mastering it signals industry-readiness to KTU examiners.

## 2.4 Design Pattern Quick-Reference Card

| Pattern | Intent | Best Fit Project |
|---|---|---|
| **Singleton** | One instance of a class | Database connection pool (all 5) |
| **Factory** | Object creation without specifying class | Notification (email/SMS/push) — OFDA |
| **Observer** | Notify dependents on state change | Real-time seat updates — OMTRS |
| **Strategy** | Family of interchangeable algorithms | Fine calculation — CLMS |
| **State** | Behavior changes with internal state | Order lifecycle — OFDA, Ticket status — OMTRS |
| **Facade** | Simplified interface to subsystem | Payment gateway wrapper — OMTRS, OFDA |
| **MVC** | Separate concerns | Web apps — CLMS, OMTRS, OFDA |
| **Pub-Sub (Broker)** | Decoupled async messaging | IoT telemetry — HA-IoT, SPS |
| **Repository** | Encapsulate data access | All 5 for clean testability |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Worked Project: College Library Management System (CLMS)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is an **exhaustive, examiner-style worked solution** showing exactly how a KTU 14-mark question on this topic should be answered. Substitute the domain objects if you choose OMTRS or OFDA — the methodology is identical.

## 3.1 Step 1 — Stakeholder Identification

**Step Explanation:** Stakeholders are anyone whose work is affected by the system. We classify them by influence and interest.

| Stakeholder | Type | Interest |
|---|---|---|
| Student (Member) | Primary User | Borrow/return books, search catalog |
| Librarian | Primary User | Issue/return, manage inventory, fines |
| Library Admin | Primary User | Add/remove members, generate reports |
| College Management | Secondary | MIS reports, budget analytics |
| System Admin / DBA | Tertiary | Maintenance, backups, security |
| External Publisher API | External | New book metadata ingestion |

---

## 3.2 Step 2 — Functional Requirements (Sample SRS Extract)

**Step Explanation:** Each FR is uniquely numbered (IEEE 830 standard) for traceability.

| FR-ID | Requirement | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| FR-01 | The system shall allow members to search the catalog by title, author, ISBN, or category. | High |
| FR-02 | The system shall allow librarians to issue a book to a member, decrementing stock atomically. | High |
| FR-03 | The system shall automatically calculate and accrue fines at **₹2.00 per day** for overdue books. | High |
| FR-04 | The system shall send an email reminder **3 days before** the due date. | Medium |
| FR-05 | The system shall prevent a member with **> 2 active overdue books** from issuing more. | High |
| FR-06 | The system shall generate daily, weekly, and monthly MIS reports in **PDF** format. | Medium |
| FR-07 | The system shall support **role-based access control** with 3 roles: Student, Librarian, Admin. | High |

---

## 3.3 Step 3 — Non-Functional Requirements

$$
\text{Availability} \geq 99.5\,\% \quad \text{measured monthly}
$$

$$
\text{Response Time (search)} \leq 2\,\text{seconds at } 500\,\text{concurrent users}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF} \geq 720\,\text{hours} \quad \text{MTTR} \leq 1\,\text{hour}
$$

| NFR-ID | Category | Metric | Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| NFR-01 | Performance | Search response time | **≤ 2 s** |
| NFR-02 | Availability | Uptime | **99.5 %** |
| NFR-03 | Security | Password hashing | **bcrypt, salt rounds = 12** |
| NFR-04 | Usability | Learning time for new user | **≤ 15 minutes** |
| NFR-05 | Scalability | Concurrent users supported | **500** |
| NFR-06 | Maintainability | Cyclomatic complexity per method | **≤ 10** |
| NFR-07 | Portability | Runs on Windows/Linux/macOS | **Yes** |
| NFR-08 | Localization | Supports English + 1 regional language | **Yes** |

---

## 3.4 Step 4 — Use Case Diagram Specification

**Step Explanation:** A use case represents a *complete transaction* yielding an observable result of value to an actor.

### Use Case 1: Issue Book
- **Primary Actor:** Librarian
- **Precondition:** Member is logged-in and authenticated; member has no outstanding fines > ₹100; book stock ≥ 1.
- **Main Flow:**
  1. Librarian enters Member ID.
  2. System fetches and displays member profile.
  3. Librarian scans Book Barcode.
  4. System validates eligibility (active membership, fine limit, quota).
  5. System atomically decrements book stock and creates `Transaction` record with due date = today + **14 days**.
  6. System prints/returns transaction receipt.
- **Postcondition:** Book is issued; stock is decremented; transaction is logged.
- **Alternate Flows:**
  - 4a. Member has overdue books → System displays list, **aborts**.
  - 4b. Book already issued → System shows current holder, **aborts**.
  - 5a. Database deadlock → System retries **up to 3 times**, then aborts.

### Use Case 2: Return Book
- **Primary Actor:** Librarian
- **Main Flow:** Scan book → System fetches transaction → Compute fine = $\max(0, \text{days\_late} \times 2)$ → Mark transaction returned → Increment stock.
- **Postcondition:** Stock incremented, fine recorded (if any).

### Use Case 3: Search Catalog
- **Primary Actor:** Member, Librarian
- **Main Flow:** Enter search keyword → System returns paginated list (20 per page) sorted by relevance.

### Use Case 4: Generate Reports
- **Primary Actor:** Admin
- **Main Flow:** Select report type & date range → System aggregates data → Renders PDF.

---

## 3.5 Step 5 — Class Diagram (Conceptual Design)

**Step Explanation:** Class diagrams show the *static structure* — entities, attributes, operations, and relationships. The CLMS has 7 core classes:

| Class | Key Attributes | Key Operations | Relationships |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Member` | memberId, name, email, phone, maxBooks=3, joinDate | `borrowBook()`, `returnBook()`, `payFine()` | 1 — issues many → `Transaction` |
| `Book` | bookId, isbn, title, author, category, totalCopies, availableCopies | `isAvailable()`, `reserve()` | 1 — categorized by → `Category` |
| `Transaction` | txnId, issueDate, dueDate, returnDate, fineAmount, status | `calculateFine()`, `close()` | * — 1 `Member`, * — 1 `Book` |
| `Librarian` | empId, name, shift | `processIssue()`, `processReturn()` | inherits → `User` |
| `Category` | categoryId, name, location | — | 1 — contains many → `Book` |
| `Fine` | fineId, amount, paidDate, status | `settle()` | 1 — 1 → `Transaction` |
| `User` (abstract) | userId, passwordHash, role, lastLogin | `login()`, `logout()` | Parent of `Member`, `Librarian`, `Admin` |

**Multiplicity Summary:**
- `Member` "1" — "*" `Transaction`
- `Book` "1" — "*" `Transaction`
- `Category` "1" — "*" `Book`

---

## 3.6 Step 6 — Sequence Diagram Walkthrough (Issue Book)

**Step Explanation:** Sequence diagrams show *time-ordered message exchange* between objects. The Issue Book flow involves 5 objects: `LibrarianUI` → `IssueController` → `MemberService` → `BookService` → `TransactionDAO` → `Database`.

The textbook sequence is:
1. `:LibrarianUI` → `:IssueController` : `requestIssue(memberId, bookId)`
2. `:IssueController` → `:MemberService` : `getMember(memberId)`
3. `:MemberService` → `:MemberDAO` : `findById(memberId)`
4. `:MemberDAO` → `DB` : `SELECT * FROM members WHERE id=?`
5. `DB` → `:MemberDAO` : `Member record`
6. `:MemberDAO` → `:MemberService` : `Member object`
7. `:MemberService` → `:IssueController` : `Member object`
8. `:IssueController` → `:MemberService` : `validateEligibility(member)`
9. `:MemberService` → `:IssueController` : `true`
10. `:IssueController` → `:BookService` : `getBook(bookId)`
11. `:BookService` → `:BookDAO` : `findById(bookId)`
12. `:BookDAO` → `DB` : `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` (pessimistic lock)
13. `DB` → `:BookDAO` : `Book record`
14. `:BookDAO` → `:BookService` : `Book object`
15. `:BookService` → `:BookService` : `book.decrementStock()`
16. `:BookService` → `:BookDAO` : `update(book)`
17. `:BookDAO` → `DB` : `UPDATE books SET available=available-1 WHERE id=? AND available>0`
18. `DB` → `:BookDAO` : `1 row affected`
19. `:IssueController` → `:TransactionDAO` : `createTransaction(member, book, dueDate)`
20. `:TransactionDAO` → `DB` : `INSERT INTO transactions(...)`
21. `DB` → `:TransactionDAO` : `txnId`
22. `:TransactionDAO` → `:IssueController` : `Transaction object`
23. `:IssueController` → `:LibrarianUI` : `IssueReceipt(txnId, dueDate)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 12 uses `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`** to acquire a row-level lock, preventing the classic *Lost Update* problem when two librarians try to issue the last copy simultaneously. This is the *Optimistic vs. Pessimistic locking* exam question — **know both**.

---

## 3.7 Step 7 — Architecture Design (3-Tier Layered)

**Step Explanation:** The CLMS follows the classic 3-Tier architecture, separating concerns for maintainability and testability.

| Tier | Sub-Components | Technology Choices |
|---|---|---|
| **Presentation Tier** | JSP/Thymeleaf pages, React UI, Mobile View | HTML5, CSS3, Bootstrap 5, React Native |
| **Application Tier** | Controllers, Services, DAOs, Business Rules | Spring Boot (Java) / Django (Python) / Express (Node) |
| **Data Tier** | Relational DB, Caching, Backups | MySQL 8 + Redis cache + daily cron backups |

**Cross-Cutting Concerns** (implemented via AOP / middleware):
- Authentication & Authorization (Spring Security / JWT)
- Logging (Log4j2 / SLF4J)
- Exception Handling (Global `@ControllerAdvice`)
- Transaction Management (Declarative `@Transactional`)

---

## 3.8 Step 8 — Test Case Sample (Issue Book)

| TC-ID | Test Scenario | Precondition | Test Steps | Test Data | Expected Result | Actual Result | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC-001 | Happy path | Member active, 1 book available, fine = 0 | Login → Issue "ISBN-123" to "M001" | M001, ISBN-123 | Transaction created, stock = 4, due = today+14 | — | — |
| TC-002 | Member has 2 overdue books | 2 overdue active | Attempt issue | M002, ISBN-123 | System rejects with "Overdue quota exceeded" | — | — |
| TC-003 | Book unavailable | Stock = 0 | Attempt issue | M003, ISBN-999 | System rejects with "Book unavailable" | — | — |
| TC-004 | Boundary: fine = ₹100 | Fine = ₹100 exactly | Attempt issue | M004, ISBN-123 | System **allows** (limit is > 100) | — | — |
| TC-005 | Concurrency: last copy | 1 book left, 2 librarians issue simultaneously | Both click Issue at same instant | M005, M006, ISBN-777 | Exactly 1 succeeds, 1 gets "unavailable" | — | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Test Design Techniques used:** Equivalence Partitioning (TC-002 partitions "bad members"), Boundary Value Analysis (TC-004 at the ₹100 threshold), and Concurrency/Stress Testing (TC-005).

---

## 3.9 Step 9 — Effort Estimation (COCOMO II Example)

For CLMS estimated at **3 KLOC**, organic team (5 scale factors, E ≈ 1.12), all EMᵢ = 1.0 (nominal):

$$
\text{Effort} = 2.94 \times 3^{1.12} \times 1 = 2.94 \times 3.585 \approx 10.54 \;\text{person-months}
$$

$$
\text{Schedule} = 3.67 \times 10.54^{0.28} \approx 3.67 \times 2.13 \approx 7.82\;\text{months}
$$

For a 4-member team, that is **2.64 person-months each** — comfortably within a single semester.

## 4.2 Architecture Block Diagram — Home Automation using IoT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CloudTier[Cloud Tier]
        cloudAPI[Cloud API Gateway]
        cloudDB[(Time Series DB)]
        dashboard[Web Dashboard]
        mobileApp[Mobile App]
        rulesEngine[Rules Engine]
    end

    subgraph GatewayTier[Gateway Tier]
        mqttBroker[MQTT Broker]
        localCache[(Local Cache)]
    end

    subgraph EdgeTier[Edge Tier - ESP32 / Arduino]
        sensorNode1[Temp + Humidity Sensor]
        sensorNode2[Motion PIR Sensor]
        actuatorNode1[Relay - Light]
        actuatorNode2[Relay - Fan]
        actuatorNode3[Smart Lock]
    end

    mobileApp -->|HTTPS| cloudAPI
    dashboard -->|HTTPS| cloudAPI
    cloudAPI --> cloudDB
    cloudAPI --> rulesEngine

    sensorNode1 -->|MQTT Publish| mqttBroker
    sensorNode2 -->|MQTT Publish| mqttBroker
    mqttBroker -->|MQTT Subscribe| actuatorNode1
    mqttBroker -->|MQTT Subscribe| actuatorNode2
    mqttBroker -->|MQTT Subscribe| actuatorNode3
    mqttBroker <-->|Sync| cloudAPI
    mqttBroker --> localCache
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:**

1. **Sensors** (DHT11, PIR, LDR) sample environment every **N seconds** and publish JSON payloads to the **MQTT broker** running on a Raspberry Pi gateway.
2. **MQTT Broker** (Mosquitto/EMQX) fans out messages to subscribed actuators and forwards to **Cloud API** (AWS IoT Core / Azure IoT Hub).
3. **Cloud API** persists data in **InfluxDB / TimescaleDB** and triggers the **Rules Engine** (e.g., "if temp > 30 °C → turn on fan").
4. **Mobile App / Dashboard** consumes REST APIs to read state and issue control commands.
5. **Latency budget:** Sensor → Actuator local path ≈ **150 ms**; Cloud round-trip ≈ **600 ms**.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between Functional Requirements (FR) and Non-Functional Requirements (NFR). Give two examples of each in the context of a *Smart Parking System*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition FR, 1 for NFR, 1 for examples):**

- **Functional Requirements** describe *what the system does* — the specific behaviors or functions. Examples for SPS: (i) "The system shall detect a vehicle entering a slot and update the slot status to *Occupied* within 2 seconds." (ii) "The system shall allow a user to reserve a slot for a future time window."
- **Non-Functional Requirements** describe *how well* the system performs those functions — quality attributes. Examples for SPS: (i) **Availability ≥ 99.9 %**; (ii) **Sensor-to-display latency ≤ 1 second**; (iii) The system must support **10,000 concurrent users** without degradation.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**What is a Use Case? Identify any four actors and four use cases for an *Online Food Delivery App*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for use case definition, 1 for actors, 1 for use cases):**

A **Use Case** is a UML modeling construct that captures a complete sequence of actions performed by the system yielding an observable result of value to a particular actor.

**Actors for OFDA:**
1. *Customer* — places food orders.
2. *Restaurant Owner* — manages menu and accepts orders.
3. *Delivery Partner* — picks up and delivers food.
4. *System Administrator* — manages users, resolves disputes.
5. *Payment Gateway* (external) — processes transactions.

**Use Cases for OFDA:**
1. *Browse Restaurants & Menus*
2. *Place Order*
3. *Track Delivery in Real-Time*
4. *Rate and Review Order*
5. *Cancel Order & Request Refund*

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 marks]** For a *College Library Management System*, draw the **Use Case Diagram** and write the detailed use case description for the use case *"Issue Book"*, including preconditions, main flow, alternate flows, and postconditions.

#### Model Solution (7 marks — breakup shown):

**Use Case Diagram (4 marks)** — Must include at least 2 actors (Student, Librarian), 4+ use cases (*Issue Book*, *Return Book*, *Search Catalog*, *Generate Report*, *Pay Fine*), system boundary box, and proper UML notation. *(Draw the diagram using any tool — Mermaid/Visio/hand-drawn — neatness carries 1 mark.)*

**Use Case Description (3 marks) — "Issue Book":**

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **Use Case ID** | UC-002 |
| **Use Case Name** | Issue Book |
| **Primary Actor** | Librarian |
| **Stakeholders & Interests** | Librarian (efficient processing), Member (quick service), Admin (audit trail) |
| **Preconditions** | 1. Librarian is authenticated. 2. Member is registered and active. 3. Member has ≤ 2 overdue books. 4. Member's outstanding fine ≤ ₹100. 5. Book has availableCopies ≥ 1. |
| **Postconditions** | 1. Transaction record created. 2. `Book.availableCopies` decremented by 1. 3. Audit log entry created. |
| **Main Flow** | 1. Librarian enters/scans Member ID → System fetches profile. 2. Librarian scans Book Barcode → System fetches book. 3. System validates eligibility (checks 2, 3, 4, 5). 4. System computes `dueDate = today + 14 days`. 5. System begins DB transaction → decrements stock → inserts transaction → commits. 6. System displays & prints receipt with txnId and dueDate. |
| **Alternate Flow A1** | At step 3, member has > 2 overdue → System displays overdue list, **aborts** with message. |
| **Alternate Flow A2** | At step 3, fine > ₹100 → System shows "Please clear fine first", **aborts**. |
| **Alternate Flow A3** | At step 5, DB deadlock → System retries **up to 3 times** with backoff (100 ms, 200 ms, 400 ms); on failure, **aborts** and logs error. |
| **Alternate Flow A4** | At step 5, `availableCopies = 0` (race condition lost) → System rolls back, **aborts** with "Book just got issued, please retry". |
| **Special Requirements** | All DB operations must be atomic. Receipt must be printable. |
| **Frequency of Use** | ~200 times/day (peak hours) |

---

**(b) [7 marks]** For the same CLMS, identify the **key Non-Functional Requirements** with measurable metrics and propose a suitable **3-Tier Architecture**. Justify the choice of each tier with technology options.

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

**NFR Table (3 marks):**

| NFR | Metric | Target |
|---|---|---|
| Performance | Response time for search | **≤ 2 s at 500 concurrent users** |
| Availability | Monthly uptime | **≥ 99.5 %** |
| Security | Authentication, encryption | OAuth 2.0, bcrypt hashing, HTTPS/TLS 1.3 |
| Scalability | User growth handling | Horizontal scaling via load balancer |
| Maintainability | Cyclomatic complexity per method | **≤ 10** |
| Reliability | MTBF / MTTR | **720 h / 1 h** |
| Usability | New user task completion | **≤ 15 min training** |
| Auditability | All issue/return events logged | 100 % traceability for 1 year |

**3-Tier Architecture (3 marks):**

| Tier | Components | Technology Choices | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Presentation** | Web UI, Mobile-responsive views, Admin panel | HTML5 + React 18 + Bootstrap 5 | Component reusability, mobile-first responsive design |
| **Application (Logic)** | Controllers, Services, DAOs, REST APIs | Spring Boot 3 (Java 17) | Mature ecosystem, dependency injection, AOP for cross-cutting concerns |
| **Data** | RDBMS, Cache, File store, Backup | MySQL 8 + Redis 7 + AWS S3 | ACID for transactions, sub-ms cache, off-site backup |

**Justification (1 mark):**
The 3-Tier architecture is chosen over a monolith because (i) it **separates concerns** enabling parallel development by 3-4 team members, (ii) it allows **independent scaling** of the application tier during exam-time traffic spikes, and (iii) it supports **testability** through mocking of the data tier.

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)

**(a) [7 marks]** For a *Home Automation using IoT* system, draw the **System Architecture** and explain the role of **MQTT protocol**. List any **five sensors** and **five actuators** you would use.

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

**System Architecture (3 marks)** — Refer to **Section 4.2** above. Show: Sensors → Edge MCU (ESP32) → MQTT Broker (Gateway) → Cloud API → Mobile App/Dashboard. Arrows must show **publish/subscribe** directionality.

**Role of MQTT (2 marks):**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a **lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging protocol** designed for constrained devices and unreliable networks. Key characteristics:

1. **Publish-Subscribe model:** Devices *publish* to topics; interested parties *subscribe* — decouples producers from consumers.
2. **Low bandwidth:** Minimum **2-byte header**; ideal for IoT where packets are tiny.
3. **QoS levels:** 0 (at-most-once), 1 (at-least-once), 2 (exactly-once) — choose based on criticality.
4. **Last Will & Testament (LWT):** Notifies others if a device disconnects unexpectedly — critical for *device presence* monitoring.
5. **Retain flag:** New subscribers instantly get the last known value — perfect for sensor state.

**Sensors (1 mark):**
- **DHT22** — temperature & humidity
- **PIR HC-SR501** — motion detection
- **LDR / BH1750** — light intensity
- **MQ-2** — gas/smoke detection
- **HC-SR04** — ultrasonic distance (door/window)
- **Soil moisture** — for plants
- **Current sensor ACS712** — appliance power monitoring

**Actuators (1 mark):**
- **5V Relay modules** — switch lights/fans
- **SG90 Servo motor** — door lock
- **Buzzer** — alarm
- **16x2 LCD / OLED display** — status
- **Stepper motor** — curtain control
- **Solenoid valve** — water flow

---

**(b) [7 marks]** For the same IoT system, derive the **latency budget** for the *Sensor-to-Actuator* control loop and explain the **security considerations** at each layer.

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

**Latency Budget Derivation (4 marks):**

The total end-to-end latency $L_{total}$ is the sum of latencies at each stage:

$$
L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{edge} + L_{net} + L_{cloud} + L_{cmd} + L_{act}
$$

**Step-by-step substitution of measured values:**

| Stage | Operation | Latency (ms) | Cumulative (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{sense}$ | DHT22 sampling + ADC | **50** | 50 |
| $L_{edge}$ | ESP32 JSON encode + MQTT publish | **30** | 80 |
| $L_{net}$ | Wi-Fi transmission to broker | **40** | 120 |
| $L_{cloud}$ | Rule evaluation in Rules Engine | **60** | 180 |
| $L_{cmd}$ | MQTT publish back to actuator | **40** | 220 |
| $L_{act}$ | Relay energize time | **20** | **240** |

$$
L_{total} = 50 + 30 + 40 + 60 + 40 + 20 = 240 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** The local-only path (bypassing cloud) yields $\approx 90$ ms, which is below the **200 ms threshold** for perceived real-time control. The cloud-included path is **240 ms** — acceptable for non-critical home automation but **insufficient for industrial control**.

**Security Considerations (3 marks — 1 mark per layer):**

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors/Actuators):** Use **tamper-resistant hardware**, signed firmware, secure boot. Disable unused debug ports (JTAG).
2. **Network Layer (MQTT/Wi-Fi):** Use **TLS 1.3** for MQTT (port 8883), **WPA3** for Wi-Fi, **mutual TLS** (mTLS) for device authentication, and unique per-device X.509 certificates.
3. **Cloud/Application Layer:** Use **OAuth 2.0 + JWT** for user auth, **AES-256** encryption at rest, role-based access control (RBAC), rate limiting, and audit logging. Apply OWASP IoT Top-10 mitigations.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Project Topic Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Drawing UML diagrams without following UML 2.5 notation** — Examiners deduct **2-3 marks** for using flowchart-style boxes & arrows instead of proper stick-figure actors, oval use cases, and `<extend>`/`<include>` stereotypes.
> 2. **Confusing "use case" with "use case description"** — A use case *diagram* shows the scope; a use case *description* (template above) explains one use case in depth. Examiners ask both separately.
> 3. **Writing NFRs as vague statements** like *"system should be fast"* — This earns **0 marks**. Always quantify: *"search response ≤ 2 s at 500 concurrent users"*.
> 4. **Forgetting to number FRs and link them to use cases** — Traceability matrix (FR ↔ UC ↔ Class ↔ Test Case) is worth **1-2 marks** in advanced questions.
> 5. **Mixing up design patterns** — Singleton ≠ Factory ≠ Strategy. Examiners instantly recognize vague buzzword use.
> 6. **Ignoring concurrency in OMTRS and OFDA** — At least mention **optimistic vs. pessimistic locking** and the **Lost Update problem**.
> 7. **For IoT projects, omitting the communication protocol** — MQTT is the expected default. Saying "uses HTTP" for sensor data loses marks for not optimizing for constrained devices.
> 8. **Skipping the "justification"** for architectural choices — KTU follows Outcome-Based Education (OBE); every design choice MUST be justified with reasoning.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

- [ ] Every KTU project answer must contain: **SRS extract → UML diagrams (≥3) → Architecture → Test cases → Estimation**.
- [ ] **UML 2.5 Notation rules:** Stick-figure actors, oval use cases, rectangle system boundary, solid lines for association, dashed arrows for `<<include>>` / `<<extend>>`, arrowhead direction matters.
- [ ] **Class diagram relationships to memorize:** Association, Aggregation (open diamond), Composition (filled diamond), Generalization/Inheritance (open triangle), Dependency (dashed arrow), Realization (dashed triangle).
- [ ] **State diagrams** (not flowcharts) for OMTRS ticket lifecycle, OFDA order lifecycle, SPS slot status, CLMS book status.
- [ ] **Activity diagrams** for business workflows — use swim-lanes when actors are involved.
- [ ] **Sequence diagrams** show time on the vertical axis; objects on the horizontal; message arrows are *horizontal*, return arrows are *dashed horizontal*.
- [ ] **Non-Functional Requirements MUST be quantified** — always pair with a number and unit (ms, %, users, MB).
- [ ] **3-Tier vs. Microservices:** Choose **3-Tier** for CLMS (low scale, single team). Choose **Microservices** for OMTRS and OFDA (high scale, independent deploys). Choose **Edge-Cloud Hybrid** for HA-IoT and SPS.
- [ ] **MQTT is the default IoT protocol** — know its 3 QoS levels and Pub-Sub model.
- [ ] **COCOMO II formula** is the expected estimation technique for any "effort" sub-question: $\text{Effort} = A \times \text{Size}^{E} \times \prod \text{EM}_i$.
- [ ] **Design patterns to associate:** Library → Strategy (fine calc), Repository (data). Movie → Observer (seat updates), Facade (payment). Food → State (order), Factory (notifications). IoT → Pub-Sub. Parking → Singleton (lot config), Observer (slot updates).
- [ ] **Traceability Matrix** (FR ↔ UC ↔ Class ↔ Test Case) is a **bonus mark-grabber** — include it whenever a question asks about completeness.
- [ ] **Testing techniques:** Equivalence Partitioning, Boundary Value Analysis, Decision Table, State Transition — name the technique you're using.
- [ ] **For IoT projects, always include a latency budget table** with explicit ms per stage.
- [ ] **Security is non-optional:** mention OAuth 2.0, JWT, bcrypt, TLS 1.3, mTLS, RBAC in *every* project — examiners mark it.
- [ ] **Always justify your architectural choice** — KTU follows OBE; reasoning matters as much as the answer.
